The BCR method shows substantial gains, suggesting that controlled retrieval and reasoning boundaries can significantly improve model performance and interpretability in this domain, though further validation and generalization are warranted.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have recently shown immense potential in automated content moderation, sparking growing interest in developing harmful-video benchmarks. However, we identify two primary limitations in existing works: 1) The multi-layered characteristics of harmful videos are overlooked. Existing benchmarks predominantly formulate evaluation as a binary classification task, failing to capture implicit or deep contextual harms. 2) Explanatory rationales are completely absent. Current frameworks measure exclusively whether a model flags a video correctly rather than explaining why, turning evaluation into a black box where models can succeed through superficial shortcuts. To address these problems, we present HarmVideoBench, a multi-layered diagnostic benchmark comprising 1,379 videos paired with 4,137 multiple-choice questions. HarmVideoBench benchmarks three hierarchical dimensions: Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, and Beyond-Clip Reasoning, aiming to evaluate models' deep understanding beyond surface cues with carefully balanced and curated samples. We evaluate 19 leading models on HarmVideoBench to assess their multidimensional understanding of harmful videos. Moreover, we introduce BCR, a benchmark-aligned method that predicts reasoning boundaries and dynamically retrieves context only when needed. Experimental results show that BCR substantially improves the base model's performance in harmful video understanding, raising the macro average from 61.7 percent to a state-of-the-art 84.4 percent.

</details>


### [78] [LISA: Likelihood Score Alignment for Visual-condition Controllable Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27192)
*Yanghao Wang,Hongxu Chen,Jiazhen Liu,Zhenqi He,Rui Liu,Zhen Wang,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LISA regularizes the side network by aligning its intermediate features with an approximated likelihood score, accelerating training and improving conditional generation without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve the role of the dual-branch paradigm in score-based generation; main network provides prior unconditional score, side network adds conditional control via a likelihood score; existing training is inefficient and insufficiently disentangled.

Method: Hook designated side network layer features, project into a score latent space with a lightweight decoder, construct an approximated likelihood score target, minimize the distance between decoder output and target as a regularization loss, jointly train side network and decoder with standard diffusion loss; no extra inference cost.

Result: LISA consistently accelerates training convergence and improves final generation quality across image/video tasks and architectures, and yields more disentangled side-network features for conditional modeling, with negligible training overhead and zero extra inference cost.

Conclusion: Explicit likelihood-score alignment is an effective regularization for dual-branch conditioning in diffusion/flow models, generalizable across tasks and architectures, enabling more efficient and controllable generative modeling.

Abstract: The prevalent dual-branch paradigm, i.e., training a side network to encode visual conditions and fusing its intermediate-layer features to a frozen pretrained main network, has shown remarkable success in visual-condition controllable generation. Despite its widespread adoption, the role of the side branch and its training efficiency remain underexplored. In this paper, we first revisit this mainstream paradigm through the lens of score-based generative modeling: 1) The main network preserves visual perceptual quality by providing a prior unconditional score. 2) The side network steers conditional control by implicitly contributing a likelihood score. Guided by this perspective, we propose LIkelihood Score Alignment (LISA), an effective regularization method that explicitly aligns the intermediate feature of the side network with an approximated likelihood score. Specifically, we first hook features from a designated layer of the side network and project them into the score latent space by a lightweight decoder. Then, we construct an approximated likelihood score target and calculate the distance between the decoder's output and this target as an additional regularization loss. Finally, we jointly optimize the side network and decoder with both standard diffusion loss and our regularization loss. Experiments across various image/video tasks, architectures, and diffusion/flow models demonstrated that LISA can not only consistently accelerate the training convergence and improve final synthetic results, but also encourage the side network's features to be more disentangled for conditional modeling with negligible additional training cost and zero extra inference cost.

</details>


### [79] [SatSplatDiff: Geometry-preserving generative refinement for high-fidelity satellite Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27223)
*Jiyong Kim,Shuang Song,Ronjgun Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SatSplatDiff advances Gaussian Splatting for satellite 3D reconstruction by incorporating monocular depth supervision, multi-scale geometry refinement, and shadow-guided generative refinement to preserve geometry while improving visual fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art results on IARPA2016 and DFC2019 with up to 5x resolution and minimal hallucination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited top-down satellite imagery provides weak supervision for surface geometry, especially facades, leading to holes and degraded fidelity. While generative refinement can boost appearance, independent per-view refinement can cause hallucinations and geometric degradation. A geometry-aware refinement approach is needed.

Method: Build on photogrammetric DSM initialization and 2DGS-based shadow casting from SatSplat. Introduce monocular depth supervision and multi-scale geometric refinement to enforce geometric regularization. Apply shadow-guided generative refinement where computed shadow maps steer Gaussians to stay consistent with underlying geometry, thus improving visual fidelity without compromising geometry.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on IARPA2016 and DFC2019, reducing geometric MAE by up to 18% and improving visual fidelity (FID-CLIP) by 28–45% over baselines. Achieves up to 5x resolution with minimal hallucination and strong sensor-consistent cross-tile appearance, supporting scalability to large-scale reconstructions.

Conclusion: Shadow-guided, geometry-regularized generative refinement effectively reduces geometric degradation while enhancing visual fidelity, yielding high-quality, scalable satellite 3D reconstructions. Source code available.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has been recently explored for satellite 3D reconstruction, demonstrating flexibility and efficiency in representing radiometrically diverse satellite scenes. However, the limited top viewpoint of satellite imagery results in insufficient supervision on building facades, leaving surface holes and degraded visual fidelity. Generative refinement, which leverages pretrained generative priors to iteratively refine and update the rendered images used as supervision targets, has recently been investigated to improve the visual fidelity of Gaussian-rendered images. However, since these models refine each view independently, the resulting images can generate hallucinations and break photo-consistency, leading to geometric degradation. To address these limitations, we propose SatSplatDiff, which aims to minimize geometric degradation prevalent in generative refinement. Building on photogrammetric DSM initialization and 2DGS-based shadow casting established in our prior work SatSplat, we first introduce monocular depth supervision and multi-scale geometric refinement to establish a geometrically accurate and well-regularized surface representation. We then apply shadow-guided generative refinement, where geometrically calculated shadow maps guide the Gaussians to maintain consistency with the underlying geometry, improving visual fidelity while reducing geometric degradation. Extensive evaluations on the IARPA2016 and DFC2019 datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, reducing geometric MAE by up to 18% and improving visual fidelity (FID-CLIP) by 28-45% over existing baselines. Our method delivers up to 5x resolution enhancement with minimal hallucination and sensor-consistent appearance, demonstrating seamless cross-tile consistency and strong scalability for large-scale reconstruction. Source code is available at https://github.com/GDAOSU/SatSplatDiff

</details>


### [80] [CORTEX: A Structured Reasoning Benchmark for Trustworthy 3D Chest CT MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27264)
*Hashmat Shadab Malik,Anees Ur Rehman Hashmi,Numan Saeed,Muzammal Naseer,Salman Khan,Christoph Lippert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CORTEX offers a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT with four-stage traces and a verification protocol, built on CT-RATE; 76k traces across VQA and report generation to enable trainable, explainable MLLMs; release upon acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for interpretable, traceable reasoning in 3D radiology; existing datasets provide only final answers and lack structured evidence linking findings to conclusions; absence of supervised reasoning and verification protocol.

Method: Generate four-stage diagnostic traces using frontier LLMs, then filter/verify with a stage-level rubric and radiologist review; design with clinicians; apply on CT-RATE data; create 76,177 validated traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation; provide evaluation protocol.

Result: A large curated dataset of structured reasoning traces and an accompanying evaluation rubric; demonstrates viability of scalable, clinician-aligned reasoning supervision for 3D chest CT MLLMs; code/data to be released publicly upon acceptance.

Conclusion: CORTEX supplies the required structured supervision and evaluation framework to train and assess trustworthy reasoning in 3D chest CT MLLMs, advancing interpretable AI in radiology.

Abstract: Reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, this reasoning is usually free-form text judged only by its final answer, making it hard to interpret and verify, especially in 3D radiology, where a diagnosis should be traceable to evidence in the scan. Existing chest CT question-answering datasets compound this by reducing expert radiology reports to answer-only pairs, dropping the reasoning that links findings to conclusions and omitting the patient history clinicians rely on. As a result, reasoning-capable 3D chest CT MLLMs remain out of reach, as neither the structured supervision needed to train them nor the protocol needed to verify their reasoning yet exists. We introduce CORTEX (Clinically Organized Reasoning and sTructured EXplanation), a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT. For each question, CORTEX restores the missing reasoning as a four-stage diagnostic trace mirroring a radiologist's workflow: task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, and answer synthesis. We generate these traces using frontier large language models with broad medical and general-domain knowledge, then filter and verify them with a stage-level evaluation protocol combining automated rubric scoring with expert radiologist review. Crucially, both the reasoning structure and evaluation rubrics are designed in close collaboration with clinicians. Built on CT-RATE, a large, publicly available chest CT dataset without reasoning annotations, CORTEX comprises 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation, providing both the structured supervision and the stage-level evaluation protocol needed to build and evaluate trustworthy reasoning models for 3D chest CT. Our dataset and evaluation code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [81] [Exact and Deterministic Patch Descriptor Retrieval via Hierarchical Normalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27280)
*Koichi Sato*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HN enables exact, deterministic patch descriptor retrieval with provable pruning, achieving significant speedups over brute-force while preserving exactness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide exact nearest-neighbor retrieval for image patch descriptors without the nondeterminism or inaccuracies of approximate NN methods, enabling reproducible results and efficient search.

Method: Introduce Hierarchical Normalization (HN) that splits a 128-dim descriptor into a K-dim major component with norm sqrt(1-alpha) and a (128-K)-dim minor component with norm sqrt(alpha). The major inner product is used for an admissible upper bound on full similarity via Cauchy–Schwarz, enabling a provably exact branch-and-bound scan: scan the major component for all entries, then evaluate the full 128-dim vector only on candidates that cannot be pruned. Train HN-modified HardNet on the Notre Dame split of the UBC patch dataset and evaluate on Trevi and Half Dome. Use a cache-optimized Structure-of-Arrays layout and test (K=8, alpha=1/32) and (K=16, alpha=1/8).

Result: Achieves 13.7x (Trevi) and 12.7x (Half Dome) speed-up over brute-force 128-dim search with only 0.4% of entries requiring full evaluation for K=8, alpha=1/32. For K=16, alpha=1/8, FPR@95 rises slightly from 0.0062 to 0.0064 on Trevi at 7.2x speed-up, with 98.8% of entries bypassing full evaluation.

Conclusion: The approach delivers provable exactness with substantial speedups and deterministic behavior, outperforming common ANN methods in reproducibility while incurring minimal accuracy loss at chosen parameters. Practical deployment depends on the required exactness and acceptable FPR, and the method shows strong promise for patch-descriptor retrieval and similar high-dimensional similarity tasks.

Abstract: We present a patch descriptor retrieval method that returns the exact nearest neighbour -- provably identical to exhaustive full-vector search -- while evaluating only a small fraction of the database, and does so deterministically: the same (database, query) pair always produces the same result, independent of run order, thread count, or hardware. This contrasts with approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) approaches such as HNSW and IVF-PQ, which trade exactness for speed and may return different results across runs. The enabling mechanism is Hierarchical Normalization (HN): a normalisation scheme that splits the pre-normalisation feature vector into a K-dim major component (norm sqrt(1-alpha)) and a (128-K)-dim minor component (norm sqrt(alpha)). Since the minor inner product is bounded by alpha (Cauchy-Schwarz on the prescribed norms), the major similarity plus alpha is an admissible upper bound on the full similarity: the search scans the K-dim major component for all entries, then applies full 128-dim evaluation only to entries that cannot be pruned -- a provably exact branch-and-bound scan. We train HN-modified HardNet on the notredame split of the UBC patch dataset and evaluate on trevi and halfdome. With a cache-optimised Structure-of-Arrays layout and K=8, alpha=1/32, the search achieves 13.7x (trevi) / 12.7x (halfdome) speed-up over brute-force 128-dim search, with only 0.4% of entries requiring full evaluation. At K=16, alpha=1/8, FPR@95 rises from 0.0062 to 0.0064 on trevi at 7.2x speed-up, with 98.8% of entries bypassing full evaluation.

</details>


### [82] [Sculpting NeRF Geometry: Human-Preference Fine-Tuning of a 3D-Aware Face GAN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27305)
*Archer Moore,Mingming Gong,Liam Hodgkinson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RLHF on a NeRF-based 3D face generator (EG3D) trained directly from a density-field reward; no meshes or 2D supervision; improves geometry with a modest FID increase; high user preference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on explicit surface representations and mesh priors by optimizing directly on 3D density fields using human feedback, enabling geometry improvements with a lightweight reward signal.

Method: Fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware generator (EG3D) from a learned reward over radiance-field density σ values; reward model requires no pretraining and uses a small preference dataset; density-consistency constraint preserves 2D appearance; no text conditioning, mesh extraction, or multi-view rendering; trained on an unconditional 3D-aware face GAN with a single annotator.

Result: Geometry improved; 74.4% of pairwise comparisons prefer the fine-tuned generator; FID-50k increases from 4.09 to 6.66.

Conclusion: RLHF directly on density signals can steer NeRF-based 3D generators toward better geometry with a controllable, modest impact on 2D appearance and without surface supervision or mesh priors; demonstrates viability of density-field rewards for 3D generation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for 3D generation is now established across a number of works, but most existing pipelines optimise explicit surface representations, often by converting radiance fields into meshes and training heavily on surface-supervised data. We instead fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware generative model directly from a learned reward over radiance-field density ($σ$) values, with no externally supplied mesh or shape prior. The reward model requires no pretraining, trains easily on a small set of preference samples, and yields robust improvement in 3D geometry. Working on an unconditional 3D-aware face GAN (EG3D), our reward reads the continuous 3D density field of the neural radiance field (NeRF) directly and supplies a geometry-only learning signal, requiring neither text conditioning, mesh extraction, nor multi-view rendering. A density-consistency constraint keeps the 2D appearance qualitatively similar while the geometry is reshaped, at a measurable but bounded distributional cost (FID-50k rises from 4.09 to 6.66): the fine-tuned generator, trained from the preferences of a single annotator as a proof of concept, produces face geometries preferred by users in 74.4% of pairwise comparisons.

</details>


### [83] [See & Sniff: Learning Visuo-Olfactory Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27307)
*Seongyu Kim,Seungwoo Lee,Hyeonggon Ryu,Joon Son Chung,Arda Senocak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SmellNet-V dataset and See & Sniff framework for learning joint visuo-olfactory representations; achieves smell classification, cross-modal retrieval, and odor localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Olfaction is underexplored in multimodal learning due to scarce paired visuo-olfactory data. The authors exploit semantic invariance of odor identity to visually augment unimodal olfactory data, enabling scalable cross-modal benchmarking.

Method: Construct SmellNet-V by synthetically pairing smell-only samples with semantically aligned in-the-wild images; propose See & Sniff, a self-supervised framework that learns dense visuo-olfactory representations and generates smell saliency maps; introduce a pixel-level smell localization task and benchmark.

Result: SmellNet-V-enabled method surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification from smell alone and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and smell localization.

Conclusion: Visuo-olfactory learning is a viable, scalable direction for multimodal perception, enabling spatial grounding of odor sources and cross-modal tasks via synthetic data pairing and self-supervised training.

Abstract: While modern multimodal models integrate vision with language, audio, or touch, olfaction remains largely unexplored due to the lack of paired visuo-olfactory data. We introduce SmellNet-V, a scalable visuo-olfactory dataset built on the insight that odor identity is largely invariant to visual transformations within a semantic category. This allows us to synthetically pair smell-only samples with semantically aligned in-the-wild web images, converting a unimodal olfactory dataset into a cross-modal benchmark without costly co-collection. Building on this dataset, we propose See & Sniff, a self-supervised framework that learns joint visuo-olfactory representations via dense local alignment and naturally produces smell saliency maps for spatial grounding of odor sources. We further introduce pixel-level smell localization task and a benchmark for evaluation. Our method surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification from smell alone and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and smell localization, establishing visuo-olfactory learning as a new direction in multimodal perception.

</details>


### [84] [ViQ: Text-Aligned Visual Quantized Representations at Any Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27313)
*Xumin Yu,Zuyan Liu,Zhenyu Yang,Yuhao Dong,Shengsheng Qian,Jiwen Lu,Han Hu,Yongming Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViQ introduces Visual Quantized Representations, a two-stage framework that balances semantics and reconstruction details while supporting native-resolution inputs; it achieves competitive multimodal performance and substantial training speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Representing images as discrete signals often causes information loss and forces a trade-off between low-level details and high-level semantics. A unified, efficient discrete visual representation is needed to enable scalable multimodal modeling with text and vision.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) text-aligned pre-training to inject semantic supervision from a pretrained language model and enable native-resolution visual inputs; (2) discretization via proximal representation learning to progressively compress the feature space, plus a position-aware, head-wise quantization mechanism to handle arbitrary resolutions.

Result: ViQ achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art multimodal encoders with continuous and high-dimensional features, while preserving high-fidelity low-level reconstruction. Multimodal training with ViQ yields significant efficiency gains, accelerating training by 20%–70% across different base LLMs and training recipes.

Conclusion: ViQ provides a unified, efficient discrete representation for arbitrary visual inputs, enabling effective multimodal modeling and training with improved efficiency while maintaining reconstruction detail.

Abstract: A unified representation for text and vision is a natural pursuit, as it enables simpler multimodal modeling and more efficient training. However, representing images as discrete signals in the same way as text inevitably introduces severe information loss. Existing work struggles to balance low-level details and high-level semantics in discrete representations: reconstruction-oriented representations often lack semantic information, whereas semantically stronger features typically suffer from severe loss of detail. We present ViQ, a Visual Quantized Representations framework, which is designed to balance semantics and details in discrete representations while supporting inputs at native resolutions, thereby enabling it to serve as a unified and general discrete representation for arbitrary visual inputs. Our approach structures quantization learning into two stages: text-aligned pre-training and feature discretization. With text-aligned pre-training, we enhance the visual encoder semantic-rich supervision from the pretrained language model and enable it to process native-resolution visual inputs. During discretization, we propose a proximal representation learning strategy to progressively compact the feature space, along with a position-aware head-wise quantization mechanism that enables flexible processing of arbitrary resolutions. Extensive experiments on multimodal tasks demonstrate that ViQ achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art multimodal vision encoders with continuous and high-dimensional visual features, while maintaining high precision in low-level reconstruction. We also show that multimodal training with visual quantized representations largely improves efficiency, yielding up to 20\%-70\% acceleration with different base LLMs and training recipes.

</details>


### [85] [Not All Actions Are Equal: Rethinking Conditioning for Dexterous World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27325)
*Zizhao Yuan,Zhengtu Liang,Taowen Wang,Qiwei Liang,Yichi Wang,Yunheng Wang,Yuetong Fang,Lusong Li,Zecui Zeng,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DexAC-WM introduces structured action conditioning for high-DoF control via per-dimension action tokenization and a semantic grounding branch, improving action-conditioned video prediction realism and fidelity with scalable backbone applicability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-DoF dexterous actions are heterogeneous across dimensions; global compression of actions causes imbalance and hinders modeling of fine-grained effects. A structured, semantically grounded approach is needed to improve action-conditioned world models.

Method: Use action tokenization to preserve dimension-level semantics with local refinement and global modulation of visual dynamics; add a semantic branch that supplies rich object-scene priors; validate on EgoDex/EgoVerse and test backbone scalability.

Result: Significant improvements in FID, FVD, and PCK, indicating better visual-temporal realism and action-following; evidence of scalability across backbones and effectiveness of combining structured action conditioning with semantic grounding.

Conclusion: Structured action modeling coupled with semantic grounding is essential for scaling world models to high-DoF control and improved action-conditioned video prediction.

Abstract: Recent advances in action-conditioned world models show promising progress in modeling complex interactions and forecasting future states under diverse action sequences. While these models are often driven by stronger visual representations and model capacity, action conditioning itself remains underexplored. Most existing approaches compress the entire action sequence into a single representation, which works well for low-DoF control but becomes less reliable in high-DoF scenarios. We observe that high-DoF dexterous actions are inherently heterogeneous, spanning multiple orders of magnitude, where large-scale motions coexist with subtle but important signals. When uniformly aggregated, optimization exhibits an imbalance across action components, which hinders the modeling of fine-grained effects and affects action fidelity. We therefore propose DexAC-WM, which treats action conditioning as a structured process rather than global compression. DexAC preserves dimension-level semantics via action tokenization and aligns action signals with visual dynamics through local refinement and global modulation. To address the limited high-level semantic grounding in existing world models, we further introduce a semantic branch that provides rich object-scene priors, which enables world model to capture dynamic visual details while supporting high-DoF action-conditioned video prediction. Experiments on EgoDex and EgoVerse show that combining the semantic branch with DexAC significantly improves FID, FVD, and PCK, demonstrating gains in visual-temporal realism and action-following consistency. We further verify that DexAC extends to other backbones, showing the scalability of our structured action-conditioning design. These results suggest that scaling world models to high-DoF control requires both structured action modeling and semantic grounding.

</details>


### [86] [RoPEMover: Depth-Aware Object Relocation via Positional Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27332)
*Ipek Oztas,Duygu Ceylan,Aybars Bugra Aksoy,Aysegul Dundar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geometry-aware object motion in a single image using depth-aware RoPE diffusion transformers; manipulates rotational positional embeddings to induce motion, extended to 3D, trained with synthetic data and limited real images via parameter-efficient fine-tuning; achieves state-of-the-art results while preserving object identity and scene-consistent effects like shadows and illumination under large displacements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve geometry-consistent object rearrangement in single-image motion tasks where occlusions, revealed regions, and scene-level lighting/shadows must be maintained, addressing shortcomings of existing methods that lack scene-level consistency.

Method: Operate on positional representations of diffusion transformers with rotary positional embeddings (RoPE). Extend RoPE to depth-aware 3D spatial encoding to support consistent object displacement. Train on synthetic data plus a small real-image subset using parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across standard object motion benchmarks. The method preserves object identity under large displacements, generates plausible content in newly revealed regions, and maintains scene-dependent effects such as shadows and illumination.

Conclusion: A depth-aware RoPE-based diffusion framework enables geometry-consistent single-image object motion, achieving strong performance with minimal real supervision and maintaining scene-level coherence.

Abstract: Moving an object in a single image requires geometry-consistent spatial rearrangement, including handling occlusions, revealing previously unseen regions, and maintaining coherent shadows and reflections. Existing approaches are not well suited to this setting and often fail to preserve such scene-level consistency. We address this problem by introducing a geometry-aware object motion method that operates directly on the positional representations of diffusion transformers. Our key insight is that rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) define a structured spatial field that can be explicitly manipulated to induce controlled motion. We extend 2D RoPE into a depth-aware formulation that encodes 3D spatial structure, enabling consistent object displacement and scene-aware updates. Our model is trained using synthetic data combined with a small set of real images via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Despite minimal real supervision, it preserves object identity under large spatial displacements, generates plausible content in newly revealed regions, and consistently updates scene-dependent effects such as shadows and illumination. Experimental results on standard object motion benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [87] [SAM2Matting: Generalized Image and Video Matting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27339)
*Ruiqi Shen,Guangquan Jie,Chang Liu,Henghui Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAM2Matting: a tracker-to-matting framework that augments VOS trackers with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads, decoupling temporal tracking from fine-grained matting to achieve state-of-the-art, image-trained video matting with strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between frame-wise tracking and pixel-accurate matting, avoid reliance on costly video matting datasets, and improve out-of-domain generalization and robustness of video matting.

Method: Enhance a foundational VOS tracker (e.g., SAM2/SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads to decouple tracking and matting; the tracker maintains temporal consistency while the matting heads refine details; supports diverse prompts and is trained on image data.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on video matting, exhibits strong temporal consistency, generalizes across human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios, and demonstrates robustness across prompt types despite image-only training.

Conclusion: A decoupled tracker-to-matting framework can unlock high-fidelity video matting with image-only training, by leveraging region proposals and specialized matting heads to preserve temporal coherence and capture fine details, with strong generalization.

Abstract: Despite impressive advances in image matting, video matting remains challenging due to the inherent gap between high-level tracking, which requires frame-wise understanding, and low-level matting, which focuses on extremely fine-grained details. Existing methods attempt this with expensive and narrowly-scoped video matting datasets, which may limit out-of-domain generalization and compromise tracking robustness. We rethink the paradigm with SAM2Matting, a tracker-to-matting framework that advances VOS trackers to high-fidelity video matting. Specifically, it decouples the task by enhancing a foundational tracker (e.g., SAM2, SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads, enabling the uncompromised tracker to handle temporal consistency while the matting components resolve fine-grained details. Notably, despite being trained only on images, SAM2Matting establishes new state-of-the-art performance on video matting, supports diverse prompt types, maintains strong temporal consistency, and demonstrates robust generalization across both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios.

</details>


### [88] [RayPE: Ray-Space Positional Encoding for 3D-Aware Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27345)
*Minghao Yin,Jiahao Lu,Wenbo Hu,Wang Zhao,Shan Ying,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RayPE adds 6D Plucker coordinates to Q/K in self-attention to encode 3D geometry of camera rays in video diffusion transformers. It yields a geometry-aware attention with additive injection and decomposed terms (content, geometry, and cross-terms), shows stability across heterogeneous camera scales, uses minimal parameter overhead (<0.1%), and improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality across four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RoPE on the (u,v,t) sampling grid ignores the 3D structure of the scene. The geometric relation between camera rays is bilinear, captured by the Plucker reciprocal product, which mirrors the attention dot product. Leveraging this analogy can inject geometry directly into self-attention.

Method: Introduce RayPE: per-token 6D Plucker coordinates injected additively into queries and keys. Use a query/key flip so the symmetric identity aligns with the reciprocal product. The attention score decomposes into content, geometry, and cross-terms. Stabilize across varying translation scales by decoupling ray direction from magnitude, gating by a learned function of log magnitude, and applying RMSNorm to align with the content branch. The module adds <0.1% parameters to a pretrained video DiT, is zero-initialized, and trained on a four-dataset mixture.

Result: Ablations show that each term (content, geometry, and cross-terms) is individually necessary. The encoding improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality across four datasets, with negligible parameter overhead and stable performance across heterogeneous camera scales.

Conclusion: RayPE provides a geometry-aware positional encoding for video diffusion transformers, enabling improved 3D consistency and controllability with minimal parameter overhead, validated on a four-dataset mixture.

Abstract: Modern video diffusion transformers position their tokens through RoPE on the (u,v,t) axes -- a description of the camera's sampling grid that says nothing about the 3D structure of the scene. We observe that the geometric relation between two camera rays is captured by the Plucker reciprocal product, which is bilinear in the two rays -- the same algebraic form as the dot product in Transformer attention. Building on this analogy, we propose RayPE, a positional-encoding extension that injects per-token 6D Plucker coordinates additively into the queries and keys of self-attention, with a query/key flip arrangement under which the symmetric identity configuration coincides exactly with the reciprocal product. The injection is additive, the resulting attention score decomposes into a content term, a geometry term, and two content and geometry cross-terms -- all of which our experiments find individually necessary. To make the encoding stable across video data with heterogeneous camera-translation scales (SfM, deep SLAM, metric), we further decouple ray direction from moment magnitude, gate the encoding by a learned function of the log-magnitude, and apply RMSNorm to align it with the QKNorm-normalized content branch. The full module adds less than 0.1% parameters to a pretrained video DiT, is zero-initialized to start from the pretrained weights, and improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality on a four-dataset training mixture.

</details>


### [89] [PhysiFormer: Learning to Simulate Mechanics in World Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27364)
*Yiming Chen,Yushi Lan,Andrea Vedaldi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based transformer models directly predict 3D object vertex trajectories in world coordinates, enabling physically plausible, uncertainty-aware motion of rigid and elastic meshes without hard inductive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a view-invariant, geometry-aware world model for robotics/graphics that can handle unobserved uncertainty and multi-object scenes without relying on ad-hoc latent spaces or explicit rigidity constraints.

Method: Represent objects as 3D meshes in world coordinates and model vertex trajectories via a coordinate-space denoising diffusion process. Use object-level attention with time/space/object factorization to achieve permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning. Train on 100k+ simulated trajectories to capture rigid and elastic dynamics and generalize to mixed-materials, unseen geometries, and more objects.

Result: The method yields accurate vertex trajectories, preserves rigidity and momentum-based physics better than autoregressive baselines, provides diverse futures through uncertainty, and generalizes across materials, geometries, and object counts.

Conclusion: Coordinate-space diffusion is a promising direction for view-invariant, geometry-aware world modeling with potential applications in robotics, graphics, and physical design; code and models are publicly available.

Abstract: We present PhysiFormer, a diffusion transformer for physically-plausible 3D object motion. Unlike video world models that operate in view-dependent pixel space, PhysiFormer represents objects as 3D meshes expressed in world coordinates. Given the initial vertex positions and velocities, as well as object material type, rigid or elastic, the model samples future vertex trajectories. While related neural physics approaches build on ad-hoc latent spaces or explicitly enforce rigidity and causality, PhysiFormer shows that excellent results can be obtained without any such inductive biases, by casting vertex trajectory prediction as a single denoising diffusion process directly in world coordinates. The probabilistic formulation captures uncertainty in the learned dynamics, enabling diverse plausible futures from initial conditions, making this framework potentially useful for applications with unobserved uncertainty. The model features attention factorised over time, space, and objects for efficiency, enabling permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning without needing explicit object encoding. Trained on over 100k simulated trajectories, PhysiFormer generates rigid and elastic mechanics, and generalises to mixed-material settings, unseen real-world geometries, and larger object counts. It substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based physical consistency. Our results position coordinate-space diffusion as a promising step toward view-invariant, geometry-aware world modelling for robotics, graphics, and physical design. Visualisations, code, and models are available at https://yimingc9.github.io/physiformer.

</details>


### [90] [Don't Settle at the Mode! Mitigating Diversity Collapse in Pretrained Flow Models via Feature Self-Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27371)
*Pradhaan S Bhat,Rishubh Parihar,Abhijnya Bhat,R. Venkatesh Babu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free self-guidance mechanism for conditional flow models that mitigates diversity collapse by dispersing internal features during batch generation and applying manifold regularization; plug-and-play with pretrained models and minimal inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diversity collapse in conditional flow-based image generation during sampling; reliance on latent guidance or external reward models is costly.

Method: Introduce feature self-guidance that disperses internal features during batch generation, plus a manifold regularization step to project dispersed features back to the data manifold; integrates as a plug-and-play module with low overhead.

Result: Experiments show notable improvements in sample diversity while preserving fidelity across multiple conditional flow models, including multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation.

Conclusion: The approach offers an efficient, training-free, plug-and-play solution to diversity collapse in conditional flow models, balancing diversity and fidelity with minimal inference cost.

Abstract: State-of-the-art flow models generate stunning images from text or image prompts. However, they suffer from diversity collapse when generating multiple samples under the same conditioning. Existing methods address this issue via either latent guidance, which has limited effectiveness, or sample selection, which relies on external reward models that incur significant inference-time overhead. In this work, we introduce an efficient, training-free self-guidance mechanism to mitigate diversity collapse without requiring additional reward models. Specifically, we disperse the internal features of the flow model during batch generation with feature self-guidance. Further, to keep the features close to the manifold, we introduce a manifold regularization step that projects these dispersed features back onto the data manifold, ensuring diverse generation without sacrificing alignment with the input conditions. Our method integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module into pretrained flow models, adding only a marginal inference cost. Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in diversity while preserving fidelity across several conditional flow models, including multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation.

</details>


### [91] [DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27372)
*Ron Campos,Subhajit Maity,Xin Li,Srijan Das,Aritra Dutta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DnA introduces a denoising attention mechanism that uses a positive and a negative query to sift image features, projecting interactions into two separated subspaces with larger principal angles to improve discriminability. It yields consistent gains across image and video understanding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard softmax-based attention in multihead attention often produces noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features, limiting performance in visual perception tasks.

Method: In DnA, a positive query identifies features belonging to the correct class while a negative query identifies correlated but irrelevant features. These interactions are projected into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles, promoting subspace separation and improved discriminability. The approach is evaluated with a ViT-B backbone.

Result: ImageNet-1K with ViT-B: absolute gain of 0.8 percentage points over baseline. Also improves video understanding: 1.8% on video transformers and 0.5% on video LLMs. Extensive analyses support the two-subspace design and denoising effect.

Conclusion: DnA demonstrates that separating attended features into two interacting subspaces and denoising attention patterns yields clearer discriminative signals and cross-domain gains in image and video understanding tasks.

Abstract: The softmax activation in multihead attention (MHA) is the de facto standard for attention-based models in visual perception tasks. However, standard softmax can produce noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features and degrade its performance. In this paper, we propose Denoising Attention or DnA, in which, first, a positive query identifies which image features belong to the correct class, and a negative query identifies closely associated but irrelevant image features. DnA then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles, promoting subspace separation and improved discriminability. Using a ViT-B backbone, our proposed DnA achieves an absolute gain of 0.8% on ImageNet-1K compared to the baseline. We further show improvements across multiple visual understanding tasks, including video understanding with video transformers (1.8%) and video LLMs (0.5%). Our extensive empirical analyses justify the design choices involving two interacting subspaces and the denoising effect of DnA.

</details>


### [92] [Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27373)
*Shravan Venkatraman,Ritesh Thawkar,Omkar Thawakar,Rao Muhammad Anwer,Hisham Cholakkal,Salman Khan,Fahad Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised self-evolution via two invariance rewards (geometric and semantic) to fix visual under-conditioning in LMMs, improving alignment between vision and language without labels; strong CIDEr gains and reduced hallucination on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving LMMs optimize answer agreement but rely on language priors, causing the decoder to under-attend to visual content. This under-conditioning degrades vision-language tasks. A need exists for an unsupervised mechanism that enforces visual conditioning.

Method: Introduce VISE: a single-model, unsupervised self-evolution framework with two invariance-based rewards. Geometric invariance enforces spatial consistency under known transformations; semantic invariance penalizes evidence-agnostic generation when predicted regions are perturbed. No external reward models or annotations; trained on raw unlabeled images.

Result: Evaluated on 18 benchmarks using Qwen3-VL-2B as the base model. Reported gains: +16.85 CIDEr on COCO and +19.66 CIDEr on TextCaps; object hallucination reduced by 5.0 Chair-I points. Demonstrates generalization across four model families and scales.

Conclusion: VISE successfully mitigates visual under-conditioning in unsupervised self-evolution by enforcing visual conditioning through dual invariance rewards, yielding substantial improvements on image captioning and VQA benchmarks without supervision and with broad generalization.

Abstract: Recently, self-evolving large multimodal models (LMMs) have received attention for improving visual reasoning in a purely unsupervised setting. However, multi-role self-play and self-consistency reward schemes in existing self-evolving LMMs optimize answer agreement without ensuring the decoder attends to visual content, relying instead on statistical language priors to produce self consistent outputs. This leads to a persistent failure mode we term visual under-conditioning, where the decoder relies on language priors rather than the image during generation, manifesting as insufficient attention to visual tokens. As a result, current self-evolving LMMs struggle on vision--language understanding tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. To address this, we propose VISE (Visual Invariance Self-Evolution), a purely unsupervised self-evolving framework that directly regularizes the model's visual conditioning policy through two complementary invariance-based rewards: a geometric invariance reward that enforces spatial consistency under known transformations, and a semantic invariance reward that penalizes evidence-agnostic generation by requiring the model to recognize the absence of evidence when predicted regions are perturbed. VISE operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or annotations, and is trained on raw unlabeled images. Experiments on 18 benchmarks demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Using Qwen3-VL-2B as the base model, VISE achieves gains of $+16.85$ CIDEr on COCO and $+19.66$ CIDEr on TextCaps, reduces object hallucination by $5.0$ Chair-I points, and generalizes across four model families and scales. Our code and models are available at https://mbzuai-oryx.github.io/VISE

</details>


### [93] [Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27376)
*Ritesh Thawkar,Shravan Venkatraman,Omkar Thawakar,Abdelrahman Shaker,Fahad Khan,Hisham Cholakkal,Salman Khan,Rao Muhammad Anwer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-evolving, fully self-supervised framework for unified large multimodal models (LMMs) using unlabeled images. It introduces internal roles (Proposer, Solver, Generator) and Solver Token Entropy (STE) to enable autonomous improvement, plus a solver-mediated multi-scale evaluation for image generation. Architecture-agnostic; improves across eight understanding metrics (e.g., BAGEL MMMU +3.5%; GenEval image generation 82%→85%). Code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce or remove reliance on labeled data for training unified LMMs that handle both understanding and generation. Enable autonomous improvement by leveraging internal signals and self-consistency from unlabeled imagery, while preserving the existing model backbones and prompting interfaces.

Method: Define three internal roles: Proposer (generates visual questions), Solver (answers and evaluates), Generator (synthesizes images). Train using self-derived consistency signals without human annotations or external reward models. Introduce Solver Token Entropy (STE) as a continuous difficulty signal based on token-level prediction uncertainty to stabilize learning. Implement a multi-scale internal evaluation for image generation combining QA fidelity and cycle-consistent captioning, enabling a solver-mediated coupling between understanding and generation. The framework is shown to be compatible with diffusion-based BLIP3o, rectified-flow BAGEL, and autoregressive VARGPT-v1.1, requiring only native prompting/generation interfaces and consistent training schedules.

Result: Empirical improvements across eight understanding metrics on BAGEL and related bases, with notable gains such as +3.5 percentage points on MMMU and GenEval image generation performance rising from 82% to 85%. Code and models publicly released.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that unified LMMs can autonomously improve understanding and generation capabilities using purely unlabeled data through an internally coherent, architecture-agnostic self-evolving training framework. The combination of STE and solver-mediated internal evaluation provides stable learning signals and yields consistent performance gains across multiple backbones.

Abstract: Most unified large multimodal models (LMMs) that support both visual understanding and image generation still rely on curated post-training supervision, such as human annotations, preference labels, or external reward models. We ask whether a unified LMM can improve both abilities autonomously using only unlabeled images. We propose a self-evolving training framework with three internal roles: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images. Training uses only self-derived consistency signals, without human annotations, preference labels, or task-trained external reward/judge models. To stabilize learning, we introduce Solver Token Entropy (STE), a continuous difficulty signal based on token-level prediction uncertainty that remains useful even when sample-level consistency becomes unreliable. For image generation, we design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme that combines question-answer fidelity scoring with cycle-consistent captioning. This creates a solver-mediated coupling, where better visual understanding enables more reliable generation assessment and stronger internal training signals. The framework preserves the same role decomposition, reward logic, and training schedule across diffusion-based BLIP3o, rectified-flow BAGEL, and autoregressive VARGPT-v1.1 architectures, requiring only each backbone's native prompting and generation interface. Across eight understanding metrics, our method consistently improves over the corresponding base models. On BAGEL, it achieves a $+3.5\%$ absolute gain on MMMU and improves GenEval image generation performance from $82\%$ to $85\%$. Code and models are publicly released.

</details>


### [94] [DanceOPD: On-Policy Generative Field Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27377)
*Wei Zhou,Xiongwei Zhu,Zelin Xu,Bo Dong,Lixue Gong,Yongyuan Liang,Meng Chu,Leigang Qu,Lingdong Kong,Wei Liu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DanceOPD is an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that unifies T2I, local editing, and global editing by routing samples to capability velocity fields and training a low-noise student via velocity MSE, enabling absorption of guidance operators and improved multi-capability performance while preserving anchor quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The abstract targets a core challenge in modern generative modeling: unifying diverse capabilities (text-to-image, local editing, global editing) within a single model. These capabilities are often misaligned and conflict, causing editing to degrade T2I or interfering with global/local edits. A principled distillation framework is needed to compose expert capabilities on a shared state space.

Method: Introduce DanceOPD, an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models. Each sample is routed to one capability field (a velocity field over a shared flow state space). The framework queries a low-noise student-induced state and trains with a velocity-based mean-squared error objective. The student learns from fields queried on its own rollout states, enabling composition of expert capabilities. Fields can absorb operator-defined guidance such as classifier-free guidance (CFG).

Result: Comprehensive experiments on text-to-image generation, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption demonstrate improved multi-capability composition, strengthening targeted capabilities while preserving anchor generation quality.

Conclusion: DanceOPD offers a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models, showing that on-policy, field-based distillation effectively fuses multiple capabilities without sacrificing base generation performance.

Abstract: Modern image generation demands a single model that unifies diverse capabilities, including text-to-image (T2I), local editing, and global editing. However, these capabilities are rarely naturally aligned and often conflict. For instance, editing tends to degrade T2I performance, while global and local editing interfere with each other. Consequently, effectively composing these capabilities has become a central challenge for image generation model training. To tackle this, we introduce DanceOPD, an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that routes each sample to one capability field, queries one low-noise student-induced state, and trains with a simple velocity MSE objective. With each capability source defined as a velocity field over the shared flow state space, the student learns from fields queried on its own rollout states to compose expert capabilities. This formulation also absorbs operator-defined fields such as classifier-free guidance. Comprehensive experiments on T2I, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption show that our approach improves multi-capability composition, strengthening target capabilities while preserving anchor generation quality. We believe this work establishes a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [95] [Unsupervised Memory-Enhanced Video Transformers: Obstacle Detection for Autonomous Agricultural Rover](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26151)
*Théo Biardeau,Anne-Sophie Capelle-Laizé,Salwan Alwan,David Helbert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A fully unsupervised Video Memory Transformer (VMTAD) for real-time anomaly detection in dynamic agricultural scenes, combining temporal memory with transformer encoding to detect obstacles; achieves state-of-the-art AUC on rapeseed dataset; lightweight variant runs at 14 ms for safe operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional safety sensors miss obstacles under plant canopies; supervised vision struggles with unseen obstacles; unsupervised anomaly detection can detect anomalies but struggles with dynamic rover motion; need robust real-time unsupervised detection for safe autonomous farming.

Method: Transformer-based architecture with a dedicated memory module that processes encoded representations of preceding frames to capture temporal context; trained only on normal operation data; real-time capable.

Result: On Grillion rover and rapeseed dataset: AUC detection 0.973 and 0.997 segmentation; 14 ms inference for lightweight variant; validated by rover stopping distance analysis.

Conclusion: VMTAD enables effective unsupervised obstacle detection in dynamic agricultural settings with high accuracy and real-time performance, addressing motion-induced dynamics via memory-augmented transformer; suitable for safety-critical rover operation.

Abstract: While autonomous rovers have become indispensable to precision farming, achieving consistent operational safety remains a critical challenge. Conventional safety sensors, such as LiDAR, fail to detect obstacles positioned below the plant canopy, posing a significant risk. While camera-based supervised learning methods can detect common objects, they perform poorly when faced with obstacles that were not present in their training data. Actual unsupervised anomaly detection offers a solution by learning the normal visual patterns of an environment, but often fails for the dynamic scenes captured by a moving rover.\\ This paper introduces Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection (VMTAD), a fully unsupervised method designed for real-time obstacle detection in dynamic agricultural scenes. VMTAD utilizes a transformer-driven architecture augmented with a dedicated memory module. This memory module leverages temporal context by processing encoded representations of preceding frames. This approach enables the system to effectively address the dynamic context caused by the robot's movement. The model is trained using only images that represent normal operation, requiring no data labels.\\ VMTAD was rigorously evaluated on the 'Grillion' agricultural rover. On a challenging rapeseed dataset, VMTAD achieved state-of-the-art performance, reaching a 0.973 detection and 0.997 segmentation Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. A lightweight variant provides an optimal balance of high accuracy and real-time inference (14 ms), which is critical for safety, as confirmed by our analysis of the rover's total stopping distance.

</details>


### [96] [Reinforcement Learning Enables Autonomous Microrobot Navigation and Intervention in Simulated Blood Capillaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26154)
*Jannik Drotleff,Samuel Tovey,Paul Hohenberger,Christoph Lohrmann,Julian Hoßbach,Konstantin Nikolaou,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RL-trained autonomous microrobots navigate realistic capillary networks; reveal universal strategies; can manipulate flow to restore baseline throughput; identify a Brownian/flow-dominated forbidden regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior RL studies of microrobotic navigation used idealized geometries lacking realistic hydrodynamics, red blood cell dynamics, and branching vasculature. A physically grounded simulation is needed to evaluate control policies in biologically relevant environments.

Method: Develop a physically grounded capillary network simulator with realistic hydrodynamic flow fields, explicit red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching geometry. Train deep RL agents to navigate via chemotaxis. Systematically map navigation limits across robot size and swimming speed; identify regimes where Brownian motion and flow overcome propulsion; analyze emergent strategy types.

Result: Agents autonomously discover multiple universal strategies (e.g., run-and-rotate, energy-efficient search-and-sit) independent of robot parameters. Without retraining, agents perform targeted blocking and unblocking of capillary flow, restoring throughput to healthy baseline levels.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning is a viable framework for developing autonomous microrobotic intervention strategies in complex biological environments, with insight into physical limits and practical capabilities for drug delivery or thrombolysis.

Abstract: Autonomous microrobots navigating biological vasculature could enable targeted drug delivery and thrombolysis, yet training control policies for realistic environments remains an open challenge. Prior reinforcement learning (RL) studies of microrobotic navigation have been limited to idealized geometries that omit complex hydrodynamic flow fields, confined branching structures, and dense cellular obstacles found in vivo. Here, we develop a physically grounded simulation of a blood capillary network, incorporating realistic hydrodynamic flow fields, explicit red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching geometry, and train deep RL agents to navigate it via chemotaxis. We systematically map the physical limits of navigation across robot size and swimming speed, revealing a forbidden regime where Brownian motion and flow overcome propulsion. Successful agents independently discover multiple universal strategy types, including run-and-rotate and energy-efficient search-and-sit policies, regardless of robot parameters. Without retraining, these agents perform targeted blocking and unblocking of capillary flow, restoring throughput to healthy baseline levels. These results establish RL as a viable framework for developing autonomous microrobotic intervention strategies in complex biological environments.

</details>


### [97] [RMTL: Reinforced Micro-task Learning for Long-Horizon Manipulation with VLM Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26175)
*Anıl Can Ateş,Orhan Kahraman,Cihan Topal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RMTL decomposes VLM-based rewards into micro-task prompts and uses multi-view averaging with a reverse curriculum and hierarchical policy for micro-task switching, improving learning speed in robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single global prompts from vision-language models yield near-flat rewards over long-horizon tasks with randomized starts, hindering credit assignment and sample efficiency.

Method: Decompose tasks into a small set of language-described micro-tasks; at each step compute a multi-view VLM reward using the current micro-task prompt and average across camera views. Apply a reverse curriculum to gradually expose harder initial conditions. Start with a fixed rule-based micro-task selection during PPO training, then replace with a learned hierarchical manager for fully learned micro-task selection.

Result: In Fetch manipulation experiments with three stage-specific prompts and no extra prompt tuning, RMTL provides more informative rewards and faster learning than single-prompt baselines.

Conclusion: Decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task prompts can significantly improve the scalability of language-guided RL for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic manipulation often requires manually designing a dense reward function, which is difficult to tune and often fragile, or learning a reward from human demonstrations or preferences, which can be expensive. A recent line of work uses pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) as zero-shot reward models, replacing these costs with a single text prompt. However, we argue that a single global prompt is too coarse for long-horizon manipulation tasks with randomized initial conditions. The single-prompt VLM reward is near-flat for much of the trajectory, making early progress hard for the agent to detect. We propose Reinforced Micro-Task Learning (RMTL), an approach that decomposes a manipulation task into a small set of language-described micro-tasks and trains the agent to switch between them. At each step, the agent receives a multi-view VLM reward computed using the prompt of the currently active micro-task and averaged across multiple camera views to reduce the effect of view-specific occlusions. A reverse curriculum gradually exposes the agent to harder initial conditions, while a PPO worker is first trained with a fixed distance-based rule that selects the active micro-task. We then replace this rule with a learned hierarchical manager, turning rule-based phase selection into a fully learned hierarchical policy. We instantiate RMTL on the Fetch manipulation environment using three short stage-specific prompts and without additional prompt tuning. Experiments show that RMTL provides more informative reward signals than single-prompt VLM rewards, enabling faster learning. These results suggest that decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task-specific language prompts can substantially improve the scalability of language-guided reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [98] [LiMoDE: Rethinking Lifelong Robot Manipulation from a Mixture-of-Dynamic-Experts Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26183)
*Zhihao Gu,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiMoDE introduces a two-stage lifelong mixture of dynamic experts for robot manipulation: a multi-task pre-training stage with a dynamic MoE to capture priors and a task-adaptation stage where lifelong MoE learns lifelong experts and combines them with frozen ones for new tasks, achieving strong lifelong adaptation with modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and poor reuse of skills in generalist robots. Prior single-task fine-tuning struggles to extract reusable skills and model interactions between skills; an architectural approach to lifelong learning can enable knowledge transfer and scalable adaptation.

Method: Stage 1 (multi-task pre-training): dynamic mixture of experts (MoE) with a variable number of heterogeneous experts activated by motion information to encode short-term manipulations. Stage 2 (task adaptation): lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism that learns lifelong experts and dynamically blends them with frozen experts to facilitate transfer to new tasks.

Result: Empirical evaluation on simulated lifelong learning benchmarks and real-world tasks shows superior performance and strong lifelong adaptation, achieved with a moderate increase in trainable parameters and inference overhead.

Conclusion: LiMoDE provides an architectural solution for lifelong robot manipulation by combining a dynamic MoE pre-training with a lifelong MoE adaptation strategy, enabling reuse of prior knowledge and efficient adaptation to new tasks.

Abstract: Building a generalist robot that can leverage prior knowledge for continuous task adaptation remains a significant challenge. Previous works alleviate the catastrophic forgetting problem by parameter-efficient fine-tuning for single-task adaptation. However, they fail to extract reusable skills and model the interaction with other skills effectively. Recent works try to address these issues by learning prompts. Differently, this paper presents an architectural perspective on the Lifelong Mixture of Dynamic Experts (\textit{LiMoDE}), a novel two-stage learning scheme for lifelong robot manipulation. Specifically, a dynamic MoE structure is first proposed in the multi-task pre-training stage to learn prior knowledge, where a varied number of heterogeneous experts are activated based on the motion information to address different short-term manipulations. Subsequently, in the task adaptation stage, we design a lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism % (LiMoEAM) that learns lifelong experts and dynamically combines them with frozen ones for new tasks, facilitating the knowledge transfer during adaptation. The proposed \textit{LiMoDE} is evaluated on both the simulated lifelong learning benchmark and real-world tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness in achieving superior performance and strong lifelong adaptation by introducing a moderate number of additional trainable parameters and inference overhead.

</details>


### [99] [Morphology-Specific Closed-Loop Control of Logarithmic-Spiral Continuum Arms via Online Jacobian Error Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26188)
*Partha Datta,Yi Jin,Wei Lin,C. Chase Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A morphology-specific closed-loop task-space control framework for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms with online Jacobian compensation, achieving accurate, robust control and enabling advanced morphology-enabled manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Logarithmic-spiral continuum arms have appealing reach/wrap/grasp capabilities but lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control; a physics-grounded, tunable control method is needed to handle nonlinear deformation, contact, and geometric mismatch.

Method: Developed segmented tendon-driven model (centerline backbone, equilateral tendon routing) in MuJoCo; derive analytical task-space Jacobian from log-spiral kinematics; couple with online Jacobian error compensation using Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation; implement closed-loop controller; validate in planar/spatial simulations; compare to PCC baseline; demonstrate morphology-enabled tasks.

Result: Achieved improved tracking accuracy, reduced attitude drift, bounded Jacobian estimation error across planar/spatial scenarios; robust disturbance rejection; successful obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release, adaptive whole-arm grasping, cooperative multi-arm handling; provides physics-grounded baseline for hardware and learning-augmented control.

Conclusion: The framework enables accurate, robust, scalable control for logarithmic-spiral continuum manipulators and lays groundwork for hardware implementation and learning-based enhancements; morphology-specific Jacobian compensation is key to handling nonlinearities and contacts.

Abstract: Logarithmic spirals are ubiquitous in biological appendages and provide an attractive morphology for continuum manipulators capable of reaching, wrapping, and grasping. Recently reported logarithmic-spiral robots demonstrated scalable fabrication and versatile grasping but lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control. This work presents the first morphology-specific closed-loop task-space control framework for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms. A segmented tendon-driven model with a centerline backbone and equilateral tendon routing is developed in MuJoCo to capture tapered compliance and contact dynamics. An analytical task-space Jacobian is derived directly from the logarithmic-spiral kinematics and combined with online Jacobian error compensation using a Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation. The resulting controller continuously corrects modeling errors arising from nonlinear deformation, contact, and geometric mismatch. The framework is validated through planar and spatial simulations, including trajectory tracking, attitude regulation, disturbance rejection, three-dimensional position tracking, and simultaneous position-orientation control. Compared with a piecewise-constant-curvature (PCC) baseline, the proposed method consistently reduces tracking errors, suppresses attitude drift, and maintains a bounded Jacobian estimation error. The controller is further applied to morphology-enabled manipulation tasks, including obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release motions, adaptive whole-arm grasping, and cooperative multi-arm object handling. Results demonstrate that combining logarithmic-spiral morphology with online Jacobian compensation enables accurate, robust, and scalable control of highly underactuated continuum manipulators. The proposed framework establishes a physics-grounded baseline for future hardware implementation and learning-augmented soft robotic control.

</details>


### [100] [OmniContact: Chaining Meta-Skills via Contact Flow for Generalizable Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26201)
*Runyi Yu,Xiaoyi Lin,Ji Ma,Yinhuai Wang,Koukou Luo,Jiahao Ji,Huayi Wang,Wenjia Wang,Runhan Zhang,Ping Tan,Ting Wu,Ruoli Dai,Qifeng Chen,Lei Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical contact-flow framework (CF-Track/CF-Gen) with an OmniContact dataset enabling robust long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation, featuring a compact contact-flow interface for low-level skills and high-level planning, achieving strong success and improved skill chaining, with VLM integration for semantic task decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon loco-manipulation requires robust meta-skills and reliable, closed-loop composition; explicit humanoid-object representations are precise but hard to plan, while implicit skill embeddings lack interpretability. A compact, interpretable contact-flow interface can enable robust execution, failure recovery, and modular skill composition.

Method: Introduce contact flow (CF) as time-series of key body trajectories and binary contact signals. CF-Track: a low-level policy that builds a unified library of loco-manipulation skills around CF. CF-Gen: a high-level module that heuristically synthesizes future CF sequences. Collect OmniContact, a MoCap-based HOI dataset for humanoid loco-manipulation. Demonstrate integration with vision-language models for semantic task decomposition and show autonomous recovery and chaining.

Result: OmniContact achieves 98.7% on Carry Box and 76.5% on Push-Stack Boxes; outperforms baselines by ~40.9% in meta-skill and ~66.5% in skill chaining on average. Semantically grounded loco-manipulation tasks (e.g., arranging boxes into a heart shape) are enabled via VLM integration.

Conclusion: A CF-based hierarchical framework enables robust execution, autonomous recovery, and flexible composition of meta-skills for long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation, with OmniContact providing a suitable benchmark for HOI under MoCap data and enabling semantically grounded behaviors via VLMs.

Abstract: Learning long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation poses a dual challenge: it requires not only the robust execution of meta-skills but also their seamless, closed-loop chaining equipped with autonomous recovery. Existing approaches remain limited: explicit humanoid-object interaction representations offer precision but are notoriously difficult for high-level planning, whereas implicit skill embeddings are compact but lack the interpretability required for reliable composition. We propose \ours, a hierarchical framework centered on \textbf{contact flow (CF)}, a compact representation consisting of key body trajectories and time-series binary contact signals. Leveraging this shared interface, our low-level policy \textbf{CF-Track} learns a unified library of loco-manipulation skills, while our high-level module \textbf{CF-Gen} heuristically synthesizes future contact-flow sequences. To support this setting, we additionally collect the OmniContact dataset, a MoCap-based HOI corpus for humanoid loco-manipulation (Appendix~\ref{sec:dataset}). Together, they enable robust execution, autonomous failure recovery, and flexible composition of meta-skills for long-horizon tasks. Experiments show that OmniContact achieves \(98.7\%\) success on \textit{Carry Box} and \(76.5\%\) on \textit{Push-Stack Boxes}, outperforming prior baselines by average margins of \(40.9\%\) in meta-skill and \(66.5\%\) in skill chaining. Besides, our framework naturally integrates with VLMs for semantic task decomposition, enabling complex, semantically grounded loco-manipulation behaviors, such as arranging scattered boxes into a heart shape.

</details>


### [101] [RoboTales: ROBOTic Anthropomorphic LEarning Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26213)
*Andrew Chen,Ju-Hung Chen,Phurinat Pinyomit,Alexis E. Block*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost Baxter-based puppet storytelling system (RoboTales) synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements via expressive sock puppetry; pilot data show puppet-based storytelling enhances engagement and recall compared to a gesture-only mode; modular and platform-agnostic, with screen-free learning potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore embodied storytelling with affordable robotics to boost children's engagement and narrative comprehension, addressing limitations of gesture-only or screen-based media and providing a reusable framework for child-centered education.

Method: Autonomous implementation on a Baxter robot; puppet-based storytelling with synchronized narration, gestures, and mouth movements; pilot study comparing puppet mode to a gesture-only mode; evaluation using HRIES ratings and story recall; modular design enabling adaptation to other manipulators.

Result:  Puppet-based storytelling outperformed gesture-only mode, yielding higher HRIES ratings and improved story recall; embodied puppetry enhances engagement and narrative comprehension.

Conclusion: The RoboTales framework is modular and platform-agnostic, suitable for adaptation to other manipulators and offering a screen-free alternative to passive media; holds promise for deployment in child-centered learning environments and future multi-manipulator extensions.

Abstract: RoboTales is a low-cost robotic storytelling system that animates narratives using expressive sock puppetry. Implemented autonomously on a Baxter robot as a test case, RoboTales synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements to perform character-driven stories. In a pilot study, puppet-based storytelling outperformed a gesture-only mode, producing higher HRIES ratings and improved story recall, suggesting that embodied puppetry enhances engagement and narrative comprehension.
