Designed to be modular and platform-agnostic, RoboTales can be adapted to other manipulators and offers a screen-free alternative to passive media, supporting future deployment in child-centered learning environments.

</details>


### [102] [TaskNPoint: How to Teach Your Humanoid to Hit a Backhand in Minutes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26215)
*Blake Werner,Ilona Demler,Pietro Perona,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaskNPoint enables fast learning of dynamic humanoid skills via a coaching paradigm: a small set of discrete skills, one demonstration per skill, an explicitly identified interaction window, and randomized target sampling in sim to achieve zero-shot generalization from short demos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic skills in robotics hinge on a short, crucial interaction window where control, physics, and morphology must align. A coach–learner division can drastically reduce data requirements and simplify training.

Method: Introduce TaskNPoint: the human coach provides a discrete skill set, one demonstration per skill, identification of the interaction window, and the goal. Train in a physics-based simulation that completes each action trajectory; use randomized target sampling to promote robustness and generalization. Test on a Unitree G1 humanoid performing forehand/backhand, soccer-kick, and box-picking tasks with novel locations; no per-task reward tuning.

Result: Learning succeeds from short human video demonstrations and under an hour of training on a single GPU; robust to unmodeled events; zero-shot generalization to unseen goal locations due to randomized target sampling; no per-task reward tuning required.

Conclusion: A coach–learner framework with randomized target sampling supports data-efficient learning of diverse, high-level dynamic skills in humanoid robots and generalizes to novel goals with minimal supervision.

Abstract: How do we learn to hit a tennis backhand? Not from a thousand hours of tennis tournaments on TV - we work with a coach and practice. We argue this is also the right recipe for teaching dynamic skills to humanoid robots. This follows from a structural property of dynamic skills: the outcome is decided by a short, crucial portion of the trajectory - for a backhand, the ~20cm of racket travel around ball contact. Getting this interaction window right requires coordinating the whole motion, so that control, physics, and morphology act in concert. Learning thus reduces to mastering a handful of distinct actions and, for each, practicing until the window comes out right. To this end, we introduce TaskNPoint, a training protocol which makes the coach-learner division of labor explicit. The human coach contributes four inputs: a discrete set of skills (e.g. different shots), one demonstration per skill, identification of the interaction window, and the goal. Learning in a physically realistic simulation environment fills in each action trajectory and provides robustness to unmodeled events. Crucially, randomized target sampling during training lets a single demonstration generalize zero-shot to unseen goal locations. We test this approach on a Unitree G1 humanoid that hits forehands and backhands against balls thrown by a human, kicks incoming soccer balls, and picks and places boxes from novel locations. We find that learning is successful from short human video demonstrations and under an hour of training on a single GPU, with no per-task reward tuning.

</details>


### [103] [NavIsaacLab: Generating Realistic Crowd via Parallel Robot Learning for Benchmarking Human-aware Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26265)
*Bingyi Xia,Han Bao,Jingyu Zhu,Hanjing Ye,Yuhan Pang,Guangcheng Chen,Liang Lin,Wenjun Xu,Jiankun Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NavIsaacLab is a GPU-accelerated, physics-based, photo-realistic simulation framework for benchmarking and training human-aware navigation, featuring data-driven pedestrian models (trajectory diffusion and adversarial motion learning) and cross-scale scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses scarcity of diverse, high-quality scene data and limitations of rule-based, perfect-observation simulations; aims to provide realistic sensor signals and scalable benchmarking for human-aware navigation.

Method: Based on Isaac Lab; photo-realistic scene rendering; GPU-parallel simulation; real-time 3D visual feedback; data-driven pedestrian behaviors via trajectory diffusion model and adversarial motion learning controller; integration of cross-scale scenes.

Result: Enables real-time, accurate 3D feedback and a robust benchmark for state-of-the-art human-aware navigation methods; improves realism and diversity of simulation data.

Conclusion: The framework mitigates data scarcity and realism gaps, enabling training and evaluation of navigation policies with more representative human behaviors and sensor signals, thereby advancing safe and natural human-robot interaction.

Abstract: Robot autonomous navigation that accounts for surrounding human activities is crucial for ensuring both safety and natural human-robot interaction in real-world environments shared by humans and robots. Simulation of complex and diverse navigation scenarios serves as the foundation for training reliable robot navigation policies and accurately evaluating the performance of algorithms, offering an efficient alternative to manual supervision of real data. However, current human-aware navigation research faces significant challenges due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality scene data. Existing simulation platforms often rely on handcrafted rules to approximate pedestrian behavior and lack the capability to provide extensive sensor signals, typically assuming perfect observations. To address these limitations, this paper presents NavIsaacLab, a comprehensive framework for benchmarking and training human-aware navigation policies through physics-based and photo-realistic simulations of pedestrians and scenes. Based on Isaac Lab, the proposed framework employs photo-realistic scene rendering capabilities and supports parallel simulation on GPU, delivering real-time and accurate 3D visual feedback to robots. To enhance the realism of human behavior, a data-driven approach is employed that incorporates a trajectory diffusion model and an adversarial motion learning controller, enabling controllable, physics-based pedestrian simulation. Furthermore, the integration of diverse cross-scale scenes provides a robust benchmark for state-of-the-art human-aware navigation methods.

</details>


### [104] [Racing a Wheeled Quadruped: Active Load Transfer Mitigation via Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26313)
*Marla Eisman,Brian Lam,Samuel Sonnino,Francesco Borrelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical MPC-RL framework for active roll control in autonomous wheeled quadruped racing, achieving substantial LTR reduction and performance gains via offline raceline optimization, online MPC planning, and low-level RL on 16 actuators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce lateral load transfer and improve high-speed stability and lap performance in legged/wheeled racing; address stability challenges during aggressive cornering.

Method: Offline time-optimal raceline; online MPC planner minimizing lateral load transfer ratio using a bicycle-model dynamics; low-level whole-body RL controlling 16 actuators with knee joints generating anti-roll torque, acting as active suspension; model-based control integrated with RL; experiments on Unitree Go2-W platform.

Result: Physical experiments show mean LTR reduction up to 44%, fastest lap time improvement of 8.7%, peak lateral acceleration increase of 21.3% to 1.98 m/s^2; stable high-speed performance beyond non-tilting baseline.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and benefits of an MPC-RL hierarchical control for active roll management in autonomous racing; potential for broader application and future improvements; code/video available.

Abstract: This paper presents a hierarchical control framework using model predictive control (MPC) and reinforcement learning (RL) for active roll control to manage lateral load transfer during autonomous racing of a wheeled quadruped. The framework integrates offline time-optimal raceline generation, an online MPC planner that actively minimizes the lateral Load Transfer Ratio (LTR), and a low-level, whole-body RL policy deployed directly onto the robot's 16 actuators. The MPC is based on a vehicle dynamics bicycle model of the Unitree Go2-W platform. The robot's leg actuators act as active suspension where knee joints generate anti-roll torque to bank into turns. Physical track experiments demonstrate that active roll control reduces mean LTR by up to 44%, improves the fastest lap time by 8.7%, and boosts peak lateral acceleration capability by 21.3% to 1.98 $m/s^2$, maintaining robust high-speed stability beyond the range of a non-tilting baseline controller. Supplementary code and video can be found at https://github.com/meisman-ucb/go2w-roll-control-mpc

</details>


### [105] [Layered Outer-Loop Control for Disturbance-Robust Multi-Waypoint UAV Arrival](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26315)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Two-phase layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAVs evaluated across PyBullet, PX4/Gazebo, and hardware; demonstrates separation of core control from benchmark refinements; Phase I achieves 0.024 m wind error; Phase II selects a robust final controller; Grace analysis highlights semantics-driven residuals; conclusion emphasizes deployment-readiness and transferable gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve disturbance-robust UAV position control and assess transferability of benchmark results to deployment-like, hardware-validated performance.

Method: Phase I: PyBullet with stochastic wind to rapidly identify a core controller separating smooth approach, persistent-bias compensation, and near-target terminal regulation. Phase II: Deploy outer-loop into PX4/Gazebo with a cascaded flight stack; assess transferability under delay-sensitive dynamics; two-level hardware study with Vicon-tracked Tello; Grace retrospective analysis.

Result: Phase I achieves 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. Phase II selects the final controller using a transfer-oriented rule, emphasizing absence of benchmark priors, cross-scenario balance, and deployable supervisory logic; evaluation identifies which benchmark gains survive autopilot dynamics and notes residual failures sensitive to completion semantics.

Conclusion: Separating main controller design from benchmark refinements yields more informative benchmark success and defensible performance under harder closed-loop evaluation; deployment-like validation remains essential.

Abstract: Disturbance-robust UAV position control is easy to demonstrate in benign simulations but much harder to make fast in approach, well behaved near the target, and credible beyond a single benchmark. This letter presents a layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAV position regulation together with a staged evaluation across PyBullet, PX4/Gazebo, and hardware. Phase I uses a PyBullet benchmark with stochastic wind for rapid structural selection, identifying a controller core that separates smooth approach generation, persistent-bias compensation, and supervised near-target terminal regulation. Phase II carries only that main architecture into a more demanding PX4/Gazebo closed loop, where the outer-loop controller acts through a cascaded flight stack with delay-sensitive settling and stronger transit-to-hover coupling. This step exposes which benchmark gains survive autopilot-mediated dynamics and which refinements collapse once the loop becomes more deployment-like. In Phase I, the bare controller attains 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. In Phase II, the final controller is selected using a transfer-oriented rule emphasizing absence of benchmark priors, cross-scenario balance, and deployable supervisory logic. Strict is used as the primary reporting reference; the supplementary retrospective Grace analysis shows that part of the residual failure set is sensitive to completion semantics rather than gross waypoint-miss behaviour. The evaluation is completed on one Vicon-tracked Tello stack through a two-level hardware study. Taken together, the results suggest that benchmark success becomes more informative when the main controller design is separated from benchmark-specific refinement and remains defensible under harder closed-loop evaluation.

</details>


### [106] [KRVF: A Source-Aware Semantic Voxel World Representation for Edge Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26321)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KRVF is a source-aware semantic voxel world representation for edge mobile manipulation that encodes occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source; it separates measured occupancy from semantic priors, enables depth-failure-aware reasoning, and supports map repair via rendering map-prior depth and task-level queries; implemented as a ROS 2 pipeline converting RGB-D to task-facing robot memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile manipulators require up-to-date, queryable, semantically meaningful world models that fit edge-compute constraints. Reconstruction-centric pipelines often optimize geometric fidelity but fail to support robust task reasoning under occlusions, depth failures, or semantic grasping, limiting manipulation performance.

Method: Introduce KRVF as a voxel-based, source-aware representation where local voxels store occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source. The representation separates measured occupancy from semantic priors, enabling depth-failure-aware object reasoning. A feedback loop renders map-prior depth for repair, and ROS 2 components expose task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates. The work formalizes KRVF and documents an end-to-end ROS 2 implementation that converts online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

Result: Formalization of the KRVF representation and a ROS 2 implementation that turns online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory. Demonstrates a practical, edge-ready memory suitable for query-driven manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: KRVF provides a current, queryable, semantically meaningful world model suitable for edge mobile manipulation. By decoupling measured occupancy from semantic priors, enabling depth-failure-aware reasoning, and integrating mapping with sensing for repair, KRVF supports robust task-level queries (semantic objects, grasp candidates) and practical robot memory pipelines.

Abstract: Mobile manipulators need world models that are current, queryable, semantically meaningful, and usable under edge-compute constraints. This technical report presents KRVF, a source-aware semantic voxel world representation for edge mobile manipulation. Unlike reconstruction-centric mapping pipelines that primarily optimize global geometric fidelity, KRVF represents local world state as task-oriented voxels that encode occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source. The representation separates measured occupancy from semantic-prior hypotheses, enabling depth-failure-aware object reasoning without silently corrupting persistent geometry. KRVF also closes a feedback loop between mapping and sensing by rendering map-prior depth for repair, and exposes task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates. The report formalizes the KRVF representation and documents a ROS 2 implementation that turns online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

</details>


### [107] [Scaling Nonlinear Optimization: Many Problems One GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26341)
*John Viljoen,Johanna Haffner,Masayoshi Tomizuka,Negar Mehr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces jaxipm, a GPU-batched NLP solver based on IPOPT implemented in JAX, enabling solving many NLPs concurrently with significant throughput gains (up to 32.85x) for robotics MPC tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NLP-based robotics problems are typically solved on CPU, one problem at a time, hindering integration with GPU-batched learning and simulation pipelines. A GPU-batched NLP solver would enable large-scale concurrent constrained optimization.

Method: Redesign of IPOPT to remove control flow via heterogeneous iteration fusion and to maximize GPU utilization via iteration-level batching; implemented in JAX to leverage GPU acceleration; evaluated on quadrotor MPC benchmarks.

Result: Demonstrates up to 32.85× throughput improvement over IPOPT across various quadrotor MPC tasks (reference tracking with obstacles, multi-quadrotor collision avoidance, cluttered environments); complete open-source code at GitHub.

Conclusion: GPU-batched NLP solving is feasible and effective for robotics, enabling integration with GPU-based simulators and learning pipelines, and paving the way for faster, constrained optimization in real-time planning and control.

Abstract: Many robotics problems, including trajectory optimization, inverse kinematics, and contact-rich motion planning, reduce to nonlinear programs (NLPs). Mature NLP solvers such as IPOPT can solve these problems, offering hard constraint satisfaction, optimality guarantees, and favorable scaling with problem dimension. These solvers underpin gradient-based methods in robotics, yet remain CPU-bound and solve only one problem at a time, preventing their integration into GPU-batched learning pipelines. On the other hand, sampling-based approaches such as reinforcement learning, model predictive path integral, and imitation learning have become the core of modern robotics research due to their ability to leverage GPU-batched simulators. These simulators can generate orders of magnitude more dynamics rollouts per second than was previously possible. If a GPU-batched NLP solver existed, it would unlock similar speedups in the number of constrained, locally optimal solutions generated per second. This regime of solving many problems concurrently versus solving a single problem at a time is a key requirement for integrating NLP solvers in modern GPU-batched robotics frameworks. To this end, we introduce \texttt{jaxipm}, the first GPU-batched NLP solver, based on IPOPT, and implemented in JAX. We accomplish this by redesigning IPOPT's algorithm to eliminate control flow with \textit{heterogeneous iteration fusion}, and by minimizing GPU idle time with \textit{iteration level batching}. We evaluate \texttt{jaxipm} on a variety of quadrotor nonlinear model predictive control benchmarks, including reference tracking in the presence of obstacles, multi-quadrotor navigation without collision, and navigation in a cluttered environment. We demonstrate up to a $32.85\times$ increase in throughput over IPOPT. Our complete open-source codebase is available at https://github.com/johnviljoen/jaxipm.

</details>


### [108] [MPC-Injection: Biasing Off-Policy Locomotion RL Toward Controller-Induced Behavior Basins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26392)
*Roy Xing,Seyoung Ree,Brian Plancher*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPC-Injection steers RL locomotion toward a designer’s gait by injecting MPC-derived transitions into the replay buffer, avoiding reward shaping and discriminators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for locomotion often converges to undeployable local optima; a low-overhead method is needed to bias learning toward usable gaits without redesigning reward functions or employing adversarial imitation components.

Method: Insert transitions from a model predictive controller solving the same MDP into the RL replay buffer; leverage the controller’s preferred behavior to bias actor-critic updates via the replay distribution; uses simple (one–two-term) task rewards rather than complex shaping.

Result: In simulation (2D walker) and sim-to-real (Go2 quadruped), MPC-Injection yields gaits qualitatively comparable to reward shaping with many terms and to adversarial motion priors, while using substantially less overhead; policy shifts toward the controller’s behavior basin and learns otherwise hard-to-reach behaviors.

Conclusion: MPC-Injection is a low-cost, effective approach to guide RL toward designer-preferred gaits by borrowing transitions from an MPC policy, with analysis showing biased updates toward controller-visited states and broad applicability to other domains where MPC guidance is beneficial.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for locomotion frequently converges to locally optimal but undeployable behaviors, such as vibrating limbs or scooting on the torso, that maximize return without producing a usable gait. We present MPC-Injection, a low-overhead method that steers RL toward a designer-preferred gait by inserting transitions into the replay buffer from a model predictive controller solving the same Markov decision process. Unlike reward shaping, MPC-Injection does not require redesigning the task reward, and unlike adversarial imitation learning, it adds no discriminator, no kinematic retargeting, and no auxiliary objective. Instead, the controller's preferred behavior is transferred to the policy purely through the replay state distribution. On a 2D walker in simulation and with sim-to-real evaluation on a Go2 quadruped, we show that MPC-Injection drives the policy into the controller's behavior basin using a one to two-term task reward, producing gaits qualitatively comparable to those of reward shaping with twenty-one tuned terms and of adversarial motion priors without their discriminator and retargeting overhead. We further analyze how the injected transitions bias actor-critic updates toward controller-visited states, allowing the policy to learn behaviors that pure RL may fail to reach under simple reward functions.

</details>


### [109] [Exploring the Intrinsic Geometry of Diffusion Models with Constrained Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26408)
*Miguel Angel Rogel Garcia,Phone Thiha Kyaw,Jonathan Kelly*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diffusion models learn a geometry-consistent representation of IK constraint manifolds: the score's intrinsic dimension matches the manifold DOF, and latent interpolations stay on/near the manifold, validating diffusion geometry in a controlled setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether diffusion models capture the intrinsic geometry of data manifolds beyond natural images by using a tractable geometry (constrained inverse kinematics) with known degrees of freedom.

Method: Train a single conditional diffusion model on seven constraint families for 6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka robots. Analyze the score-function's implied intrinsic dimension against the analytic DOFs of the constraint manifolds. Use linear interpolation in the latent space to examine whether generated solutions remain on or near the corresponding manifolds.

Result: The intrinsic dimension recovered from the model’s score matches the analytical DOFs of each constraint manifold for both robots. Linear latent-space interpolation yields solutions that stay close to the appropriate constraint manifold, implying the learned representation encodes geometric structure beyond mere dimensionality.

Conclusion: Constrained inverse kinematics provides a controlled setting to study the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models. The results indicate diffusion models capture meaningful geometric structure of constraint manifolds, supporting their use in geometry-aware generative modeling for robotics.

Abstract: Recent studies suggest that diffusion models can recover geometric structure in the data manifolds they are trained on, yet the supporting evidence has so far come mostly from natural-image data, where the underlying geometry itself is unknown. We study this question in a setting where the geometry is analytically tractable: constrained inverse kinematics (IK). Each task-space constraint defines a configuration-space manifold with known intrinsic dimension, giving direct ground truth for evaluating the geometry learned by the model. For each of the 6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka, we train a single conditional diffusion model across seven constraint families, spanning solution manifolds from discrete IK branches to self-motion manifolds. Our empirical results reveal that the intrinsic dimension recovered from the model's score function matches the analytical degrees of freedom of the corresponding constraint manifold across both robots. Moreover, linear interpolation in the latent space leads to generated solutions that remain close to the appropriate constraint manifold, indicating that the learned representation further captures geometric structure of the constraint family beyond intrinsic dimension alone. Constrained IK therefore offers a controlled setting for studying the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models.

</details>


### [110] [CoStream: Composing Simple Behaviors for Generalizable Complex Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26423)
*Haonan Chen,Yuxiang Ma,Stephen Tian,Xiaoshen Han,Wenlong Huang,Feiyang Wu,Yunzhu Li,Jiajun Wu,Edward H. Adelson,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A compositional, three-behavior framework (semantic, predictive, reactive) orchestrates foundation-model-driven perception, learned predictions, and tactile feedback to drive SE(3) pose control, enabling long-horizon, precise, and generalizable manipulation without a fixed end-to-end policy or rigid pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off between high-precision, task-specific pipelines and generalizing end-to-end policies by enabling flexible decomposition and recomposition of manipulation capabilities.

Method: Introduce \ourshort framework: three core behaviors—semantic (foundation-model-based spatial constraints), predictive (keypoint trajectory forecasting from imagined videos), reactive (high-frequency tactile/force corrections)—on a shared SE(3) interface, right-multiplying outputs to produce a single pose command; executed by a compliant controller; validated on 8 real-world tasks.

Result: Strong gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer; robust perturbation recovery; demonstrated real-world applicability across 8 tasks; outperforms baseline pipelines and end-to-end policies in precision and generalization aspects (as claimed).

Conclusion: Composable core behaviors can emergently yield complex manipulation capabilities, achieving both precision and generalization without retraining monolithic policies, suggesting a viable path for scalable manipulation systems.

Abstract: Long-horizon, contact-rich complex manipulation tasks, such as seating a GPU into a PCIe slot, demand both millimeter high precision and out-of-the-box generalization to new tasks. Existing paradigms struggle to satisfy both: classical pipelines use brittle, task-specific interfaces to achieve high-precision control but require costly pipeline redesigns to adapt to new tasks, whereas monolithic end-to-end policies provide better generalization but lack high precision on complex, out-of-distribution tasks unless retrained with new data. Both paradigms share an implicit assumption: once a manipulation capability is acquired, it must be deployed as a rigid pipeline or monolithic whole, rather than being freely decomposed and recomposed. In this paper, we show that complex manipulation capabilities can emerge naturally from the composition of simple, independent behaviors. Rather than deploying a monolithic policy or a rigid pipeline, we propose \ourshort, a framework orchestrating foundation models and diverse sensing modalities into multiple composable core behaviors: a semantic behavior extracting spatial constraints via foundation models; a predictive behavior forecasting trajectories by tracking keypoints in imagined videos; and a reactive behavior providing high-frequency tactile and force corrections. On a shared $SE(3)$ interface, these outputs compose by right-multiplication into a single pose command at each control step, executed by a compliant controller. We demonstrate \ourshort on 8 real-world tasks spanning everyday manipulation and precision assembly, with the strongest gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer, and show robust recovery from manual perturbations during execution. {Website:} https://costream-simple.github.io

</details>


### [111] [A System for Fast, Resilient, and Adaptable Loco-Manipulation Behaviors on Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26425)
*Duncan William Calvert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A runtime-editable, robot-local behavior authoring system for humanoid robots that integrates object-centric Affordance Templates, Behavior Trees, and editable perception; enables whole-body loco-manipulation with concurrent action layering; validated across 20+ real-robot tasks and deployed on Atlas, Valkyrie, Nadia, H1-2, and Alex; supports rapid creation and combination of novel behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable observable, predictable, and directable operation of humanoid robots in human-centric spaces by coordinating locomotion, whole-body manipulation, perception, contact, and operator supervision through an editable, modular behavior framework.

Method: Developed a robot-local, runtime-editable behavior authoring system that combines object-centric Affordance Templates, Behavior Tree-inspired organization, and runtime-editable perception via a behavior scene and primitive scene actions. Action primitives leverage a whole-body controller capable of moving arms while walking and use a concurrent action layering algorithm for speed. Built a library of 20+ real-robot tasks (doors, exploration, obstacle clearing, table-to-table manipulation) and deployed on multiple humanoids.

Result: Demonstrated the ability to adapt, extend, and compose existing behaviors into novel loco-manipulation behaviors in minutes to hours. Empirical evaluation across capability, speed, reliability, and speed of behavior creation, with deployments on Atlas, Valkyrie, Nadia, H1-2, and Alex; supporting video evidence.

Conclusion: The system achieves high observability, predictability, and directability, enabling runtime authoring, repair, and rapid extension of loco-manipulation behaviors across diverse humanoid platforms.

Abstract: Humanoid robots could take on physically demanding, hazardous, and repetitive work in spaces built for humans. However, a useful robot for these spaces must coordinate locomotion, whole body motion, perception, contact, and operator supervision. This thesis presents a robot-local, runtime-editable behavior authoring and runtime system.
  Our system strives to be maximally observable, predictable, and directable following Coactive Design principles developed during the DARPA Robotics Challenge. Our operator interface remains continuously synchronized to the robot for runtime authoring, monitoring, and repair. Our behavior architecture uniquely combines object-centric Affordance Templates, organization and logic inspired by Behavior Trees, and runtime-editable perception through a behavior scene and primitive scene actions. Action primitives build on a whole-body controller that supports moving the arms while walking, and use a concurrent action layering algorithm for speed.
  The behavior library developed during this work covers more than twenty real-robot task variants, including push and pull doors with knob, push-bar, and lever-handle mechanisms, multi-step exploration sequences, obstacle clearing, and reactive table-to-table manipulation tasks. This behavior system has been deployed on many humanoid robots, such as Boston Dynamics' DRC Atlas, NASA's Valkyrie, IHMC and Boardwalk Robotics' Nadia, Unitree's H1-2, and IHMC's Alex.
  We evaluate our system across capability, speed, reliability, and speed of behavior creation, adaptation, extension, and combination. Our experiments demonstrate that we can adapt, extend, and combine existing behaviors to create novel loco-manipulation behaviors in minutes or hours. Videos: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLJK5CTyotYqsfgfnXb-09YNFeBose6uEY.

</details>


### [112] [Play2Perfect: What Matters in Dexterous Play Pretraining for Precise Assembly?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26428)
*Tyler Ga Wei Lum,Kushal Kedia,C. Karen Liu,Jeannette Bohg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Play2Perfect pretrains general manipulation priors via play (diverse objects/goals) to enable efficient fine-tuning for precise assembly, achieving sim-to-real transfer and strong performance on tight insertions and long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise assembly with multi-fingered hands is data-hungry and contact-rich; RL from scratch is inefficient. A task-agnostic pretraining regime could learn reusable manipulation skills to accelerate assembly.

Method: Train a reinforcement learning agent in a 'play' phase with diverse objects and goals to acquire priors (grasping, in-hand reorientation, pose reaching). Then fine-tune on assembly tasks, focusing exploration on contact-rich final phases. Systematically study design choices: object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, goal precision. Demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and sample efficiency gains.

Result: Prior is 33x more sample-efficient than RL from scratch even with dense multi-stage rewards. Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer achieves 60% success on tight insertions with 0.5 mm clearance; >50% success on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

Conclusion: Task-agnostic play pretraining yields reusable manipulation priors that can be adapted to precise assembly; play-based curricula can bridge gap between generic manipulation and high-precision tasks, enabling better sample efficiency and sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Multi-fingered robots promise the speed and dexterity of human hands, yet challenging problems such as precise assembly have remained out of reach. These tasks are contact-rich, making data collection for imitation learning difficult, and sparse-reward, making direct exploration with reinforcement learning (RL) intractable. Consequently, prior work has made progress by structuring the problem with specialized grippers, tool attachments, and environment fixtures. In this work, we argue that before a robot can perfect precise assembly, it must first learn to play. We further ask the question: what factors in the process of learning to play matter for precise assembly? We propose Play2Perfect, an RL framework for task-agnostic pretraining through play on diverse objects and goals, which is then perfected on precise assembly. The goal of play is to acquire reusable manipulation priors, such as grasping, in-hand reorientation and pose reaching. Finetuning then adapts this general prior to assembly, focusing exploration on the final contact-rich, high-precision interactions needed for success. We systematically study key design choices in play pretraining, including object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, and goal precision. We show that our prior is 33x more sample-efficient than RL training from scratch, even when provided with dense, multi-stage rewards. We demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, achieving 60% success on tight insertions with only 0.5 mm contact clearance, and over 50% success on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

</details>


### [113] [WatchAct: A Benchmark for Behavior-Grounded Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26443)
*Baiqi Li,Ce Zhang,Yu Fang,Yue Yang,Shangzhe Li,Mingyu Ding,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WatchAct introduces a large-scale, vision-language benchmark for robot manipulation grounded in observed human behavior, enabling evaluation of video-to-plan reasoning, policy execution, and end-to-end task completion across 3,000 long-horizon instances in 14 tasks; results show current systems lag human performance, highlighting substantial room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to evaluate robot reasoning about observed human actions over time, not just single-image instructions. Existing benchmarks pair an instruction with a single image, failing to test parsing events, inferring intent, and tracking scene changes across time. WatchAct provides a realistic, scalable testbed tying real human-action videos to executable tasks in simulation and on hardware.

Method: Construct 3,000 long-horizon instances by pairing real-world human-action videos with language instructions and aligned LIBERO simulator scenes and executable tasks. Propose a disentangled evaluation protocol that separately measures video-to-plan reasoning (vision-language models), policy execution under oracle plans, and full task completion with integrated planner–policy pipelines. Evaluate in both simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot across four cognitive domains: Event Grounding, Procedural Reasoning, Implicit Intent Inference, and Episodic Reasoning.

Result: State-of-the-art pipelines (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro with π0.5) achieve 16.3% Success Rate in simulation and 14.0% on real robot; Plan SR is 36.8% (humans 97.1%); Task SR under oracle plans is 21.5% and drops to 10.6% on out-of-domain data, indicating substantial gaps between current systems and human capabilities.

Conclusion: WatchAct reveals that current vision-language planning and robot manipulation systems are far from solving complex, time-evolving, human-behavior–grounded tasks; the dataset and code provide a foundation for evaluating and advancing Video-to-Plan, policy execution, and end-to-end robotic planning.

Abstract: A robot working alongside people must reason about what they have done, in what order, and with what intent. Video carries the spatial layouts, object histories, and gestures that language leaves underspecified, yet today's manipulation benchmarks pair an instruction with a single current image, offering no way to evaluate reasoning over observed human behavior. We introduce WatchAct, a benchmark for robot manipulation grounded in observed human behavior. Each instance pairs a real-world human-action video and a language instruction with an aligned simulator scene and an executable LIBERO task, enabling scalable and reproducible evaluation. WatchAct comprises 3,000 long-horizon instances across 14 tasks in four capability domains drawn from the cognitive demands of watching another agent: parsing events (Event Grounding), recovering procedural structure (Procedural Reasoning), inferring unstated intent (Implicit Intent Inference), and tracking how the scene was changed (Episodic Reasoning). We further propose a disentangled evaluation protocol that separately measures (i)~video-to-plan reasoning by vision-language models, (ii)~policy execution under oracle plans, and (iii)~full task completion by integrated planner--policy pipelines. In both simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot, current systems remain far from solving WatchAct. The best pipeline, Gemini-3.1-Pro with $π_{0.5}$, reaches only 16.3% Success Rate (SR) in simulation and 14.0% on the real robot. Gemini-3.1-Pro attains just 36.8% Plan SR (vs. 97.1% for humans), while $π_{0.5}$ reaches only 21.5% Task SR under oracle plans and drops to 10.6% on out-of-domain scenarios. Dataset and code are available at https://baiqi-li.github.io/watchact_page/.

</details>


### [114] [OSC2Runner: OpenSCENARIO 2.x Compliant High-Fidelity AV Simulation in CARLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26533)
*Thoshitha Gamage,Lasanthi Gamage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OSC2Runner provides a native OpenSCENARIO v2.x execution backend mapping to CARLA via a multi-pass transpiler that emits type-safe ASTs into dynamic, deterministic behavior trees, enabling precise, boundary-respecting co-simulation and HWIL workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OpenSCENARIO 1.x is legacy; v2.x DSL lacks native execution support in common simulation frameworks, leading to spatiotemporal drift, asynchronous latencies, and kinematic snapping when retrofitting interpreters.

Method: Introduce OSC2Runner: an orchestration framework that compiles v2.x scenarios into CARLA-executable behavior trees using a multi-pass transpiler; generates type-safe ASTs converted to py_trees BTs mapped to CARLA APIs; avoids static trajectory playback by synthesizing dynamic BTs.

Result: Empirical evaluation in adversarial, highly concurrent scenarios shows tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization; kinematic analysis confirms adherence to continuous environmental boundaries.

Conclusion: The approach provides a deterministic backend for co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines, moving Scenario-Based Testing toward mathematically rigorous execution.

Abstract: Scenario-Based Testing predominantly relies on the legacy ASAM OpenSCENARIO 1.x XML standard because existing continuous simulation frameworks lack native execution support for the recently matured v2.x Domain-Specific Language (DSL). Adapting legacy interpreters to evaluate v2.x logic introduces spatiotemporal drift, asynchronous event latencies, and artificial kinematic snapping. Addressing this execution gap, OSC2Runner introduces the first orchestration framework capable of natively mapping the OpenSCENARIO v2.x DSL to CARLA. The framework achieves this by formalizing scenario translation as a compilation pipeline through a multi-pass transpiler architecture. Bypassing static trajectory playback, the architecture synthesizes type-safe Abstract Syntax Trees directly into dynamic deterministic behavior trees (py_trees) natively mapped to CARLA's atomic APIs. Empirical validation in highly concurrent adversarial case studies demonstrates tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100.0 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization. Kinematic analysis proves the strict adherence to continuous environmental boundaries. This architecture transitions Scenario-Based Testing from approximate behavioral interpretation to mathematically rigorous execution, establishing the deterministic backend required for co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines.

</details>


### [115] [IDEA: Insensitive to Dynamics Mismatch via Effect Alignment for Sim-to-Real Transfer in Multi-Agent Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26575)
*Chenlong Liu,Zhuohui Zhang,Xinyan Chen,Zhipeng Wang,Bin Cheng,Bin He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A sim-to-real method for multi-agent control using effect alignment and semantic action abstraction to mitigate dynamics mismatch; includes action synchronization; shows improved training efficiency and real-world performance on four tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based multi-agent control struggles with sim-to-real transfer due to inaccurate dynamics models and low-level control sensitivity; need dynamics-insensitive training and robust transfer.

Method: Integrates random environmental structure with discrete semantic actions in a closed-loop control framework, introduces effect alignment to reduce dynamics mismatch, and implements an action synchronization mechanism to align inter-agent timing.

Result: Training efficiency markedly improves over mainstream transfer methods; higher success rates in real-world deployments; demonstrated robustness under dynamics mismatch across four multi-agent navigation tasks.

Conclusion: Semantic-action-based abstraction plus timing synchronization enhances sim-to-real transfer for multi-agent control, improving robustness and deployment stability in the presence of dynamics mismatch.

Abstract: Complex multi-agent control tasks remain challenging for traditional rule-based and model-based approaches, motivating the adoption of learning-based methods. However, learning-based methods often struggle with sim-to-real transfer because they rely on accurate dynamics modeling or system identification and learn policies in low-level control spaces that are highly sensitive to dynamics mismatch, making them costly and fragile in complex environments. To address this issue, we propose a sim-to-real method for multi-agent control, which is insensitive to dynamics mismatch via effect alignment. Our method combines random environmental structure with discrete semantic actions through closed-loop control, elevating policy learning to a semantic abstraction level. Additionally, we develop an action synchronization mechanism that mitigates inter-agent action timing mismatches, thereby enhancing the temporal consistency of the system. Experiments on four multi-agent navigation tasks demonstrate that our method substantially improves training efficiency over mainstream transfer methods and achieves higher success rates in real-world scenarios, thereby improving the robustness and deployment stability of multi-agent systems under dynamics mismatch.

</details>


### [116] [Inference-Time Robot Behavior Steering through Physically-Aware Reconfiguration of Task-Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26588)
*Yiyuan Pan,Hanjiang Hu,Shangtao Li,Xusheng Luo,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReStruct steers learned robot policies at inference time by reconfiguring a neural automaton-based skeleton with a fixed low-level controller, enabling physically-aware control under new task structures and outperforming prior methods (up to 25% in success and preference following).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time steering of learned policies is essential but challenging: end-to-end approaches need retraining or expert-level guidance; neuro-symbolic methods rely on predefined symbols that can yield logically valid but physically infeasible plans. A flexible, physically-aware method for steering via task-structure reconfiguration is needed.

Method: Decompose the visuomotor policy into a high-level state-machine skeleton and a low-level continuous controller (residual policy). Represent user preferences as a neural automaton and incorporate them into the skeleton via a synchronous product to reconfigure the task structure. Keep the controller frozen; update the action priors from the skeleton to achieve physically-aware control under the modified task structure.

Result: In simulation and real-world experiments, ReStruct can steer a broad range of preferences—from object-centric specifications to temporal-logic constraints. After steering, it outperforms existing methods, including VLA models, by up to 25% in both task success and preference-following.

Conclusion: ReStruct provides a general, inference-time mechanism to steer learned policies by reconfiguring task structure with an automaton-guided skeleton, enabling diverse preference types and improved performance without retraining.

Abstract: A central challenge in deploying learned robot policies is inference-time behavior steering: redirecting a policy at test time to satisfy user preferences not anticipated during training, without retraining. Existing methods fail in two modes: end-to-end methods require fine-tuning or expert-level guidance, while neuro-symbolic methods rely on predefined symbols whose edits can result in logically reasonable but physically infeasible plans. To address this challenge, we propose ReStruct, which builds upon a neural automaton policy that decomposes a visuomotor policy into a high-level state-machine skeleton capturing task structure and a low-level continuous controller represented as a residual policy. Specifically, ReStruct adopts the automaton to represent the preference and incorporates it into the skeleton through a synchronous product, thereby reconfiguring the task structure. With the controller kept frozen, the action priors provided by the skeleton are updated accordingly to enable physically-aware control under a modified task structure. Extensive experiments from simulation and real-world show that ReStruct steers a wide range of preferences, from object-centric specifications to temporal-logic constraints, and after steering surpasses existing methods, exceeding VLA models in both task success and preference-following by up to 25%.

</details>


### [117] [Bridging Handheld and Teleoperated Supervision for Contact-Rich Manipulation via State-Gated Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26603)
*Vidullan Surendran,Neehar Peri,David Watkins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BRIDGE uses a gated expert mixture of diffusion policies to route phase-specific actions, enabling efficient hybrid imitation by combining handheld data with targeted teleoperation and outperforming handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% in three contact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Handheld data collection enables scalable supervision but often lacks dynamic feasibility in contact-rich phases; teleoperation provides desired actions but is costly to collect. A phase-aware, efficient strategy is needed to leverage both data sources without detrimental mismatch.

Method: Bi-modal Routing for Imitation Data via Gated Experts (BRIDGE): a mixture of diffusion-policy experts, each with a phase-specific task head. A gating mechanism conditioned on the current robot state routes to the appropriate head, enabling the use of desired actions during contact-sensitive segments. Training uses handheld data augmented with a small number of targeted teleoperated demonstrations; naive mixing underperforms handheld data alone, which BRIDGE overcomes by phase-aware routing.

Result: BRIDGE improves success rates over handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% across three contact-rich manipulation tasks, demonstrating effective phase-specific use of desired actions and improved data efficiency.

Conclusion: BRIDGE addresses the observed mismatch between observed (handheld) and desired (teleoperated) supervision by introducing a state-conditioned, phase-aware routing mechanism that selectively uses teleoperation where needed, yielding substantial gains in contact-rich manipulation performance.

Abstract: Handheld data collection systems, such as the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI), enable scalable data collection across diverse environments but only capture observed actions rather than the desired actions executed by a robot controller. In contrast, teleoperation captures desired actions directly, but is prohibitively time-consuming to collect. We revisit this trade-off through the lens of action validity across task phases. We observe that handheld trajectories provide valid supervision in tolerant, free-space phases, but lack dynamic feasibility in contact-sensitive phases, where tracking observed trajectories at high stiffness produces large, unsafe contact forces. We study the interaction between these two supervision types for contact-rich manipulation and find that training policies that combine handheld data with a small number of targeted teleoperated demonstrations provide an efficient hybrid strategy. Specifically, rather than teleoperating the entire task, we only collect partial teleoperated demonstrations for task segments where base handheld policies fail. However, naively mixing handheld and teleoperated phase-specific data yields worse performance than training on handheld data alone. To address this mismatch between observed and desired supervision, we propose Bi-modal Routing for Imitation Data via Gated Experts (BRIDGE), a mixture of diffusion policy experts that routes between specialist task phase heads conditioned on the current robot state. Notably, our approach enables task-phase specific use of desired actions during contact sensitive segments and improves success rates over handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% across three contact-rich manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [118] [A Closed-Form 4-DoF Inter-Robot Pose Estimator using Bearing-only Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26616)
*Qixin De,Ao Zhuang,Yechen Zhang,Zhuozhou Qian,Danping Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a closed-form 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator for bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization, analyzes observability under degenerate motions, and introduces an adaptive observability test to select estimation instants. Demonstrates improved accuracy and lower computational cost with reliable estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the observability degeneracy and high computational cost in 6-DoF bearing-odometry cooperative localization, especially under specific motion patterns that hinder reliable inter-robot pose estimation.

Method: Develop a closed-form 4-DoF estimator by relaxing rotation constraints and using error projection for translations; perform theoretical observability analysis identifying degeneracies (collinear and shape-preserving motions); introduce an autonomous observability test module to choose optimal estimation instants rather than fixed sliding windows; validate via extensive simulations and real-world experiments.

Result: The approach yields higher estimation accuracy with significantly lower computational cost; the observability test module provides reliable estimation while minimizing the data collection interval.

Conclusion: 4-DoF bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization can achieve robust and efficient inter-robot pose estimation across a broader set of maneuvers, owing to relaxed rotational constraints and adaptive observability-based timing.

Abstract: Bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization has attracted increasing research interest due to its minimal infrastructure requirements, low communication bandwidth and broad applicability in complex environments. However, existing 6-DoF approaches still face challenges in rapidly obtaining accurate and reliable inter-robot pose estimation, as the system is prone to observability degeneracy under specific motion patterns. To address these issues, we first propose a closed-form 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator, which relaxes nonlinear constraints for rotations estimation and employs error projection for translations estimation. We then conduct a theoretical analysis of the system's observability, identifying degeneracy under two typical motion patterns: collinear and shape-preserving formations. The analysis further shows that the proposed 4-DoF system requires less stringent motion excitation for observability, enabling reliable estimation under a broader range of cooperative maneuvers. Furthermore, an observability test module is introduced to autonomously determine the optimal estimation instant, eliminating reliance on a predefined fixed-length sliding window. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves higher estimation accuracy with significantly low computational cost, and the observability test module ensures estimation reliability while minimizing the data collection interval.

</details>


### [119] [Hardware Design for Table Tennis Robot Capable of Beating Professional Players](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26643)
*Nobuhiko Mukai,Pavel Adodin,Stefan Heusser,Alexander Sigrist,Divij Grover,Guillem Torrente,Farshad Khadivar,Takekazu Kakinuma,Peter Dürr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 8-DoF table tennis robot Ace achieves pro-level performance via hardware optimization and RL control; demonstrates 0.8 s stroke, 22 m/s racket velocity, and victories against professional players.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine hardware and control requirements to beat elite players and translate elite-player motions into hardware specifications.

Method: Extract target workspace, payload, external-force resistance, physical performance, serve capability, and end-effector accuracy from elite players. Apply topology optimization to minimize mass while preserving stiffness. Use inverse-dynamics torque model for motor/gearbox selection. Develop low-order per-joint dynamics with delay compensation and integrate into simulations to support an RL control policy. Validate with experiments including full-stroke swings and matches against pros.

Result: Development of Ace with updated mechanism; successful execution of repeatable full-stroke swings (cycle time 0.8 s) and peak racket-center velocity 22 m/s; defeated multiple professional players.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates a feasible hardware-control pipeline for pro-level table tennis robots, combining topology-optimized architecture, torque-aware actuation, and RL-based control; further details on robustness, generalization, and comprehensive benchmarking would strengthen claims.

Abstract: This paper focuses on the hardware specifications required for a table tennis robot to beat professional players. After analyzing the motions of elite players, we defined target specifications for the workspace, payload, external-force resistance, physical performance, serve capability, and end-effector accuracy. Based on these specifications, we developed "Ace", a custom 8-DoF robot. The mechanical structure was improved through topology optimization to minimize mass while preserving stiffness. Motor and gearbox selection was optimized using an inverse-dynamics torque model. Low-order per-joint dynamics models with delay compensation were identified and integrated into simulation to enable the use of an RL control policy. Experiments demonstrated repeated full-stroke swings with a cycle time of 0.8 s and a peak racket-center velocity of 22 m/s. The robot successfully defeated multiple professional players.

</details>


### [120] [LAMP: Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives for Feasible Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26661)
*Sangjin Han,Hoseong Jung,Jeongtae Her,Changhyun Choi,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LAMP introduces lane-aligned motion primitives via VQ-VAE and a feasibility-aware selector to produce diverse, topology-consistent multimodal forecasts with competitive accuracy on Argoverse 2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional predictors optimize displacement error but yield topology-inconsistent multimodal predictions, risking unsafe planning; need topology-aware, feasible predictions.

Method: Train a VQ-VAE to learn discrete motion primitives capturing spatiotemporal patterns; use lane-topology priors to filter queries via a feasibility-aware intention selector; decoder prioritizes topology-consistent intentions while maintaining diversity.

Result: Achieves parity with SOTA in prediction accuracy; outperforms baselines in feasibility and diversity metrics on Argoverse 2.

Conclusion: Topology-aware primitive representation and feasibility filtering improve reliability and diversity of multimodal forecasts; promising for safe autonomous planning and could generalize to other topology-rich domains.

Abstract: Motion forecasting is essential for autonomous driving systems to enable safe decision-making and planning in complex driving scenarios. While existing predictors excel at minimizing standard displacement errors, they often overlook the adherence to lane topology of multimodal predictions, particularly for lower-probability modes. Consequently, predicted trajectories may violate physical and logical constraints, making the prediction set unreliable for safety-critical planning. In this paper, we propose LAMP (Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives), a topology-aware forecasting framework that anchors multimodal prediction to structured motion primitives aligned with lane topology. Specifically, we use a VQ-VAE to learn shape-aware motion primitives as discrete intention queries, capturing spatiotemporal patterns beyond endpoint-based intentions. We further introduce a feasibility-aware intention selector trained with a lane-topology prior for filtering unreachable intention queries, guiding the decoder to prioritize topology-consistent intentions while preserving behavioral diversity. Extensive experiments on the Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that LAMP achieves prediction accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art baselines while outperforming them in feasibility and diversity metrics.

</details>


### [121] [Tactile-WAM: Touch-Aware World Action Model with Tactile Asymmetric Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26663)
*Siyu Wu,Linjing You,Junjie Zhu,Yaozu Liu,Changhao Zhang,Jian Liu,Weiqiang Wang,Qi Li,Jituo Li,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tactile-WAM with TAAM mitigates tactile pollution in World Action Models by using a VideoClean mask to separate tactile tokens from visual prediction and a touch-aware bias to guide action attention, yielding significant gains in contact-rich manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visually plausible futures in WAMs can be physically incomplete in contact-rich tasks due to subtle tactile interactions (slip, jamming, contact normals) that are weakly visible. Unconstrained tactile signals can pollute video dynamics, degrading predictions.

Method: Introduce Tactile-WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM). TAAM uses a VideoClean mask to block video-query access to tactile key/value tokens while keeping action-query access, and a touch-aware bias derived from predicted touch changes to modulate action attention toward tactile tokens during denoising.

Result: On ManiFeel, mean success rate increases by 38.9% overall and 86% on contact-rich tasks.

Conclusion: TAAM effectively mitigates tactile pollution, enabling tactile information to improve action generation without degrading visual predictions, demonstrating the value of touch-aware mechanisms in WAMs for contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) generate actions together with predicted futures, offering a powerful interface for robot decision making. In contact-rich manipulation, however, visually plausible futures can be physically incomplete: insertion, assembly, search, and reorientation often depend on slip, jamming, contact normals, or small alignment errors that are weakly visible or hidden in RGB. A natural solution is to predict future tactile states, however, we identify tactile pollution, a failure mode where unconstrained tactile-token injection degrades video and action prediction by forcing a visual dynamics model to absorb sparse, local, event-driven contact signals. To address this, we propose Tactile-WAM, a touch-aware WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM). TAAM combines a VideoClean mask, which blocks video-query access to tactile key/value tokens while preserving action-query access, with a touch-aware bias for action attention. The VideoClean mask protects visual prediction while keeping contact information available for action generation; the touch-aware bias is derived from predicted touch changes and modulates action attention to tactile tokens during denoising. On ManiFeel, Tactile-WAM improves the mean success rate by 38.9% overall and by 86% on contact-rich tasks.

</details>


### [122] [Learning Motion Feasibility from Point Clouds in Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26700)
*Sajid Ansari,Arthi,Girish Varma,Antony Thomas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Large-scale study predicting grasp feasibility from raw RGB-D for a 7-DOF manipulator in cluttered scenes. Introduces 2.7M feasibility labels across 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes, comparing MLP-, volumetric-CNN-, and point-cloud Transformer-based classifiers. The best model, GRASPFC-PTX (a point-cloud transformer), achieves AUROC 0.996 on novel objects and runs significantly faster than sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feasibility checks in cluttered environments are a major bottleneck for sampling-based motion planning. Existing infeasibility certification methods are limited to low-dimensional spaces and simplified environments. Learning feasibility directly from raw RGB-D observations aims to accelerate planning and enable robust manipulation.

Method: Construct a large-scale RGB-D dataset with 2.7 million grasp feasibility labels spanning 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. Benchmark three classifier families (MLP-based, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud Transformer) under matched training conditions. Propose and evaluate GRASPFC-PTX, a point-cloud transformer for feasibility prediction.

Result: Best model GRASPFC-PTX achieves AUROC of 0.996 on novel objects. The approach provides predictions significantly faster than SBMPs. Dataset comprises 2.7M labels, 88 objects, and 190 cluttered scenes.

Conclusion: Learning-based motion feasibility prediction from raw RGB-D is viable and scalable for cluttered real-world manipulation. The large benchmark enables systematic evaluation across architectures, with the point-cloud Transformer delivering state-of-the-art performance and practical speedups over traditional planning methods.

Abstract: Motion feasibility prediction plays a central role in robotics, particularly in task and motion planning and manipulation. A major bottleneck for this problem in cluttered environments is that infeasible planning attempts by Sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) can incur substantial computational cost. Also existing approaches for infeasibility certification are limited to low-dimensional configuration spaces and often assume simplified geometric environments represented by primitive objects with known parameters. We study the complementary problem of learning motion feasibility prediction directly from raw RGB-D observations for a 7-DOF manipulator operating in realistic cluttered scenes. We introduce the first large-scale benchmark for this setting, comprising 2.7M grasp feasibility labels over 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. We benchmark three representative classifier families spanning MLP- based, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud-based Transformer architectures under matched training conditions. Our best model, GRASPFC-PTX (a point-cloud transformer), achieves an AUROC of 0.996 on Novel objects while providing predictions significantly faster than SBMPs.

</details>


### [123] [PressMimic: Pressure-Guided Motion Capture and Control for Humanoid Robot Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26741)
*Yi Lu,Shenghao Ren,Tianyu Xiong,Zhaoxiang Li,Jiaqi Li,He Zhang,Tao Yu,Qiu Shen,Xun Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PressMimic introduces pressure as a unified physical grounding signal to bridge perception and control in humanoid motion imitation, addressing contact dynamics to reduce artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing perception-to-control pipelines rely on vision-based motion capture and kinematic imitation, which ignore contact dynamics and cause artifacts like foot sliding and floor penetration; there is a need for a physically grounded framework using pressure to resolve contact ambiguities.

Method: Perception: FRAPPE++—a multimodal RGB+pressure model for 3D pose and global motion estimation; Control: PSP—a pressure-supervised reinforcement learning policy; Dataset: MotionPRO with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture data.

Result: Incorporating pressure improves pose estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability across perception and control stages.

Conclusion: Pressure acts as an effective physical grounding signal that unifies perception and control, enabling physically consistent humanoid motion imitation.

Abstract: Humanoid motion imitation requires not only accurate perception of human kinematics but also faithful reproduction of physical interactions with the environment. However, existing pipelines rely primarily on vision-based motion capture and kinematic imitation, largely ignoring contact dynamics, leading to artifacts such as foot sliding, floor penetration, and unstable behaviors. In this work, we revisit humanoid motion imitation from the perspective of physical grounding and leverage pressure as a unified modality across perception and control. We present PressMimic, a framework that integrates pressure into the full pipeline from motion capture to humanoid control. In the perception stage, we introduce FRAPPE++, a multimodal model that fuses RGB and pressure to jointly estimate 3D pose and global motion, where pressure provides explicit contact and support constraints to resolve ambiguity in vision-based estimation. In the control stage, we propose a pressure-supervised policy (PSP) that incorporates pressure-derived signals into reinforcement learning, enabling physically consistent contact patterns during execution. We further construct MotionPRO, a large-scale dataset with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture data. Experiments show that pressure improves motion estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability. These results demonstrate that pressure serves as an effective physical grounding signal, bridging perception and control for physically consistent humanoid motion imitation.

</details>


### [124] [SSI-Policy: Learning Structured Scene Interfaces for Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26800)
*Kaijun Wang,Zikai Ouyang,Xuping Wu,Jinyi Hong,Wei Pan,Haibo Lu,Jia Pan,Wei Zhang,Linfang Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSI-Policy introduces a robot-agnostic RGB-only Structured Scene Interface (SSI) that fuses monocular depth, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D trajectories to decouple perception from control; achieves strong few-shot performance on LIBERO (10 demonstrations) and 13 real-world tasks, with ablations showing complementary geometric and motion cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address low-data real-world robotic manipulation where scalable task-level reasoning and explicit physical structure are hard to achieve; existing video- or depth-based approaches drift or require depth sensors; a unified RGB-only interface can enable robust, sample-efficient learning.

Method: Propose SSI-Policy built around Structured Scene Interface (SSI): an RGB-only intermediate representation encoding monocular depth features, language-grounded layouts, and 2D motion trajectories conditioned on instructions; SSI is robot-agnostic and trainable from action-free video, decoupling perception from control; train policy with few demonstrations; validate on LIBERO with 10 demos per task and on 13 real tasks; ablation studies.

Result: On LIBERO, SSI-Policy outperforms the strongest prior by ~15%; competitive with methods using 50 demonstrations and large-scale pretraining; ablations show geometric and motion cues provide complementary benefits within SSI; real-world evaluation across spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

Conclusion: The SSI-Policy demonstrates effective perception-control decoupling via a unified RGB-only representation, enabling scalable, data-efficient learning for complex robotic manipulation; spatial grounding and motion cues within SSI are complementary and beneficial across tasks and embodiments.

Abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation demands spatial grounding, task-aware reasoning, and precise control. Learning such capabilities becomes particularly challenging in the low-data regime. Prior methods often trade off scalable task-level reasoning and explicit physical structure: video-based approaches can drift geometrically over long horizons, 3D approaches often require depth sensing, and many flow/trajectory interfaces emphasize motion without an explicit RGB-only geometric representation. We introduce SSI-Policy, a modular framework built around a Structured Scene Interface (SSI) -- a unified, RGB-only intermediate representation that jointly encodes monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories. Critically, SSI is robot-agnostic and trainable from action-free video, decoupling perception from control so that the downstream policy can learn from few demonstrations. On the LIBERO benchmark with only 10 demonstrations per task, SSI-Policy improves over the strongest prior method by nearly 15\% and remains competitive with 50-demo methods that leverage large-scale external pretraining. Ablations show that geometric and motion cues provide complementary benefits within the shared interface. We further validate on 13 real-world tasks spanning spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [125] [Improving Vision-Language-Action Model Fine-Tuning with Structured Stage and Keyframe Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26801)
*Yuan Xu,Yixiang Chen,Kai Wang,Jiabing Yang,Peiyan Li,Qisen Ma,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StaKe introduces plug-in auxiliary supervision for Vision-Language-Action models by deriving a stage classifier and a keyframe predictor from demonstrations; these lightweight heads enrich training, improving long-horizon manipulation performance without altering inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address uniform action supervision during fine-tuning that fails at gripper-event transitions; need structured guidance on manipulation stages and next target actions.

Method: Train two auxiliary heads (stage classifier and keyframe predictor) from demonstration gripper states; integrate as plug-in supervision during training; not affecting base VLA policy or inference; evaluated on bimanual simulation and real-robot Franka tasks.

Result: Consistent improvements in success rates: 14% relative gain in bimanual simulation and 56% in Franka real-robot tasks; larger gains on longer-horizon tasks with more transitions; ablation studies and qualitative analysis support design choices.

Conclusion: Structured auxiliary supervision is effective and general for enhancing VLA fine-tuning in long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. During fine-tuning, however, action supervision applies equally across all timesteps, without structured supervision on which manipulation stage the robot is in or what the next gripper-event target should be. This causes failures to concentrate around challenging gripper-event transitions. To address this, we propose StaKe, a plug-in auxiliary supervision framework that automatically derives two complementary signals from demonstration gripper states without manual annotation: a stage classifier that identifies the current manipulation stage, and a keyframe predictor that estimates the target joint action at the next gripper transition. Both are modeled as lightweight auxiliary heads that enrich the learned representations during training, while leaving the base VLA policy architecture and inference loop unchanged. Experiments on bimanual simulation and single-arm Franka real-robot tasks show that StaKe consistently improves success rates (relative gains of 14% and 56%, respectively), with larger improvements on longer-horizon tasks that involve more gripper-event transitions. Ablation studies validate each design choice, and qualitative analysis confirms that the learned representations faithfully track manipulation stages. These results indicate that structured supervision is an effective and general strategy for enhancing VLA fine-tuning in long-horizon manipulation. Project website: https://hi-yuanxu.github.io/StaKe-Web/

</details>


### [126] [Ordinal Neural Collapse as a Representation Prior for Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26839)
*E-In Son,Jung-Taak Kim,Seung-Woo Seo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ORION imposes an ordinal-structured latent space for navigation actions, integrated with diffusion-based planning, yielding superior performance over end-to-end and neural-collapse baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end imitation with a single action loss yields action-agnostic encoders; large variations and distractors degrade reliability; an ordinal-aligned representation should reduce ambiguity at decision points.

Method: Introduce Ordinal Neural Collapse for Visual Navigation (ORION): organize encoder representations along an ordinal axis corresponding to sorted navigation actions (e.g., Far Left to Far Right). Enforce sequential arrangement on axis and suppress off-axis variance within each action class; pretrain encoder; integrate into diffusion-based navigation framework; fine-tune end-to-end.

Result: In simulation and real-world experiments, ORION outperforms end-to-end and neural-collapse baselines in navigation success rate and progress, especially in complex multi-way intersections and visually challenging scenes.

Conclusion: Aligning latent space with ordinal action structure improves robustness and consistency of navigation policies; the approach can be integrated with diffusion planners and extended to other ordinal-control tasks.

Abstract: Learning robust navigation policies directly from visual observations remains a fundamental challenge in vision-based robotic navigation. In end-to-end imitation learning approaches, the visual encoder and action decoder are jointly optimized using a single action loss, which provides only an indirect supervisory signal to the encoder. This indirect supervision frequently results in the encoder learning ambiguous, action-agnostic representations. The problem is further complicated by substantial variations in scene structure and appearance across diverse environments, as well as the prevalence of visual distractors inherent to real-world navigation settings. Such action-agnostic features cause the navigation policy to produce inconsistent actions at ambiguous decision points, leading to navigation failure. To overcome these limitations, we propose ORION (Ordinal Neural Collapse for Visual Navigation), a method that explicitly organizes the encoder's representation space according to the ordinal structure of navigation actions. In the context of goal-directed navigation, ego-centric control categories from Far Left to Far Right exhibit a natural ordinal relationship in which neighboring classes share similar visual contexts, while semantically opposing classes differ substantially in appearance. We encourage class representations to be arranged sequentially along a single discriminative axis, while suppressing off-axis variance within each class. The pretrained encoder is then integrated into a diffusion-based navigation framework, and the full pipeline is fine-tuned end-to-end. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show that ORION consistently outperforms end-to-end and neural collapse baselines in navigation success rate and goal progress, with notable gains in visually challenging scenarios such as complex multi-way intersections.

</details>


### [127] [Humanoid-DART: Humanoid Loco-Manipulation using Diffusion-guided Augmentation through Relabeling and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26855)
*Pranav Debbad,Kanish Thiagarajan,Victor Dhédin,Shafeef Omar,Majid Khadiv*

Main category: cs.RO
