TL;DR: A self-supervised, goal-conditioned loco-manipulation framework that uses diffusion-based trajectory generation and RL to expand behavior from sparse demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reducing the cost and need for continual human intervention in collecting diverse demonstrations for humanoid loco-manipulation; enabling automatic exploration of the goal space with minimal supervision.

Method: Bootstraps from sparse demonstrations using diffusion-based generation of trajectory intents, then uses reinforcement learning to track those goal-conditioned trajectories across a range of loco-manipulation skills, effectively learning a goal-conditioned policy.

Result: Ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods show the framework is effective across multiple humanoid loco-manipulation skills, validating the approach.

Conclusion: Self-supervised bootstrap from sparse demos coupled with diffusion-guided trajectory generation and RL tracking can progressively expand a humanoid's behavioral repertoire for goal-conditioned control with reduced expert supervision.

Abstract: Imitating human demonstrations has emerged as a dominant paradigm for learning humanoid loco-manipulation policies. However, scaling these approaches remains challenging due to the high cost of collecting diverse demonstrations and the need for continual human intervention to correct policy failures. In this paper, we present a self-supervised framework that bootstraps from sparse demonstrations and progressively expands its behavioral repertoire, enabling the learning of a goal-conditioned policy that automatically explores the goal space with minimal expert supervision. Our approach combines diffusion-based trajectory generation with reinforcement learning, where the latter is used to track goal-conditioned trajectories produced by the diffusion model for a range of loco-manipulation skills. Through extensive ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on multiple humanoid loco-manipulation skills.

</details>


### [128] [PlanRL: A Trajectory Planning Architecture for Reinforcement Learning-based Driving Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26858)
*Joonhee Lim,Yongjae Lee,Jangho Shin,Dongsuk Kum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hybrid RL-based driving policy with a polynomial trajectory planner in Frenet coordinates, incorporating a kinematic feasibility check, achieving notable improvements on CARLA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interpretability, geometric learning complexity, and incompatibility of RL-based direct-control driving agents with modern end-to-end planning architectures by introducing a structured, feasible trajectory planning layer.

Method: Integrates an RL policy with a polynomial-based trajectory planner in a Frenet-frame (curvilinear) coordinate system; applies a kinematic feasibility check during planning to respect vehicle dynamics and reduce tracking errors.

Result: On CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1: driving score +5% and success rate +8%; On CARLA NoCrash: driving score +11% and success rate +19%.

Conclusion: The approach yields meaningful gains over state-of-the-art control-based RL experts, delivering improved interpretability and planning feasibility, with demonstrated compatibility to end-to-end planning pipelines; promising for broader deployment and further ablation study.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prominent framework for developing driving experts in autonomous vehicles. However, most existing RL-based experts are designed to output direct control commands (e.g., throttle, steering), which suffer from a lack of interpretability, high spatial complexity in learning road geometries, and poor compatibility with modern end-to-end planning architectures. To address these limitations, we propose a novel trajectory planning architecture for RL driving experts that integrates an RL policy with a polynomial-based trajectory planner. By employing a Frenet-frame coordinate system, our method simplifies complex road geometries into a curvilinear framework, offering a structured coordinate prior that facilitates policy learning. Furthermore, we incorporate a kinematic feasibility check into the planning stage to ensure that generated trajectories remain within the vehicle's physical limits, effectively mitigating cumulative tracking errors typically found in planning-based systems. We evaluate our approach on key CARLA benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art control-based RL experts. On the CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1 and NoCrash benchmarks, our method improves the driving score by 5% and 11%, respectively, and increases the success rate by 8% and 19%.

</details>


### [129] [Risk-Aware Selective Multimodal Driver Monitoring with Driver-State World Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26922)
*Daosheng Qiu,Haozhuang Chi,Hao Su,Shu Long,Xinyue Miao,Yongle Dong,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cost-aware selective inference for edge multimodal driver monitoring; RGB-physiological student with gating and driver-state world model; improves Macro-F1 to 0.7440 and balanced accuracy to 0.9099 with 11.39M params and 3.08ms latency; reduces unsafe false negatives from 17.37% to ~5% while preserving latency; calibration drift in worst groups remains a challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable low-latency, reliable driver monitoring in automated vehicles under uncertain driver states. Large vision-language models offer priors but are too slow or unreliable for always-on in-cabin use; a deployable, cost-aware framework is needed.

Method: A lightweight RGB-physiological student fusing visual data with window-level HR/EDA; a learned gate that decides when to accept fast predictions or abstain; a driver-state world-modeling module that rolls out latent driver-state features to predict future fast-model errors and counterfactual costs; comprehensive evaluation including scenario-induced driver-demand recognition and ablation studies, with attention to sample-level information in scores and synchronization limitations.

Result: On scenario-induced driver-demand recognition, achieved 0.7440 Macro-F1 and 0.9099 balanced accuracy with 11.39M parameters and 3.08ms inference latency; cost-aware inference reduced unsafe false negatives from 17.37% (always-fast baseline) to ~5% across seeds, with maintained latency; RGB-physiological model outperforms RGB-only and physiology-only baselines; world-modeling provides predictive signals but worst-group calibration drift persists.

Conclusion: Reliable edge driver monitoring requires advances in perception backbones, risk-aware selective control, and group-robust calibration; approaches should address synchronization limitations and ensure robust calibration across diverse operating conditions.

Abstract: Continuous driver monitoring in automated vehicles requires low-latency inference while avoiding unsafe decisions under uncertain driver states. Large vision-language models provide broad multimodal priors, but their latency and limited reliability in this setting make them unsuitable as always-on in-cabin monitors. We propose a cost-aware selective inference framework for deployable multimodal driver monitoring. The core system is a lightweight RGB-physiological student that combines in-cabin visual observations with window-level HR/EDA signals, and a learned gate that decides when to accept the fast prediction or abstain for safety intervention. Additional controls show that the learned scores contain sample-level information beyond scenario priors, while exact physiological synchronization remains a limitation. To incorporate predictive evidence, we further study a compact driver-state world modeling module that rolls out latent driver-state features and estimates future fast-model errors and counterfactual system-level action costs. On scenario-induced driver-demand recognition, the RGB-physiological student improves over RGB-only and physiology-only baselines, reaching 0.7440 Macro-F1 and 0.9099 balanced accuracy with 11.39M parameters and 3.08ms inference latency. Cost-aware selective inference reduces unsafe false negatives from 17.37% under always-fast inference to approximately 5% across seeds, while maintaining deployment-level latency. While driver-state world modeling offers valuable predictive signals, worst-group evaluations highlight persistent operating-point calibration drift. Ultimately, reliable edge driver monitoring requires advancing not only perception backbones, but also risk-aware selective control and group-robust calibration.

</details>


### [130] [UAV-MapFusion: RTK-Aligned Uncertainty-Aware Coarse-to-Fine Multi-Session UAV Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26928)
*Feng Pan,Chunran Zheng,Bing Xue,Yukang Cui,Jiayu Wen,Zhiyu Chen,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware multi-session point cloud map merging for UAVs that combines scene-graph-based initial fusion, RTK spatiotemporal alignment with DTW and MOGP, and a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph with plane-factor refinement to reduce long-range drift while preserving local geometry; validated on real data with plans to release code/dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable large-scale, accurate mapping from UAVs despite limited flight time and storage, addressing the trade-off between long-range drift suppression and local geometric fidelity in multi-session map merging.

Method: 1) Initial multi-session map merging based on a scene graph. 2) RTK spatiotemporal alignment module incorporating: (a) Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) to estimate temporal offsets, (b) Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGP) to recover continuous RTK constraints under incomplete sampling and frame dropouts. 3) Build a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph. 4) Iterative plane-factor refinement to improve local geometry.

Result: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method.

Conclusion: The proposed uncertainty-aware framework successfully mitigates long-range drift while maintaining local geometric accuracy in large-scale UAV-driven map merging, with public release of code and dataset planned.

Abstract: Large-scale point cloud maps are essential for robotics and spatial intelligence tasks. UAVs provide an efficient means for large-scale map acquisition; however, due to limited flight endurance and onboard storage, mapping a large-scale scene within a single flight remains difficult. Existing multi-session map merging methods can extend the mapping range, yet in UAV scenarios they still struggle to simultaneously suppress long-range drift and preserve local geometric accuracy. To address this issue, an uncertainty-aware multi-session point cloud map merging and coarse-to-fine optimization system is proposed. The proposed method first performs initial multi-session map merging based on a scene graph, and then incorporates RTK observations through an RTK spatiotemporal alignment module, where temporal offsets are estimated using Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), and continuous RTK constraints are recovered using Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGP) under incomplete sampling and frame dropouts. On this basis, a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph is constructed, and local geometric accuracy is further improved through iterative plane-factor refinement. Experiments on real-world datasets validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method. To facilitate further research and development in the community, our code and dataset will be publicly released.

</details>


### [131] [RobOralScan: Learning Active Intraoral Scanning for Robotic Dental Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26955)
*Jinhyung Lee,Haeun Yun,Siwon Kim,Gihyun Baek,Sungho Moon,Sehyun Hwang,Sunghoon Im*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First RL-based robotic intraoral scanning pipeline (RobOralScan) that uses a geometric memory observation space and tooth-wise coverage learning to improve full-arch scan coverage and reconstruction quality, with promising sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-arch/long-span intraoral scanning remains labor-intensive with limited automation. In confined oral spaces, narrow FOV and operator workload lead to scan quality degradation due to missing tooth surfaces. An automated, robust RL-based control strategy is needed to reduce manual effort and improve coverage.

Method: Introduce a geometric memory-based observation space that accumulates partial scans into a tri-state geometric representation to enable reasoning over history and unobserved regions. Implement tooth-wise coverage learning with coverage-aware rewards and progressive training to balance global coverage and per-tooth coverage. Use a learned policy to select relative scanner motions from accumulated memory and proprioception for closed-loop scan control in the oral workspace.

Result: Chamfer Distance: 0.00838; average coverage: 92.58%; lower-tail per-tooth coverage: 88.45%; normalized AUC: 0.6674. Completed the scan criterion in 8 of 10 evaluation episodes. Zero-shot sim-to-real experiments demonstrate practical feasibility on a physical robot-scanner setup.

Conclusion: RobOralScan is the first RL-based pipeline for robotic intraoral scanning, showing improved reconstruction coverage and scan quality, with demonstrated sim-to-real transfer capability.

Abstract: Intraoral scanning is widely used for digital optical impressions in prosthodontic, implant, and orthodontic treatment, but full-arch and long-span scanning remain labor-intensive tasks with limited automation. In the confined oral cavity, operators must continuously adjust scanner motion while accumulating narrow field-of-view observations, making reconstruction quality sensitive to missing tooth surfaces and operator workload.
  We propose RobOralScan, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first reinforcement learning (RL)-based pipeline for robotic automatic intraoral scanning. RobOralScan introduces a geometric memory-based observation space that accumulates partial scan observations into a tri-state geometric representation, allowing the policy to reason over scan history and insufficiently observed regions. It further introduces tooth-wise coverage learning, combining coverage-aware reward signals and a progressive training scheme to improve global reconstruction coverage while reducing uneven coverage across individual teeth. The learned policy selects relative scanner motions from accumulated geometric memory and robot proprioception for closed-loop scan control within the oral workspace.
  RobOralScan achieves a Chamfer Distance of 0.00838, an average coverage of 92.58%, a lower-tail per-tooth coverage of 88.45%, and a normalized AUC of 0.6674, completing the scan criterion in 8 of 10 evaluation episodes. Furthermore, zero-shot sim-to-real experiments demonstrate its practical feasibility on a physical robot-scanner setup.

</details>


### [132] [In-Context Model Predictive Generation: Open-Vocabulary Motion Synthesis from Language Models to Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26981)
*Xiaomeng Fu,Junfan Lin,Yang Liu,Yaowei Wang,Guanbin Li,Liang Lin,Ziliang Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ICMPG introduces a closed-loop, MPC-like framework that combines LLM-based semantic planning with physics-based evaluation for text-driven motion synthesis. It consists of CAMG (LLM planner generating candidate motion tokens) and MPG (physical simulation and semantic alignment evaluating candidates with a composite reward). It shows improved physical plausibility and semantic fidelity in open-vocabulary settings and is flexible to different LLM backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the persistent trade-off between semantic fidelity and physical realism in text-driven motion synthesis by integrating language-model planning with inference-time physical feedback, without task-specific policy retraining.

Method: A two-module, closed-loop MPC-like pipeline: (1) CAMG uses an LLM to decompose textual commands and generate candidate motion sequences from motion tokens; (2) MPG evaluates these candidates via physics simulation and semantic alignment, computes a composite reward, and selects the best sequence to guide subsequent generation. The process iterates to adapt to input semantics and the simulated environment.

Result: Extensive experiments in standard and zero-shot open-vocabulary settings show ICMPG generalizes well to diverse commands and yields motions that are more physically plausible and semantically faithful than representative baselines on the evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: The framework bridges semantic interpretation and physical simulation, offering flexibility to integrate different LLM backbones and enabling more versatile and controllable text-driven motion synthesis without task-specific retraining.

Abstract: Synthesizing human motion from textual descriptions is essential for immersive digital applications, yet existing methods face a persistent trade-off between semantic fidelity and physical realism. Large language model (LLM)-based approaches can interpret diverse open-vocabulary instructions and compose high-level action plans, but they often generate motions that violate physical constraints. Physics-aware models improve realism through simulation or control, but they struggle with semantic complexity, fine-grained instructions, and novel concepts. To address this gap, we propose In-Context Model Predictive Generation (ICMPG), a framework that integrates language-model planning with inference-time physical feedback. ICMPG reformulates motion synthesis as a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-like process with two modules. The Context-Aware Motion Generation (CAMG) module uses an LLM as a planner to decompose textual commands and generate candidate motion sequences from motion tokens. The Model Predictive Generation (MPG) module evaluates these candidates through physical simulation and semantic alignment, estimates a composite reward, and selects the best sequence to guide subsequent generation steps. Unlike open-loop generation, this closed-loop refinement enables ICMPG to adapt motions to both the input semantics and the simulated physical environment without task-specific policy retraining. Extensive experiments across standard and zero-shot open-vocabulary settings show that ICMPG generalizes robustly to diverse commands and produces motions that are more physically plausible and semantically faithful than representative baselines on the evaluated benchmarks. The framework bridges semantic interpretation and physical simulation while remaining flexible enough to incorporate different LLM backbones, enabling more versatile and controllable text-driven motion synthesis.

</details>


### [133] [RelAfford6D: Relational 6D Affordance Graphs for Constraint-Driven Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27036)
*Guodong Zhang,Qichen He,Wenyuan Xie,Shaokai Wu,Yanbiao Ji,Qiuchang Li,Bayram Bayramli,Yue Ding,Hongtao Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A training-free framework RelAfford6D connects semantic 6D affordance relations to precise SE(3) control for open-world manipulation, formulating kinematic constraints and manifold-tracking trajectories with closed-loop replanning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between abstract instruction and exact geometric control for articulated objects, addressing the limitations of point-contact or latent affordance methods.

Method: Build Relational 6D Affordance Graph from free-form instruction to derive a semantic topology linking a primary interacting part to a physical anchor; lift graph nodes to SE(3) poses using vision foundation models; cast execution as a kinematic constraint satisfaction problem; generate trajectories by tracking predefined physical manifolds (e.g., revolute, prismatic, orbital paths); implement closed-loop tracking for dynamic replanning under disturbances.

Result: Achieves superior zero-shot success, cross-category generalization, and robustness in both simulation and real-world tests, outperforming existing data-driven baselines.

Conclusion: A physically grounded, training-free approach that integrates semantic topology with geometric constraints to enable robust, generalizable manipulation of articulated objects.

Abstract: Bridging abstract semantics and precise physical control remains a fundamental challenge in open-world robotic manipulation. While recent data-driven policies show promise, their reliance on isolated contact points or latent affordance embeddings lacks the rigorous kinematic constraints necessary for complex articulated objects.To overcome the limitation, we introduce RelAfford6D, a novel training-free framework centered on a Relational 6D Affordance Graph. Given a free-form instruction, our system deduces a semantic topology linking a primary interacting part to its physical anchor. By elevating these topological nodes into precise metric $SE(3)$ poses via vision foundation models, we analytically formulate downstream execution as a kinematic constraint satisfaction problem. The robot synthesizes continuous trajectories by tracking strictly defined physical manifolds (e.g., revolute or prismatic orbits). Coupled with a closed-loop tracking mechanism for dynamic replanning against disturbances, our physically grounded approach achieves superior zero-shot success rates, cross-category generalization and execution robustness in both simulation and the real world environments, outperforming existing data-driven baselines.

</details>


### [134] [ForesightSafety-VLA: A Unified Diagnostic Safety Benchmark for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27079)
*Mingyang Lyu,Yinqian Sun,Yiyang Jia,Sicheng Shen,Moquan Sha,Huangrui Li,Feifei Zhao,Yi Zeng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diagnostic benchmark, ForesightSafety-VLA, assesses embodied safety in vision-language-action models using a 13-category safety taxonomy, structured variation, and process-level risk metrics to diagnose safety failures beyond task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied AI safety is critical for real-world deployment, but current VLA models lack comprehensive safety diagnostics. A focused benchmark is needed to identify failure sources across perception, language, and control.

Method: Introduce a 13-category safety taxonomy (Safe-Core, Safe-Lang, Safe-Vis). Evaluate policies under three variation axes: scene structure, language command, and visual observation. Use process-level risk metrics—cumulative safety cost (CC) and risk exposure time (RET)—and a four-quadrant safe/unsafe decomposition. Instantiate 66 safety-augmented base scenarios in RoboTwin across 5 embodiments and test representative VLA baselines.

Result: Across baselines, even the strongest policy incurs non-trivial safety cost and unsafe nominal success. Structure and visual variation degrade safety more than language variation. Performance indicates embodied safety is tightly linked to perception, grounding, and control competence and cannot be achieved by post-hoc safety filtering alone.

Conclusion: Embodied safety requires integrated perception-grounding-control competence. ForesightSafety-VLA enables diagnosis of safety failures and highlights the need for safety-aware design beyond task achievement, addressing the interaction of perception, grounding, and control.

Abstract: In embodied intelligence, safety is a prerequisite for reliable robot deployment in the physical world. Current vision-language-action (VLA) models continue to advance toward general-purpose task capability, yet their embodied safety limits remain poorly understood. To address this gap, we introduce ForesightSafety-VLA, a diagnostic benchmark that makes safety the primary evaluation target for VLA systems. We define a 13-category safety taxonomy covering physical interaction safety (Safe-Core), instruction-side safety (Safe-Lang), and perception-side safety (Safe-Vis), and evaluate policies under three controlled dimensions of variation -- scene structure, language command, and visual observation -- so that failure sources can be diagnosed rather than hidden in a single aggregate score. Beyond binary task success, ForesightSafety-VLA measures process-level risk through cumulative safety cost (CC) and risk exposure time (RET), together with a four-quadrant decomposition of safe/unsafe success and failure. We instantiate 66 safety-augmented base scenarios in RoboTwin across 5 embodiments and report results on representative VLA baselines. Across the evaluated baselines, even the strongest policy incurs non-trivial safety cost and unsafe nominal success, while structure and visual variation induce substantially stronger safety degradation than ordinary language variation. These results suggest that embodied safety is tightly coupled to perception, grounding, and control competence rather than being reducible to post-hoc safety filtering alone.

</details>


### [135] [Proposal-Conditioned Latent Diffusion for Closed-Loop Traffic Scenario Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27123)
*Shubham Vaijanath Phoolari,Aleyna Kara,Christoph Lauer,Steven Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based closed-loop traffic scenario generator conditioned on scene context and multimodal priors, with optional test-time guidance, enabling efficient, controllable, realistic multi-agent behavior for autonomous vehicle planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve realistic multi-agent traffic simulation within time-constrained replanning loops while ensuring scene-consistency and controllability; existing diffusion methods are accurate but slow.

Method: A diffusion-based framework conditioned on instance-centric scene context and multimodal proposal priors; uses a compact action-latent representation and proposal-based initialization to speed sampling; supports test-time guidance to shape safety-critical behaviors; requires no retraining.

Result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset, achieves favorable balance among realism, safety, and controllability across diverse scenarios; shows improved sampling efficiency and reduced per-step runtime; test-time guidance enables tunable trade-offs among objectives.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides an efficient, controllable, and realistic closed-loop traffic generator suitable for planning loops, with test-time guidance offering adjustable safety-realism trade-offs.

Abstract: Closed-loop traffic simulation remains challenging because it must generate interactive multi-agent behaviors that are scene-consistent and controllable throughout rollout. Prior diffusion-based approaches achieve strong realism, but their computational cost can hinder deployment in time-constrained replanning loops for autonomous vehicle planning and simulation. We present a diffusion-based scenario generation framework conditioned on instance-centric scene context and multimodal proposal priors, with optional test-time guidance for shaping safety-critical behaviors. A compact action-latent representation and proposal-based initialization improve sampling efficiency and reduce per-step runtime without retraining. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset demonstrate a favorable balance among realism, safety, and controllability across diverse interactive scenarios, while showing that test-time guidance enables systematic trade-offs among competing objectives.

</details>


### [136] [PAMAE: Phase-Aware-MoE Action Experts Towards Reliable Flow-Matching Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27144)
*Jiayu Yang,Tao Yang,Xiang Chang,Fei Chao,Changjing Shang,Qiang Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAMAE introduces a phase-aware mixture-of-experts action module for flow-matching VLA policies to achieve phase-consistent action generation, improving multi-stage manipulation success by up to 9.2% in simulation through a two-stage training and a phase-aware router.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow-matching VLA policies typically use a single shared action expert, hindering phase-specific control across execution stages.

Method: Replace action expert with a sparse mixture of experts, implement a phase-aware router driven by execution-phase cues, add a lightweight phase prediction head and a routing alignment loss, and train in two stages (warm-up with standard loss, then phase-consistent routing with auxiliary supervision).

Result: In multi-stage manipulation simulations, PAMAE improves task success by up to 9.2% over strong baselines; ablations show both phase-supervised routing and staged optimization are essential.

Conclusion: Phase-aware expert allocation enhances reliability and action quality of flow-matching VLA policies and is a promising direction for robust multi-stage robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Reliable action generation for multi-stage robotic manipulation remains challenging for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. While existing flow-matching VLA policies offer strong multimodal grounding and generalization, they typically employ a single shared action expert, limiting their ability to capture phase-specific control patterns across distinct execution stages. We propose a plug-and-play Phase-Aware Mixture-of-Experts Action Module (PAMAE), as a step towards more reliable phase-consistent action generation. PAMAE replaces the original flow-matching action expert with a sparse expert mixture while preserving the pretrained VLA backbone. PAMAE introduces a phase-aware router that leverages execution-phase cues to allocate action generation across experts, supported by a lightweight phase prediction head and a routing alignment objective. To stabilize specialization, we adopt a two-stage training scheme that first warms up the expert module under the standard flow-matching loss and then optimizes phase-consistent routing under auxiliary supervision. On multi-stage manipulation simulation tasks, PAMAE improves task success by up to \textbf{9.2\%} over strong VLA baselines. Further ablations show that both phase-supervised routing and staged optimization are essential for the observed gains. Our results highlight phase-consistent expert allocation as an effective mechanism for improving the reliability and action quality of flow-matching VLA policies.

</details>


### [137] [PhysReflect-VLA: Physical Feasibility and Self-Reflective Regulation for Reliable Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27146)
*Jiayu Yang,Tao Yang,Weijun Li,Xiang Chang,Fei Chao,Changjing Shang,Qiang Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhysReflect-VLA augments Vision-Language-Action policies with online physical feasibility checks, explanations, and reflection to enable reliable, long-horizon manipulation; achieves about 5.4% average improvement in task success on real, multi-stage tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon robotic manipulation is vulnerable to physically infeasible transitions, contact disturbances, and lack of online self-correction. Standard VLA models are typically feed-forward and do not verify feasibility or diagnose execution errors in real time.

Method: Introduce a Feasibility Operator to assess dynamically feasible state transitions for candidate actions; an Action Explanation Operator to verify transition coherence; and an LLM-based Reflection Module to analyze state discrepancies and generate corrective guidance. Integrate these in a plug-and-play, closed-loop control pipeline with a two-stage training procedure for stabilizing feasibility modeling and reflection integration.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multi-stage, contact-rich real-world manipulation tasks shows consistent improvements in stage-wise stability and overall task success compared with representative VLA baselines, with an average gain of 5.4%. Ablation studies indicate both feasibility checking and reflection-based correction contribute to robustness.

Conclusion: Embedding physical consistency checks and online self-reflection into VLA policies yields more reliable long-horizon manipulation. The framework is plug-and-play and enhances robustness without requiring fundamental changes to the underlying VLA models.

Abstract: Long-horizon robotic manipulation is highly sensitive to physically infeasible transitions, contact-induced disturbances, and the lack of effective self-correction during execution. Although Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide strong task grounding through multimodal learning, they typically generate actions in a feed-forward manner without explicitly checking physical feasibility or diagnosing execution errors online. We present PhysReflect-VLA, a plug-and-play execution-time reliability framework that augments VLA policies with physical feasibility evaluation and structured self-reflection in a closed-loop control pipeline. A Feasibility Operator evaluates whether candidate actions induce dynamically consistent state transitions; an Action Explanation Operator verifies transition coherence; and an LLM-based Reflection Module analyzes state discrepancies to generate corrective guidance for subsequent actions. A two-stage training procedure stabilizes feasibility modeling and integrates reflection into the control loop. Experiments on multi-stage, contact-rich real-world manipulation tasks show consistent improvements in stage-wise stability and overall task success compared with representative VLA baselines with an average gain of 5.4\%. Ablation results further indicate that feasibility checking and reflection-based correction both contribute to improved execution robustness. These results highlight the importance of embedding physical consistency checks and online self-reflection for reliable long-horizon robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [138] [Learning to Fold: prizewinning solution at LeHome Challenge 2026 (1st place online, 2nd offline)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27163)
*Ilia Larchenko*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A RL-enhanced VLA policy for bimanual garment folding achieving top results in LeHome Challenge 2026, featuring a self-valuing network, combined AWR/RECAP, distributed training, Thompson-sampling hyperparameter search, and a robust sim-to-real pipeline with HIL data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle robust bimanual garment manipulation by unifying perception, decision, and action under a single network that predicts both actions and relevant success metrics, enabling effective learning and sim-to-real transfer.

Method: A vision-language-action policy that is its own value function (predicts actions and success/progress/future quantities); uses AWR + RECAP for flow-matching VLA; asynchronous distributed training via HuggingFace Hub; inference-time hyperparameter optimization by Thompson sampling; sim-to-real recipe with camera alignment tooling, heavy augmentation, and DAgger-like HIL data collection.

Result: Online round: 1st of 62 teams; Real-world final: 2nd place.

Conclusion: The work blends established RL ideas into a practical, reusable recipe for VLA policies with strong sim-to-real performance, highlighting the value of self-predictive value functions, modular training pipelines, and targeted data collection strategies.

Abstract: I describe my solution to the LeHome Challenge 2026, an ICRA 2026 competition on bimanual garment folding. The system placed 1st of 62 teams in the online (simulation) round and 2nd in the real-world final. It improves a vision-language-action (VLA) policy with a reinforcement-learning loop. The policy is its own value function: the same network that predicts actions also predicts success, progress, and a few task-relevant future quantities, and those predictions drive advantage estimation, live failure detection, and candidate selection. The work mostly recombines existing RL ideas with engineering and optimization contributions that can be used together as one recipe or individually: AWR + RECAP combined for flow-matching VLA; an asynchronous distributed training / rollout pipeline through HuggingFace Hub; inference-time hyperparameters optimization via Thompson sampling; a sim-to-real recipe with camera-alignment tooling, heavy augmentation and DAgger-like HIL data collection.

</details>


### [139] [HumanoidUMI: Bridging Robot-Free Demonstrations and Humanoid Whole-Body Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27239)
*Hongwu Wang,Chenhao Yu,Youhao Hu,Jiachen Zhang,Yuanyuan Li,Shaqi Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A portable, robot-free data-collection framework (HumanoidUMI) using VR and UMI-inspired grippers for humanoid whole-body demonstrations, enabling a high-level policy to be learned and retargeted to robot controllers, validated in five real-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data-collection bottlenecks in humanoid whole-body skill learning due to reliance on teleoperation, hardware access, and operator expertise; enable scalable, robot-free demonstrations.

Method: Integrate lightweight VR devices with UMI-inspired grippers to capture sparse human keypoint trajectories, wrist-view observations, and gripper actions. Train a high-level policy to predict future keypoints, retarget them to robot-native whole-body references, and execute via a whole-body controller.

Result: Experiments across five real-world scenarios demonstrate the framework's effectiveness and validate the collected demonstrations for transferable humanoid whole-body skill learning.

Conclusion: HumanoidUMI offers a portable, robot-free data-collection solution that reduces barriers to data collection for humanoid whole-body skills and supports transfer to robot platforms.

Abstract: High-quality demonstration data are essential for humanoid robot skill learning, especially for whole-body behaviors that require coordinated perception, locomotion, and manipulation. Existing data-collection methods largely rely on robot teleoperation, which is constrained by hardware accessibility, operator expertise, and limited efficiency. Inspired by the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI), we propose HumanoidUMI, a portable and robot-free framework for humanoid whole-body data collection. HumanoidUMI uses lightweight VR devices and UMI-inspired grippers to collect sparse human keypoint trajectories, wrist-view observations, and gripper actions. These demonstrations train a high-level policy to predict future keypoints, which are retargeted to robot-native whole-body references and executed by a whole-body controller. Experiments in five real-world scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and validate the collected demonstrations for transferable humanoid whole-body skill learning.

</details>


### [140] [Advancing Omnimodal Embodied Agents from Isolated Skills to Everyday Physical Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27251)
*Junhao Shi,Zezheng Huai,Siyin Wang,Jia Chen,Yubang Wang,Zhaoye Fei,Hechang Chen,Jingjing Gong,Xipeng Qiu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OmniAct presents a hierarchical asynchronous architecture for persistent embodied agents, integrating a multimodal semantic planner, adaptive memory with event-boundary compression, and an asynchronous visual preemption engine to unify cyber-physical action spaces and handle failures. Real-world tests (40 tasks, 2 robots, 4 IoT devices) show improved end-to-end success, near-flat token growth (under 100k+ tokens), and performance gains for mid-scale models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent autonomy in unstructured environments requires a unified cyber-physical action space, scalable long-horizon memory, and robust failure detection/verification. Current systems treat planning, memory, and verification separately, leading to degraded coherence and resilience.

Method: OmniAct combines three components: (1) a multimodal semantic planner for skill routing across unified action spaces; (2) an adaptive hierarchical memory with event-boundary-driven compression to control context growth; (3) an asynchronous visual preemption engine to close the semantic loop during execution, enabling online verification and recovery.

Result: Empirical evaluation over 40 long-horizon real-world tasks across two robotic platforms coordinating four IoT devices shows consistent improvements in end-to-end success across complexity levels, near-flat token consumption even with >100k accumulated interactions, and a notable performance boost for mid-scale open-weight models approaching proprietary-level performance.

Conclusion: A hierarchical asynchronous architecture—separating planning, memory, and verification—enables persistent autonomy in cyber-physical systems. OmniAct demonstrates practical gains in success, efficiency, and model performance, suggesting this architecture as a viable path for scalable, robust long-horizon autonomous agents.

Abstract: Building persistent embodied agents in unstructured environments demands unified orchestration of heterogeneous tools spanning both cyber (APIs, IoT) and physical (manipulation, navigation) domains, coupled with autonomous recovery from physical failures that inevitably arise over extended operation. Existing systems treat these as separate problems: VLM-based planners lack a unified cyber-physical action space, agent frameworks accumulate unbounded context that degrades temporal coherence, and VLA policies execute open-loop without detecting their own failures. We argue that persistent autonomy requires not a monolithic model but a hierarchical asynchronous architecture with explicit separation of planning, memory, and verification. To this end, we present OmniAct, a framework integrating a multimodal semantic planner for skill routing across unified action spaces, an adaptive hierarchical memory with event-boundary-driven compression for sub-linear context growth, and an asynchronous visual preemption engine that closes the semantic loop during physical execution. Across 40 real-world long-horizon tasks on two robotic platforms coordinating four IoT devices, OmniAct achieves consistent improvements in end-to-end success across all complexity levels, maintains near-flat token consumption over under 100k+ accumulated interaction tokens, and elevates mid-scale open-weight models to proprietary-level performance.

</details>


### [141] [E-TTS: A New Embodied Test-Time Scaling Framework for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27268)
*Wen Ye,Peiyan Li,Tingyu Yuan,Yuan Xu,Xiangnan Wu,Chaoyang Zhao,Jing Liu,Nianfeng Liu,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposal of E-TTS, a modular history-aware test-time scaling framework for embodied robotic manipulation that jointly reasons and scales actions via history buffers and vision-language verifiers, with a closed-loop iterative refinement; shows substantial gains without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied tasks benefit from reasoning to improve policy performance, but how to scale reasoning and leverage history over long-horizon tasks remains underexplored; test-time scaling should harness both reasoning and action scaling, using history to provide context.

Method: E-TTS introduces history-aware iterative refinement with four components (reasoning-action joint sampling and scoring in pairwise manner; history buffer; vision-language verifiers; feedback-driven closed loop). Each module is modular and plug-and-play. Evaluation across multiple benchmarks/environments/embodiments; no extra data collection or retraining.

Result: Consistent improvements across tested settings, up to 33.14% in simulation and 26.62% in real-world; shows better inference efficiency and adaptability; performs with various base models.

Conclusion: E-TTS is a practical, generalizable framework that unifies reasoning and action scaling for embodied tasks; modular design supports flexible configurations; history-aware, feedback-driven sampling yields robust improvements without retraining.

Abstract: Recently, a few works have made early attempts to study test-time scaling for embodied tasks. However, two major challenges remain unsolved: (1) reasoning can effectively improve the performance of the policy, but its scaling mechanism has seldom been studied; (2) historical information is essential, as embodied tasks are inherently long-horizon and sequential, making sole reliance on current observations for action scaling inadequate due to the lack of historical context utilization. To address these challenges, we introduce E-TTS, a modular and plug-and-play Embodied Test-Time Scaling framework that unifies reasoning and action scaling for robotic manipulation via history-aware iterative refinement with vision-language verifiers. To support joint reasoning-action scaling, E-TTS performs reasoning-action joint sampling and scoring in a pairwise manner. To better utilize historical information, E-TTS uses a history buffer to store historical context, which is then used by reasoning and action verifiers to evaluate the sampled candidates. Unlike conventional open-loop TTS methods, E-TTS introduces feedback generation into the sampling process to form a closed-loop iterative refinement mechanism, enhancing both inference efficiency and environmental adaptability. Each component functions as an independent and composable module, allowing flexible and adaptive configuration depending on task requirements. To evaluate the advantages of our framework, we conduct experiments across 4 different benchmarks, 6 environments, 3 embodiments, and 4 base vision-language-action models. The experimental results demonstrate that, without requiring additional expert data collection or retraining, E-TTS consistently improves performance, achieving up to a 33.14% increase in simulation and 26.62% in real-world scenarios.

</details>


### [142] [BOWConnect: Parallel Bayesian Optimization over Windows with Learned Local Cost Maps for Sample-Efficient Kinodynamic Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27292)
*Sourav Raxit,Abdullah Al Redwan Newaz,Jose Fuentes,Leonardo Bobadilla*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bidirectional, parallel kinodynamic motion planner that uses Bayesian Optimization over Windows (BOW) as a learning-based steering function to overcome sample inefficiency, unreliable dynamic constraints, and narrow passages, achieving 100% success and real-time planning in real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling-based kinodynamic planning faces (1) sample inefficiency in high-dimensional state spaces, (2) unreliable cost heuristics under dynamic constraints, and (3) poor performance in narrow passages. A learning-guided, efficient, and robust planner is needed.

Method: Integrate Bayesian Optimization over Windows (BOW) as a learning-based steering function within a parallel, tree-based exploration framework. Each worker learns local cost maps and dynamic constraints to guide sampling toward feasible controls. The planner is bidirectional, growing forward and backward trees in parallel. Spatial hashing enables fast connection queries, and a boundary value problem solver generates kinodynamically consistent bridge trajectories.

Result: Extensive evaluations across ten benchmark environments show 100% success and fastest or near-fastest planning times in complex scenarios, including narrow passages and non-convex spaces. Real-world deployment on a ground vehicle and a quadrotor demonstrates real-time planning with no collisions.

Conclusion: BOWConnect demonstrates that learning-based steering via Bayesian Optimization over Windows within a bidirectional parallel planner provides robust, fast kinodynamic planning suitable for real-time robotic systems and challenging environments; code and demos are publicly available.

Abstract: This paper presents BOWConnect, a bidirectional parallel kinodynamic motion planner that addresses three fundamental limitations of existing sampling-based methods: sample inefficiency in high-dimensional state spaces, unreliable cost heuristics under dynamic constraints, and poor performance in narrow passage environments. Unlike classical planners that rely on random control sampling and geometric distance heuristics, BOWConnect integrates Bayesian Optimization over Windows (BOW) as a learning-based steering function within a parallel tree-based exploration framework, enabling each worker to learn local cost maps and constraints to guide sampling toward dynamically feasible and collision-free controls. A bidirectional architecture simultaneously grows forward and backward trees from the start and goal regions in parallel threads, with a spatial hashing mechanism enabling fast connection queries and a boundary value problem solver generating kinodynamically consistent bridge trajectories. Extensive evaluations across ten benchmark environments demonstrate that BOWConnect achieves 100\% success while delivering the fastest or near-fastest planning time in complex scenarios, including narrow passages and non-convex spaces where state-of-the-art planners fail or degrade substantially. Real-world deployment on a ground vehicle and a quadrotor confirms real-time planning with no collisions. Videos of real-world and simulated experiments, high-resolution versions of the figures, and the open-source code are available at https://bow-connect.github.io/.

</details>


### [143] [LA4VLA: Learning to Act without Seeing via Language-Action Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27295)
*Tao Lin,Yuxin Du,Yiran Mao,Zewei Ye,Yilei Zhong,Bing Cheng,Yiming Wang,Jiting Liu,Yang Tian,Junchi Yan,Feiran Wu,Zenan Meng,Hu Wei,Yuqian Fu,Gen Li,Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LA4VLA introduces language-action pretraining to learn language-conditioned action priors without relying on visual inputs, using a new LA dataset and a lightweight 1B model; mixed LA-VLA pretraining yields the strongest gains over VLA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense visual-action supervision in vision-language-action (VLA) models can dominate language signals, causing policies to rely on visual shortcuts rather than language-conditioned action execution. There is a need for language-conditioned action priors that generalize across tasks and scenes.

Method: Decompose expert demonstrations into atomic action segments and pair each with a low-level action description to form LA4-33K. Use LA4VLA-1B, a 1B-parameter model. Explore three pretraining paradigms: LA-only pretraining, sequential LA-to-VLA pretraining, and mixed LA-VLA pretraining.

Result: LA-pretrained policies outperform matched VLA-pretrained counterparts across simulation and real-world tasks. Combining LA and VLA supervision yields further gains. Mixed LA-VLA pretraining improves the average success rate of LA4VLA-1B over no-pretraining by up to 17.8 percentage points in simulations and 45.0 percentage points in real-world tasks.

Conclusion: LA4VLA demonstrates that language-action priors learned independently of visual observations are effective complements to VLA training, enhancing robustness and performance when integrated with VLA supervision.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are commonly pretrained on robot demonstrations by jointly mapping visual observations and language instructions to actions. However, dense visual-action supervision can dominate the comparatively sparse language-action signal. As a result, policies may rely on visual shortcuts rather than learn how language conditions action execution, making them sensitive to visual variations. To address this limitation, we propose LA4VLA, a language-action pretraining framework that enables policies to acquire language-conditioned action priors without visual observations. These priors capture reusable manipulation skills shared across tasks and scenes, reducing reliance on scene-specific visual cues. Specifically, LA4VLA decomposes expert demonstration trajectories into atomic action segments and pairs each segment with a corresponding low-level action description. This yields LA4-33K, a dataset of 33K Language-Action (LA) episodes derived entirely from existing demonstrations without additional robot data collection. We further develop LA4VLA-1B, a lightweight 1B-parameter VLA model, and investigate three paradigms for incorporating language-action supervision into VLA learning: LA-only pretraining, sequential LA-to-VLA pretraining, and mixed LA-VLA pretraining. Across simulation and real-world tasks, LA-pretrained policies consistently outperform matched VLA-pretrained counterparts, while combining LA and VLA supervision leads to further gains. In particular, mixed LA-VLA pretraining improves the average success rate of LA4VLA-1B over the no-pretraining baseline by up to 17.8 and 45.0 percentage points in simulation and real-world tasks, respectively. These results establish LA4VLA as an effective and complementary pretraining strategy for building stronger and more robust VLA policies.

</details>


### [144] [VibeAct: Vibration to Actions for Contact-Rich Reactive Robot Dexterity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27344)
*Yuemin Mao,Uksang Yoo,Jean Oh,Jonathan Francis,Jeffrey Ichnowski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VibeAct bridges real vibro-acoustic sensing with simulation-based RL by learning a shared contact-slip representation from piezo mic data, enabling end-to-end sim-to-real policy learning for dexterous manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-bandwidth tactile sensing is essential for fast, local contact events on dexterous hands; vibro-acoustic signals from microphones are difficult to simulate, hindering end-to-end RL. A shared representation of contact and slip enables training in simulation that transfers to the real world.

Method: 1) Real world: embed piezoelectric microphones in a dexterous hand and collect vibro-acoustic data via teleoperation; replay recordings in a calibrated digital clone to automatically label per-finger contact and slip. 2) Train a tactile estimator to predict contact and slip from real microphone waveforms. 3) In simulation, compute the same contact-slip representation from simulated contacts. 4) Train manipulation policies in simulation on this representation rather than raw audio. 5) Transfer learned policies to a physical dexterous hand-arm platform.

Result: Across five contact-rich tasks (regrasping, in-hand reorientation, insertion), VibeAct outperforms a proprioception-and-point-cloud baseline in simulation, with the largest gains in tasks requiring sustained reactive control; the continuous slip-magnitude channel is particularly informative. Policies transfer to real hardware and improve success rates on deployed tasks.

Conclusion: VibeAct demonstrates that decoupling tactile sensing from raw audio while sharing a physics-grounded contact/slip representation enables effective sim-to-real RL for dexterous manipulation, with strong performance across multiple tasks and successful transfer to physical hardware.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation depends on contact events that are fast, local, and often visually occluded. Piezoelectric microphones offer a compact and high-bandwidth way to sense these interactions, but the resulting vibro-acoustic signals are difficult to simulate faithfully enough for end-to-end sim-to-real policy learning on dexterous robot hands. We propose VibeAct, a framework that bridges real vibrotactile sensing and simulation-based reinforcement learning through a shared physical representation of contact and slip. In the real world, we embed piezoelectric microphones into a dexterous robot hand and collect vibro-acoustic data through teleoperation, then replay the recordings in a calibrated digital clone to automatically label per-finger contact and slip. A tactile estimator learns to predict contact and slip from real microphone waveforms, while manipulation policies are trained in simulation on the same representation computed directly from simulated contacts. This decoupling lets policies exploit rapid tactile feedback without simulating raw audio. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning regrasping, in-hand reorientation, and insertion, VibeAct consistently outperforms a proprioception-and-point-cloud baseline in simulation, with the largest gains on tasks requiring sustained reactive control, where the continuous slip-magnitude channel proves the most informative observation. The learned policies transfer to a physical dexterous hand-arm platform, improving success rates on deployed tasks. Project videos and additional details are at https://vibeact.github.io/.

</details>


### [145] [Bridging Performance and Generalization in Reinforcement Learning for Agile Flight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27348)
*Jonathan Green,Jiaxu Xing,Nico Messikommer,Angel Romero,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A zero-shot generalization framework for RL-based drone racing using learning-progress-based task switching and a physics-informed procedural track generator, achieving 7.4x generalization gains with competitive speeds and real-world validation, including vision-based end-to-end control without state estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot generalization in high-speed agile flight is hard due to high task variation and the tight coupling between safety and performance; existing generalization methods often sacrifice speed and fail in unseen configurations.

Method: Combine task-aware switching based on learning progress with a physically informed procedural track generator to train a fast generalist policy, avoiding test-time adaptation.

Result: Significant generalization improvement across unseen racetracks (7.4x over state-of-the-art); maintains competitive racing speeds; validated in simulation and real-world settings, including a challenging vision-based end-to-end control scenario where prior methods fail to generalize.

Conclusion: The framework enables robust zero-shot generalization for RL-based drone racing, reducing the need for test-time adaptation and enabling operation in diverse real-world tracks.

Abstract: Autonomous drone racing is a fundamentally challenging regime for autonomous aerial robots, requiring time-optimal control while operating under persistent actuation saturation. While reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved human-level performance in this domain, current methods fail to generalize; policies trained on specific environments often crash immediately in unseen configurations. This failure reflects the intrinsic difficulty of zero-shot generalization in agile flight, arising from high-dimensional task variation and the tight coupling between safety and performance at high speeds. Existing approaches that improve generalization impose a substantial cost on flight speed: control policies must significantly degrade performance to achieve even modest levels of generalization. In this work, we propose a framework for zero-shot generalization in agile flight for RL-based drone racing. By combining task-aware switching based on learning progress with a physically informed procedural track generator, the framework produces a fast and robust generalist policy without test-time adaptation. Our method achieves strong zero-shot performance across a wide range of unseen racetracks in the real world, demonstrating a 7.4x improvement in generalization over the state-of-the-art approaches, while maintaining competitive racing speeds. We validate our method's results in both simulation and real-world settings, including a challenging vision-based, end-to-end control setting that operates without explicit state estimation, where all prior approaches fail to generalize.

</details>


### [146] [Continual Robot Policy Learning via Variational Neural Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27353)
*Jiaxu Xing,Zhiyuan Zhu,Yunfan Ren,Ismail Geles,Yifan Zhai,Rudolf Reiter,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continual learning framework for robots with changing, hidden dynamics that combines a physics-based prior with a neural residual, uses a recurrent encoder to infer latent conditions, and trains policies in differentiable simulation; at deployment, online condition inference replaces sampled latent conditions to adapt quickly to recurring disturbances, achieving faster recovery and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robots encounter non-stationary and recurring dynamics (wind, payload shifts, wear). Traditional learning controllers are trained once and fail to leverage deployment data for further improvement, limiting robustness under hidden disturbances.

Method: Construct a condition-aware dynamics model by fusing an analytical physics prior with a neural residual for unmodeled effects. A recurrent encoder infers the current hidden condition from recent interactions, conditioning both the residual dynamics and the policy. Policy learning occurs in differentiable simulation using a distribution over learned dynamics sampled from the latent model. During deployment, sampled conditions are replaced by online-inferred conditions from recent real interactions, enabling recognition-based adaptation to recurring dynamics without residual re-fitting.

Result: Extensive simulations and real-world tests show improved policy performance under diverse unobserved disturbances. For real quadrotor trajectory tracking under changing wind, the policy recovers from recurring disturbances in about 1s (≈5× faster than online residual re-fitting) and reduces large-disturbance hover and tracking errors by 65.7% and 53.3% over state-of-the-art online adaptation methods.

Conclusion: The framework enables continual learning for non-stationary, recurring dynamics by leveraging physics priors and latent condition inference, enhancing robustness and speed of adaptation in real-world robotic control.

Abstract: Robots deployed in the real world rarely operate under a single fixed dynamics model: wind changes, payloads vary, batteries drain, contacts shift, and hardware wears. Yet most learning-based controllers are trained once and deployed as if learning were complete. This prevents the robot from using deployment experience to further improve task performance. In this work, we propose a continual learning framework that uses real-world experience to improve robot policies under hidden and recurring dynamics. Our method learns a condition-aware dynamics model from real state-action trajectories by combining an analytical physics prior with a neural residual for unmodeled effects. A recurrent encoder infers the current hidden condition from recent interaction, and this estimate conditions both the residual model and the policy. Policy learning is performed via differentiable simulation using diverse learned dynamics sampled from the latent model. At deployment, these sampled conditions are replaced by conditions inferred online from recent real interaction, allowing the policy to recover recurring dynamics by recognition rather than residual re-fitting. Through extensive simulation studies and real-world experiments, we demonstrate that the framework improves policy performance under diverse unobserved disturbances. On real quadrotor trajectory tracking under changing wind, the policy recovers from recurring disturbances in roughly 1s, about 5x faster than online residual re-fitting. It also reduces large-disturbance hover and tracking errors by 65.7% and 53.3% over the state-of-the-art online adaptation approaches

</details>


### [147] [RouterVLA: Turning Smoke Tests into Supervision for Heterogeneous VLA Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27355)
*Xingyu Ren,Chugang Yi,Ge Ma,Youran Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RouterVLA assesses whether reusing pre-deployment probes with outcome-disjoint cross-fitting can improve policy selection for vision-language-action policies. It reports a substantial held-out accuracy gain but reveals potential measurement inflation if trials are reused; simple scoring rules can match learned scorers under scalar-only profiles. The work emphasizes outcome separation and commissioning-aware routing to achieve credible gains beyond policy scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine if pre-deployment evaluation rollouts can supervise policy selection by reusing probes to score candidate experts without contaminating the evaluation; quantify gains, compare scoring strategies, and assess the impact of routing design on system performance and measurement integrity.

Method: Analyze 34,752 LIBERO-Plus rollout records. Use a transparent probe-success rule and outcome-disjoint cross-fitting: probes build per-expert profiles (frozen experts), and a separate trial scores the chosen expert without access to its profile. Compare scalar-only profile scorers to the baseline rule; examine gain in held-out success, statistical indistinguishability of learned scorers vs rule, and the effect of reusing scored trials on purported gains. Examine model scaling and commissioning-aware routing.

Result: Held-out success rose from 0.4686 to 0.6149 (≈+0.1464 absolute; +14.64 percentage points). Under scalar-only profiles, learned scorers are statistically indistinguishable from the simple rule. Reusing the scored trial inflates measured gain by about 1.87×. Model scaling improves individual policies; commissioning-aware (outcome-aware) routing improves the overall system built from these policies.

Conclusion: Outcome separation is essential for credible ledger routing; scalable models help but do not replace robust routing with outcome-aware evaluation. Commissioning-aware routing yields system-level gains beyond policy scaling, while care must be taken to avoid inflation from replayed trials.

Abstract: We study whether pre-deployment evaluation rollouts can be reused to supervise policy selection. Robot teams routinely smoke test candidate vision-language-action (VLA) policies, then compress those trials into a global winner. RouterVLA evaluates this idea with outcome-disjoint cross-fitting: recorded probes build a profile for each frozen expert, and a separate trial scores the selected expert without entering its profile. Across 34,752 LIBERO-Plus rollout records, a transparent probe-success rule raises held-out success from 0.4686 to 0.6149, a +14.64pp gain. Under the scalar-only profiles studied here, learned scorers are statistically indistinguishable from this rule, showing that commissioning carries the routing value while extra scalar scorer capacity does not create it. Reusing the scored trial inflates the measured gain by $1.87\times$, so credible ledger routing needs outcome separation; model scaling improves individual policies, while commissioning-aware routing improves the system built from them.

</details>


### [148] [World Action Models Enable Continual Imitation Learning with Recurrent Generative Replays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27374)
*Manish Kumar Govind,Dominick Reilly,Smit Patel,Hieu Le,Srijan Das*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: REGEN uses World Action Models to generate pseudo-replay trajectories for continual imitation learning, enabling rehearsal of past tasks without stored demonstrations and reducing forgetting near the level of privileged replay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in continual robot learning when only sequential fine-tuning is used; practical need to avoid storing human demonstrations or real replay data, while maintaining performance.

Method: Introduce REGEN, a recurrent generative replay framework that queries a World Action Model to synthesize pseudo-replay trajectories conditioned on prior task instructions and current observations. The replay data trains the policy during continual adaptation, with recursive generation to cover past tasks.

Result: In simulation and real-world manipulation, REGEN reduces catastrophic forgetting by up to 50% vs sequential fine-tuning and approaches the performance of privileged replay methods that use real replay data.

Conclusion: World Action Models enable continual robot learning without stored demonstrations, though challenges remain in long-horizon visual fidelity and action-observation consistency which limit generated replay.

Abstract: Going beyond predicting robot actions, World Action Models (WAMs) can also generate future visual observations. We build on this generative capability to propose Recurrent Generative Replay (REGEN), a continual imitation learning framework that synthesizes pseudo-replay trajectories, enabling a robot policy to rehearse previously learned tasks without storing their original human demonstrations. During continual adaptation, REGEN recursively queries the WAM to synthesize pseudo-replay trajectories conditioned only on prior task instructions and current-task observations. Experiments in both simulation and real-world manipulation settings show that REGEN reduces catastrophic forgetting by up to $50\%$ relative to sequential fine-tuning, while approaching the performance of privileged experience replay methods that require access to real replay data. Finally, we analyze the factors limiting generated replay, identifying long-horizon visual degradation and action-observation inconsistency as the primary bottlenecks. Our results establish WAMs as a promising foundation for continual robot learning without stored demonstrations.

</details>


### [149] [Scalable Behavior Cloning with Open Data, Training, and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27375)
*Arthur Allshire,Himanshu Gaurav Singh,Ritvik Singh,Adam Rashid,Hongsuk Choi,David McAllister,Justin Yu,Yiyuan Chen,Huang Huang,Pieter Abbeel,Xi Chen,Rocky Duan,Phillip Isola,Jitendra Malik,Fred Shentu,Guanya Shi,Philipp Wu,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Open-source manipulation stack ABC with a large teleoperation dataset and reproducible workflow for behavior cloning, enabling real-world dexterous tasks via Diffusion Transformers and Vision-Language-Action models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a large, diverse, open dataset and end-to-end pipeline to advance reproducible behavior cloning for dexterous manipulation, reducing the cost and risk of real-world evaluation.

Method: Release ABC-130K (3,500 hours, 130K episodes, 195 tasks); publish 400 hours of sim-teleop data; provide accessible hardware, training infrastructure, and simulation pipeline; introduce a co-training recipe linking sim and real evaluation; compare training recipes and architectures (DiT and VLA) with real-world evaluations.

Result: Policies successfully execute dexterous tasks (e.g., box folding, extracting credit cards from wallets); provides a reproducible toolkit and baseline for future work; demonstrates utility of co-training and architectural comparisons in real-world settings.

Conclusion: Open-source release to democratize manipulation research, establishing a foundation for the community to learn the ABCs of Behavior Cloning and to build upon a common, reproducible framework.

Abstract: We introduce ABC, a fully open-source stack for manipulation with behavior cloning. At its core is ABC-130K: the largest open-source teleoperation dataset to date, featuring 3,500 hours of data spanning over 130K episodes across 195 diverse tasks. Furthermore, we open-source our accessible hardware setup, training infrastructure, and simulation pipeline. We also release 400 hours of sim-teleop data and provide a co-training recipe that produces correlated simulation and real-world evaluation, offering a reliable proxy for ablating model-design and training decisions before costly real-world evaluation. We explore various training recipes and compare common architectural choices for Diffusion Transformers (DiT) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, grounding our findings in real-world evaluations. The resulting policies successfully execute dexterous tasks such as box folding and extracting credit cards from wallets. By providing a reproducible toolkit, we aim to place researchers on an equal footing, establishing the necessary foundation to learn the ABCs of Behavior Cloning together as a community.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [150] [Geometry-Aware MCTS for Extremal Problems in Combinatorial Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26399)
*Luoning Zhang,Xu Zhuang,Tianhao Wang,Nathan Kaplan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for extremal combinatorial geometry on n x n grids, enforcing geometric constraints incrementally, reducing constraint-checking complexity, exploiting symmetries, and achieving new best-known results on several problems including Max-N3IL and Smallest Complete Set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion; standard RL/transformers face sparse rewards and quadratic token costs. A geometry-aware search framework that enforces feasibility and leverages structure could substantially improve discovery of large, valid configurations.

Method: Geometry-Aware MCTS with incremental updates to the feasible action space to strictly enforce geometric constraints (notably collinearity constraints reducing checking from O(n^3) to O(n^2)); symmetry-aware techniques include canonical pruning during node expansion and symmetric batch transitions; extensive experiments on several extremal problems in combinatorial geometry.

Result: Achieves new best-known results on five of six considered problems. For Max-N3IL, constructs configurations of size ~1.8n for grid sizes 82–119. For Smallest Complete Set, finds configurations ~0.95n, providing new upper bounds within tested grids.

Conclusion: Geometry-Aware MCTS is a versatile framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry and can address both feasibility and search efficiency challenges in extremal grid problems.

Abstract: We study certain extremal problems in combinatorial geometry that ask about configurations of points in an $n \times n$ grid that satisfy strict, global geometric constraints. Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion for these types of problems, and standard reinforcement learning and transformer-based models struggle with the sparse reward "validity cliff" and quadratic token-consumption limits. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework. Our approach strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For constraints about collections of collinear points, like those that occur in the classic No-Three-in-Line problem (Max-N3IL), this mechanism reduces the constraint checking complexity from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. To improve search efficiency, we exploit geometric symmetries in two ways: canonical pruning during node expansion to reduce the branching factor, and symmetric batch transitions to accelerate the discovery of promising configurations. We perform extensive experiments and establish new best-known computational results on five out of six of the problems that we considered. Notably, for Max-N3IL we find configurations of size roughly $1.8 n$ for grids of size $82 \le n \le 119$. For the Smallest Complete Set problem, we find configurations of size roughly $0.95 n$, providing new upper bounds within the tested grids. This work establishes Geometry-Aware MCTS as a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

</details>


### [151] [Detecting and Controlling Sycophancy with Cascading Linear Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26155)
*Maty Bohacek,Rishub Jain,Nicholas Dufour,Thomas Leung,Chris Bregler,Roma Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An iterative data-generation pipeline using cascading linear-feature samples improves interpretability and steering of model behaviors, demonstrated on deterring sycophancy, yielding linearly separable subspaces and efficient, robust steering with competitive baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce reliance on large numbers of contrastive sample pairs and enable scalable, interpretable feature-disentanglement for behavior control in LLMs, focusing on sycophancy as a test case.

Method: Generate cascading samples that exhibit graded feature strength instead of binary pairs; identify features whose strength scales linearly with the target behavior; demonstrate that their activation subspaces are linearly separable; select activations associated with the desired behavior; evaluate detection, scoring, and steering against baselines.

Result: Discovery of linearly separable subspaces for sycophancy features; improved interpretability and stable, robust steering; comparable or better performance than LLM-as-judge and system prompts with lower compute; practical data/compute efficiency.

Conclusion: Cascading, linearly structured features enable more reliable detection and steering of complex model behaviors, offering interpretable targets and potential computational benefits.

Abstract: Interpreting and controlling model behaviors through activation steering methods requires many pairs of contrastive samples that clearly exhibit desired or undesired behavior. These data pairs determine the degree to which interpretability frameworks can reliably detect model features responsible for a behavior, and therefore the ability to steer models toward or away from such behavior. In this work, we present an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features responsible for a behavior. Specifically, we show how moving beyond simple binary pairs of samples, and instead isolating samples that show degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, allows for better disentanglement of features. We focus on detecting and steering away from sycophancy -- the tendency of language models to prioritize user validation. We demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, and allow for selection of model activations that more clearly correspond to the desired behavior than baseline approaches. We also evaluate their ability to enable detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering, and see that they either match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while providing lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees. Code & Data: https://cascading-feats.github.io/

</details>


### [152] [Life After Benchmark Saturation: A Case Study of CORE-Bench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26158)
*Nitya Nadgir,Sayash Kapoor,Kangheng Liu,Peter Kirgis,Matilda Orona,Stephan Rabanser,Tilman Bayer,Abhishek Shetty,Yue Ling,Derrick Chan-Sew,Rumi Nakagawa,Saiteja Utpala,Zachary S. Siegel,Arvind Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues for multi-dimensional benchmark evaluation beyond accuracy; demonstrates with CORE-Bench Hard, v1.1, and OOD that construct validity, efficiency, reliability, model-vs-scaffold contributions, and human-agent collaboration uplift reveal meaningful insights after accuracy saturation.
