<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accuracy saturation biases evaluation; a richer set of performance dimensions is needed to understand agent behavior, generalization, and collaboration effects.

Method: Critically analyze CORE-Bench Hard to surface construct validity threats; develop CORE-Bench v1.1 and CORE-Bench OOD; quantify efficiency, reliability, model-vs-scaffold contributions; conduct a small randomized experiment to measure uplift from human collaboration on reproducibility tasks.

Result: CORE-Bench v1.1 remains informative across multiple axes despite accuracy saturation; reveals previously unanticipated threats; OOD tasks broaden generalization assessment; collaboration yields ~2x speedup; time-limit constraints suggest potential underestimation; several additional findings.

Conclusion: A multi-dimensional evaluation framework provides deeper insights than accuracy alone; proposes concrete benchmarks and methodologies to assess efficiency, reliability, generalization, and human collaboration uplift.

Abstract: When a benchmark's accuracy saturates, it is often retired and replaced with a more challenging version. We show that this approach privileges accuracy and misses the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance: construct validity issues such as shortcuts, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, the relative importance of the model versus the scaffold, and uplift from human-agent collaboration. We use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as a case study to demonstrate that measuring agents along these dimensions yields meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates. First, we surface threats to construct validity in CORE-Bench Hard that are difficult to anticipate with less capable agents. We introduce an improved benchmark, CORE-Bench v1.1, and an out-of-distribution task suite, CORE-Bench OOD. Second, we find that despite accuracy saturation, CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance. Finally, we conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure uplift from human-agent collaboration on real-world computational reproducibility tasks. We find a statistically significant speedup by about a factor of two -- likely underestimated due to one-fifth of human-only reproductions reaching the time limit before completing -- and describe various other findings. Together, our contributions present a more rigorous alternative to the dominant accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm.

</details>


### [153] [Refusal Lives Downstream of Persona in Chat Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26161)
*Viola Zhong,Qirui Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Study reveals that compliant persona and refusal are represented by interacting linear directions in activation space; persona can gate/refusal, with gating occurring at late-layer expression; cannot treat refusal as an isolated direction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how instruction-tuned chat models encode and interact separate behavioral controls (refusal vs. persona), and how these interact across layers.

Method: Identify linear directions for 'compliant persona' and 'refusal' in activation space for Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Extract and intervene on both directions; manipulate layer-wise representations (late vs early layers) by projecting out directions or reintroducing them; measure changes in refusal rate.

Result: Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal (e.g., Llama: refusal rate 97% → 2%). Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not early layers. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores refusal to baseline; projecting out a random direction has no effect. Refusal is gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Refusal depends on persona and cannot be treated as a single isolated direction.

Conclusion: Refusal should be analyzed jointly with persona; a single-direction model misses their dependence. Layer-specific dynamics imply gating at expression time, suggesting design considerations for alignment and controllability.

Abstract: Linear directions in activation space have been identified for both refusal and persona traits in instruction-tuned chat models, but the two have been studied as separate mechanisms. We show they interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we extract a compliant model-persona direction and a refusal direction and intervene on both. Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal -- in Llama, the refusal rate falls from 97% to 2%. Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not at early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores it to baseline; projecting out a random direction does not. Refusal is therefore gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Treating refusal as a single isolated direction misses its dependence on persona.

</details>


### [154] [AlgoEvolve: LLM-driven Meta-evolution of Algorithmic Trading Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26173)
*Dhruv Sharma,Gautam Shroff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM-driven evolutionary framework (AlgoEvolve) for automated trading strategy synthesis, featuring a meta-outer loop that evolves prompts to guide program synthesis, yielding regime-adaptive rules and outperforming human-designed baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend semantic mutation with LLMs from static benchmarks to the noisy, non-stationary domain of algorithmic trading, enabling continual program synthesis and adaptive strategy generation.

Method: Inner loop: LLM generates executable Python trading strategies evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Outer loop: meta-evolution of synthesis prompts to improve search heuristics, balancing exploration and exploitation and reducing zero-trade failures.

Result: The system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive logic with autonomous trading-rule shifts. The meta-loop discovers improved prompts and heuristics that consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions.

Conclusion: LLM-based semantic evolution is a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex, dynamic environments like financial markets, though practical evaluation and safety considerations remain crucial.

Abstract: Recent work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can act as semantic mutation operators for the evolutionary discovery of programs and proofs. Most current applications focus on static coding benchmarks. We extend this paradigm to algorithmic trading. This domain is uniquely challenging because it is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous. We present AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies. These strategies are expressed as Python code and evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Across multiple experiments, the system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic, including autonomous shifts in trading rules. We further introduce a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis in the inner loop. This outer loop discovers improved search heuristics. These heuristics balance exploration and exploitation while reducing zero-trade failures. They consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions. The results demonstrate that LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex environments.

</details>


### [155] [Agentic Analysis for Agentic Infrastructure: An LLM-Powered Pipeline for Comparative Governance of DAO and Corporate AI Protocols](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26203)
*Yutian Wang,Luyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM-assisted pipeline analyzes governance discourse across two AI-agent interoperability standards, showing form shapes topics but participation inequality remains, with denser discourse alignment in permissionless settings; data/code open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirically under-examined governance structures shaping AI interoperability standards; need scalable, automated methods to study socio-technical power in governance discourse at scale.

Method: LLM-powered comparative pipeline combining automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis; applied to 4,323 governance participation records for ERC-8004 (on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led).

Result: Institutional design influences thematic priorities; participation inequality and community fragmentation are similar across regimes; discourse alignment is denser in permissionless settings, suggesting open governance may foster thematic convergence despite decentralized participation.

Conclusion: LLM-assisted methods enable scalable empirical study of technology governance; findings have implications for designing more equitable AI standards; all data and code are openly available.

Abstract: As AI agent protocols proliferate, the governance structures shaping their interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined. We introduce an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for large-scale governance discourse analysis, integrating automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to study socio-technical power structures at scale. We validate it on two contrasting standards for agent interoperability: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). Analyzing 4,323 governance participation records, we combine LLM-assisted coding, topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to examine how institutional design shapes thematic priorities and community structure. We find that while governance form influences substantive focus, both regimes exhibit comparable levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting that open governance may foster greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation. These findings illustrate how LLM-assisted methods can advance the empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards. All data and code are openly available.

</details>


### [156] [Knowledge-augmented Agentic AI for Mental Health Medication Information Seeking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26205)
*Huizi Yu,Jian Liu,Wenkong Wang,Lingyao Li,Jiayan Zhou,Zhaoqian Xue,Xiang Li,Xinxin Lin,Zhiying Liang,Zhuoru Wu,Siyuan Ma,Xin Ma,Lizhou Fan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Provenance-aware knowledge-graph integration of regulatory adverse-event data with patient narratives for antidepressants, enabling auditable safety signals and early detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragmentation of safety information in psychiatry, where regulatory records are authoritative but abstract while patient narratives are detailed but unvalidated; integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote can improve safety signaling and adherence.

Method: Build a Neo4j-based provenance-traceable, multi-agent system. Ingest 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and 20 years of FDA FAERS records for nine antidepressants. Use a large-language-model entity-recognition (NER) pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations (F1 ~0.969 meds, ~0.973 conditions). Compare concordance across sources; detect early warnings (e.g., sertraline signals precede FDA dates by hundreds of days). Map to ATC-N, ICD-10, MedDRA vocabularies; preserve provenance so each claim is traceable and regulatory facts remain distinct from patient experiences.

Result: LLM NER achieved high F1: 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. Community sources (Reddit, WebMD) showed strong concordance with each other (up to Jaccard similarity 0.905) and were more divergent from regulatory reports. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before FDA dates. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA preserved provenance, keeping every claim traceable and separating regulatory facts from patient experiences.

Conclusion: Source-aware integration enables auditable, potentially more timely safety information for psychiatric medications; utility to patients and clinicians is promising but requires prospective testing.

Abstract: Patients increasingly seek medication information online, yet safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between regulatory adverse-event records, which are authoritative but abstract, and patient narratives, which are experience-near but unvalidated. Integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote is especially consequential in psychiatry, where poorly contextualised information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence. Here we develop a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and twenty years of U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. A large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations reached highest F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. The two community platforms were far more concordant with each other (overlap up to a Jaccard similarity of 0.905) than with regulatory reports, indicating that patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance, keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience. These results establish source-aware integration as a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information, with usefulness and patient benefit to be tested prospectively.

</details>


### [157] [Accelerating Skill Assessment in Chess: A Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26267)
*Tianyuan Zhou,Zhizheng Fu,Tianming Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DD-Elo introduces drift-diffusion modeling to integrate move-level data into Elo, enabling faster adaptation to skill changes with a bounded deviation from classic Elo and backward compatibility; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Elo’s outcome-only updates incur response lag and fail to capture granular skill expression; move-level data is noisy and high-dimensional, necessitating a principled framework to leverage it.

Method: Model skill expression as a decision process via drift-diffusion dynamics, updating ratings using move-by-move information while ensuring theoretical alignment with Elo by proving a bounded deviation; validate through experiments comparing adaptation speed to Elo.

Result: DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than traditional Elo while remaining explainable and backward-compatible; empirical results support faster responsiveness; code released on GitHub.

Conclusion: DD-Elo provides a transparent, responsive rating mechanism that extends Elo with move-level data without sacrificing compatibility; suitable for chess rating ecosystems and potentially other domains.

Abstract: Rating systems such as Elo serve as the gold standard for matchmaking in competitive chess. However, they inherently suffer from response lag due to their exclusive reliance on match outcomes, neglecting the granular quality of gameplay. Nevertheless, incorporating move-by-move information into rating adjustments presents a significant challenge given the substantial noise and the vastness of the game-state space. To address this, we propose the Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo), a novel skill assessment framework inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. By modeling skill expression as a decision-making process, our model integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. We provide a rigorous mathematical derivation proving that DD-Elo maintains a bounded deviation from the traditional Elo system, ensuring theoretical alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than Elo. Our findings suggest that DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aquila-zhou1/DD-Elo .

</details>


### [158] [Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26298)
*Jakob Salfeld-Nebgen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A computational governance model for AI agents restricts execution of high-risk actions to preconditions that are independently attested and cryptographically bound to intent, with a tamper-evident log and deterministic evaluation, preserving planning autonomy while ensuring accountable execution; demonstrated via software deployment and clinical prescribing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address governance of powerful autonomous actors by emphasizing independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action, mirroring human institutions that regulate action through attestations rather than directly monitoring reasoning.

Method: Formalize a computational governance model where agents retain planning/reasoning autonomy but lack execution authority for high-risk actions. Execution requires independently attested preconditions from separate authoritative sources, cryptographically bound to declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. All decisions are logged in a tamper-evident log suitable for independent verification. A proof-of-concept implementation is provided, with demonstrations in software deployment and clinical prescribing.

Result: A proof-of-concept implementation is presented, illustrating how high-risk actions are gated by independently attested preconditions and verifiable logs, with concrete demonstrations in software deployment and clinical prescribing.

Conclusion: The model offers a viable path to safely govern autonomous AI agents by decoupling planning from execution through attestations and verifiable records, enabling accountability without constraining autonomous reasoning; further work could explore scalability, trust architectures, and policy design.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.

</details>


### [159] [COrigami: An AI Pipeline for Co-Designing Flat-Foldable Visually Recognisable Origami](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26299)
*Tom Zahavy,Shaobo Hou,Thomas Tumiel,James Doran,Francesco Faccio,Xidong Feng,Alex Havrilla,Igor Khytryi,Chenglei Li,Lisa Schut,Vivek Veeriah,Arijan Abrashi,Michał Kosmulski,Robert J. Lang,Nick Robinson,Brandon Wong,Marcus Chiam,Gloria Fang,Satinder Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An AI-driven pipeline (COrigami) for generating crease patterns from natural language to assist computational origami, combining semantic intent, base packing, flat-foldability, shaping, and RL-based aesthetic evaluation to support co-creativity with mathematical constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of satisfying both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics in physical art, specifically computational origami within a mathematically rigid flat-foldability framework.

Method: End-to-end pipeline: generate a semantic stick figure; compute a base packing; solve for a flat-foldable crease pattern; shape the flat-folded crease pattern; refine with reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop.

Result: Produces structural starting points that artists can further develop; demonstrates integration of algorithmic optimization with autonomous aesthetic critique to satisfy multi-objective physical constraints in co-creative design.

Conclusion: AI systems can enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity in constrained physical design domains like computational origami.

Abstract: While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in solving problems with verifiable solutions, generating physical art that satisfies both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics remains a challenge. This paper presents an approach to tackle these difficulties in the domain of computational origami, a mathematically rigid environment that grounds artistic design within the equations of flat foldability. We present COrigami, an end-to-end AI-driven pipeline that assists the design cycle by generating crease patterns from natural language. Our pipeline involves generating a semantic stick figure, computing a base packing, solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and refining the generated model using reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. Our system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape. By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

</details>


### [160] [The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26300)
*Binghai Wang,Chenlong Zhang,Dayiheng Liu,Jiajun Zhang,Jiawei Chen,Mouxiang Chen,Rongyao Fang,Siyuan Zhang,Xuwu Wang,Yuheng Jing,Zeyao Ma,Zeyu Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Verification signals across AI agents must balance scalability, faithfulness to intent, and robustness; no fixed reward remains effective as policy capability grows; targeted verification designs can suppress reward hacking and improve outcomes, and verification must co-evolve with the generator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As model reasoning and capabilities grow, generating complex solutions becomes easier relative to reliably verifying them. Verification is constrained by underspecified human intent and training-time optimization that causes reward hacking and signal saturation. Understanding and improving verification signals across scalability, faithfulness, and robustness is essential to align agents with intent.

Method: Propose a framework to evaluate verification signals along three dimensions (scalability, faithfulness, robustness). Analyze four reward constructions—test verifier for general coding tasks, rubric verifier for frontend tasks, the user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, and an automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Conduct experiments across task types and increasing policy capabilities to diagnose reward design challenges and effectiveness.

Result: Empirical and conceptual analysis shows that targeted verification design can suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and yield significant gains across multiple benchmarks. Across growing policy capabilities, no fixed reward function remains effective; verification must co-evolve with the generator.

Conclusion: The central claim is that verification cannot be static. Effective alignment requires co-evolution of verification signals with generator capabilities, guided by scalability, faithfulness, and robustness, and supported by diversified verifier architectures tailored to task type and capability level.

Abstract: A classical intuition holds that verifying a solution is easier than producing one. For today's coding agents, this intuition is being inverted: as foundation models develop stronger reasoning capabilities and engineering harnesses grow more sophisticated, generating complex candidate solutions is no longer difficult -- reliably verifying them has become the harder problem. Every verifier we can build is only a proxy for human intent, never the intent itself. This makes verification subject to a twofold difficulty: first, intent is underspecified by nature, making it inherently hard to faithfully check whether it has been fulfilled; second, during model training, optimization widens the gap between proxy and intent -- manifesting as reward hacking or signal saturation. To address this, we characterize the quality of verification signals along three dimensions -- scalability, faithfulness, and robustness -- and argue that achieving all three simultaneously is the central challenge. We further study four reward constructions: a test verifier for general coding tasks, a rubric verifier for frontend tasks, the user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, and an automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Across different task types and policy capability levels, we conduct in-depth analysis and experiments on the core challenges of reward design and how to more effectively leverage reward signals. Experiments show that targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and achieve significant gains across multiple internal and public benchmarks. These experiences collectively point to a core observation: no fixed reward function can remain effective as policy capability continues to grow; and verification must co-evolve with the generator.

</details>


### [161] [How Do Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Perform on Real-World Energy Analytics Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26346)
*David Akinpelu,Akintonde Abbas,Rereloluwa Alimi,Ayodeji Lana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents for real-world energy market analytics (243 problems) across three categories: Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics; compares open- and closed-source LLMs; emphasizes live data tooling and reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the energy domain gap where benchmarks focus on static recall; require live data retrieval, regulatory knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning in high-stakes settings.

Method: Configurable domain tooling including live electricity market APIs, regulatory docket search, tariff databases, asset optimization models, and retrieval-augmented generation over energy market documents; 243 problems across three categories with multiple difficulty levels; category-aware scoring; evaluation across closed- and open-source LLMs; public release of artifacts for reproducibility.

Result: Demonstrates that tool-augmented LLMs can tackle live energy analytics tasks with varying performance across task type and model; validates a multi-dimensional scoring protocol and the value of domain tooling; provides a reproducible research setup.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented LLM agents show promise for high-stakes energy analytics; public artifacts enable further research and benchmarking; suggests broader adoption and refinement of domain-specific tooling and evaluation methodologies.

Abstract: Agentic benchmarks have emerged across general-purpose and domain-specific settings, including finance, coding, law, and drug discovery, yet energy-domain evaluations remain largely limited to static knowledge recall. This is a critical gap for a sector that requires live data retrieval, specialized regulatory and market knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints.
  We present an empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents on real-world energy market analytics tasks. Our evaluation environment includes 243 expert-curated problems across three categories: (1) Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, (2) Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and (3) Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics. Tasks include price and demand analysis, tariff impact modeling, asset revenue and returns estimation, hedging strategy analysis, and optimization modeling, with problems spanning multiple difficulty levels.
  Agents are equipped with a configurable suite of domain tools, including live electricity market APIs for major U.S. ISOs, regulatory docket search, utility tariff databases, asset optimization models, and retrieval-augmented generation over energy market documents. We assess agent responses using a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol that scores approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity, with category-aware routing to match scoring criteria to question type. We evaluate both closed-source and open-source LLMs, providing a comparative analysis of how model capability and domain tooling interact in a high-stakes professional domain. Key artifacts are publicly released to support reproducibility and future research.

</details>


### [162] [What We are Missing in Multimodal LLM Evaluation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26348)
*Po-han Li,Shenghui Chen,Sandeep Chinchali,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current MLLM benchmarks do not adequately test cross-modal integration; key gaps include temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention; addressing these gaps is essential to measure true progress in multimodal intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure evaluation benchmarks reflect the cross-modal reasoning and integration capabilities of multimodal LLMs, not just isolated tasks.

Method: Literature survey and taxonomy review of existing benchmarks for MLLMs, followed by gap analysis to identify missing capabilities.

Result: Identification of four major gaps in current benchmarks: (1) temporal-spatial coherence, (2) physical world understanding, (3) multimodal consistency, (4) selective attention; argue that addressing these is necessary to measure real progress and boundary capabilities.

Conclusion: Develop comprehensive benchmarks that target cross-modal integration and dynamic reasoning to accurately assess multimodal intelligence and reveal capability boundaries.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can process diverse inputs, e.g., text, images, audio, and video, and generate textual responses. While their capabilities have advanced rapidly, evaluation of such models has not kept pace. Most existing evaluation benchmarks are limited to isolated tasks and reveal little about whether a model integrates information across modalities. We examine current means for evaluating MLLMs and review the existing benchmark taxonomy to identify gaps, including temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. Addressing these gaps is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence and exposing capability boundaries.

</details>


### [163] [OpenFinGym: A Verifiable Multi-Task Gym Environment for Evaluating Quant Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26350)
*Kaicheng Zhang,Wen Ge,Lei Jiang,Weixin Yang,Jordan Langham-Lopez,Jialin Yu,Lukasz Szpruch,Hao Ni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenFinGym offers a unified, end-to-end quantitative-finance agent development environment that consolidates forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection under one interface, with automated task construction, containerized execution, a low-latency paper trading engine, and support for long-horizon and SFT/RL post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of large language model agents in quantitative finance is fragmented across isolated tasks and lacks alignment with real-market relevance; a unified, multi-task framework is needed to assess generalization, robustness, and financially meaningful decision-making across the full workflow.

Method: Develop OpenFinGym with (i) a single interface covering forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection; (ii) automated task-construction pipeline that converts quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; (iii) containerized runtime with a host-side verifier to prevent runtime train–test leakage and enable scalable agent rollouts; (iv) a low-latency paper trading engine; (v) deferred-resolution support for long-horizon/event-market forecasts; (vi) integration for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) post-training.

Result: A unified platform enabling multi-task evaluation and development of finance-oriented agents; scalable, leakage-free execution; rapid, low-latency paper trading; support for long-horizon forecasts and post-training workflows; automated generation of executable tasks from literature.

Conclusion: OpenFinGym addresses fragmented evaluation in quantitative finance by providing an end-to-end, reproducible framework that aligns research tasks with real-market considerations, enabling robust assessment of generalization and financially meaningful decision-making.

Abstract: Although large language model agents are increasingly applied to quantitative-finance workflows, their evaluation remains fragmented across isolated tasks, while the financial relevance of benchmark tasks is often overlooked. Yet financial workflows are inherently multi-stage, spanning interdependent tasks such as forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, and trading. Existing platforms typically focus on a single task, and can therefore overstate agent competence and fail to reveal weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making. We introduce OpenFinGym, a unified gym environment for quantitative-finance agent development that covers forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection under a single execution and verification interface. OpenFinGym additionally provides an automated task-construction pipeline that turns quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; a containerised runtime with a host-side verifier service that supports scalable agent rollouts and prevents runtime train-test leakage; a paper trading engine with a low-latency data-stream design; deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-market forecasts; and integration for SFT and RL post-training

</details>


### [164] [Instruction Bleed: Cross-Module Interference in Prompt-Composed Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26356)
*Ching-Yu Lin,Yifan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces compositional behavioral leakage (CBL) in prompt-composed agentic systems—the unintended cross-module interference due to non-isolated transformer attention—demonstrated via a three-channel perturbation protocol on a Claude Sonnet 4.6 job-evaluation agent, with content-channel effects indicating measurable leakage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and quantify inter-module interference in modular, prompt-driven agents and ensure reliability of composed prompt systems.

Method: Operationally define CBL, deploy a reusable three-channel perturbation protocol (volume, content, form), conduct 144 trials on a deployed agent, and assess paired effects across channels using effect size (Cohen's d) and bootstrap CIs.

Result: Content-channel perturbations produced a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63; bootstrap 95% CI excludes zero); volume and form channels showed no detectable effect; CBL is orthogonal to known failure axes (adversarial, cognitive degradation, multi-agent faults, privacy leakage).

Conclusion: Cross-module interference measurement should be an evaluative requirement for prompt-composed agents; the work provides an operational definition, a reusable protocol, falsifiable predictions, and system-class characterization for CBL.

Abstract: Practitioners of prompt-composed agentic systems report a recurring failure mode: editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variable or executable dependency. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL): interference between modules sharing a context window. CBL is enabled by architectural non-isolation: transformer self-attention provides no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) through a reusable three-channel protocol that perturbs non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero); no recommendation flipped -- a sub-threshold regime invisible to standard QA but compounding across the thousands of decisions a deployed agent makes. CBL is orthogonal to known agent-failure axes (adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, privacy leakage). We contribute an operational definition, a reusable protocol, a falsifiable prediction set, and a system-class characterization, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a requirement for prompt-composed agent evaluation.

</details>


### [165] [Accelerating Returns and the Qualitative Engine for Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26359)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A concise synthesis: accelerating returns may boost quantitative capabilities but does not inherently resolve scientific discovery; the paper argues for a qualitative reasoning capacity as essential, introduces the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) to preserve and organize human scientific wisdom, independent of AGI timing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically assess the Kurzweil accelerating-returns thesis and its implications for scientific discovery, and to propose a framework (QES) that captures the qualitative reasoning underlying discovery.

Method: Offers a simple mathematical interpretation of accelerating returns, discusses ARC-AGI-3 results and Hassabis' perspective, and proposes QES as a remedy; yields a conceptual/analytic treatment rather than empirical validation.

Result: Accelerating returns are argued to apply mainly to executional/infrastructural capability; genuine discovery relies on qualitative reasoning about when current frameworks are structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next; current frontier AI systems lag behind humans; QES is presented as a response to preserve and organize the processes of scientific discovery as human wisdom.

Conclusion: Even with accelerating quantitative capability, the central problem of scientific discovery remains; the value of accelerating returns is orthogonal to AGI timing; the paper advocates preserving and making accessible human scientific wisdom via QES.

Abstract: Ray Kurzweil described a thesis of accelerating returns, which is the most influential narratives in discussions of technological progress. Its central claim is that advances in multiple technological fields, especially compute, artificial intelligence, brain science, and biotechnology, interact in such a way that progress becomes self-amplifying and approximately exponential. This paper gives a simple mathematical interpretation of that claim and then argues that, even if such acceleration is real, it does not by itself resolve the central problem of scientific discovery. The reason is that accelerating returns apply most naturally to executional and infrastructural capability, whereas genuine discovery often depends on a different capacity: qualitative reasoning about when a current framework is structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results sharpen this distinction: humans solve the benchmark at ceiling, whereas frontier AI systems remain below 1%, indicating that the gap between current AI and human flexible reasoning is still very large. At the same time, Demis Hassabis has emphasized that humans must retain their sense of meaning and what they choose to focus their lives on, a reminder that the future of AI is not only a technical forecast but also a question of what forms of human understanding are worth preserving and transmitting. This paper positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) [3] as a response to that missing capacity. In this view, the Kurzweil theory helps explain why quantitative capability may accelerate, while QES addresses the central problem in scientific discovery that acceleration alone does not solve. Its value does not depend on when AGI arrives, but on the fact that the processes of scientific discovery themselves constitute a form of human wisdom worth preserving, organizing, and making accessible.

</details>


### [166] [Narration-of-Thought: Inference-Time Scaffolding for Defeasible Ethical Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26366)
*Patrick Cooper,Alvaro Velasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NoT structures chain-of-thought into five sections (protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, commitment) without training or added parameters; it reduces stakeholder collapse and uncertainty suppression in moral-dilemma reasoning across models, yielding auditable traces and improved multi-round consensus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two failure modes of standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas (stakeholder collapse and uncertainty suppression) and provide an auditable, dependable reasoning substrate for agentic deployment.

Method: Introduce a five-section narration-of-thought (NoT) prompt; evaluate on 100 DailyDilemmas across four generators from three vendors; compare to matched-budget verbose-CoT; perform section ablations; apply textual-gradient-descent initialization; conduct cross-family vs in-family judge comparisons; extend to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol.

Result: NoT reduces stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to <1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1–24%; Cliff's delta improvements of +0.79 to +0.90 (stakeholders) and +0.65 to +0.93 (uncertainty) on three of four generators; ablations attribute effects to sub-instructions; cross-family training judge dominates in-family; five-round debate yields 95% full consensus on calibration set and 100% combined convergence on replication; traces externalise stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty for auditable deployment.

Conclusion: NoT adds no training or extra parameters but imposes a structured, auditable reasoning scaffold that improves reliability and interpretability of model decisions in moral-dilemma contexts and supports deployment through verifiable traces; extended methods (gradient initialization, cross-family judgment) enhance robustness across generators.

Abstract: Standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas exhibits two failure modes: stakeholder collapse (the trace names at most one party with a stake in the outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns or hedges before committing to an action). We introduce narration-of-thought (NoT), a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. NoT adds no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. On 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four generators from three vendors, NoT cuts stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% on every model. A matched-budget verbose-CoT control rules out token spend as the active ingredient; NoT retains Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators, and a section ablation attributes each shift to its specific sub-instruction. Textual-gradient descent initialised at NoT improves the scaffold further; a cross-family training judge (different vendor from the generator) dominates an in-family one on every measured axis. Extended to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol, the scaffold converts a 6% standoff into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% combined convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication. The resulting traces externalise the stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty grounding each commitment, providing an auditable substrate for dependable agentic deployment.

</details>


### [167] [When Agents Meet Electric Bus Fleet Operations: Pricing Behavior, Trade-offs, and Policy Implications in an Aggregator Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26400)
*Jônatas Augusto Manzolli,Ali Eslami,Luis Miranda-Moreno,Jiangbo Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic aggregator couples optimization with supervisory agents for EV fleet scheduling; improves feasibility and adaptive coordination under disturbances, but may extract PTO value under profit-driven pricing; governance and transparent tariffs needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the complexity of coordinating reliability, SOC, charging, price volatility, and V2G opportunities in electric bus fleets by integrating optimization with disturbance-detecting and tariff-adapting agents.

Method: Optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G; supervisory agents interpret conditions, trigger re-optimization, and allocate flexibility between the aggregator and PTO; evaluated on a realistic depot case study under day-ahead and real-time operations with profit- and operation-based coordination modes.

Result: Agentic aggregation maintains feasible schedules, enables selective re-optimization, and improves charging/V2G utilization; however, a trade-off exists where profit-oriented pricing can enable value extraction from the PTO, highlighting the need for transparent coordination modes and auditable tariff-setting.

Conclusion: Agentic aggregators are useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but deployment in public fleets requires governance: transparent coordination, auditable tariffs, and explicit value-sharing rules.

Abstract: Agentic systems are changing how complex operational tasks are coordinated, introducing a new paradigm for connecting heterogeneous data sources and automating processes. Electric bus fleets provide a relevant test case. Their operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) opportunities. This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that streamlines this decision environment by coupling an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, while the agentic layer interprets changing operating conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization when needed, and defines how flexibility value is allocated between the aggregator and the public transport operator (PTO). A realistic depot case study evaluates day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes, considering service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and combined disturbances. The results show that agentic aggregation can support adaptive fleet-grid coordination by maintaining feasible schedules, activating re-optimization selectively, and improving the use of charging and V2G flexibility. However, they also reveal a critical trade-off: the same agentic capability that reduces operational complexity can extract value from the PTO when configured around profit-oriented pricing. These findings suggest that agentic aggregators can become useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but their deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules.

</details>


### [168] [Unbiased Canonical Set-Valued Oracles Via Lattice Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26418)
*Jobst Heitzig*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lattice-theoretic account of self-consistent credal sets for self-referential probability reporting; canonical nontrivial solution via fixed points of an isotone operator on closed credal sets, extending from binary events to arbitrary random variables; results include existence, self-consistency, nonemptiness; reduces to a classical point for non-performative questions; for binary events, yields an interval under hull-factoring; open questions on generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address self-reference in oracle-like probability reporting, where information learned from an answer can alter the very probability queried; aim to avoid trivial counterfactuals and compute a canonical, self-consistent, nontrivial probability set that remains meaningful after learning.

Method: Model the space of closed credal sets as a complete lattice and define an isotone operator that maps a credal set to a self-consistent one; apply the Knaster–Tarski fixed-point theorem to obtain a least fixed point (canonical) and a variant containing all self-consistent estimates; extend the framework from binary events to general random variables by replacing conditional probabilities with conditional laws.

Result: Proves existence of fixed points, self-consistency, and nonemptiness of the constructed credal sets; demonstrates that the construction collapses to a classical point answer for non-performative questions; for a binary event, the canonical credal set is an interval under a hull-factoring assumption; extends the theory from B to X with L(X|A,C) replacing P(B|A,C).

Conclusion: A purely lattice-theoretic framework yields a principled method for self-consistent, nontrivial probability reporting in self-learning contexts; opens questions about the interval characterization in the generalized setting and about extensions to arbitrary random variables.

Abstract: A non-agentic "oracle" AI that estimates probabilities of future events faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it can change the very probability it was asked to report. One response, advocated for the Scientist AI programme, is to ask only counterfactual questions, evaluated as if the answer had no influence. We observe that such answers tend to become irrelevant the moment they are learned, precisely because their premise is then false. We therefore explore a self-referential alternative in which the oracle reports not a single probability but a credal set that is simultaneously unbiased and self-consistent with the consequences of being learned. The naive self-consistency requirement is satisfied by too many sets (including the useless answer $[0,1]$), so the problem is to single out a canonical, nontrivial member. We do so with the Knaster--Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets, taking the least fixed point of a suitably defined isotone operator; a variant instead reports the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point estimate. We prove existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness, show that the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions, and that for a binary event the canonical answer is, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, an interval. The development is purely lattice-theoretic and extends unchanged from a binary event $B$ to an arbitrary random variable $X$, with $P(B\mid A,C)$ replaced by the conditional law $\mathcal{L}(X\mid A,C)$. We close with open questions, including whether the interval characterization itself survives that generalization.

</details>


### [169] [Estimating Uncertainty in Classifier Performance with Applications to Large Language Models and Nested Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26422)
*Kylie Anglin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evaluates confidence interval (CI) methods for text-classification performance metrics under typical social-science conditions; finds standard methods often miscover; endorses several improved methods and a novel bootstrap approach; highlights importance of accounting for nesting (texts within individuals) and effective sample size in CI construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve validity of reported uncertainty for text-classification performance metrics (e.g., recall, precision, F1) in social science contexts where datasets are small, constructs infrequent, and data are hierarchically structured.

Method: Simulation study comparing multiple CI methods (Wald, basic percentile bootstrap, Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, pseudo-count regularized bootstrap) under conditions with small-to-moderate samples, rare constructs, and nested data. Examines coverage accuracy and the impact of nesting via effective N and degrees of freedom; compares hierarchical and cluster bootstrap.

Result: Wald and basic percentile bootstrap yield poor coverage (often well below 95%). Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and the novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap improve coverage, especially for F1. For nested data, adjusting for effective N and degrees of freedom is necessary. Among bootstrap methods, hierarchical bootstrap outperforms cluster bootstrap with a moderate number of texts per individual but is overly conservative when individual text counts are very small.

Conclusion: Guidance provided for appropriate CI construction in social-science text classification, promoting transparent reporting and attention to validation sample size. The work underscores the need to adjust for data structure (nesting) and endorses specific CI methods to improve interval accuracy.

Abstract: Researchers increasingly use text classification--supervised models or large language models--to measure constructs from natural language, providing metrics such as recall and precision as evidence of their validity. Yet, though these metrics are point estimates subject to sampling variation, measures of uncertainty are inconsistently reported alongside them. Further, when they are reported, they are often estimated with methods that are not appropriate when relevant labelled datasets are small or performance is high. To increase and improve confidence interval reporting in the field, this paper evaluates confidence interval methods for performance metrics under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. Across simulations, default methods such as the Wald interval and the basic percentile bootstrap are the least accurate, with coverage sometimes far below the nominal 95% level. Accuracy is improved with the use of Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (which is particularly relevant to the calculation of F1). When texts are nested within individuals, we demonstrate that adjustment for both effective N and the appropriate degrees of freedom is necessary for producing accurate analytic intervals. Among bootstrap intervals, the hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than the cluster bootstrap when individuals produce a moderate number of texts but overly conservative when individuals produce only a few. By providing guidance to the field on appropriate interval estimation, we aim to improve the transparency of machine learning applications, and to encourage greater attention to the validation sample size at the design stage.

</details>


### [170] [Data-driven Machine Learning Cannot Reach Symbolic-level Logical Reasoning -- The Limit of the Scaling Law](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26454)
*Tiansi Dong,Mateja Jamnik,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Supervised learning cannot reach symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning due to two issues: (1) training data fail to distinguish all 24 valid syllogisms; (2) end-to-end premises-to-conclusion mapping creates conflicting targets for pattern recognition vs. logical reasoning. Euler Net fails to achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning; surface-form of input affects reasoning in GPT-5/5-nano; 100% accuracy does not guarantee correct explanations. Therefore, data-driven ML alone cannot attain rigorous symbolic reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the limits of scaling laws for logical reasoning in data-driven ML and whether increasing data/time can reach symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning; identify structural and architectural bottlenecks; compare with state-of-the-art LLMs on syllogistic tasks across surface forms.

Method: Theoretical analysis highlighting two limitations; empirical evaluation of Euler Net on syllogistic clausal tasks; empirical testing of GPT-5 and GPT-5-nano on satisfiability of syllogistic statements across four surface forms (words, double words, simple symbols, long random symbols); measure reasoning accuracy and quality of explanations; examine training targets.

Result: Two main limitations prevent data-driven models from achieving symbolic-level reasoning: inadequate training signal to distinguish all 24 syllogisms and conflicting end-to-end training targets; Euler Net fails to implement rigorous syllogistic reasoning; surface form impacts reasoning performance in GPT-5 variants; GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but provide incorrect explanations; stopping at 100% accuracy leaves gaps in reasoning rigor.

Conclusion: Supervised machine learning systems are unlikely to achieve the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning; purely data-driven scaling is insufficient; explicit symbolic or hybrid approaches are warranted for rigorous logical deduction.

Abstract: Sphere neural networks have achieved symbolic level syllogistic reasoning without training data, raising the question of where the limit of the scaling law for logical reasoning lies, i.e., whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve the same level by increasing training data and training time. We show two methodological limitations that prevent supervised deep learning from reaching the symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data can not distinguish all 24 types of valid syllogistic reasoning; (2) end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusion introduces contradictory training targets between neural components for pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Beside theoretical analysis, we experimentally illustrate that Euler Net cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. We further challenge the most recent ChatGPTs (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) to determine the satisfiability of syllogistic statements in four surface forms (patterns): words, double words, simple symbols, and long random symbols, showing that surface forms affect the reasoning performance and that ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provide incorrect explanations. As empirical training processes are stopped after achieving 100% accuracy, we conclude that supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.

</details>


### [171] [MKG-RAG-Bench: Benchmarking Retrieval in Multimodal Knowledge Graph-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26458)
*Xiaochen Wang,Bao Hoang,Han Liu,Ting Wang,Fenglong Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MKG-RAG-Bench: a cross-domain benchmark for evaluating retrieval in multimodal knowledge-graph RAG, with two aligned graphs (general and medical), QA datasets, and an LLM-driven curation pipeline to assess retriever impact on generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks under-represent retrieval in MKG-RAG due to modality heterogeneity and alignment issues; isolating retrieval is necessary to diagnose and improve end-to-end MKG-RAG performance.

Method: Construct two multimodal KGs (general and medical); develop carefully aligned QA datasets with exact supervision; employ an LLM-based curation pipeline to filter knowledge, generate structure-grounded queries, and cover diverse modality configurations; evaluate multiple retriever families and modality settings.

Result: Shows retrieval remains challenging and crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG; retrieval quality strongly influences generation outcomes; provides a principled framework to diagnose limitations and guide improvements in MKG-RAG systems.

Conclusion: Isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target via MKG-RAG-Bench enables principled assessment and advancement of multimodal knowledge-graph RAG research.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over knowledge graphs has emerged as a promising approach for grounding large language models, yet existing benchmarks largely overlook the challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG (MKG-RAG). In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck: multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and often poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora. To address this gap, we introduce MKG-RAG-Bench, a cross-domain benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate retrieval in MKG-RAG. MKG-RAG-Bench is constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains, and includes carefully aligned question-answering datasets that support controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation. The benchmark is built using an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations. Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, we show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes. By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

</details>


### [172] [auto-psych: Automating the science of mind using agent-driven theory discovery and experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26460)
*Ben Prystawski,Kushin Mukherjee,Daniel Wurgaft,Linas Nasvytis,Michael Y. Li,Noah D. Goodman,Michael C. Frank*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Auto-psych demonstrates automated theory discovery and data collection in computational cognitive science using nested agent-based discovery loops that design and run crowdsourced experiments, successfully recovering ground-truth theories from synthetic data and outperforming literature-based theories in human experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data bottlenecks in AI-driven scientific discovery by leveraging computational cognitive science where theories are code and crowdsourcing enables scalable data collection; show feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery.

Method: An inner loop generates, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; an outer loop designs experiments, deploys them online, and analyzes collected data. The system, auto-psych, executes nested discovery loops with a testbed on judging the randomness of coin flip sequences, collecting human data via crowdsourcing; it evaluates theories against synthetic and real data.

Result: The system rapidly and reliably recovers ground-truth theories from synthetic data and, across three independent human experiments, identifies theories that fit the data better than those derived from the scientific literature; the nested structure is essential for performance.

Conclusion: Automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science is feasible and effective, with potential for broader application in AI-driven scientific exploration.

Abstract: AI-based scientific automation is increasingly possible by using agents to generate hypotheses, design experiments, and analyze data. Data collection is a major bottleneck in this pipeline, however. Psychology, and computational cognitive science in particular, is well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation because theories are often represented as code and crowdsourcing platforms enable programmatic human data collection at scale. Here, we apply automated discovery techniques to the project of generating theories in computational cognitive science, with an agent-based system collecting human data independently through crowdsourced survey experiments. As a testbed, we use a classic case study from cognitive psychology: judging which sequences of coin flips seem subjectively more random. Our system, auto-psych, uses nested agent-based discovery loops to generate explanatory theories of human behavior. The inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; the outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online, and analyzes the data. This system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, but the nested structure is critical to model performance. Further, in three independent sequences of human experiments, the system finds theories that fit the data better than theories generated from the scientific literature. This work thus demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science.

</details>


### [173] [Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26494)
*Tianhan Xu,Lei Bao,Yongxiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces the Clinical Harness, a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding, and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities, demonstrated via knowledge-, data-, and physics-driven osteoporosis skills to support lifecycle care.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fragmentation of medical AI into isolated models and the need for persistent, accountable AI in clinical care.

Method: Proposes a framework of clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness; implements an osteoporosis exemplar to show integration of knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced skills under runtime governance.

Result: Demonstrates that such skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance, enabling ongoing management rather than one-off models.

Conclusion: Shifts from isolated models to skill-based runtimes improve accountability and continuity in clinical AI, with osteoporosis as a valid exemplar.

Abstract: Medical AI remains organized around isolated models, whereas clinical care requires accountable capabilities that persist across time. We propose clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness: a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, we show how knowledge-driven, data-driven and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance.

</details>


### [174] [Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26502)
*Han-yu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LRMs show longer trace lengths when uncertain and wrong, while humans spend less time on incorrect trials; across-item difficulty aligns for both, but within-item allocation diverges: humans spend less on wrong items, LRMs spend more. The split reflects two stopping policies (allocation vs registration) with similar difficulty signals, consistent with resource-rational metareasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle how response time (seconds) and token-length relate to problem difficulty and to compare within-item allocation of effort between humans and thinking LRMs, revealing whether cross-item difficulty alignment masks deeper policy differences.

Method: Analyze a public matched human-LRM corpus (H-ARC) across five thinking LRMs and humans. Measure two levels: (1) registration (difficulty vs response time across items); (2) allocation (within fixed item, time vs tokens spent on wrong vs correct outcomes). Use item fixed effects, replicate across datasets, and compare against a non-thinking baseline. Report effect sizes (Cohen's d) on wrong-vs-right within items.

Result: Humans and all five LRMs reproduce cross-item alignment between difficulty and response metrics (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): LRMs show a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47–3.13 on H-ARC), humans show the opposite sign. The divergence persists with item fixed effects and across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. The patterns align with engagement (humans) vs abandonment (LRMs) concepts, or uncertainty-driven trace length (LRMs). Despite similar cross-item correlations with difficulty, the divergence becomes apparent when item identity is fixed.

Conclusion: Cross-item difficulty signals alone do not reveal underlying control policies. Humans and LRMs appear to use distinct stopping policies: humans stay on solvable items and abandon others (engagement vs abandonment), while LRMs lengthen traces when uncertain and likely to fail. Under resource-rational metareasoning, this reflects two complementary control strategies that share a common difficulty signal but implement opposite control; evaluation metrics should separate signal (registration) from control (allocation).

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation). On a public matched human-LRM corpus, humans and all five thinking LRMs reproduce the known cross-item alignment (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans show the opposite sign. The comparison stays inside each agent's own scale; we never put seconds and tokens on one axis. The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. We read the human pattern as engagement versus abandonment: people stay on items they expect to solve and give up on the rest. We read the LRM pattern as length driven by uncertainty: chains grow when the model is unsure, which is exactly when it tends to fail. Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, so they look aligned on the measure prior work has used; the divergence shows up only once item identity is fixed. Under resource-rational metareasoning, the split is between two stopping policies that share a difficulty signal but implement opposite control; trace length captures the signal and misses the control.

</details>


### [175] [NeuraDock Visual Cognitive Load Agent Tutorial: A Quality-Gated Open-Source EEG Workflow for Alpha Dynamics and Real-Time Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26518)
*Zhiyuan Xu,Yueqing Dai,Junling Li,Junwen Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A tutorial on NeuraDock Agent delivering a reproducible real-time EEG workflow for Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive load, bridging offline preprocessing to online API with quality gating and LLM risk explanations; validated on a mini-dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge offline EEG toolkits with a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline; provide reproducible steps that take EEG data through preprocessing, QC, Alpha feature extraction, visualization, online API access, and interpretability.

Method: Step-by-step reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent focusing on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. Uses a quality-gated workflow where downstream Alpha/workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating. Includes 18-recording mini-dataset validation (10 within-subject contrasts; 7 show task-related posterior Alpha suppression), generation of figures, within-subject comparisons, online dashboard, real-time API, and an LLM interpretation layer for quality risks.

Result: Demonstrates a feasible end-to-end pipeline from EEG files to real-time cognitive-load prototypes; shows specific validation metrics (7/10 contrasts with posterior Alpha suppression; initial within-subject repeatability; measured API latency) and readiness for deployment.

Conclusion: Provides a transparent, end-to-end path from EEG data to real-time cognitive-load prototypes and closes the offline-to-online gap, targeting researchers, developers, and applied teams.

Abstract: This tutorial paper provides a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent focused on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. The goal is practical: a reader should be able to install the agent, run EEG preprocessing and quality control, generate Alpha dynamics figures, perform within-subject Rest/Task visual cognitive-load comparison, run the public mini-dataset analyses and compare them with the reference validation summary, start an online dashboard, call the real-time API from an external application, and use the LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks. Existing EEG toolkits provide excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline often requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API; this tutorial closes that offline-to-online gap. The tutorial uses a quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. In the included mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. The tutorial is intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams who want a transparent path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes.

</details>


### [176] [Boundary-Aware Context Grounding for A Low-Channel EEG Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26519)
*Zhiyuan Xu,Yueqing Dai,Junling Li,Junwen Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-source EEG grounding architecture (NeuraDock Agent) separates a deterministic local EEG engine from an LLM-aware layer to enable hardware-, workflow-, and data-aware analysis for seven-channel EEG, keeping raw data local and ensuring reproducible results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs cannot automatically know sensor capabilities, implemented algorithms, or justified conclusions; in low-channel EEG, plausible but unsupported interpretations are risky, so hardware-/implementation-aware grounding is needed.

Method: A deterministic local numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts; the LLM receives a compact allowlisted summary plus a versioned context pack describing hardware, workflows, result fields, boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases; raw EEG data and dense per-sample arrays remain local.

Result: Evaluation at three levels: (1) 12 recordings yielded identical structured results over 10 repetitions; a full Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over 3 repetitions; (2) request-capture and failure-injection tests confirmed data boundary preservation under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures; (3) a boundary-awareness benchmark with 36 ordinary/adversarial questions across four context ablations and two LLMs produced 288 outputs; findings support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses, but do not claim clinical validity or an absolute cognitive-load index.

Conclusion: The proposed grounding framework is practical for safely calibrating EEG-agent behavior by separating deterministic local processing from a hardware-aware LLM layer, while not asserting clinical validity.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can make scientific software easier to use. However, a general model does not automatically know which measurements a particular sensor can support, which algorithms are implemented in the current software, or which conclusions are justified by a computed result. These distinctions are especially important for low-channel electroencephalography (EEG), where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce. We present NeuraDock Agent, an open-source architecture that separates a deterministic local EEG engine from a hardware-aware language layer. The numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts. The LLM receives only a compact, allowlisted summary and a versioned context pack. The context describes the seven-channel hardware, reviewed workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense per-sample arrays remain local
  We evaluate the system at three levels. First, 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten numerical repetitions, and a complete Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Second, request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed the tested data boundary and preservation of local artifacts under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures.
