Third, a boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary and adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs, yielding 288 outputs.These results support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses; they do not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

</details>


### [177] [Radical AI Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26523)
*Daniel A. Herrmann,Benjamin A. Levinstein*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A holistic, normative framework for interpreting AI as agents, integrating radical interpretation with mechanistic interpretability; argues that beliefs, desires, and their propositional structure must be inferred jointly, and provides criteria and tests for when interpretability succeeds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety and trust in AI systems: we need reliable methods to infer or detect an AI's goals and potential deception; current interpretability tools lack a settled account of success.

Method: Theoretical framework combining representationalist and interpretationist criteria, linked to current interpretability tests; emphasizes holism; uses mechanistic interpretability to measure both attitudes and the propositional structure that underpins them.

Result: A unified account with criteria and testable guidance for interpretability; highlights how holism constrains attribution and enables leverage via mechanistic interpretability; clarifies limitations of piecemeal approaches.

Conclusion: Beliefs/desires and their propositional content are mutually constrained; interpretability requires joint consideration of attitudes and structure; mechanistic interpretability is central to measuring both, guiding safer AI deployment.

Abstract: We develop a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents, drawing on the philosophical tradition of radical interpretation and the tools of mechanistic interpretability. The core question is: given the computational facts about a system, how do we solve for its beliefs, desires, and meanings? This matters increasingly for safety. We want to be able to trust the systems we deploy, whether by understanding their goals or, more modestly, by reliably detecting deception. Interpretability researchers are building tools to read beliefs and desires off a model's internals, but there is no settled account of when such a tool has succeeded. This book supplies one. We propose criteria on both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, and tie each to tests current interpretability methods can carry out. A central lesson is that these attributions cannot be made piecemeal. Beliefs, desires, and the propositional structure they presuppose are jointly constrained, and a method that fixes one while measuring the others inherits whatever distortions that introduces. This holism becomes pressing for AI systems, which may not share the interpreter's concepts. However, it also provides leverage: a system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure, that structure constrains which attitudes can be attributed, and mechanistic interpretability can help us measure both.

</details>


### [178] [PMDformer: Patch-Mean Decoupling Information Transformer for Long-term Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26549)
*Ao Hu,Liangjian Wen,Jiang Duan,Yong Dai,He Yan,Dongkai Wang,Jun Wang,Yukun Zhang,Ruoxi Jiang,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PMDformer introduces patch-mean decoupling (PMD) to improve shape similarity modeling in long-term time series forecasting, with Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) to restore trend and focus cross-variable attention on recent segments; reported state-of-the-art results on LTSF benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term forecasting with patch-based transformers struggles to capture shape similarities across patches and variables due to scale differences. Decoupling trend from shape via PMD, and enhancing attention with TRA and PVA, aims to improve robustness and accuracy.

Method: Patch-mean decoupling (PMD) subtracts the mean of each patch to separate trended and residual shape information. Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) reintegrates the decoupled trend during attention computation. Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) emphasizes cross-variable attention on the most recent time segments to avoid outdated correlations. These components are integrated into PMDformer for LTSF.

Result: Extensive experiments show PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: PMDformer provides a novel, effective approach to capturing shape similarity in LTSF by decoupling trend and residuals and by refining attention for cross-variable and long-range dependencies; further empirical validation and ablation studies would strengthen the claims.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) plays a crucial role in fields such as energy management, finance, and traffic prediction. Transformer-based models have adopted patch-based strategies to capture long-range dependencies, but accurately modeling shape similarities across patches and variables remains challenging due to scale differences. To address this, we introduce patch-mean decoupling (PMD), which separates the trend and residual shape information by subtracting the mean of each patch, preserving the original structure and ensuring that the attention mechanism captures true shape similarities. Futhermore, to more effectively model long-range dependencies and capture cross-variable relationships, we propose Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA). The former module reintegrates the decoupled trend from PMD while calculating attention output. And the latter focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to avoid overfitting on outdated correlations. Combining these components, we propose PMDformer, a model designed to effectively capture shape similarity in long-term forecasting scenarios. Extensive experiments indicate that PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/aohu1105/PMDformer.

</details>


### [179] [Explainable Ensemble-Based Machine Learning Models for Detecting the Presence of Cirrhosis in Hepatitis C Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26561)
*Abrar Alotaibi,Lujain Alnajrani,Nawal Alsheikh,Alhatoon Alanazy,Salam Alshammasi,Meshael Almusairii,Shoog Alrassan,Aisha Alansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Extra Trees classifier achieved best performance for detecting cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients using 16 of 28 attributes, with ~97% accuracy, high precision, and AUC ~0.96 on an Egyptian dataset (n=2038).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable early detection of cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients, a domain where prior work applying ML for this specific task was lacking.

Method: Dataset of 2038 Egyptian patients with 28 attributes from UCI ML Repository. Evaluated four ML models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, Extra Trees). Used 16 features (out of 28). Metrics: accuracy, recall, precision, AUC.

Result: Extra Trees outperformed others with accuracy 96.92%, recall 94.00%, precision 99.81%, AUC 96% using 16 features; other models underperformed in comparison.

Conclusion: ML can accurately detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients; Extra Trees with a reduced feature set shows strong performance. Findings indicate potential clinical utility but require external validation and assessment of generalizability across populations.

Abstract: Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by a virus, which results in mild to severe inflammation of the liver. Over many years, hepatitis C gradually damages the liver, often leading to permanent scarring, known as cirrhosis. Patients sometimes have moderate or no symptoms of liver illness for decades before developing cirrhosis. Cirrhosis typically worsens to the point of liver failure. Patients with cirrhosis may also experience brain and nerve system damage, as well as gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Treatment for cirrhosis focuses on preventing further progression of the disease. Detecting cirrhosis earlier is therefore crucial for avoiding complications. Machine learning (ML) has been shown to be effective at providing precise and accurate information for use in diagnosing several diseases. Despite this, no studies have so far used ML to detect cirrhosis in patients with hepatitis C. This study obtained a dataset consisting of 28 attributes of 2038 Egyptian patients from the ML Repository of the University of California at Irvine. Four ML algorithms were trained on the dataset to diagnose cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients: a Random Forest, a Gradient Boosting Machine, an Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an Extra Trees model. The Extra Trees model outperformed the other models achieving an accuracy of 96.92%, a recall of 94.00%, a precision of 99.81%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 96% using only 16 of the 28 features.

</details>


### [180] [EvoOptiGraph: Weakness-Driven Coevolution via Graph-Based Structural Generation for Optimization Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26578)
*Qingcan Kang,Mingyang Liu,Xiaojin Fu,Shixiong Kai,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoOptiGraph co-evolves data and model to improve MILP modeling with LLMs by generating diverse, valid graph-encoded MILP instances and training via supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle two key issues in LLM-based optimization modeling: lack of structural diversity in training data and static, decoupled data-generation pipelines. Aim to produce more robust models that handle varied optimization structures.

Method: Represent each MILP as an attributed bipartite graph and apply validity-preserving evolutionary operators to create structurally diverse instances. Convert evolved graphs into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation with back-translation verification. Train in two stages: supervised fine-tuning on an initial dataset, then reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) using weakness signals from the graphs to generate new instances targeting model failures.

Result: On six public datasets, EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization.

Conclusion: Targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy to improve LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

Abstract: Automating optimization modeling from natural language with large language models (LLMs) faces two key challenges. First, training corpora lack structural diversity. Second, data generation pipelines remain static and decoupled from model learning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoOptiGraph, a novel framework where data and model co-evolve, driven by model weaknesses. EvoOptiGraph represents each mixed-integer linear program (MILP) as an attributed bipartite graph and applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. The evolved graphs are converted into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verified back-translation. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where graph-derived weakness signals guide the generation of new instances targeting the model's failures. This forms a closed loop that continuously updates the training distribution. Empirical results on six public datasets show that EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization. These results demonstrate that targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

</details>


### [181] [A Multi-Level Validation and Traceability Framework for AI-Generated Telescope Scheduling Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26585)
*Hengchu Xiao,Chuanjun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework to improve reliability and executability of AI-based telescope scheduling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable AI decisions that hinder high-reliability observational tasks.

Method: A formal framework combining data-reference validation, logical consistency checks, and constraint verification, plus the introduction of atomic reasoning units and dependency graphs to represent decisions as interconnected steps for error localization and post hoc analysis; includes feedback/correction mechanisms.

Result: Experimental evaluation shows improved executability and reliability, reduced loss of transient opportunities, and enhanced ability to repair or block erroneous decisions in complex scenarios; preserves AI flexibility while boosting reliability.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

Abstract: With the gradual introduction of AI into telescope scheduling, AI-based decision-making has shown advantages in handling complex multi-constraint problems. However, its outputs often suffer from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks. In this work, we propose a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that performs systematic reliability verification of AI-generated decisions prior to execution, and enables explicit representation of the reasoning process to support traceable decision-making. The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational and instrumental constraint verification to filter and correct invalid decisions. It also introduces atomic reasoning units and their dependency relationships, representing scheduling decisions as a sequence of interconnected reasoning steps that support error localization and post hoc analysis. Experiments show that the framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling and reduces loss of transient opportunities. In particular, feedback correction and structured validation of reasoning steps enhance the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios. Compared with pure AI methods, the framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability. These results demonstrate a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

</details>


### [182] [Content-Based Smart E-Mail Dispatcher Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26593)
*K. Paramesha,K R Sriram,Sujan Shetty,Shamanth Kishore,R. Tejaswini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automated email-to-WhatsApp dispatcher using LLM-powered agents to route emails to relevant student WhatsApp groups, without labelled data, aiming to improve information flow and reduce cognitive load.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce email overload, minimize manual forwarding errors, and enhance timely information dissemination in organizations.

Method: Agent-based framework where email content is fed into LLM prompts to determine target WhatsApp groups and dispatch messages; no labelled datasets required; structured prompts guide decision-making.

Result: Conceptual framework demonstrating feasibility; the system can analyze email content and identify relevant groups for dispatch to ensure timely information sharing; no empirical evaluation or datasets reported.

Conclusion: LLM-driven automated dispatcher is a feasible approach to streamline internal communication, potentially boosting productivity and reducing cognitive load; further empirical validation recommended.

Abstract: Email communication has become an integral part of personal and professional life, but handling its vast volume is still a significant issue for large organisations. Manual perusal of emails and forwarding their contents and attachments to intended recipients using other instant messaging platforms has proved to be error-prone and time-consuming leading to losses in terms of productivity and creating undue stress. The main objective of this paper is to explore an alternative mechanism that is to automate the task of dispatching emails based on their contents to the respective WhatsApp groups of students of various semesters of programs in an engineering college, facilitating a smooth flow of information from one end to another end in an organisation. The dispatcher system is built using agents querying large language models (LLMs) to enable it to analyze the contents of emails and route them to the relevant groups of students for their information and consumption. The system harnesses the capabilities of LLMs in analysing the textual contents for decision-making. With a well-structured agent framework prompt that includes email content as input with instructions and context, the system figures out the relevant groups to which the email message is dispatched, thus providing the required information on time. The proposed system does not rely on labelled datasets and provides several benefits, including enhanced productivity and a reduction in the cognitive load associated with reading emails.

</details>


### [183] [LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26595)
*Mahsa Tavakoli,Ruth Bankey,Cristián Bravo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to analyze multilingual tax feedback, aiming to detect emerging service quality topics and potential inequities. It uses similarity analysis and human-in-the-loop evaluation, showing better alignment with expert judgments than baselines and promoting scalable, responsible public-sector AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public sector feedback is growing and multilingual; identifying emerging service quality issues and inequities requires scalable, nuanced analysis beyond manual review or fixed indicators.

Method: Integration of fine-tuned, quantized large language models with statistical techniques and a human-in-the-loop. Evaluation includes similarity analysis to expert judgments and assessments by experienced tax officers to validate findings.

Result: The approach achieved stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models and demonstrated practicality for scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public organizations, with reduced LLM fabrication due to human oversight.

Conclusion: Shows promise for responsible AI in public services by enhancing service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through multilingual feedback analysis.

Abstract: Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

</details>


### [184] [Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26649)
*Adam Mondl,Matthew Maisel,John H. Brock*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autoformalization pipeline translates agent safety prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural-language policies into Cedar policies via an LLM-based generator-critic loop, achieving greater coverage than hand-coded symbolic enforcement on MedAgentBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal guarantees for policy enforcement in high-stakes AI are needed; probabilistic guardrails and hand-coded approaches either lack guarantees or fail to scale with policy breadth.

Method: LLM-based generator-critic loop that ingests prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural-language policy docs, producing Cedar Policy Language specifications; evaluation on MedAgentBench comparing coverage to prior symbolic enforcement.

Result: Autoformalized Cedar policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than prior hand-coded symbolic enforcement.

Conclusion: LLM-driven autoformalization can scale formal policy enforcement by translating natural-language policies into verifiable Cedar programs, improving coverage and potential guarantees in high-stakes domains.

Abstract: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

</details>


### [185] [SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26669)
*Zhongxin Guo,Danrui Qi,Hanwen Gu,Peng Cheng,Yongqiang Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillDisCo distills reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs from successful traces in FSM-defined tasks and compiles them into executable procedural skills, improving success rates and reducing agent turns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agents repeatedly solving related tasks incur high reasoning costs and long traces. There is a need to identify when procedural skills exist and how to represent shared structure across traces; existing workflow reuse and skill induction methods do not clearly address these questions in FSM-defined settings.

Method: Treat successful traces as paths in an unknown transition graph and define procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs (PFSMs). Distill PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compile them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Validate on ALFWorld and WebArena across model scales.

Result: SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

Conclusion: Shared experience in FSM-defined tasks can be captured as reusable PFSM subgraphs; distillation-and-compilation yields practical procedural skills that generalize across instances.

Abstract: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

</details>


### [186] [NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26671)
*Qiaobo Hao,Yangqian Wu,Shunyi Wang,Zhongjian Zhang,Ziqun Li,Yayin He,Muqing Li,Chen Zhong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NebulaExp proposes a fully transparent post-training pipeline for 8B-scale LLMs (Qwen3-8B-base) with two branches—general instruct and reasoning-specialized. It assembles a 3.84M multi-source SFT corpus and a 200K RL candidate pool, employing an end-to-end data stack (distillation, filtering, difficulty grading, task classification, diversity sampling). It reports improvements via a three-stage SFT, GRPO RL, and multi-teacher OPD, and provides a reproducible recipe with an analysis of capability trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve post-training alignment and human-preference following while ensuring reproducibility and lightweight optimization, overcoming the opacity of data construction rules and training recipes in prior work.

Method: End-to-end data processing stack: response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification, and diversity-aware sampling. Two model branches receive tailored training: (i) Instruct branch with NebulaExp-Ins-SFT (three-stage supervised fine-tuning) plus GRPO RL; (ii) Reasoning branch with medium-difficulty GRPO RL. The study also compares single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD) for RL using small instruction-following datasets (as few as 4K) and 10K samples for domain-specialist teachers.

Result: Instruct branch: baseline 55.01 (Qwen3-8B-nothink); after NebulaExp-Ins-SFT: 60.99; with GRPO RL: 61.85. Reasoning branch: medium-difficulty GRPO RL raises from 73.88 to 75.17. For RL verifiers, single-teacher OPD with 4K samples improves IFEval by 3.26 points (plus 4.43 average gain overall) over RL baseline; MOPD with four domain-specialist teachers and 10K samples raises average by 4.18 over the base model.

Conclusion: The work delivers a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs and analyzes the trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general knowledge, highlighting the benefits of transparent data pipelines and multi-teacher RL for improved capability and reliability.

Abstract: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

</details>


### [187] [Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26686)
*Dongbin Na*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lightweight, label-only guard (LeanGuard) matches larger reasoning-based guards in moderation performance with ~100x less inference compute, challenging the need for chain-of-thought in safety guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is necessary for safety guardrails, given deployment constraints (on-device, low latency), and to explore more efficient alternatives.

Method: Train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus; remove the reasoning module while keeping other components fixed to enable a controlled comparison; evaluate on public moderation benchmarks; measure average F1 and compute; assess robustness to training-label noise; compare recall at strict false-positive rates; release code and models.

Result: LeanGuard (a 395M label-only encoder) achieves an average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26, matching a larger decoder-based reasoning guard, with only a single forward pass over inputs up to 512 tokens (~100x faster). It remains robust under training-label noise and retains higher recall at strict FPRs than the reasoning guard.

Conclusion: CoT-based reasoning is not demonstrated to improve moderation in this setup; current guardrail benchmarks may not reward reasoning, suggesting LeanGuard as a viable, efficient alternative for on-device safety filtering; code and models are released.

Abstract: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

</details>


### [188] [Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26710)
*Mohammed Ayalew Belay,Lucas Ferreira Bernardino,Adil Rasheed,Rubén M. Montañés,Pierluigi Salvo Rossi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Kalman Prototypical Network for few-shot fault diagnosis in CCGTs using dynamic latent prototypes; improves accuracy and stability over classic FSL methods; uses synthetic Modelica data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and complex thermo-fluid interactions in CCGT fault detection; reduce episodic variance and produce robust embeddings under transient faults.

Method: Integrates Kalman filtering with prototypical networks; models class prototypes as latent stochastic states; trains on synthetic offshore CCGT data; compares with Matching Networks, Relation Networks, MAML.

Result: Outperforms baseline FSL methods in accuracy and stability across varying support/query configurations; faster training convergence and better generalization.

Conclusion: Promising approach for real-world CCGT fault detection with limited labeled data; dynamic prototype evolution enhances robustness; further validation on real data recommended.

Abstract: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

</details>


### [189] [LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26713)
*Yuqi Jiang,Yumeng Liu,Zimu Li,Jinyuan Deng,Qian Jin,Yucheng Cui,Yu Li,Xunzhao Yin,Qi Sun,Cheng Zhuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LithoDreamer is a physics-informed World Model for computational lithography that treats the Layout–Mask–Resist–After Development Image (ADI) pipeline as a multi-step evolution; it uses stage-specific latent spaces and a contrastive variational optimization to enable interpretable intervention planning, achieving state-of-the-art forward evolution and inverse planning; the lithography dataset is publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lithography models fail to capture the continuous, multi-stage physics of masking, imaging, resist exposure, and development, hindering accurate prediction and controllable optimization across the entire process.

Method: Propose LithoDreamer, a physics-informed World Model that encodes stage-wise latent representations of adjacent-state transitions within the Layout–Mask–Resist–ADI pipeline; introduces a contrastive variational optimization that compares latent differences across intervention paths while enforcing variational evolution constraints to align with lithography physics, enabling interpretable, supervision-free intervention optimization.

Result: LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning on lithography tasks; dataset is publicly available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, interpretable approach to modeling and controlling the lithography process by integrating physics-guided dynamics into a world-model framework, potentially improving yield and performance through optimized interventions.

Abstract: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

</details>


### [190] [A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26718)
*David Brüggemann,Ekaterina Krymova,Firat Özdemir,Jochen von Spiczak,Sebastian Kozerke,Samia Mora,Robert Manka,Mathieu Salzmann,Olga V. Demler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A heart-rate–aware latent ODE plus graph autoencoder learns continuous 3D+t ventricular motion and improves HF risk prediction when added to standard risk models (C-index 0.785 in UK Biobank).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional risk models rely on few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases; full-cycle, bi-ventricular motion encoding may reveal richer, prognostically relevant phenotypes.

Method: Develop a latent dynamical model with heart-rate-aware neural ODE dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. Use a covariate-conditioned prior for end-diastolic latent state and a Cox model to test associations with incident HF. Evaluate on 72,386 participants (367 events) with held-out subset; compare against seven established markers and non-graph/ODE baselines.

Result: Latent score improved stratified C-index from 0.704 (PCE baseline) to 0.785; seven established markers yielded 0.764. The graph- and ODE-based approach offered the best trade-off among reconstruction fidelity, realism, and prognostic performance.

Conclusion: Continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion yields informative CMR-derived phenotypes beyond traditional summaries; external validation in more diverse cohorts is needed before clinical deployment.

Abstract: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

</details>


### [191] [Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26722)
*Xianrui Zeng,Pengfei Liu,Yirui Zang,Yang Shen,Fei Yu,Chunlei Yu,Minghao Liu,Yang Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that uses Socratic midwifery to drive autonomous, evidence-grounded experiments; demonstrates self-correcting discovery in a real optical system with strong classification results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance from workflow automation to epistemic autonomy in AI-driven scientific discovery, enabling systems to construct, challenge, and revise physical explanations in response to evidence.

Method: A physics-critic agent performs causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and falsification-criteria formulation within closed-loop experimentation on a real multimode-fiber platform. The system operates without pre-encoded schemes, discovers encoding strategies, diagnoses failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination, detector noise), and translates a published protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration."

Result: The system proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis; discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies; achieved 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9; MNIST accuracy 76.97% and Fashion-MNIST accuracy 83.17%; ablations show Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and experimental-plan validity.

Conclusion: A significant step toward autonomous, self-correcting scientific discovery in complex physical environments, bridging workflow automation to evidence-grounded autonomous inquiry.

Abstract: The automation of scientific discovery has reached an inflection point. While AI systems now operate instruments, optimize parameters and generate hypotheses, most remain procedural: they execute workflows fixed by human designers. True autonomous science demands epistemic autonomy--the capacity to construct, challenge and revise physical explanations in response to evidence. Here we introduce AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A physics-critic agent interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation and falsification-criteria formulation. We evaluate AHOIS on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, a high-dimensional system with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift and multi-modal acquisition. Without prior encoding schemes, classifiers or speckle models, the system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination and detector noise) and translated a published imaging protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration. The discovered encoding yielded 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations show that Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration and experimental-plan validity. These results establish a route from workflow automation towards evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments.

</details>


### [192] [Scientific discovery as meta-optimization: a combinatorial optimization case study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26728)
*Yuan-Hang Zhang,Chesson Sipling,Massimiliano Di Ventra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A meta-optimization framework for scientific discovery that jointly optimizes evaluation criteria. Introduces consensus objective aggregation (correlation-weighted voting) to create a self-correcting, evolving evaluation metric, demonstrated by improved algorithm discovery for 3-SAT with MemComputing machines (scaling from N^2.51 to N^1.33; ~67x speedup).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery is effectively an optimization over a vast state space of theories and experiments using an evaluation criterion. The key gap is that the evaluation criteria themselves are often fixed. The work argues for meta-optimization, where the objective is also optimized, enabling self-correction and adaptation of what counts as good science as understanding deepens.

Method: Propose meta-optimization with consensus objective aggregation: generate candidate objective functions via LLMs and combine them using correlation-weighted voting to form a stable, evolving global objective.

Result: Applied to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT with digital MemComputing machines, achieving a reduction in scaling from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33 and about a 67-fold speedup on largest instances.

Conclusion: A problem-agnostic meta-optimization framework that can adapt evaluation criteria over time, potentially accelerating scientific discovery across domains using self-correcting, ensemble-driven objective aggregation.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is fundamentally an optimization problem, defined by a vast "state space" of theories and experiments, and an evaluation criterion based on quality, novelty, and validity. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled automated exploration of this space, but we argue that simultaneous modification of the evaluation criteria is equally important. Here, we propose formalizing research as meta-optimization, where the optimization objective itself is also being optimized. Our key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," where LLM-generated objective functions are combined via correlation-weighted voting, yielding a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens. We apply this framework to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, reducing the baseline scaling with problem size $N$ from $\sim N^{2.51}$ to $\sim N^{1.33}$ and delivering a $\sim 67\times$ speedup on the largest instances tested. As a problem-agnostic framework, we hope this approach will considerably aid scientific discovery.

</details>


### [193] [EGG: An Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26758)
*Yaochen Han,Ke Fan,Hongxu Jiang,Wanqi Xu,Weiyu Xie,Runhua Zhang,Chenhui Zhu,Yixiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EGG uses an expert-guided multi-agent framework to steer large-language-model–driven kernel generation, achieving staged optimization (algorithmic structure design followed by hardware tuning) that yields 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-performance GPU kernels are essential for efficient LLM inference but require manual, expert-tuned optimization. LLM-based kernel generation lacks domain-specific guidance, limiting correctness and performance.

Method: A two-stage design: (1) algorithmic structure design to establish a high-quality computation graph; (2) hardware-specific tuning for parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimizations. A stage-aware, multi-agent collaboration mechanism manages intra- and inter-stage context to guide optimization along explicit objectives.

Result: EGG achieves 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real workloads, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

Conclusion: Explicit stage-wise decomposition guided by expert principles yields stable optimization trajectories and improved kernel performance, suggesting a viable path for combining expert insight with LLM-based kernel generation.

Abstract: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing the exponentially growing computational costs of large language models (LLMs), but their development heavily relies on manual tuning by domain experts. While recent advances in LLM-based approaches show promise for automating kernel generation, they still struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance. This limitation primarily arises from the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, hindering effective exploration of the optimization space. We propose EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which incorporates expert optimization principles to guide LLMs' decisions. Inspired by expert workflows, we decompose kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: 1) algorithmic structure design, which establishes a high-quality computational structure foundation; 2) hardware-specific tuning, which performs targeted adjustments through parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. This staged decomposition defines explicit optimization objectives, structuring the design space to achieve progressive refinement. To this end, a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism is designed for inter and intra-stage context management, ensuring stable optimization trajectories. Experiments on KernelBench and real-world workloads show that EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

</details>


### [194] [ResilPhase: Plug-and-Play Phase Mapping and Noise-Resilient Macro-Trajectory Extrapolation for Diffusion Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26769)
*Qicheng Zhao,Yu Li,Qi Sun,Zheyu Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ResilPhase is a derivative-free, noise-resilient acceleration framework for diffusion models, featuring macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space with a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator and a bounded Phase Mapping, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Powerful diffusion models suffer from high inference latency. Cache-then-forecast approaches reduce latency but cause severe quality degradation at high acceleration due to misaligned, unstable discrete extrapolation. There is a need for stable, end-to-end state (Global Drift) aligned extrapolation to suppress error growth and memory overhead.

Method: Reformulate inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space. Align forecasting with Global Drift (end-to-end state evolution) rather than intermediate features. Replace derivative-based extrapolation with a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to avoid derivative instability. Introduce a bounded Phase Mapping to constrain the extrapolation domain and suppress oscillatory error growth.

Result: Empirical results on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo show state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration, with improved stability and reduced memory overhead.

Conclusion: ResilPhase provides a robust, noise-resilient acceleration framework for diffusion models by eliminating feature misalignment and derivative instability, achieving high-quality accelerated inference and demonstrating strong potential for broader diffusion-model acceleration applications.

Abstract: The adoption of powerful diffusion models is hindered by their significant inference latency. Recent ``cache-then-forecast'' schemes alleviate this issue by accelerating DiTs using derivative-based polynomials, but they suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios. Our analysis reveals its root cause: the discrete extrapolation performed on representations that are misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and are numerically unstable. Thus, accelerated DiTs suffer from accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability. We therefore reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space. Instead of predicting intermediate features, we align forecasting with the model's Global Drift (GD), i.e., the end-to-end state evolution, thereby eliminating feature inconsistency and memory overhead. However, even this smooth macro-trajectory remains vulnerable to the derivative fallacy: its higher-order temporal derivatives are intrinsically noisy. Thus, we introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to effectively bypass derivative instability and approximation error. We further propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, suppressing oscillatory error growth. These elements collectively constitute ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo demonstrate state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

</details>


### [195] [Memory Depth, Not Memory Access: Selective Parametric Consolidation for Long-Running Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26806)
*Haoliang Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper defines memory depth and tests it via a loop-drift protocol; it introduces EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Retrieval excels at shallow factual recall, while parametric consolidation supports goal persistence after unloading. The work analyzes selection and actuation as two controllable dimensions, demonstrates model-dependent dynamics and asymmetric coupling under miscalibration, and uses public Memora streams as a diagnostic. Memory depth is complementary to retrieval and provides a durable, goal-conditioned memory store.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-running language agents require more than passive memory access. Retrieval can fetch past facts, but it does not determine which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. The study formalizes a separate, parametric memory depth mechanism to encode durable, goal-directed tendencies.

Method: Introduce the loop-drift protocol to stress test memory depth while the working context is unloaded. Propose EVAF (surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation) and evaluate it across GPT-2 and TinyLlama (and ablations on Mistral-7B). Measure short-fact recall (0.956–0.973) and goal persistence/post-unload recovery (0.812–0.904) with only 2–3 parametric writes per ~200 events. Analyze mechanism controls: factorize into selection and actuation; use matched random gates to isolate selection; fixed-inner controls to show inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; matched-gate inversion exposes asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibration. Use Public Memora streams as external diagnostics to reveal stale-memory invalidation. Conclude that selective parametric consolidation provides memory depth distinct from retrieval.

Result: Key empirical findings: (1) Retrieval dominates shallow factual recall, achieving high short-fact accuracy. (2) EVAF consolidations improve goal persistence and post-unload recovery, with modest write budgets. (3) Mechanistic decomposition yields two controllable axes—selection and actuation—and reveals model- and gate-dependent dynamics, including asymmetry under miscalibration. (4) Public Memora streams help diagnose memory invalidation, highlighting boundaries of the approach. (5) Memory depth is complementary to retrieval and offers a distinct, durable memory trace beyond active retrieval.

Conclusion: Memory depth via selective parametric consolidation enables durable, goal-directed tendencies that survive context unloading and long-loop interference, complementing retrieval-based access. The loop-drift protocol provides a concrete diagnostic framework for evaluating durable, goal-conditioned memory in large language models.

Abstract: Long-running language agents need more than memory access. Retrieval systems can fetch past facts at query time, but they do not decide which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. We study this separate problem as memory depth: durable goal-conditioned tendencies written into a small parametric store. We introduce the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test in which the retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded and goal-conditioned behavior must persist under long-loop interference. We evaluate EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, retrieval is strongest on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956--0.973), while EVAF is strongest on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812--0.904) with only 2--3 parametric writes per 200 events. Mechanism controls show that selective consolidation factorizes into two controllable dimensions: selection and actuation. Matched random gates isolate selection beyond sparse writing; fixed-inner controls across GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B show that inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; and a Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora event streams serve as an external diagnostic, exposing stale-memory invalidation as an unresolved boundary. Within this probe, selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth distinct from and complementary to retrieval access.

</details>


### [196] [KARLA: Knowledge-base Augmented Retrieval for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26807)
*Francois Crespin,Fabian M. Suchanek,Nils Holzenberger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A retrieval-augmented generation approach where an LLM produces special tokens to trigger knowledge-base queries during token generation, enabling knowledge updates without retraining, traceability of facts, and improved factual grounding with potentially smaller models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rigidity of LLMs’ fixed parameters for factual knowledge, enabling dynamic updates and transparent sourcing while maintaining or improving factual accuracy without retraining.

Method: Train the model to emit special query-trigger tokens that invoke external KB lookups during generation. Facts are sourced from a knowledge base in real-time and incorporated into output. The approach also supports KB-driven factual revisions via edits, not parameter updates.

Result: Improved factual grounding in both short and long-form generation; enables factual revisions to take effect through KB edits; potential parity with larger models’ factual accuracy using smaller models.

Conclusion: Retrieval-augmented generation with explicit KB-trigger tokens can enhance factual accuracy, transparency, and adaptability of LLMs, reducing reliance on large parameters and enabling dynamic knowledge updates.

Abstract: We propose a new method that allows an LLM to automatically pull in factual knowledge from a knowledge base during token generation. This means that (1)~factual knowledge in the LLM output can be updated without retraining the LLM, (2)~facts in the LLM output can be traced to the knowledge base for transparency and explainability, and (3)~smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models. Our core idea is to train the model to produce special tokens that trigger a query to the knowledge base. Our experiments show that our method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and allows factual revisions to take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.

</details>


### [197] [Computational Analysis of Heart Rate Variability in Healthy Adults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26816)
*María J. Lado,Arturo J. Méndez,Leandro Rodriguez-Liñares,Baltasar García Pérez-Schofield,Pedro Cuesta-Morales,Brais Iglesias-Otero,Xose A. Vila*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study of HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20M/20F, 30–50y) evaluates five criteria: normality, stability, correlation, reproducibility, and consistency across time, frequency, and nonlinear domains. It finds normality in time-domain and nonlinear indices (notably global and LF), gender differences, general stability except HF indices, redundancy among HF indices, modest cross-database error with Fantasia (mostly <10%), but higher error for SD2 and SDNN in women, and high cross-study variability in frequency-domain indices. The proposed key indices for robust HRV representation are ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish reliable, generalizable HRV indicators in healthy individuals and address the lack of a gold standard by evaluating normality, stability, correlations, reproducibility, and cross-study consistency across HRV domains.

Method: Cross-sectional analysis of 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women; age 30–50). HRV was computed using time-domain, frequency-domain, and nonlinear metrics via computational signal processing. Five analyses addressed: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) cross-study consistency. A Fantasia database comparison was performed to assess external validity.

Result: Time-domain and nonlinear indices (notably global and LF) followed normal distributions with observed gender differences. Most indices were stable, except HF-related metrics. High correlations among HF indices indicate redundancy, suggesting only one HF index may be needed. Fantasia comparison yielded <10% error for most indices, but SD2 and SDNN in women exceeded 15% error. Time-domain and nonlinear indices showed low inter-study variability; frequency-domain indices exhibited high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. Proposed robust indices: ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF).

Conclusion: A compact set of HRV indices can effectively represent HRV components in healthy individuals, enhancing clinical and research utility, with emphasis on stable, non-redundant metrics and careful consideration of cross-study variability.

Abstract: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis is a key indicator of cardiac physiological state and aids in disease diagnosis. However, research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals remains limited, and no gold standard exists. This study evaluates HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) to improve HRV's clinical utility. Using computational methods for signal processing and data analysis, time, frequency, and nonlinear indices were analyzed to address five questions: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) consistency. Key findings: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices, particularly global and LF (low frequency), follow normal distributions, with gender differences noted. (2) Most indices are stable except HF (high frequency)-related ones. (3) High correlations in HF-related indices suggest redundancy, indicating only one is necessary in studies. (4) Comparisons with the Fantasia database revealed less than 10% error for most indices, except SD2 and SDNN in women (greater than 15%). (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, while frequency-domain indices exhibit high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. The selected indices-ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF)-are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

</details>


### [198] [The Capability Frontier: Benchmarks Miss 82% of Model Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26836)
*Bradley Fowler,Ryan Smith,Daniel Thi Graviet,William Myers,Joshua Greaves,Narmeen Fatimah Oozeer,Antía García,Philip Quirke,Amirali Abdullah,Fazl Barez,Shriyash Kaustubh Upadhyay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Capability Frontier, a Pareto frontier over models and generations to quantify best possible performance under cost in heterogeneous data; corrects biases from single-model and single-run evaluations, revealing substantial underestimation of LLM capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional benchmarks evaluate a single model on a single run, which underestimates capabilities in heterogeneous, multi-domain settings. The work asks: what is the best achievable performance at each cost when optimally selecting across models and generations?

Method: Define Capability Frontier as the Pareto frontier over model choices and generation counts with an oracle selecting the best mix under a fixed cost. Evaluate 21 LLMs across 16 benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks. Compare frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark’s top model. Correct for two biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from maxima over noisy samples. Supplement with controlled probabilistic simulations to study how topic entropy affects the gap between oracle routing and the best single model.

Result: The Frontier yields substantial gains: correcting for single-model evaluation reduces error rate by 54%; correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement; state-of-the-art accuracy is achievable at roughly 85% cost reduction. Across simulations, higher topic entropy correlates with a near-monotonic increase in the gap between oracle routing and the best single model.

Conclusion: Evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings have likely underestimated collective LLM capabilities. The Capability Frontier framework provides a more faithful assessment of what can be achieved under cost constraints and suggests new directions for benchmarking and deployment strategies.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks typically report accuracy for a single model on a single run. This systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities, particularly under heterogeneous data distributions: (i) different models get different questions correct according to their specializations, and (ii) given a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. To quantify this gap, we introduce the Capability Frontier: a Pareto frontier over a set of models that characterizes the best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal selection across models and generations (i.e., via an oracle). Our construction corrects for two opposing biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. We study 21 LLMs across 16 widely used benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks, comparing Capability Frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark's top-performing model. Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Complementing these empirical results, we use controlled probabilistic simulations to show that higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model. Our findings suggest collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated, with implications for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

</details>


### [199] [Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines for Warehouse Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26852)
*Janik Bischoff,Anne Meyer,Uta Mohring,Fabian Dunke,Maximilian Barlang,Özge Nur Subas,Hadi Kutabi,Stefan Nickel,Kai Furmans*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CASOP enables context-aware optimization pipeline synthesis for warehouse order fulfillment, via a modular algorithm repository, semantic data/cards, a taxonomy, a pipeline synthesizer, and a pipeline evaluator. Demonstrated on 7 benchmark sets, yielding 1,063,044 valid pipelines; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical warehouse systems often require decomposed approaches due to organizational boundaries and data limits, and there is no general mechanism to select, compose, and evaluate algorithm configurations across subproblems.

Method: Five components: (1) modular repository of order-fulfillment algorithms; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards describing context and requirements; (3) taxonomy structuring order-fulfillment subproblems; (4) pipeline synthesizer that identifies applicable algorithms and composes valid pipelines; (5) pipeline evaluator to assess pipeline performance. Applied to seven benchmark instance sets across four problem classes to generate pipelines.

Result: Generated 1,063,044 valid pipelines across the benchmark sets, enabling automatic synthesis and evaluation of context-specific pipelines.

Conclusion: CASOP provides a scalable framework for designing, synthesizing, and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines in warehouse operations; supports researchers and practitioners; software is open-source.

Abstract: Order fulfillment in manual picker-to-goods warehouses involves interconnected decisions such as item assignment, order batching, and picker routing. While integrated models capture interactions between these decisions, practical warehouse systems often require decomposed approaches due to organizational boundaries, differing responsibilities, or limited data availability. Existing studies primarily evaluate algorithms for isolated subproblems or fixed subproblem combinations for specific warehouse settings, but lack a general mechanism to determine applicable algorithm configurations, compose them into valid solution pipelines, and assess their performance.
  With Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines (CASOP), we propose a framework for constructing and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines and apply these to order fulfillment. The framework comprises: (1) a modular repository of algorithms for common order fulfillment problems; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards to describe warehouse context and algorithm requirements; (3) a taxonomy that structures order fulfillment problems into relevant subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer that identifies applicable algorithms for a given warehouse context and composes all valid optimization pipelines; and (5) a pipeline evaluator that assesses all resulting pipelines. We demonstrate the framework on 7 benchmark instance sets covering four problem classes, resulting in 1,063,044 valid pipelines. The framework supports researchers and practitioners in designing, automatically synthesizing, and selecting valid, high-performing algorithmic pipelines for warehouse operations. The software is open-source and available at https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_pipes and https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_algos.
  Keywords: Warehouse optimization, Algorithm selection, Pipeline synthesis, Order fulfillment

</details>


### [200] [LCAi: Life Cycle Assessment with big data fusion and retrieval-augmented generation-assisted interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26857)
*Georgios Tsironis,Juan D. Medrano-Garcia,Gonzalo Guillen-Gosalbez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework (RAG) for LCA interpretation that fuses multiple perspectives to translate impact results into strategic pathways under uncertainty, demonstrated with a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple facility, using GPT-5 nano.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LCA interpretation often lacks structured, cross-domain mechanisms to convert quantified improvements into actionable strategies under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. The work proposes an AI-assisted, multi-perspective retrieval and synthesis approach to reduce hallucinations and preserve diversity.

Method: Develop a perspective fusion RAG architecture covering academic, industry, public discourse, and EU funding datasets. Operate in three steps: (1) define a scenario anchor with system boundaries and decarbonization targets; (2) generate perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval; (3) perform a neutral synthesis that only uses ledger-stored outputs with no additional retrieval.

Result: Demonstrated on a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility using GPT-5 nano. Findings suggest structured retrieval and constrained synthesis mitigate hallucination while maintaining cross-domain diversity, enabling implementation-oriented interpretation beyond conventional LCA studies.

Conclusion: The framework supports disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and expands opportunities for AI tools in LCA, particularly for scalable technologies. It provides a proof-of-concept of AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation to inform decision-making beyond traditional LCA.

Abstract: The interpretation phase of life cycle assessment often lacks structured mechanisms for translating quantified improvement opportunities addressing environmental hotspots into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework for LCA interpretation, where a multi-perspective retrieval and controlled synthesis is incorporated in the artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted LCA. To operationalise large language models in LCA interpretation, a perspective fusion RAG architecture was developed, covering academic, industry, public discourse, and European union (EU) funding datasets. Our approach comprises three steps: (1) a scenario anchor defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets, (2) a set of perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval, and (3) a neutral synthesis step integrating only ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval. The framework is demonstrated through a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility using GPT-5 nano as the reasoning model. Overall, the structured retrieval and constrained synthesis are designed to mitigate the risk of hallucination while preserving cross-domain diversity. The approach presented can support more disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and opens up new avenues for the use of advanced AI tools in LCA studies, particularly those focused on technologies that could be deployed at scale. This proof-of-concept demonstrates how AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation can support implementation-oriented decision-making beyond conventional LCA studies.

</details>


### [201] [AgentX: Towards Agent-Driven Self-Iteration of Industrial Recommender Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26859)
*Changxin Lao,Fei Pan,Guozhuang Ma,Han Li,Huihuang Lin,Jijun Shi,Kangzhi Zhao,Kun Gai,Mo Zhou,Qinqin Zhou,Quan Chen,Ruochen Yang,Shifu Bie,Shuang Yang,Shuo Yang,Wenhao Li,Wentao Xie,Xiao Lv,Xuming Wang,Yijun Wang,Yiming Chen,Yusheng Huang,Zhongyuan Wang,Zibo Zhao,Zijie Zhuang,Baoning Xia,Chao Liu,Chaoyi Ma,Chubo He,Dawei Cong,Feng Jiang,Gang Wang,Guilin Xia,Hanwen Xu,Jiahong Xie,Jiahui Qiao,Jian Liang,Jiangfan Yue,Jing Wang,Jinghan Yang,Jinghui Jia,Kan Qin,Lei Wang,Ming Li,Peilin Song,Pengbo Xu,Qiang Luo,Ruiming Tang,Shiyang Liu,Shuxian Jin,Tao Wang,Tao Zhang,Xiang Gao,Xianghan Li,Yingsong Luo,Yiwen Ning,Yongcheng Liu,Yuan Guo,Zhaojie Liu,Zhenkai Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentX introduces a production-deployed multi-agent system to automate recommendation experimentation, enabling autonomous hypothesis generation, implementation, evaluation, and learning through a four-agent loop that self-improves over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current recommendation development is bottlenecked by manual processes; innovation scales with headcount. A scalable, evidence-driven loop is needed to accelerate idea-to-launch.

Method: Four tightly coupled agents: Brainstorm Agent (synthesize evidence into executable proposals), Developing Agent (generate production-ready code with repository-grounded generation and reliability checks), Evaluation Agent (safe online rollout with guardrails and A/B judgment), Harness Evolution layer SGPO (semantic-gradient updates to improve agents). Closed-loop pipeline that self-improves via learned updates.

Result: Claims scalable, autonomous experimentation at a pace and scale beyond manual workflows; system produces, evaluates, and learns from experiments, turning knowledge into improved agents.

Conclusion: If realized, AgentX restructures the production function of recommendation engineering into a self-improving, autonomous loop, potentially revolutionizing how production experiments are conducted and deployed.

Abstract: Recommendation algorithm iteration is moving from an artisanal, engineer-bound process toward an industrialized research loop, but this transition remains blocked by a structural execution bottleneck: the idea-to-launch cycle still depends on human engineers to generate hypotheses, modify production code, launch A/B experiments, and attribute online results. Innovation therefore scales linearly with headcount rather than compounding with evidence, compute, and accumulated experimental knowledge. We present AgentX, a production-deployed multi-agent system that fundamentally restructures this production function.
