AgentX operates as a self-evolving development engine: it autonomously generates, implements, evaluates, and learns from recommendation experiments at a scale and pace that no manual workflow can sustain.
  The system orchestrates four tightly coupled stages in a closed loop. A Brainstorm Agent synthesizes evidence from historical experiments, system architecture, data analysis, and external research into ranked, executable proposals. A Developing Agent translates each proposal into production-ready code through repository-grounded generation and multi-dimensional reliability verification. An Evaluation Agent conducts safe online rollout with guardrail-vetoed A/B judgment, converting both successes and failures into structured knowledge assets. A Harness Evolution layer (SGPO) then distills execution trajectories into semantic-gradient updates that continuously sharpen the agents themselves -- making the system not merely automated, but self-improving.

</details>


### [202] [TAVR-VLM: Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding for Hallucination-Resistant Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26874)
*Zhixiang Lu,Xiwei Liu,Sifan Song,Changkai Ji,Anh Nguyen,Jionglong Su,Imran Razzak,Jinfeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TAVR-VLM employs risk-grounded attention to reduce multimodal hallucinations and improves planning performance on a large TAVR dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate diagnostic hallucinations and lack of anatomical grounding in multimodal large language models when applied to high-stakes TAVR planning, enabling more interpretable and evidence-based decisions.

Method: Introduce Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA) with a Risk→Region→Word grounding pathway. R-CGA compresses inputs into a global risk mask; during autoregression, a support-projected causal consistency objective constrains token grounding within the risk-defined mask.

Result: On the M^3TAVR dataset (1,482 patients), TAVR-VLM achieves AUROC 0.896, CIDEr 0.936, and reduces hallucinations to 8.1%, establishing a new state-of-the-art and improving interpretability.

Conclusion: TAVR-VLM provides a faithful, grounded multimodal framework for TAVR planning that reduces diagnostic hallucinations and enhances evidence-based surgical AI performance.

Abstract: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) planning requires meticulous multimodal reasoning. However, adapting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to this high-stakes domain is severely impeded by diagnostic hallucinations, where generated text lacks anatomical grounding. To address this, TAVR-VLM is introduced: a novel framework featuring Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA) that instantiates a model-internal ``Risk $\rightarrow$ Region $\rightarrow$ Word'' structural grounding pathway. R-CGA compresses multimodal inputs into a causal risk bottleneck, purifying dense visual features into a global risk mask. During autoregressive generation, a support-projected causal consistency objective constrains token-level grounding within the risk-defined support mask. Evaluated on $\text{M}^3\text{TAVR}$, a comprehensive 1,482-patient cohort, TAVR-VLM establishes a new state-of-the-art. It achieves an AUROC of 0.896, boosts CIDEr to 0.936, and drastically reduces the hallucination rate to 8.1\%, thereby improving interpretability for evidence-based surgical AI.

</details>


### [203] [A Pipeline for Generating Longitudinal Synthetic Clinical Notes Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26879)
*William Poulett*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Modular synthetic clinical notes pipeline and dataset with 70 virtual patients and 20–50 notes each to support privacy-preserving development and evaluation of clinical AI tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome real-patient data access restrictions and privacy concerns by providing realistic, longitudinal synthetic data for AI development and evaluation.

Method: Structured patient generation; semi-structured patient journey simulation; unstructured note generation with large language models; LLM-based validation/augmentation; multi-level validation for realism vs scalability.

Result: Dataset of 70 synthetic patients with 20–50 notes spanning a full hospital journey; demonstrates internal longitudinal consistency and variety in writing style, structure, and clinical detail; enables evaluation for summarisation, coding, and decision support without real data.

Conclusion: Synthetic clinical data, with configurable realism levels and validation, can facilitate development/testing of clinical AI tools while preserving privacy and scalability.

Abstract: Synthetic data is increasingly used to enable the development and evaluation of AI systems in domains where access to real-world data is restricted. In healthcare, clinical documentation presents particular challenges due to its sensitivity. This work introduces a synthetic clinical notes pipeline and dataset designed to support the development of clinical AI tools while avoiding the privacy risks associated with real patient data. The dataset is generated using a modular pipeline that combines structured patient generation, semi-structured patient journey simulation, and unstructured clinical note generation using large language models. The pipeline is designed to prioritise internal consistency across longitudinal patient records, while also capturing variation in writing style, note structure, and clinical detail. Additional mechanisms, including LLM-based validation and augmentation steps, are used to improve faithfulness, realism, and diversity of the generated notes. We release a dataset of 70 synthetic patients, each associated with 20-50 clinical notes spanning a full hospital journey. The dataset is provided at multiple levels of validation, enabling users to balance realism and scalability depending on their use case. This dataset supports the development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems, including summarisation tools, coding models, and decision support systems, without reliance on real patient data.

</details>


### [204] [Generative Retrieval via Diffusion Transformer with Metric-Ordered Sequence Training and Hybrid-Policy Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26899)
*Chenghao Liu,Yu Zhang,Zhongtao Jiang,Kun Xu,Zhenwei An,Renzhi Wang,Zhao Wang,Jiachen Zhang,Yuxiao Zhang,Kun Xu,Songfang Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MO-DiT+HPPO for pattern-preserving attribute retrieval via continuous generative retrieval; uses metric-ordered training and Pareto filtering to balance pattern fidelity with target attribute expansion; shows improvements across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To retrieve items that satisfy a target attribute while staying within a fine-grained pattern, balancing pattern preservation with broad attribute coverage where standard embedding-based retrieval fails.

Method: A staged framework: raw-sequence pretraining; multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining; tail-centroid fine-tuning; and HPPO (hybrid preference optimization). Metric-ordered training turns sparse online labels into in-pattern trajectories ordered by predicted attribute density; Pareto pair filter selects winner pairs that maintain pattern purity; HPPO labels a hybrid candidate pool with an online intersection metric and optimizes via reference-anchored preferences.

Result: In four attribute domains and under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT outperforms a pretrained generative retriever on the intersection metric; HPPO further improves results, with seven of eight domain splits showing significant gains and one marginal tie on the hardest split.

Conclusion: The framework achieves improved Pareto trade-offs between pattern preservation and attribute expansion, with ablations and analyses locating the sources of gains; demonstrates effectiveness of metric-ordered training, Pareto filtering, and HPPO for pattern-preserving retrieval.

Abstract: Embedding-based retrieval ranks items by their similarity to a query in a shared vector space and usually aims to return the highest-scoring items. In many production settings this is not what is wanted: given a seed set that expresses a fine-grained pattern, one needs more items that both satisfy a target attribute and stay within that pattern. We formalize this as pattern-preserving attribute retrieval. The two goals pull against each other: averaging the seeds preserves the pattern but stays in a low-attribute region, while global attribute retrieval drifts to unrelated patterns. We approach the task with continuous generative retrieval, where a model reads a sequence of item embeddings and generates query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. We propose MO-DiT+HPPO, a staged framework with raw-sequence pretraining, multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and HPPO. Metric-ordered training turns sparse online retrieval labels into in-pattern trajectories ordered from low to high predicted attribute density, teaching one model the metric-improvement direction across domains. HPPO aligns the generated query distribution with the true online objective by labeling a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applying reference-anchored preference optimization. A Pareto pair filter keeps only winner pairs that do not lower same-pattern purity, raising the attribute metric without sacrificing the pattern. Across four attribute domains under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT improves the intersection metric over a pretrained generative retriever, and HPPO improves it further, with significant gains on seven of eight domain-split cells and a marginal tie on the hardest split. Metric-predictor validation, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons, and a candidate-policy ablation show where the gains come from.

</details>


### [205] [Learning to Recover Task Experts from a Multi-Task Merged Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26902)
*Jinwook Jung,Taegyu Kim,Kumju Jo,Sungyong Baik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReTeX recovers per-task performance from a single merged multi-task model by predicting additive parameter offsets caused during merging, avoiding redundant expert storage. It uses an offline SVD-based, router-free task identifier to select the appropriate expert and achieves strong recovery in vision and NLP with good OOD generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static merging causes parameter interference that degrades task-specific performance; dynamic merging is storage-heavy due to duplicating or loading redundant components. A lightweight method to recover or interpolate expert knowledge from a single checkpoint is needed.

Method: Model interference is framed as an affine perturbation to each expert, approximated as additive offsets. ReTeX predicts these offsets to undo interference and recover task-expert performance from one merged checkpoint. A router-free task identifier based on offline SVD subspace signatures selects the task by minimizing projection residuals without explicit routing. Inference uses the predicted offsets to interpolate between experts, enabling adaptive handling of unseen tasks.

Result: ReTeX recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains and improves generalization to unseen tasks. It also demonstrates emergent adaptive interpolation for OOD tasks when faced with novel inputs.

Conclusion: A practical framework for recovering task-specific performance from a single merged model by predicting parameter offsets, eliminating the need for redundant components at inference, and offering strong in-domain and OOD generalization. Code is available at the authors’ repository.

Abstract: Multi-task model merging aims to consolidate several task-specific experts into a unified model, yet static merging consistently suffers from parameter interference. While dynamic merging models aim to bridge this gap, many works rely on the costly storage and loading of redundant expert components at inference. In this work, from the perspective of task expert, we view parameter interference as parameter perturbation introduced to each expert during merging process. We show that such parameter perturbations can be modeled as affine transformation, which can be approximated as additive offsets. Motivated by these, we propose Recover Task eXpert (ReTeX), a framework that predicts those offsets, in order to undo parameter interference and recover task-expert performance from a single merged checkpoint. To recover the appropriate expert when task identity is unknown, we introduce a router-free task identifier based on SVD subspace signatures computed offline before inference. At inference, the identifier selects the task whose subspace yields the smallest projection residual for a given input. As a result, ReTeX recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains, while significantly improving generalization to unseen tasks. Crucially, we also show that the parameter offset prediction leads to emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. ReTeX adaptively interpolates seen expert knowledge to handle unseen tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/BAIKLAB/ReTeX

</details>


### [206] [Diagnosing Task Insensitivity in Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26918)
*Jingyu Liu,Xiaopeng Wu,Kehan Chen,Chuan Yu,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization is proposed to mitigate task-insensitivity in long-horizon LLM agents, using a contrastive regularizer to enforce action dependence on the task instruction, improving task sensitivity and OOD generalization while keeping stable attention to task tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often generalize poorly to OOD tasks due to insensitivity to task instructions; agents tend to reuse patterns learned during training, drifting away from the actual task. The paper identifies training-time attention drift from task tokens to local observations and semantic corruption of instructions as evidence of shortcut biases, motivating a method to align actions with the given task.

Method: Introduce Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that perturbs task descriptions during training (or uses task variants) and penalizes deviations in action from instruction, thereby encouraging action dependence on the task. The approach aims to maintain stable attention to task tokens while improving sensitivity to task changes.

Result: Empirical evaluations show improved task sensitivity and OOD generalization, with the model maintaining more stable attention to task tokens compared to baselines.

Conclusion: The task-perturbed regularizer effectively mitigates shortcut-driven insensitivity in LLM agents, enhancing robustness to task variations without sacrificing attention stability; it offers a practical and lightweight augmentation for long-horizon prompting workflows.

Abstract: Large language models can serve as capable long-horizon agents, but their out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains weak. We identify a key source of this failure as task insensitivity: when faced with similar but distinct tasks, models might apply patterns learned during training and fail to solve the task at hand. We show that models often continue with actions aligned with the original task even when the instruction is semantically corrupted and cannot be directly answered. We further find that, when we replace the task description in a trained prompt with another similar but distinct task, the model may still output the same action. This behavior is accompanied by a consistent training-time attention drift away from task tokens and toward local observations, suggesting an optimization bias toward shortcuts. To mitigate this problem, we propose Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that explicitly encourages action dependence on the task instruction. Extensive evaluations show that our intervention improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving more stable attention to task tokens.

</details>


### [207] [Where Do CoT Training Gains Land in LLM based Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26935)
*Jingyu Liu,Zhiwen Wang,Yuxin Jing,Huanyu Zhou,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoT training does not magnify the advantage of reasoning over direct prompts; it mainly improves prompt-action quality and reduces reliance on CoT revisions, with selective masking improving out-of-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissect what CoT training actually buys: better action selection via reasoning versus better direct action prediction from the prompt, and to assess CoT faithfulness versus post-hoc reasoning.

Method: Compare prompt actions (no CoT) and CoT actions (with CoT) across checkpoints, evaluate action quality and revision rate when prompted with CoT, and apply selective masking of action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples to test generalization.

Result: Prompt-action quality improves substantially across checkpoints. The relative advantage of CoT actions remains similar, indicating CoT training does not widen its advantage. Later checkpoints revise actions less in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on the prompt. Selective masking of action-token supervision improves out-of-domain generalization.

Conclusion: CoT training appears to enhance the quality of prompt-driven decisions and reduce dependence on CoT revisions; masking action supervision can bolster generalization, with implications for designing CoT-enabled agents and training curricula.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely used in language-model agents, but prior work has shown that verbalized CoT is not always faithful and may instead reflect post-hoc reasoning, which means the model already knows the answer before reasoning. We therefore ask what CoT training is actually improving: is the model getting better at changing its action through generated reasoning, or is it getting better at predicting the action directly from the prompt? We study this question by comparing \emph{prompt actions} (predicting action without CoT) with CoT actions (predicting action with CoT). Across checkpoints, prompt-action quality improves substantially. While interacting with the environment, the relative advantage of CoT actions over prompt actions remains similar, showing that CoT training does not widen the advantage of CoT reasoning, and it helps to improve the quality of prompt actions. We further find that later checkpoints are less likely to revise the action in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on the prompt. Motivated by these patterns, we selectively mask action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples. This intervention improves out-of-domain generalization.

</details>


### [208] [Look-Before-Move: Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention in Dynamic 3D Story Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26964)
*Jiaming Bian,Bingliang Li,Yuehao Wu,Pichao Wang,Zhi Wang,Hailan Ma,Huadong Mo,Zhenhong Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular camera-planning framework (Look-Before-Move) for dynamic 3D story worlds that separates observation planning from motion execution, introducing a Semantic Observation Contract, Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search, and Semantic Trajectory Grounding, plus a Dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark; experiments show improvements in perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As embodied AI and world models operate in dynamic 3D environments, agents must actively decide what to observe under narrative goals. Current pipelines focus on motion or perception in isolation, risking inconsistent or suboptimal viewpoints. The paper argues for planning observations first to align perception with narrative intent and physical feasibility.

Method: 1) Semantic Observation Contract converts director's narrative intent into executable visual constraints. 2) Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search selects narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints. 3) Semantic Trajectory Grounding generates continuous, collision-aware, temporally coherent camera motion connecting the viewpoints. 4) Dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark (based on StoryBlender) with 50 stories, 457 scenes, 1585 shots for evaluation.

Result: The framework improves subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines. Ablations confirm the contribution of each component, demonstrating the value of organizing visual attention before motion generation.

Conclusion: Narrative-grounded observation planning is crucial for coherent embodied perception in dynamic 3D environments. Look-Before-Move, with its modular components and new benchmark, provides a practical and scalable approach to integrate visual attention with motion, paving the way for more sophisticated, narrative-consistent camera systems.

Abstract: As embodied AI and world models increasingly operate in dynamic 3D environments, visual perception must move beyond passively interpreting given observations toward actively deciding what to observe. We study this problem through camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, where the camera must not only generate smooth motion, but also decide what visual evidence should be acquired before it moves. We formulate this capability as Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention, where the camera acts as an embodied observer that determines what to observe, how to compose the observation, and how to shift attention over time under narrative intent and physical 3D constraints. To realize this capability, we propose Look-Before-Move, a camera planning framework that separates observation specification from motion execution. It first builds a Semantic Observation Contract to convert directorial intent into executable visual constraints, then performs Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to find narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints, and finally applies Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect selected viewpoints into continuous, collision-aware, and temporally coherent camera motion. We further construct a dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender, covering 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots with animated characters, semantic scene configurations, and executable 3D environments. Experiments show that our framework improves subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines, demonstrating the importance of organizing visual attention before generating camera motion.

</details>


### [209] [Einstein World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26969)
*Munachiso Samuel Nwadike,Zangir Iklassov,Ali Mekky,Zayd M. Kawakibi Zuhri,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A conceptual blueprint (Einstein World Models) for embedding visual-temporal rollouts inside LLM reasoning via a separate world-module, enabling inspectable visual hypotheses to augment text-based reasoning and extend tool-calling to visual thought experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether reasoning about counterfactual visual scenarios improves complex reasoning in LLMs and whether such visual thought experiments can be effectively integrated as part of the reasoning trace.

Method: Introduce Einstein World Models (EWMs): an LLM calls a separate world-module to produce short visual rollouts of scenes under consideration. The rollout is treated as an inspectable hypothesis that supports later reasoning, not as a final answer. This integrates visual thought experiments into the LLM's reasoning trace and broadens tool-calling to include visual reasoning actions.

Result: The abstract presents a conceptual architectural blueprint without reported empirical results. It outlines the mechanism and potential benefits but does not provide experimental validation or performance metrics.

Conclusion: EWMs propose a framework to extend LLM reasoning with visual simulations of counterfactuals, enabling reasoning beyond language alone. The approach promises richer internal deliberations but requires empirical evaluation, interface design details for the world-module, and robustness assessment.

Abstract: Does intelligence require the ability to reason about phenomena beyond direct experience? It is natural to suspect that some complex thought cannot be captured through language alone. However, of particular concern to this work, is whether visualising counterfactual events can complement language as a mechanism for complex thought. We ask whether LLMs can be trained to utilise such visualisation mechanisms, in a way that benefits their reasoning abilities. Motivated by this question, we propose Einstein World Models. EWMs are a blueprint for LLM-based reasoning systems that place visual-temporal rollouts inside the reasoning trace, allowing them to reason in ways that text alone may not support well. In an EWM, the LLM calls a world-module (not to be confused with a world model), to produce short rollouts of scenes under consideration. The returned rollout is treated not as the answer, but as an inspectable hypothesis that can support later reasoning. Einstein World Models extend the capability of LLMs for tool calling (such as web search or code execution), into the domain of visual thought experiments.

</details>


### [210] [Adaptive Utility driven Resource Orchestration for Resilient AI (AURORA-AI)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27005)
*Rahul Umesh Mhapsekar,Ilias Cherkaoui,Lizy Abraham,Indrakshi Dey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An adaptive, fairness-aware resource orchestration framework (AURORA-AI) combines HJB control, Lyapunov stability, and a composite utility to dynamically allocate compute across heterogeneous AI models under non-stationary conditions, aiming to maximize a broad utility while ensuring resilience and fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployments face non-stationarity (drift, shocks) that degrade performance and fairness; static allocations fail to maintain human-centric properties; need a control-theoretic, stability-guaranteed approach.

Method: Integrates Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control with Lyapunov-based stability monitoring and a fairness-aware composite utility; continuous budget redistribution across models to maximize global utility under disturbances; evaluated in a discrete-time stress-rich simulation with bias shocks, drift, and black-swan disruptions; comparisons against Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, PPO.

Result: AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from black-swan events (vs 88 steps for Static, 22 for PPO); increases alpha-quantile by 29% and super-quantile by 25%; reduces mean and max demographic parity gap; increases fraction of Lyapunov-stable steps.

Conclusion: Fairness-aware adaptive orchestration under stability theory is a practical, theoretically motivated path for resilient, human-centric AI deployment.

Abstract: Modern AI systems are increasingly deployed under non-stationary computational, demographic, and operational conditions in which static resource allocation strategies degrade both predictive performance and human-centric properties such as fairness and explainability. This paper presents AURORA-AI, an Adaptive Utility-driven Resource Orchestration framework for Resilient AI that unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy.The framework continuously redistributes computational budget across a population of heterogeneous AI models so that the global utility, defined jointly over predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability, remains maximised under disruption. The framework is evaluated in a stress-rich discrete-time simulation that concurrently injects demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt black-swan disruptions, and is compared against five established controllers including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and a deep reinforcement-learning agent based on Proximal Policy Optimisation. AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from the black-swan event compared to eighty-eight time steps for the Static baseline and twenty-two for Proximal Policy Optimisation, lifts the alpha-quantile and the super-quantile by twenty-nine and twenty-five percent respectively, simultaneously reduces the mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps. These results indicate that fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

</details>


### [211] [Semantic Early-Stopping for Iterative LLM Agent Loops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27009)
*Sahil Shrivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes semantic early-stopping for multi-agent LLM loops using embedding-change and quality gains as stopping criteria, with theoretical termination guarantees, an efficient judge-friendly evaluation protocol, and empirical evidence on HotpotQA showing token savings at parity quality; an oracle can select the best round for superior information gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed iteration caps in multi-agent LLM loops waste tokens and fail to adapt to input difficulty. Semantic stopping aims to halt when draft meaning converges and quality stops improving, improving efficiency without sacrificing results.

Method: Define stopping via cosine distance of consecutive draft embeddings within a patience window; require concurrent quality improvement to stop. Provide deterministic termination proofs and machine-checkable claims (viewed as empirically tested conjecture for convergence). Develop judge-efficient evaluation: replay each policy on identical drafts, cache judge outputs, separate operational (policy) vs evaluation tokens. Implement and test on HotpotQA (60-question test split).

Result: Judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% at parity quality (Delta-IS ~ -0.004, p=0.81); full quality-gated variant is costlier due to judging overhead. An oracle that selects the best round yields +0.115 Information Score (p ~ 4e-11).

Conclusion: Semantic early-stopping is effective and efficient for multi-agent LLM loops, with solid theoretical footing, an efficient evaluation protocol, and empirical gains in real tasks; reframes stopping as selecting the best round rather than predicting stop time, suggesting further research into optimal round selection.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) loops, for example a Writer that drafts and a Critic that revises, are almost always terminated by a fixed iteration cap (max_iterations). This is a syntactic kill-switch: it is blind to whether the answer is still improving, so it over-spends tokens on easy inputs and truncates hard ones. We study semantic early-stopping: the loop halts when consecutive draft embeddings stop changing in meaning (cosine distance with a patience window) and the answer's measured quality stops improving. Our work makes three contributions. First, an honest theoretical footing: we prove deterministic termination and well-definedness and machine-check these claims, while treating the convergence of the distance sequence as an empirically tested conjecture rather than a (previously over-claimed) Banach contraction. Second, a judge-efficient evaluation protocol: we generate each question's full trajectory once, replay every stopping policy over the identical drafts, and cache every LLM-judge call, yielding a strictly paired efficiency-versus-quality comparison at low cost; we further separate operational tokens (charged to a policy) from evaluation tokens (a measurement instrument). Third, an empirical study on multi-hop retrieval-augmented question answering (HotpotQA). On the 60-question test split, a judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% relative to max_iterations at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81), whereas the full quality-gated variant is counter-productive because its per-round judging dominates cost. An oracle that selects the best round attains +0.115 Information Score over every practical policy (p ~ 4e-11), reframing the problem from "when to stop" (easy) to "which round is best" (open).

</details>


### [212] [How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27061)
*Pasi Fränti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey of external validity indexes for cluster evaluation with ground truth, emphasizing set-matching-based measures; recommends centroid index (CI) for intuitive cluster-level evaluation, and pair-set index (PSI) for point-level, size-biased-neutral scoring; ACC or similar set-matching measures suit equal-point weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To summarize and compare common external validity indexes, guiding practitioners on appropriate metric choice based on granularity (cluster-level vs. point-level) and fairness with respect to cluster sizes.

Method: Literature review of external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures (CI, PSI, ACC). Qualitative comparison of properties and use-case guidance.

Result: CI is recommended as an intuitive, explainable cluster-level measure. For finer, point-level assessment, PSI offers a normalized score not biased by cluster sizes. If every point should influence the evaluation equally, clustering accuracy (ACC) or other set-matching measures are suitable.

Conclusion: For ground-truth-based cluster evaluation, CI provides interpretable cluster-level insight; PSI is suitable for point-level, size-insensitive evaluation; ACC is appropriate when all data points should contribute equally. Choose metric to match evaluation granularity and fairness requirements.

Abstract: External indexes can be used for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available. We review the most common external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures. We recommend centroid index (CI), because it is an intuitive cluster-level measure with an explainable result. If we need a more fine-tuned, point-level measure, there are more choices. Pair-set index (PSI) provides a normalized score which is not biased by cluster sizes. If all points should matter equally, then clustering accuracy (ACC) or any other set-matching measure is suitable.

</details>


### [213] [Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27136)
*Shicheng Ye,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Jointly learn experiential rules and policies for LLM agents (JERP) by maintaining a long-term rule pool and updating it alongside the policy from interaction trajectories; decision-time retrieval of rules and coupling of rule revision with policy optimization yields improved performance in complex multi-step tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of two prior paradigms: external rule-based prompting (which can drift from evolving policies) and purely parametric policy updates (which offer limited local corrections in sparse reward settings).

Method: Maintain a long-term experiential-rule pool. At decision time, retrieve task-relevant rules and condition the agent on them with interaction history. After each episode, use trajectories to (1) optimize the policy and (2) revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps rules aligned with the policy while enabling stable behaviors to be absorbed into the model.

Result: JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance on AlfWorld and WebShop across complex interactive tasks.

Conclusion: Jointly updating experiential rules and policies from the same interaction data stabilizes and improves long-term decision-making in LLM agents, with rule evolution tracking policy changes.

Abstract: For LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments, a key challenge is to make effective use of accumulated interaction experience. Existing work has typically separated two uses of such experience: keeping it outside the model as natural-language rules for later prompting, or using trajectories and feedback to update the model parameters. The former is easy to interpret but can fall out of sync with the evolving policy; the latter improves the policy more broadly but provides only limited correction for local mistakes in sparse-reward settings. We present Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for LLM Agents (JERP), which updates a long-term experiential-rule pool and the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, JERP retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with the interaction history. After each episode, it uses the collected trajectories both to optimize the policy and to revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps the rule pool aligned with the evolving policy while allowing stable and effective behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself. Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.

</details>


### [214] [OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27154)
*Aoyang Fang,Yifan Yang,Jin'ao Shang,Qisheng Lu,Junjielung Xu,Rui Wang,Songhan Zhang,Yuzhong Zhang,Boxi Yu,Pinjia He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAVE introduces step-wise causal labeling for LLM RCA, enabling propagation-path grounding; OpenRCA 2.0 with 500 instances shows large gaps between identifying root causes and grounding them in causal paths; outcome-only metrics are insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RCA benchmarks label only root causes, neglecting the propagation path from cause to observed symptom. A step-wise, forward-verification protocol using fault-injection interventions is needed to assess true causal reasoning in LLM agents.

Method: Develop PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that uses fault-injection interventions to reconstruct causal propagation paths and verify cause-to-effect chains. Build OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), a cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations, evaluated across 11 frontier LLMs.

Result: Exact root-cause set recovered in 20.7% on average. Ungrounded diagnosis (identifying at least one correct root-cause service) achieved 76.0%, but grounding that service in a verified propagation path to the symptom reached only 61.5%. Outcome-only metrics mask this failure mode.

Conclusion: Step-wise causal ground truth is essential for trustworthy LLM-based RCA; current models show substantial gaps when evaluated with path-grounded metrics. The dataset and protocol enable more rigorous evaluation and drive improvement in causal reasoning for LLM agents.

Abstract: Root cause analysis (RCA) poses a holistic test of LLM agentic capabilities, such as long-context understanding, multi-step reasoning, and tool use. However, existing datasets suffer from a fundamental gap: they label only the root cause, not the propagation path connecting it to the observed symptom, which largely simplifies the task to naive pattern matching. To support rigorous evaluation, we introduce PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that leverages known interventions from fault injection to reconstruct causal propagation paths. The mechanism is forward verification: reasoning from cause to effect rather than inferring backward from symptoms. Applying PAVE yields OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), the first cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations for LLM agents. Across 11 frontier LLMs, recovering the exact root-cause set succeeds in only 20.7% of cases on average. To locate where this difficulty lies, we relax the criterion and find what we call the ungrounded diagnosis: agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, but ground that service in a verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5%. Outcome-only evaluation hides this failure mode; step-wise causal ground truth is the missing piece for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

</details>


### [215] [TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27161)
*Tinghao Wang,Yichen Guo,Rui Huang,Zheng Lu,Qizhe Zhang,Chenxi Li,Yuan Zhang,Jiajun Cao,Zhirong Shen,Yaosong Du,Guangyan Gan,Wenya Wang,Lin William Cong,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TOPS: a training-free, model-agnostic token pruning module for multimodal LLMs that uses a principled, information-theoretic framework (Task Relevance, Information Coverage, Semantic Diversity) to construct Token Optimal Preservation Sets, achieving substantial visual token reduction with preserved or even improved performance across multiple backbones and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visual token pruning methods lack a principled objective and often trade off task-relevance, coverage, and diversity. Attention-based criteria may retain redundancy; diversity-based criteria may ignore user instructions. A principled, efficient, and model-agnostic pruning framework is needed to reduce computation without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Formulate token pruning as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets via a top-down information-theoretic analysis. Derive three guiding principles—Task Relevance, Information Coverage, Semantic Diversity—and implement TOPS as a training-free, model-agnostic module that selects a subset of visual tokens for downstream processing.

Result: Extensive experiments across 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks show TOPS outperforms prior methods under various pruning settings. On LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS prunes 77.8% of visual tokens while achieving 100.0% and 100.6% of baseline performance on 7B and 13B models, respectively, and may reduce hallucinations, suggesting practical efficiency gains and potential for lightweight MLLM design.

Conclusion: A principled, information-theoretic approach to token pruning yields a effective, training-free, model-agnostic module (TOPS) that substantially reduces visual tokens with preserved or improved task performance, offering a path toward lighter, less hallucination-prone MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their efficiency is limited by the large number of visual tokens, which introduces substantial computational overhead. Visual token pruning offers a natural solution, yet existing methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria tend to retain redundant tokens, while diversity-based criteria are often agnostic to user instructions. Even methods that combine multiple criteria still lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning. In this paper, we revisit visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective and formulate it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Through a top-down information-theoretic analysis, we identify three fundamental principles for effective token selection: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these principles, we propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module that can be applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks demonstrate that TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting that pruning redundant visual tokens can sometimes mitigate hallucination and inspire future lightweight MLLM design.

</details>


### [216] [A Process Harness for Uplifting Legacy Workflows to Agentic BPM: Design and Realization in CUGA FLO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27188)
*Fabiana Fournier,Lior Limonad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces a process harness architecture that wraps a deterministic workflow engine with a policy-governed agentic layer (TDF model) to enable reasoning, adaptation, and governance without replacing the engine; demonstrated on loan approval via CUGA FLO, balancing imperative compliance with agent autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for adaptable, compliant BPM that leverages LLM-based agents without sacrificing the predictability and control of existing workflow engines.

Method: Define Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) data schema and execution semantics; implement three agent types (TaskAgent, DecisionAgent, FlowAgent) governed by a FRAME policy; integrate as CUGA FLO around a workflow engine and validate on a loan approval workflow with hook-driven overrides.

Result: Feasibility demonstration showing all three agent types activated and a regulatory override via hooks; preserves structural control while enabling policy-guided agentic decisions.

Conclusion: Process harness provides a general framework to reconcile imperative process control with normative, policy-driven autonomy, potentially applicable across BPM domains; future work includes quantitative evaluation and broader case studies.

Abstract: We introduce the process harness, a new mechanism for uplifting legacy workflows into Agentic Business Process Management (Agentic BPM) without replacing the underlying workflow engine. A process harness places a policy-governed agentic layer around a deterministic workflow engine, intercepting designated control points to contribute reasoning, adaptation, and oversight while the engine retains structural authority over the process. To define the process harness rigorously, we develop the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model, specifying both its data schema and its execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning across three policy-governed agent types: a TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive task execution, a DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing, and a FlowAgent that governs runtime flow adaptation through a principled hook mechanism. Each agent reasons within an explicit policy drawn from the process FRAME, the aggregate policy set governing all LLM calls in the system. We then present CUGA FLO as the design and implementation realization of the TDF model, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override. The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements, realized through deterministic workflow execution that enforces structural compliance, with normative requirements, realized through policy-framed agentic autonomy invoked at designated control points wherever the process demands it.

</details>


### [217] [Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27215)
*Manjinder Singh,Alexander E. I. Brownlee,Mohamed Elawady*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GAversary is a black-box genetic-algorithm-based adversarial attack for NLP that uses GloVe-based mutations to improve semantic similarity, significantly reducing model accuracy at the cost of more perturbations and longer runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NLP models are vulnerable to adversarial inputs; existing attacks often require some access to internal model structure or struggle to preserve semantics under black-box settings. A robust, black-box, semantically-aware attack could reveal weaknesses and guide defenses.

Method: Hybrid genetic algorithm treating the target model as a black box; uses logit outputs to guide search; mutation operator leverages GloVe embeddings to propose semantically similar word substitutions; classical GA components (selection, crossover, mutation) search over text perturbations.

Result: Applied to benchmark datasets and well-known models; substantially reduces accuracy (best case: 76.8% → 5.8%); outperforms BAE and A2T in effectiveness; perturbes about ~2x as many words as BAE/A2T; semantic similarity slightly lower; runtime increases by ~5%.

Conclusion: GAversary demonstrates that embedding-informed, black-box GA attacks can be highly effective against NLP models, highlighting robustness concerns and the need for defenses against semantically-aware, access-limited adversaries.

Abstract: Deep learning models have achieved impressive performance across various fields but remain vulnerable to adversarial inputs, particularly in NLP, where such attacks can have significant real-world consequences. Adversarial attacks often involve small, semantically similar token replacements to fool NLP models, and recent methods have become more precise by targeting specific vulnerable words, often by exploiting some level of access to the model's internal structure. This paper proposes GAversary, a hybrid Genetic Algorithm (GA) to generate adversarial attacks on natural language models. The GA is able to treat the target model as a black box, requiring only the logit value output by the model to guide the search. GAversary differs from GAs previously proposed for this problem by using GloVe embeddings to propose word replacements (the mutation operator) to improve the semantic similarity of the adversarial examples. GAversary is applied to several benchmark data sets and well-known target models. GAversary is able to substantially reduce the target model's accuracy on test data compared to the BAE and A2T attacks compared against (in the best case, reducing a 76.8% accuracy to 5.8%, compared to BAE's 27.6%). The trade-off is that GAversary perturbs just under twice as many words as the other two methods, with a slightly lower semantic similarity to the original text and around a 5% increase in run-time.

</details>


### [218] [Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27226)
*Sangwoo Cho,Kushal Chawla,Pengshan Cai,Zefang Liu,Chenyang Zhu,Shi-Xiong Zhang,Sambit Sahu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BINEVAL decomposes evaluation into binary questions answered by LLMs to produce interpretable, multi-dimensional scores, improving transparency, calibration, and prompt optimization with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human evaluation of NLP outputs is costly and slow; lexical metrics correlate poorly with judgments on open-ended generation; holistic LLM judges are opaque. There is a need for an interpretable, scalable, task-agnostic evaluation framework.

Method: A meta-prompt generates a set of fine-grained, atomic binary questions for each output. An LLM answers these questions independently per output, yielding question-level verdicts and calibrated overall scores. The results are aggregated into interpretable multi-dimensional scores. The framework is task-agnostic, training-free, and supports self-update and cross-model update for iterative prompt improvement.

Result: BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines (UniEval, G-Eval) across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, with strong performance on factuality (QAGS). It better matches human score distributions and avoids ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, enabling better discrimination. The same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization for summarization and generation prompts under self-update and cross-model update settings.

Conclusion: BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

Abstract: Evaluating LLM outputs remains a major bottleneck in NLP: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges often produce opaque scores that are hard to debug. We propose BINEVAL, a framework that decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions and aggregates the resulting verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores. Given a task prompt, a meta-prompt generates fine-grained evaluation questions, and an LLM answers them independently for each output, yielding transparent question-level feedback together with calibrated overall scores. This decomposition makes evaluation easier to inspect, easier to diagnose, and directly usable for prompt improvement. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines including UniEval and G-Eval, with especially strong results on factual consistency benchmarks such as QAGS. Beyond competitive correlation with human judgments, BINEVAL better matches human score distributions and avoids the ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, leading to better discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. We further show that the same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization, improving evaluator prompts on summarization and generation prompts on IFBench under both self-update and cross-model update settings. Overall, BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

</details>


### [219] [EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27277)
*Junwei Luo,Shuai Yuan,Zhenya Yang,Yansheng Li,Zhe Liu,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EO-WM is a diffusion-based model for weather-conditioned EO forecasting that explicitly models baseline climate, anomalies, and cumulative stress to better capture response to changing weather, with new benchmarks and modest gains on standard metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting Earth surface dynamics from multispectral satellite data under varying meteorological conditions is inherently uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land states. Existing deterministic or undifferentiated conditioning approaches fail to capture weather-driven uncertainty and sustained forcing effects.

Method: Propose EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer with a physically informed conditioning framework. It splits conditioning into baseline and anomaly pathways and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to reflect heat/drought stress. Introduces two diagnostics: Extreme Summer Benchmark and Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark to assess weather-response fidelity.

Result:  EO-WM reduces NDVI decline amplitude error by 5.63% (relative) and improves directional hit rate by 7.80% (relative) while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The approach provides improved weather-responsive forecasting and introduces benchmarks; code will be open-sourced.

Conclusion: EO-WM advances weather-conditioned EO forecasting by explicitly modeling conditioning components and cumulative forcing, enabling more faithful responses to changing weather and offering robust benchmarks for future evaluation.

Abstract: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions. In this paper, we view this task as a partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem, in which weather acts as a conditioning signal, while forecasting remains uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land-surface states. However, existing methods do not fully capture this setting: deterministic models collapse uncertainty into a single future prediction, while diffusion-based methods typically treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning signals, and existing benchmarks focus mainly on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts respond correctly to changed weather forcing.We introduce EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. EO-WM incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework that represents meteorological forcing through a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Specifically, it separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways, and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress. To evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks: an Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware prediction of vegetation degradation under extreme weather, and a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather forcing. Experiments show that EO-WM reduces the error in predicted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The benchmarks and model will be made open-source at https://github.com/Luo-Z13/EO-WM.

</details>


### [220] [Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27286)
*Alina Bazarova,Johann Fredrik Jadebeck,Henrik Zunker,Carolina J. Klett-Tammen,Torben Heinsohn,Wolfgang Wiechert,Katharina Noeh,Stefan Kesselheim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Simulation-based inference with neural posterior estimation closely matches MCMC for calibrating a mechanistic SECIR model to COVID-19 ICU occupancy data, with substantial computational gains and robustness in long reconstruction problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for scalable, near-real-time Bayesian calibration of complex epidemiological models where MCMC is computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional or long-window inference.

Method: Use SBI via neural posterior estimation to infer posterior over model parameters of SECIR using ICU data; compare against MCMC across 31-day windows and a 201-day reconstruction; assess with Wasserstein distances, KL divergences, posterior predictive checks; report runtimes on CPU vs GPU.

Result: SBI yields posteriors in strong agreement with MCMC for 31-day windows; preserves dominant posterior structure in 201-day case; SBI is substantially faster (31-day: ~60-70s on single GPU vs ~1000s CPU; 201-day: ~157s vs >19,000s).

Conclusion: SBI is a rapid, efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, enabling repeated near-real-time inference; leveraging CPU+GPU resources yields major computational gains; supports rapid outbreak analysis.

Abstract: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterior predictive checks. Across the 31-day windows, SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC while accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. In the 201-day setting, SBI preserved the dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI, by combining CPU and GPU resources, substantially reduced computational runtime compared with MCMC, which was restricted to running on CPUs. Whereas MCMC required approximately 1000 seconds for the 31-day inference problems, SBI achieved comparable posterior and predictive performance in approximately 60-70 seconds on a single GPU. For the 201-day inference problem, SBI required an average of 157 seconds, while the MCMC runs took over 19,000 seconds. Our results demonstrate that SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

</details>


### [221] [Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27287)
*Preet Baxi,Jiannan Xu,Jane Yi Jiang,Stefanus Jasin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompt injection can sway LLM-based résumé screening but is highly unstable: it helps only when manipulation is rare and candidate quality is similar, and largely fails as manipulation becomes common; heterogeneity in quality reduces average impact but can still let weaker candidates win, posing fairness risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess how subtle self-promotional prompt injection affects automated résumé screening with large language models, and its implications for fairness.

Method: Controlled experiments simulating résumé screening with LLMs, varying (i) homogeneous vs. heterogeneous candidate quality and (ii) the number of injecting candidates; measuring impact on rankings and fairness.

Result: When résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject, prompt injection reliably improves rankings. The effect diminishes as more candidates inject and collapses with widespread manipulation. With heterogeneous quality, injection is less effective on average but can occasionally let lower-quality candidates outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall vulnerability is highest when manipulation is rare and quality differences are small.

Conclusion: LLM-based screening is especially vulnerable to manipulation in narrow conditions (low prevalence of manipulation, small quality gaps). Mitigations are necessary to prevent gaming and to safeguard fairness in automated hiring.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to screen and rank job applicants, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate algorithmic hiring systems. We study prompt injection in automated résumé screening, defined as subtle self-promotional text that introduces no new qualifications but is designed to influence LLM evaluations. Using controlled experiments, we show that prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject. However, its effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates inject, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. When candidate quality is heterogeneous, prompt injection is less effective on average, but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall, LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. Code and resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/preetb1199/Prompt_Injection_ACL26

</details>


### [222] [When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27288)
*Josef Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ensemble gains in multi-model LLMs are bounded by beta, the probability that all models err on a given query; rho-based diagnostics misrepresent this bound; a Clopper-Pearson bound provides a finite-sample certificate; empirically, gains arise from complementary failures and routing is essential, with little advantage from more models alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify fundamental limits of ensemble methods in LLM systems, critique reliance on correlation-based diagnostics, and provide finite-sample guarantees to bound potential gains.

Method: Theoretically derive an upper bound on accuracy for policies that select a single model per query (1-of-N). Compare beta to rho; use a Clopper-Pearson bound to bound beta. Calibrate a tetrachoric single-factor model and compare with a Gaussian copula to analyze tail dependence. Empirically evaluate across 67 models from 21 providers on tasks like open-ended mathematics and code, with variations in answer format (multiple-choice vs free-response) and human-panel evaluation (kappa). Assess ensemble performance under routing and voting/cascading schemes.

Result: Beta (all-wrong rate) estimates reveal tail underpricing by simple copula models (e.g., beta≈0.052 for math with 67 models vs 0.023 under Gaussian copula; 90% CI 1.7–3.4; k≈17). Code tasks yield beta≈0.079; re-asking in free-form raises beta to 0.127; human-panel agreement (kappa≈0.73–0.92) indicates co-failure concentrates in answer format rather than subject; low-rho ensembles can outperform high-rho Self-MoA at matched quality, but typically do not beat the single best model without strong query-level routing. Gains derive from complementary failure patterns across questions, not simply more models.

Conclusion: Gains from ensembling are limited by the all-wrong probability beta; rho-based metrics fail to bound this risk. Practical improvement requires routing signals and models with truly diverse error modes; simply increasing ensemble size offers diminishing returns unless co-failure is minimized and routing leverages diverse, task-specific signals.

Abstract: Multi-model LLM systems such as routing, voting, cascades, fusion, and mixture-of-agents are used to beat single-model accuracy. We show that their gain is capped by a quantity the field rarely reports. For any policy whose output is one member model answer, accuracy cannot exceed one minus beta, where beta is the rate at which every model is wrong on the same query. In contrast, the usual diagnostic, average pairwise error correlation rho, cannot identify beta: error laws with identical marginals and pairwise correlations can have different all-wrong rates. A Clopper-Pearson bound on beta gives a finite-sample certificate on the largest gain any router, vote, or cascade could deliver before training a router.
  Across 67 models from 21 providers, a tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model still underprices the all-wrong tail: on open-ended mathematics, observed beta is 0.052 versus 0.023 under the full 67-model Gaussian copula, about 2.5 times underpricing, with 90 percent CI 1.7 to 3.4 and k equals 17. The effect recurs on execution-graded code, where beta is 0.079. Re-asking the same GPQA-Diamond questions in free-response rather than multiple-choice form reopens the tail, with beta 0.127 and a five-judge panel with kappa 0.73 to 0.92, locating co-failure in answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks in our pool, combining models rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.

</details>


### [223] [Language-Based Digital Twins for Elderly Cognitive Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27334)
*Mohammad Mehdi Hosseini,Mohammad H. Mahoor,Hiroko H. Dodge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Language-based digital twins of elderly conversational behavior using LLMs and a multi-head cVAE to emulate identity and assess cognitive status; evaluated on I-CONECT with MoCA-prediction comparable to real data and better than GPT baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable non-invasive, scalable monitoring of cognitive health via natural language; address early MCI detection using conversational biomarkers; preserve individual identity features.

Method: Integrate stylometric cues and contextual metadata into LLM-based conversational twin; train a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) to jointly assess reconstruction fidelity and predict MoCA scores; evaluate identity preservation and cognitive predictions on I-CONECT; compare to baseline GPT responses.

Result: Digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics; reconstruction error and MoCA prediction error on par with real data; outperforms baseline GPT-generated responses.

Conclusion: Language-based digital twins show promise for scalable, non-invasive, personalized cognitive health monitoring; potential for early detection and continuous tracking, with considerations for privacy, generalization, and ethical use.

Abstract: Digital twins have emerged as a promising paradigm for personalized healthcare, enabling modeling of individual behavior and health trajectories. In cognitive health, early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging, where language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers. In this work, we propose a language-based digital twin framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to mimic the conversational behavior of elderly individuals by incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency, we introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that jointly measures reconstruction quality and predicts cognitive scores. Experiments on the I-CONECT dataset show that the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics and achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, while outperforming baseline GPT-generated responses. These results highlight the potential of language-based digital twins as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [224] [Physics-guided Convolutional Neural Network for Domain Growth Prediction in Systems with Conserved Kinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26128)
*Vijay Yadav,Madhu Priya,Manish Dev Shrimali,Prabhat K. Jaiswal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An attention-based, physics-guided CNN acts as a surrogate to predict full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation, achieving long-time stability and domain-growth behavior consistent with Lifshitz-Slyozov law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost of simulating nonlinear PDE-driven microstructure evolution with conserved kinetics, while maintaining accuracy over long-time rollouts and preserving composition.

Method: Develop a spatiotemporal CNN with attention and physics-guided constraints; train on Cahn-Hilliard dynamics to predict full time-evolution; enforce conservation and capture domain growth.

Result: Accurate, stable long-time predictions for both critical and off-critical mixtures; preservation of mixture composition; correct domain growth behavior in line with LS law.

Conclusion: The framework is effective for conserved-kinetics systems and potentially extendable to other complex dynamical systems.

Abstract: The spatiotemporal evolution of many physical, chemical, and biological systems is described by nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Recently, deep neural network-based surrogate models have gained increasing interest as efficient alternatives to computationally expensive traditional numerical solvers. In this work, we propose an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to learn the microstructural evolution of such systems. We train the model to accurately predict the full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation. We show that predictions from our trained surrogate model remain stable and accurate over long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures and preserve the mixture composition throughout evolution. We also show that our model accurately captures the growth of domain size and is consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law. The prediction results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for modeling systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

</details>


### [225] [\chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26164)
*Ira Wolfson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPU-native population optimizer CHISAO that finds all modes of a multimodal black-box function with 100% mode recovery on the SFU benchmark across dims 2–64, achieving up to 34× speedups vs CPU baselines and 39× on unimodal cases; derivative-free and robust to noise; open-source on PyPI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge is to reliably identify all local maxima (modes) of multimodal black-box functions efficiently. Existing sequential methods (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent) underutilize modern GPU parallelism and often fail on hard multimodal problems, especially in higher dimensions.

Method: CHISAO (Convergence-Halt-Invert-Stick-And-Oscillate) is a GPU-native population optimizer that evaluates an entire sample batch in parallel and employs a convergence–anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: peaks reached are frozen and preserved, while the rest continue exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster maintains diversity. Gradients are obtained via finite differences (derivative-free evaluation). The method supports substantial noise (σ_noise up to 1.0) without loss of mode-detection reliability and is released as open-source on PyPI.

Result: Empirical evaluation on the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite (42 functions) across dimensions d ∈ {2,4,8,16,32,64} shows 100% mode recovery, where CPU baselines fail at d ≥ 8 on the hardest problems. Speedups reach up to 34× over basin-hopping on problems where all methods succeed (e.g., Michalewicz at d=64) and up to 39× on unimodal cases (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid at d=64). All assessments are gradient-free in the sense that gradients come from finite differences, representing a worst-case derivative-free scenario. The algorithm is robust to substantial noise and the code is available as an open-source Python package on PyPI.

Conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates a scalable, GPU-native approach to discovering all modes of multimodal black-box functions with strong empirical performance and noise robustness. Its convergence–anticonvergence oscillation, asymmetric freezing of confirmed modes, and adaptive reseeding contribute to effective exploration–exploitation balance. While results are promising, further theoretical analysis, broader benchmarks, and exploration of scalability limits and real-world applicability would be beneficial.

Abstract: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

</details>


### [226] [Implementation of reinforcement learning in chemical reaction networks: application to phototaxis as curiosity-driven exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26168)
*Ruyi Tang,Grégoire Sergeant-Perthuis,David Colliaux*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A minimal, information-driven model of phototaxis treating navigation as a POMDP implemented by biochemical reaction networks, inferred from data via inverse reinforcement learning, with run–tumble framed as information gathering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge mechanistic run–tumble models with active sensing by embedding phototactic navigation in an information-theoretic POMDP framework realized through CRN-ODEs, thereby linking cognition, biophysics, and chemical computation.

Method: Formulate a POMDP with hidden environmental light state; update a minimal internal belief via memoryless Bayesian inference from observations; realize dynamics with chemical-reaction-network ODEs; include a biophysical photoreception observation model and a polynomial bound on information gain; use inverse reinforcement learning on 30 Chlamydomonas trajectories to infer a behavioral objective; benchmark against standard SSA-based baselines.

Result: The model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution and yields run–tumble as an information-acquisition strategy, performing comparably to SSA baselines on the dataset.

Conclusion: Intracellular biochemical networks can implement information-seeking sensorimotor strategies that govern phototaxis, offering a unified view that connects cognitive principles with cellular biophysics.

Abstract: Living systems navigate environments using noisy and incomplete sensory signals. In unicellular algae, phototaxis is often modeled as a mechanistic run--tumble process driven by stimulus--response rules. However, such descriptions overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. From a minimal cognition perspective, we reframe this navigation as a subjective, information-driven sensorimotor process.
