To this end, we propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics. Environmental variables are hidden, while the cell updates a minimal internal state from each observation through a memoryless Bayesian step. These internal dynamics balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation and can be implemented through Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN--ODEs). Our model includes a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories, we infer the behavioral objective consistent with observed phototactic motion and benchmark the resulting dynamics with standard Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines. Our model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on this dataset. Within this framework, run--tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity, demonstrating how intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation.

</details>


### [227] [Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26169)
*Abrar Alotaibi,Moataz Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A review of NAS methods for GANs, comparing search strategies, metrics, and outcomes, highlighting improvements in GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, and outlining limitations and future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate GAN architecture design to overcome manual trial-and-error tuning, improve stability, efficiency, and generalization, and provide standardized evaluation beyond traditional single-number scores.

Method: Survey and structured comparison of NAS-GAN approaches across search strategies (evolutionary, gradient-based, reinforcement learning, etc.), evaluation protocols, datasets, and reported results; analysis of metrics and scalability.

Result: Findings indicate evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods are effective in certain contexts; robust evaluation metrics beyond IS/FID are needed; diverse datasets are important for assessing GAN performance; NAS can improve performance, stability, and efficiency but faces computational and reproducibility challenges.

Conclusion: The review offers guidance for designing more effective NAS methods for GANs, highlights key gaps (metrics, datasets, efficiency, reproducibility), and outlines directions for future research including standardized benchmarks, robust evaluation, scalable search, and cross-domain validation.

Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, while also identifying limitations and areas for future research. Key findings include the superiority of evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods in certain contexts, the importance of robust evaluation metrics beyond traditional scores like Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and the need for diverse datasets in assessing GAN performance. By presenting a structured comparison of existing NAS-GAN techniques, this paper aims to guide researchers in developing more effective NAS methods and advancing the field of GANs.

</details>


### [228] [KG-TRACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Mechanistic Grounding in Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26179)
*Naman Garg,Sarika Jain,Sourav Yadav,Bharat K. Bhargava,Ghanapriya Singh,Abhishek Srivastava,Parimal Kar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KG-TRACE fuses RotatE KG embeddings from the WHO mutation knowledge graph with neural genomic features via an epistemic trust gate, grounding predictions in biological knowledge while maintaining strong AUROC for isoniazid. It introduces the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR) to quantify alignment of neural attributions with biology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of mechanistic grounding in neural AMR prediction by ensuring neural attributions align with established biological pathways, improving interpretability and clinical trust.

Method: Neuro-symbolic framework: integrate RotatE-based KG embeddings with a neural genomic model using an epistemic trust gate that dynamically weighs neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge (WHO mutation KG). Evaluated on CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort for isoniazid resistance; introduces BGR as a dataset-level metric for grounding.

Result: Achieves AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid; 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions; identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts via 'UNCERTAIN' lab flags; provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians through symbolic grounding without sacrificing competitive predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic grounding via KG-TRACE enables verifiable interpretability by aligning neural attributions with established biology, with BGR offering a concrete measure of grounding at the dataset level.

Abstract: While WGS-based AMR prediction has reached high accuracy, existing models lack a mechanism to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways. We present KG-TRACE, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that integrates the WHO mutation knowledge graph (KG) as a structured biological constraint on a neural genomic model. Unlike existing methods that learn statistical patterns in isolation, KG-TRACE fuses genomic features and RotatE-based KG embeddings through a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
  Evaluated on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, KG-TRACE achieves an AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid, achieving competitive accuracy while its primary value lies in symbolic grounding, not predictive uplift. More importantly, we introduce the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR), a dataset-level metric that quantifies alignment between neural attributions and established biology. Our framework achieves a 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions and effectively identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts by issuing laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases. We demonstrate that neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust.

</details>


### [229] [Necessary but Not Sufficient: Temperature Control and Reproducibility in LLM-as-Judge Safety Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26185)
*Hiroki Tamba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-based graders for safety evaluation are not deterministic even at low temperatures; default settings can produce pass/fail flips across runs, and forcing greedy decoding does not fully eliminate variability. A reproducible, disagreement-aware evaluation harness is needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenging the assumption that setting grader temperature to 0 yields deterministic grading in evaluation pipelines; safety decisions often gate deployment, so instability undermines reliability.

Method: Empirical test using Japan AISI's open-source aisev safety-evaluation codebase. A reproduction harness (690 API calls) evaluated two providers, three model tiers, five sampling configurations. The grader was used without explicit temperature/seed, so defaults applied (often 1.0). Assessed pass/fail consistency across multiple runs (up to 20) and evaluated impact of forcing greedy decoding (top_k=1) on borderline items.

Result: Found per-item disagreement up to ~50% over 20 runs when default settings applied. Pinning temperature to 0 reduced but did not eliminate flips; across 690 calls, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remained non-reproducible under forced greedy decoding. Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 deprecated temperature, limiting the proposed mitigation. Indicates evaluation-harness reports of single verdicts can conflate noise with safety properties.

Conclusion: There is a structural gap in current evaluation practices: lack of variance/disagreement reporting. Recommendation to treat grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside scores; provide reproducible protocols, seeds, and temperature controls, and report per-item stability across runs.

Abstract: LLM-as-judge ("grader") components are now standard in evaluation harnesses, including safety evaluations where a pass/fail verdict may gate downstream deployment decisions. A widespread assumption is that setting the grader's sampling temperature to 0 makes grading deterministic. We test this assumption against a real safety-evaluation codebase (Japan AISI's open-source aisev) and show it fails on two levels. First, the harness invokes its grader without setting temperature or seed; the underlying provider silently applies its default of 1.0, so items near the decision boundary flip pass/fail across identical runs (per-item disagreement up to ~50% over 20 runs). Second, pinning temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips: across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible even under forced greedy decoding (top_k=1). Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 has since deprecated temperature entirely, rendering the primary mitigation inapplicable to newer model generations. These findings expose a structural gap: evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. We release a reproduction harness (690 calls, 7 conditions) and recommend that harnesses treat grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside the scores themselves.

</details>


### [230] [Clue-Guided Money Laundering Group Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26189)
*Boyang Wang,Jianing Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A clue-guided, interactive framework (CGGD) for recovering laundering groups from initial clues in large-scale financial networks, using Clue2Group to build a compact local context, estimate a clue-conditioned local risk field via a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and fuse risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover coherent groups; validated on two AML benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph anomaly methods focus on node-level alerts or global group discovery, which do not align with real AML workflows where investigators start from concrete clues and iteratively expand the suspected group. There is a need for an interactive, clue-guided approach that preserves structural patterns and reduces noise to support analyst workflows in large networks.

Method: Propose Clue2Group: (1) construct a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like/cycle-like laundering structures; (2) estimate a clue-conditioned local risk field using a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN; (3) integrate risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group. The overarching CGGD framework enables progressive, clue-guided group recovery from an initial clue set via analyst interaction.

Result: Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks demonstrate that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework, offering a feasible step toward aligning graph-based AML research with real investigation workflows and improving the discovery of laundering groups.

Conclusion: Clue2Group advances AML analytics by enabling analyst-guided, progressive group recovery from clues through a compact local context and a clue-conditioned risk model, bridging the gap between research methods and real investigations.

Abstract: Money Laundering Group Discovery (MLGD) aims to identify hidden criminal groups and recover their complete structures in large-scale financial networks. Existing graph anomaly detection methods mainly produce node-level risk alerts, while global group discovery methods passively search for suspicious groups over the whole network. Both are mismatched with real Anti-money-laundering (AML) investigations, where analysts usually start from a concrete clue and gradually expand the investigation to recover the responsible group. To address this gap, we propose Clue-Guided Group Discovery (CGGD), where a laundering group is progressively recovered from an initial clue set through analyst interaction. We further propose Clue2Group, a framework that first constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like and cycle-like laundering structures. It then estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and finally integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group. Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations, offering a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

</details>


### [231] [Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26192)
*Jialan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces FHPLF, a federated hash learning framework that uses binary gradient-like updates, a projected Hamming distance-based similarity model, and a privacy-enhanced upload strategy to improve accuracy, efficiency, and privacy across four real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy, communication overhead, and limited representational capacity of binary codes in federated and hash learning, enabling effective privacy-preserving distributed representation learning.

Method: Three innovations: (a) replace real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices to cut computation, storage, communication, and bolster privacy; (b) use Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling to capture the importance of individual binary bits; (c) adopt Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) to reduce user-interaction leakage during transmission.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world datasets shows FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL baselines, achieving a favorable balance between accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.

Conclusion: FHPLF effectively integrates hash learning into federated settings with improved performance and privacy. The combination of binary-gradient representations, projected Hamming distance, and privacy-enhanced upload is a promising direction for privacy-preserving distributed hash learning, with potential applicability across privacy-sensitive domains.

Abstract: Hash Learning (HL) is an efficient representation learning approach that maps real-valued data into compact binary representations. Traditional HL methods typically require users to upload personal data to a central server, which is incompatible with increasingly stringent data security regulations. Federated Learning (FL) provides a decentralized paradigm for learning globally optimal models without centralizing private data. However, most FL methods rely on transmitting large-scale real-valued gradient information, leading to high communication overhead and potential privacy risks. Integrating HL into FL is a promising solution. Nevertheless, existing HL methods suffer from limited representational capacity of binary codes, which may degrade model accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor (FHPLF) model. FHPLF introduces three key innovations: (a) replacing real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices, significantly reducing computation, storage, and communication costs while enhancing privacy protection; (b) leveraging Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling, which captures the importance of individual binary bits to improve representation capability; and (c) proposing a Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy to further reduce the risk of user interaction leakage during transmission. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.

</details>


### [232] [Statistical and Structural Approaches to Algorithmic Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26200)
*Antonio Ferrara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two major fairness limitations are identified: reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and ignoring structural context of individuals; the work proposes context-aware, probabilistic auditing to address these gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As ML systems increasingly mediate access to economic and social opportunities, fairness must go beyond predictive accuracy by addressing embedded structural biases and uncertainty in auditing.

Method: Critical analysis of current fairness paradigms; theoretical development of a context-aware auditing framework; framing probabilistic auditing and inclusion of structural factors in evaluations.

Result: Identifies two fundamental limitations and outlines a path toward a fairness auditing framework that incorporates uncertainty and structural context; suggests potential practical implications for more equitable ML systems.

Conclusion: A shift from isolated, deterministic auditing to context-rich, probabilistic evaluation is necessary to mitigate structural inequalities embedded in AI systems.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems have outgrown their origins as isolated predictive constructs, evolving into complex socio-technical architectures that actively mediate human opportunity. As algorithms increasingly determine access to economic and social opportunities, it has become widely recognized that these systems are deeply embedded with the structural inequalities and prejudices of their environments. The field of algorithmic fairness emerged in response to the growing recognition that models optimized for predictive accuracy can systematically disadvantage marginalized groups. Early mitigation strategies, however, rested on fragile simplifications that limited their effectiveness in complex socio-technical environments. This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary fairness paradigms: the reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and the treatment of individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.

</details>


### [233] [Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26204)
*Sophia Li,Max Zhao,Raghu G. Raj,Tianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological data analysis (TDA) descriptors provide complementary, interpretable information for flood detection on SEN12-FLOOD, improving robustness when combined with CNN/ViT features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the opacity and limited interpretability of deep flood-detection models and exploit global structural cues in remote-sensing imagery to enhance detection under challenging conditions (e.g., cloud cover).

Method: Systematically evaluate multiple topological descriptors derived from each image and integrate them into neural networks; use the SEN12-FLOOD dataset to assess performance against CNN- and ViT-based baselines and demonstrate interpretability benefits.

Result: Topological features carry flood-relevant signals independently and, when fused with standard networks, improve robustness and interpretability of flood detection systems.

Conclusion: TDA-based features are a valuable addition for remote-sensing flood detection, offering complementary information to deep learning models and enabling more robust and interpretable decision-making.

Abstract: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

</details>


### [234] [A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26212)
*Tal Weissblat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general GNN-based approach to infer multiple finite-group properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) directly from Cayley graphs, showing graph representations encode significant algebraic information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend previous work by developing a property-independent framework that learns various algebraic properties of finite groups from Cayley graphs, demonstrating that substantial algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations.

Method: Utilize a common Graph Neural Network architecture and training pipeline to predict abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability from Cayley-graph representations of finite groups, evaluating across diverse group families to assess property recovery and generalization.

Result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from Cayley-graph representations across a collection of finite groups from several families.

Conclusion: Cayley-graph representations encode substantial algebraic information about finite groups, and GNNs can extract this information; the framework serves as a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to algebraic-property analysis of finite groups.

Abstract: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

</details>


### [235] [Fast LeWorldModel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26217)
*Yuntian Gao,Xiangyu Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fast-LeWM replaces iterative local one-step latent rollouts with action-prefix prediction to speed up planning and reduce latent error accumulation, achieving faster planning and better multi-horizon latent modeling than LeWM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational bottleneck and error accumulation of autoregressive rollouts in LeWorldModel during planning; enable efficient multi-horizon latent predictions by predicting action-prefix outcomes in parallel.

Method: Introduce Fast-LeWorldModel: given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, encode prefixes and predict future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. Treat action prefixes as the prediction unit, with prefix-level supervision guiding learning of state evolution under varying prefixes. During planning, use the last prefix token to evaluate the corresponding future latent without rolling through intermediate states.

Result: Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM, significantly reduces planning time, and achieves lower open-loop latent loss whose growth with rollout horizon is markedly slower.

Conclusion: Prefix-level action prediction is a viable and advantageous mechanism for latent world models in planning, offering faster planning and more robust multi-horizon latent predictions; future work could explore scalability, robustness, and broader domain applicability.

Abstract: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

</details>


### [236] [Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26257)
*Wojciech Łapacz,Stanisław Pawlak,Jan Dubiński,Franziska Boenisch,Adam Dziedzic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Practical Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) estimates what fraction of a dataset contributed to training a model without shadow models or real held-out data, by generating synthetic non-member samples and framing the problem as mixture proportion estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DUI methods rely on impractical assumptions (shadow models, known training samples, and an in-distribution held-out set absent from training). This limits applicability to modern large models and data ownership disputes. A practical DUI method is needed.

Method: Generate synthetic non-member samples; extract diverse membership signals; and formulate DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to infer the share of the candidate dataset used in training.

Result: Experiments on large image generative models show reliable quantification of dataset usage, indicating the method is practical for data owners to assess how much of their data influenced training.

Conclusion: The proposed DUI framework removes key practical constraints (shadow models and real held-out data), offering a scalable and applicable tool for assessing dataset usage in modern models.

Abstract: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

</details>


### [237] [Equivariance and Augmentation for Bayesian Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26273)
*Miaowen Dong,Axel Flinth,Jan E. Gerken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Data augmentation can induce equivariance in Bayesian neural networks trained with variational inference under exponential-family VI; derives exact equivariance conditions, provides bounds on equivariance error, and introduces three symmetrization techniques (with orbit expansion most effective) that improve both equivariance and performance; code available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge data augmentation and equivariant architectures in the Bayesian setting; extend results that augmented infinite deep ensembles are equivariant to variational Bayesian neural nets; quantify and control augmentation-induced symmetry.

Method: Theoretical analysis of BNNs trained with variational inference using exponential-family variational distributions; derive conditions for exact equivariance under augmentation; establish bounds on equivariance error; introduce three novel symmetrization techniques to boost augmentation effects; empirically compare methods.

Result: Derived sufficient conditions for exact equivariance under VI with exponential-family posteriors; provided bounds on the equivariance error; demonstrated via extensive experiments that one method, orbit expansion, outperforms baselines in both equivariance and overall performance; code released.

Conclusion: Data augmentation, when paired with appropriate symmetrization, can realize equivariance in Bayesian neural networks under variational inference; orbit expansion is particularly effective; the work offers practical methods and a public implementation.

Abstract: Symmetries are important for many deep learning tasks, ranging from applications in the sciences to medical imaging. However, there is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints on the neural network architecture (yielding equivariant neural networks) or learn them from augmented training data. Although equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation, since analyzing augmentation requires control over the training dynamics. Inspired by recent results that show that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, we study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference. We focus on variational distributions in the exponential family and derive conditions under which exact equivariance is reached. We furthermore obtain bounds on the equivariance error and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques which boost the effect of data augmentation in this setting. We conduct extensive numerical experiments which show that one of our symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms the baseline in both equivariance and overall performance. Our code is available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs

</details>


### [238] [SSM Adapters via Hankel Reduced-order Modeling: Injection Site Determines Task Suitability in Long-Context Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26290)
*Omanshu Thapliyal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An SSM-based residual adapter (HRM) for PEFT enables efficient long-context sequence modeling, matching LoRA in computation while outperforming it on long-context benchmarks; it uses Hankel-based model reduction and FFT-based parallelism, with injection as a residual in the model's recurrence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve parameter-efficient fine-tuning for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation by leveraging state-space models and to identify preferable injection sites (SSM adapters vs MLP blocks).

Method: Introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammian, exploiting time-invariance of the system matrix. HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving parity with LoRA in computation for all context lengths. Evaluated in iso-parametric settings on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable params), across LongBench tasks (QuALITY, QMSum) and 18 configurations of synthetic DFA/Parity state-tracking and enwik8, with gate analysis of recurrence modulation.

Result: HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks (QuALITY +34.8% relative accuracy; QMSum +71.6% relative ROUGE-1). Demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 synthetic/state-tracking and language modeling configurations. Provides evidence that HRM learns to modulate recurrence, offering a robust alternative to low-rank adapters for long-context sequence modeling.

Conclusion: SSM-based HRM adapters constitute a robust, parameter-efficient alternative to low-rank LoRA for long-context sequence tasks, achieving comparable computational cost with potential accuracy gains and a mechanism to modulate recurrence via learned gating.

Abstract: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) typically targets attention projectors, its efficacy for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation remains under-explored. We examine if PEFT for such tasks can benefit from state space model (SSMs) adapters, and if MLP blocks are better injection sites. We introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. By leveraging the time-invariance of the system matrix $\bar{A}$, HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths. In iso-parametric evaluations on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters), HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, including QuALITY (+34.8\% relative accuracy) and QMSum (+71.6\% relative ROUGE-1). HRM further demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8). Gate analysis reveals that HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.

</details>


### [239] [The Red Queen Gödel Machine: Co-Evolving Agents and Their Evaluators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26294)
*Alex Iacob,Andrej Jovanović,William F. Shen,Daniel Burkhardt,Meghdad Kurmanji,Nurbek Tastan,Lorenzo Sani,Niccolò Alberto Elia Venanzi,Ambroise Odonnat,Zeyu Cao,Bill Marino,Xinchi Qiu,Nicholas D. Lane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RQGM enables recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities by structuring search into epoch-based phases with fixed within-epoch evaluation, while utilities evolve at epoch boundaries. It co-evolves agents and evaluators (including agents-as-judges) across coding, paper-writing/review, and proof tasks, yielding substantial gains and reduced reliance on static benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fix the core limitation of self-improving agents: evaluation criteria that change as agents improve. By introducing evolving evaluators and dynamic utilities, the framework seeks robust, continual improvement across domains.

Method: Propose Red Queen Gödel Machine (RQGM) with epoch-based search. Within an epoch, a fixed evaluation criterion is used; at epoch boundaries, the utility is updated. Introduce agent-as-a-judge signals (code-review, writing/review tasks). Co-evolve writers and graders, and incorporate adversarial objectives to balance AI/human standards and to resist over-acceptance of AI-generated content.

Result: RQGM improves performance across tasks: verifiable coding tasks with cheaper code-review signal (1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens); scientific paper writing/reviewing and Olympiad-proof tasks show 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates for co-evolved writers and 9% higher ground-truth accuracy for graders; strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI papers by up to 1.91x, mitigated by adversarial objective.

Conclusion: Non-stationary utility and epoch-based evaluation enable robust recursive self-improvement and better alignment of evaluators. RQGM demonstrates cross-domain gains and highlights the importance of evolving evaluation criteria to sustain improvement while preventing evaluator bias and overfitting to static benchmarks.

Abstract: Self-improving agents are state-of-the-art (SOTA) on agentic coding benchmarks and have recently been extended to general domains. However, their search methods generally assume a stationary evaluation criterion: a fixed verifier, benchmark, or labeled dataset that remains valid as the agent improves. This ignores a central feature of evolution: species adapt as their environments change with them. We aim to bring the same principle to recursive self-improvement, making evaluation part of the improvement loop and opening search to evolving evaluators, adversarial objectives, and dynamic utilities that may surpass static benchmarks. We introduce the Red Queen Godel Machine (RQGM), an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities. The RQGM makes this possible through controlled utility evolution: search is organized into epochs with a fixed within-epoch evaluation criterion, while the utility can be updated at epoch boundaries, so self-improvement guarantees hold per epoch as the objective evolves across them. We begin by showing that even on verifiable coding tasks, the RQGM improves test pass rate over the prior SOTA by adding a complementary agent-as-a-judge code-review signal. This signal is cheaper and the RQGM uses 1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens. We then turn to scientific paper writing and reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof writing and grading, where the RQGM improves performance over prior self-improving agents: co-evolved writers reach 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates under a diverse agent-as-a-judge panel, while co-evolved graders reach 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. In paper reviewing, the strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI-generated papers at up to 1.91x the human rate. The RQGM corrects this by introducing an adversarial objective that discovers reviewers equally stringent on AI and human work.

</details>


### [240] [High-Probability PL-SGD with Markovian Noise: Optimal Mixing and Tail Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26316)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Aprameyo Chakrabartty,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes first-order methods under PL condition with Markovian gradient noise. Establishes optimal mixing-time dependence for light-tailed gradients via lag-blocking; provides uniform high-probability bounds matching expectation rates; proves lower bounds. Extends to heavy-tailed gradients with a clipped-block estimator achieving near-optimal rates under a transition budget, with matching lower bounds. Overall, characterizes optimal t_mix dependence and tail-exponent effects for PL-SGD with Markov noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high-probability and expectation bounds for SGD under Markovian gradient noise under the PL condition, and extend robustness to heavy-tailed gradient noise.

Method: For light tails: develop lag-blocking to obtain uniform high-probability bounds with leading term ~ t_mix/(k+K0) under geometric mixing; prove optimality with a Ω(σ^2 t_mix/k) lower bound using a persistent two-state chain. For heavy tails: design all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating bias; analyze under a transition budget T to obtain high-probability error ~ σ_p^2 (t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p}; establish matching lower bounds via reduction to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky chain.

Result: Light-tailed PL-SGD under Markov noise achieves a uniform high-probability rate with leading term Õ(t_mix/(k+K0)); this rate is optimal up to constants with a matching Ω(σ^2 t_mix/k) lower bound. Heavy-tailed gradients yield a high-probability rate Õ(σ_p^2 (t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p}) under a transition budget, with a corresponding lower bound; the paper thereby characterizes the optimal dependence on mixing time and tail exponent.

Conclusion: The work provides tight characterizations of optimal mixing-time dependence for light-tailed PL-SGD and optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size scaling in the robust regime, closing gaps between high-probability and expectation analyses and offering practical algorithms for Markovian gradient noise.

Abstract: We study first-order methods for smooth objectives satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition when gradient samples are generated by an exogenous Markov chain. In the light-tailed setting, prior uniform-in-time high-probability bounds for ordinary Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) under a standard growth envelope scale as $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. We close this gap using a lag-blocking argument to establish a uniform high-probability guarantee with a leading stochastic term of $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/(k+K_0))$ under geometric mixing. We prove this linear dependence on the mixing time is optimal via a matching $Ω(σ^2 t_{mix}/k)$ lower bound on a quadratic objective driven by a persistent two-state chain.
  We then extend this framework to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients satisfying a stationary finite-$p$-moment condition, $p \in (1,2]$. We design an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias. Under a transition budget $T$, this algorithm achieves a high-probability stochastic error of $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$. We establish a matching lower bound by reducing PL optimization to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky Markov chain. Ultimately, this work tightly characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

</details>


### [241] [EVOM: Agentic Meta-Evolution of Actor-Critic Architectures for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26327)
*Boyun Zhang,Chao Wang,Kai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVOM is an agentic meta-evolution framework that uses an LLM-based architecture designer to search for high-performance actor-critic neural architectures via bi-level optimization: inner PPO training and outer meta-evolution, decoupled from policy execution. It outperforms manual design, LLM random search, and MLES on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4; ablations show both outer loop and LLM designer are crucial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate neural architecture search (NAS) for actor-critic reinforcement learning, addressing the difficulty of evaluating candidate architectures (each must be trained) and the open-ended design space; introduce a decoupled, agentic meta-evolution approach guided by an LLM.

Method: Frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization. Inner loop trains network weights via low-fidelity PPO; outer loop performs meta-evolution to refine architecture programs. An LLM-based design agent acts purely as the architecture designer, decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experimental comparison includes manual baselines, LLM-guided random search, and MLES; ablations assess the necessity of each component.

Result: EVOM achieves superior performance versus manually designed baselines, LLM-guided random search, and MLES on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4; ablation studies indicate both the meta-evolution outer loop and the LLM Design Agent are essential for the final performance.

Conclusion: The outer meta-evolution loop and the LLM-based architecture design agent are indispensable for achieving high-performance actor-critic architectures; EVOM is a promising framework for automated architecture search in RL.

Abstract: In actor-critic reinforcement learning, network architectures are typically manually designed. Automating this design is challenging because each candidate must be trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended. To address these challenges, we introduce EVOM, an agentic meta-evolution framework for discovering high-performance actor-critic architectures. We frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization: an inner loop trains weights via the low-fidelity proximal policy optimization (PPO), while an outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs. Crucially, this outer loop is powered by an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experiments reveal that EVOM outperforms the manually designed baseline, an LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, delivering superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies validate that both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are indispensable for final performance.

</details>


### [242] [Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26333)
*Behnam Gheshlaghi,Bahador Rashidi,Shahin Atakishiyev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mesh-RL partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent TD updates to accelerate temporal-difference learning in large or sparse-reward environments, without changing the reward, Bellman operator, or planning mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slow TD reward propagation in large or sparse environments due to local updates; need efficient long-range credit assignment without altering core RL operators.

Method: Spatial domain-decomposition inspired by finite element methods: partition the world into overlapping subgrids, enforce boundary-consistent TD updates to ensure global value coherence while enabling localized learning; evaluate across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q.

Result: Consistent improvements in convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability across methods; higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration and accelerate value propagation; Dyna-Q also benefits from structured decomposition via additional gains.

Conclusion: Mesh-RL provides a principled domain-decomposition approach to accelerate TD learning, bridging FEM-inspired boundary-consistency with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments; source code will be released.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

</details>


### [243] [EMA-FS: Accelerating GBDT Training via Gain-Informed Feature Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26337)
*Yan Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS) for GBDT/LighGBM: maintain EMA of per-feature split gains and, after warmup, limit histogram construction to top-K features; extends to stochastic EMA-FS with concentration parameter beta; achieves speedups up to 2.61x with 0.11 AUC gain on certain data, fully backward-compatible with existing histo subtraction; ineffective on extremely sparse data; implemented in ~120 lines of C++.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GBDTs spend a large portion of training time on per-feature histograms. Random feature subsampling ignores feature utility, potentially harming performance. There is a need for an informed, lightweight feature screening technique that preserves predictive utility while reducing computation.

Method: Maintain an EMA of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations. After a short warmup, keep only the top-K features by historical gain when constructing histograms for each tree, preserving LightGBM's histogram subtraction compatibility. Introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS) which samples features based on gain-weighted probabilities controlled by beta; beta→∞ yields deterministic EMA-FS, beta=0 yields random subsampling. Implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners, fully backward-compatible.

Result: Significant speedups on dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark; 1.45x on IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention; at 70% retention, AUC improves by 0.11 with 1.34x speedup. No speedup on Bosch sparse data (>90% missing) where sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.

Conclusion: EMA-FS and S-EMA-FS provide informed feature screening for GBDT that reduces training time with minimal impact on performance and full compatibility with existing LightGBM mechanisms. They offer a unified framework bridging deterministic and stochastic feature selection and are lightweight to implement (~120 lines of code).

Abstract: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT), exemplified by LightGBM, spend a dominant fraction of training time -- typically 65-70% -- constructing per-feature histograms. Existing approaches such as random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) discard features without regard for their predictive utility. We propose EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS), an algorithm-level optimization that maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations and, after a short warmup, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. Unlike random subsampling, EMA-FS is informed: it retains high-gain features while screening out low-gain ones. Operating at the per-tree level, it preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, requiring no changes to core routines.
  We evaluate EMA-FS on datasets spanning financial fraud detection, advertising click-through prediction, industrial quality control, and synthetic benchmarks, with feature dimensionalities from 29 to 968. On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data it achieves significant speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark and 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention it improves AUC by 0.11 points while delivering a 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) it yields no speedup, as LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.
  We further introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS), which replaces deterministic top-K selection with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (beta -> infinity) and random subsampling (beta = 0) in one framework. Both are implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners and are fully backward-compatible.

</details>


### [244] [Does Aurora Encode Atmospheric Structure? Latent Regime Analysis and Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26361)
*Emma Kasteleyn,Ana Lucic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aurora's latent space is dominated by seasonal cycles; extreme storms don't form a separable linear cluster; LRP reveals focus on 3D vertical structure (Great Storm of 1987); targeted masking degrades forecasts far more than random masking; overall, Aurora learns meteorological coherence without explicit instruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissect the internal representations of a meteorological foundation model and assess whether it encodes physically meaningful structure and dynamics.

Method: Apply spatially pooled PCA to the model's latent space; use layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to trace influential features; perform perturbation tests by masking regions and comparing forecast degradation against random masking.

Result: Latent space organizes by seasonal cycles; no linearly separable cluster for extreme storm events; LRP indicates attention to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure; masking relevant regions degrades forecasts by 3.31x compared to random masking.

Conclusion: Aurora captures meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction; interpretable signals align with known atmospheric dynamics.

Abstract: ML foundation models are able to emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately and efficiently but operate as opaque ``black boxes''. We investigate the internal representations of the Aurora model using spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP). We find evidence that Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, whereas extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP indicates that the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts $3.31\times$ more than random masking. These findings suggest that Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.

</details>


### [245] [SOLAR: AI-Powered Speed-of-Light Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26383)
*Qijing Huang,Sana Damani,Zhifan Ye,Athinagoras Skiadopoulos,Siva Kumar Sastry Hari,Jason Clemons,Sahil Modi,Jingquan Wang,Aditya Kane,Edward C Lin,Humphrey Shi,Christos Kozyrakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Automated, validated SOL (speed-of-logic) bounds for deep learning on target hardware via SOLAR: LLM-driven translation of PyTorch/JAX code into an executable Affine Loop IR, deterministic uplift to an einsum graph, and analytic bounds (unfused, fused, cache-aware) with multi-fidelity analysis; validated across KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads; enables headroom analysis, optimization discovery, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual SOL analysis is error-prone, time-consuming, and disconnected from rapid model development. An automated, validated framework is needed to quickly derive theoretical execution-time bounds on real hardware to guide optimization.

Method: An LLM frontend translates source programs into an executable Affine Loop IR (validated by output comparisons); a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds; supports multi-fidelity analysis and broad operator/language coverage.

Result: SOLAR delivers validated SOL bounds with zero observed violations, supports comprehensive operator and language coverage, and offers multi-fidelity tightening to reveal optimization opportunities; evaluated on KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads, demonstrating headroom analysis, optimization discovery, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline provisioning.

Conclusion: SOLAR automates the derivation of validated, multi-fidelity SOL bounds from high-level code, enabling rapid performance engineering across software, hardware, and algorithms and surfacing actionable insights for optimization and hardware provisioning.

Abstract: How fast could a deep-learning model run on target hardware, and how far is today's implementation from that limit? These questions are central to software, hardware, and algorithm optimizations. Speed-of-Light (SOL) analysis answers them by computing a workload's theoretical minimum execution time on a given architecture. Yet deriving SOL bounds remains manual, error-prone, and disconnected from rapid model development. To close this gap, we introduce SOLAR, a framework that automatically derives validated SOL bounds from PyTorch and JAX source code. SOLAR leverages both generative and deterministic components in its flow: an LLM frontend translates any source programs into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by output comparison; a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; and an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds. SOLAR provides comprehensive operator and language coverage, produces validated bounds with zero observed SOL violations, and offers multi-fidelity analysis that tightens bounds and surfaces optimization insights. We evaluate SOLAR across KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads. These experiments demonstrate four use cases: headroom analysis at multiple fidelity levels, identifying optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.

</details>


### [246] [At the Edge of Understanding: Sparse Autoencoders Trace The Limits of Transformer Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26396)
*Praneet Suresh,Jack Stanley,Sonia Joseph,Luca Scimeca,Danilo Bzdok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mechanistic, distributional-shift framework for transformer robustness under OOD inputs, linking prompt shifts to internal concepts, and proposing inference-time diagnostics and a fine-tuning strategy to improve reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve transformer reliability and safety in real-world use by formalizing out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, including the behavior of internal computational processes.

Method: Systematic experiments establishing a mechanistic framework; analysis of how OOD prompts (e.g., typos, jailbreak prompts) drive the model to rely on more fallacious concepts; quantify prompt distributional shift; propose a mechanically grounded fine-tuning strategy; introduce an inference-time diagnostic that examines a model's private computations.

Result: Found that OOD inputs induce the model to activate more fallacious internal concepts; the framework enables measuring prompt shift and informs a robustization approach via fine-tuning; a new inference-time diagnostic is proposed to monitor private computations.

Conclusion: Expands the OOD concept to internal processes; inference-time diagnostics are a critical step toward safe, deployable AI across sectors, with potential broad applicability.

Abstract: Pre-trained transformers have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities, at times extending beyond the scope of their training data. Yet, real-world deployments often face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training data distributions. Without explicit mechanisms for handling such shifts, model reliability and safety degrade, urging more disciplined study of out-of-distribution (OOD) settings for transformers. By systematic experiments, we present a mechanistic framework for delineating the precise contours of transformer model robustness. We find that OOD inputs, including subtle typos and jailbreak prompts, drive language models to operate on an increased number of fallacious concepts in their internals. We leverage this device to quantify and understand the degree of distributional shift in prompts, enabling a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs. Expanding the very notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes, a new transformer diagnostic at inference time is a critical step toward making AI systems safe for deployment across science, business, and government.

</details>


### [247] [Deterministic Pareto-Optimal Policy Synthesis for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26397)
*Aniruddha Joshi,Niklas Lauffer,Sanjit Seshia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a Chebyshev-inspired, preference-conditioned Bellman operator for MOMDPs to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies; proves an enveloping property and monotone convergence to a Pareto frontier coverage set; includes extraction of deterministic policies and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of scalar reward aggregation in multi-objective RL by directly computing the Pareto frontier and enabling deterministic policy synthesis for any preference.

Method: Define a Bellman operator conditioned on a preference vector via Chebyshev scalarization. Prove that the operator upper-bounds the true Pareto frontier (enveloping property) and that repeated application converges to a coverage set of the frontier. Provide a method to extract deterministic policies from converged Q estimates and validate on MOMDP tasks.

Result: Establishes enveloping upper bounds on the Pareto frontier, proves monotone convergence to a coverage set, and shows how to extract deterministic policies from the converged estimates; experimental results show recovery of complex trade-offs and deterministic Pareto-optimal policies.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, preference-aware framework for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis in MOMDPs with theoretical guarantees and practical policy extraction, enabling full frontier coverage for diverse user preferences.

Abstract: Real-world decision-making often requires balancing multiple conflicting objectives, a challenge that standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) frequently addresses by aggregating rewards into a single scalar signal. While effective for simple tasks, this approach often fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs, known as the Pareto frontier. In this paper, we introduce a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator, motivated from the Chebyshev scalarization, designed to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs). We prove that this operator satisfies an enveloping property, where the estimated value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier, and demonstrate that it monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Furthermore, we also show how to extract deterministic policies from these converged Q-estimates. This ensures the agent can recover a policy for any given preference, capturing the entire Pareto-optimal frontier while guaranteeing each synthesized policy remains approximately Pareto-optimal. Experimental results validate that our algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs, providing a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

</details>


### [248] [Beyond Feedforward Networks: Reentry Neural Systems as the Fundamental Basis of Subjecthood and Intrinsic Safety of Next-Generation AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26406)
*A. S. Ushakov,Yu. N. Berdinsk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A bold proposal for a closed-loop, self-referential AGI architecture (D↔I cycle) that purportedly guarantees self-modeling and instrumental self-preservation via a sustained cycle (rho>1) and non-textual goal encoding. It introduces an S-measure (O(N^3)) with Lean 4 proof, and provides full software/hardware deployment details, a taxonomy, and eight falsifiable predictions, claiming deployability today despite lacking empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To realize safe artificial general intelligence by embedding self-modeling, goal stability, and safety constraints directly into a cyclical, closed-system architecture, backed by formal proofs of information-theoretic properties and a practical implementation path.

Method: Introduce a closed D↔I cycle with cycle metrics (C, rho) that enforce self-reference and amplification; encode goals as a D-vector within the architecture; define S-measure as a computable surrogate to integrated information; prove S>0 implies positive integrated information using Lean 4; provide complete Python/NumPy implementations and deployment with Kafka/Docker; present a taxonomy of AI evolution epochs, a zoo of reentry architectures, gauge-invariant networks, and eight falsifiable predictions.

Result: Claims a formal link between cycles and positive integrated information via the S-measure, with machine-verified proofs; delivers an end-to-end blueprint including software, architectures, safety features, and deployment guidance; asserts practical deployability today.

Conclusion: The approach is presented as a complete, safe-by-design path to AGI, but its bold safety guarantees and deployability require rigorous external validation, clarification of definitions (D, C, rho, S), and empirical testing.

Abstract: We propose a complete architectural blueprint for safe artificial general intelligence based on a closed reentry loop (D <-> I cycle). In contrast to feedforward networks, which are directed acyclic graphs (C=0, S=0) incapable of self-reference, the proposed architecture contains a structural cycle (C >= 1) with self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1), mathematically guaranteeing the emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behaviour. The agent's goals are encoded as a non-textual D-vector in the architecture itself, making them immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. We present the S-measure -- a polynomial-time [O(N^3)] computable alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi -- with machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The work provides full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), industrial horizontal scaling via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, a taxonomy of six epochs of AI evolution, a zoo of future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions. All formal proofs are machine-verified in Lean 4. This architecture is deployable today and represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI.

</details>


### [249] [Otter Weather: Skillful and Computationally Efficient Medium-Range Weather Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26421)
*Cristiana Diaconu,Jonas Scholz,Aliaksandra Shysheya,Stratis Markou,Payel Mukhopadhyay,Miles Cranmer,Richard E. Turner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Otter Weather is an efficient spatiotemporal AI forecasting model that achieves state-of-the-art predictive skill with far less training compute, advancing democratized weather forecasting; Otter-XL improves probabilistic forecasts (CRPS) and outperforms frontier architectures at substantially lower compute, with cross-domain applicability demonstrated on a PDE task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Democratize high-performance AI-based weather prediction by drastically reducing training budgets and enabling rapid iteration, benefiting under-resourced groups while achieving competitive or superior skill to NWP baselines.

Method: Develop the Otter family of efficient spatiotemporal models for weather forecasting and assess on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° using WeatherBench protocols. Compare a deterministic Otter to NWP baselines, quantify training cost (<3.5 A100-days), and demonstrate efficiency vs lightweight models and frontier architectures. Extend to probabilistic forecasting by CRPS training, scale to Otter-XL achieving higher CRPS improvements over IFS ENS. Compare compute budgets with GenCast. Apply Otter out-of-the-box to an acoustic scattering PDE task to test cross-domain applicability.

Result: Deterministic Otter outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at 24-hour lead time with under 3.5 A100-days of training. It delivers ~2× efficiency over lightweight AI models and ~100× reduction in compute relative to frontier architectures. In probabilistic forecasting, Otter-XL achieves 9.7% CRPS improvement over the IFS ENS baseline, yielding nearly two-fold predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets. Otter-XL also outperforms frontier architectures like GenCast by >2% with an order of magnitude less compute. The framework generalizes to a complex acoustic scattering PDE task, outperforming a state-of-the-art foundation modelling approach, suggesting broader applicability across scientific domains.

Conclusion: The Otter family delivers substantial efficiency gains on weather forecasting without sacrificing accuracy, pushing the skill–compute frontier toward democratized AI-based prediction. The approach scales to probabilistic forecasts (CRPS) and to larger architectures (Otter-XL), and shows promise for cross-domain applicability beyond meteorology.

Abstract: State-of-the-art medium-range AI weather models can outperform traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) but require massive training budgets. This restricts usage for under-resourced groups and severely limits fast model iteration. Here we develop Otter Weather, a highly efficient spatiotemporal forecasting model designed to democratise high-performance weather prediction with AI. Evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using standard WeatherBench protocols, the Otter family significantly advances the skill-compute Pareto frontier. The deterministic version outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at a 24-hour lead time while requiring fewer than 3.5 A100-days for training. It provides a 2x efficiency gain over lightweight AI models and a 100-fold reduction in compute compared to resource-intensive frontier architectures. We extend these efficiency gains into probabilistic forecasting by training via the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Scaling to a larger architecture, Otter-XL achieves a 9.7% CRPS improvement over the IFS ENS baseline. This yields an almost two-fold increase in predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets. Otter-XL also outperforms frontier architectures like GenCast by over 2%, while using an order of magnitude less compute. Finally, Otter is applied out-of-the-box to a complex acoustic scattering PDE task where it outperforms a state-of-the-art foundation modelling approach, suggesting that the advances made here might apply across a range of scientific domains.

</details>


### [250] [Rethinking Training & Inference for Forecasting: Linking Winner-Take-All back to GMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26424)
*Qiyuan Wu,Katie Z Luo,Bharath Hariharan,Wei-Lun Chao,Mark Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard assignment in WTA-trained conditional GMMs yields uninformative, over-segmented mode posteriors; the paper proposes two lightweight post-hoc fixes—test-time posterior-weighted merging and a one-step EM update—to produce informative, well-ranked mode posteriors and improve forecasts without retraining, grounded in a GMM-vs-K-means perspective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a mismatch between training (soft GMM assumptions) and inference (hard, winner-take-all assignments) that leads to fragmentation of the trajectory space, mode collapse-like effects, unstable posteriors, and poor marginalization over modes. Aligning inference with the training objective should yield better probabilistic forecasts and pruning decisions.

Method: Two post-hoc steps applied to WTA-trained architectures without retraining: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging of nearby candidate trajectories to aggregate similar modes; (2) a one-step EM update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, distributing probability mass across neighboring modes. Evaluations are reported across several WTA-trained architectures to demonstrate improvement in posterior informativeness and forecast accuracy on displacement metrics.

Result: The approach yields more informative and faithfully ranked mode posteriors and improves final displacement-based forecast metrics without retraining, suggesting the gains arise from better-aligned inference with the underlying mixture modeling assumption.

Conclusion: A GMM-versus-K-means lens clarifies recent design choices and provides principled, practical corrections that improve inference without altering training, highlighting that simple post-hoc probability smoothing and aggregation can restore usefulness of multi-modal posteriors in trajectory forecasting.

Abstract: Trajectory forecasting for autonomous driving has advanced rapidly, yet representative models often produce uninformative posteriors over forecast modes, causing problems for mode pruning. We trace this to a modeling-training mismatch: forecasters are typically modeled as conditional Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) but trained with a winner-take-all (WTA) loss that assigns each sample to its nearest mode. We argue that this K-means-like hard assignment (one-hot), while preventing mode collapse, is the source of uninformative mode probabilities: it over-segments the trajectory space, ignores relatedness among nearby modes, and yields assignment instability under small perturbations. Guided by this lens, we introduce two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging that aggregates nearby candidate trajectories; and (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes. Across several WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps produce more informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors and strengthen final forecasts on popular displacement metrics -- without retraining. Our analysis unifies recent design choices through a GMM-vs-K-means perspective and offers principled, practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference.

</details>


### [251] [DualEval: Joint Model-Item Calibration for Unified LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26429)
*Aaron J. Li,Hao Huang,Youngmin Park,Yitong Ma,Wei-Lin Chiang,Li Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Bin Yu,Ion Stoica*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualEval jointly calibrates models and items in a shared latent space to unify static benchmarks and arena-style signals, yielding reliable rankings and per-item diagnostics across 4 domains for 18 frontier LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluation combines static correctness labels and arena-style preference data but treats them separately, hindering interpretability and sample efficiency; a unified, calibrated framework is needed.

Method: Learn a shared latent space for models and evaluation items; jointly estimate model ability, item difficulty, and item sharpness; integrate static benchmark labels and reward-model scores validated against human preferences; apply across coding, math, miscellaneous knowledge, and everyday queries; produce model rankings and item-level profiles.

Result: Produces reliable and balanced model rankings; item-level profiles enable benchmark compression and anomaly/outlier detection; demonstrates cross-domain applicability and supports auditable evaluation pipelines.

Conclusion: DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, enabling more interpretable, auditable, and sample-efficient evaluation pipelines with practical downstream uses such as compression and anomaly detection.

Abstract: Current LLM evaluation relies on two complementary but often disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective correctness labels and arena-style preference data that better reflect open-ended user interactions. We introduce DualEval, a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability together with item difficulty and sharpness. We apply DualEval across four domains: coding, math, miscellaneous domain-knowledge tasks, and generic everyday user queries. Our evaluation uses 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences for open-ended model responses.
