Empirically, our framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support downstream applications such as benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outlier analysis. Overall, DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, producing model rankings and item-level diagnostics that support more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines.

</details>


### [252] [Embedding Foundation Model Predictions in Discrete-Choice Models with Structural Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26432)
*Yingshuo Wang,Xian Sun,Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage adapter embeds a foundation model's choice probabilities into a multinomial logit to enforce economic rationality; provides analytic guarantees and improved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular foundation models often violate economic logic in choice tasks (e.g., price vs. demand, negative WTP, nonzero probability for unavailable alternatives). There is a need to enforce rationality while preserving predictive power.

Method: Stage 1: fit multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints. Stage 2: freeze coefficients and train a small neural correction operating on the foundation-model predictions. The composition preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, yielding computable value-of-time.

Result: On three datasets with two foundation models, +6.4 percentage points average accuracy over the multinomial logit baseline, up to +12.8 pp; 100% cost monotonicity maintained; values of time fall within published transportation-economics range; performance degrades gracefully under limited foundation-model context, retaining at least +6 pp accuracy gain at 10% of the original context.

Conclusion: The two-stage adapter yields improved predictive accuracy while enforcing economic rationality, with an analytical guarantee on value-of-time and robustness to context constraints.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price can increase predicted demand, implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible, and unavailable alternatives receive nonzero probability. We propose a two-stage adapter that takes a foundation model's predicted choice probabilities as a precomputed feature and embeds them inside a multinomial logit's utility. In Stage 1, we fit the multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints; in Stage 2, we freeze those coefficients and fit a small neural correction operating on the foundation model's predictions. We prove that this composition exactly preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, so analytically computable value-of-time becomes a mathematical guarantee rather than an empirical accident. Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter gains 6.4 percentage points (pp) of test accuracy on average over the multinomial logit and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and produces values of time within the published transportation-economics range on the transportation datasets. Performance degrades gracefully under foundation-model context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain even at 10% of the original foundation-model context.

</details>


### [253] [Optimizing CUDA like a Human: Micro-Profiling Tools as Expert Surrogates for LLM-Based GPU Kernel Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26453)
*Jiading Gai,Shuai Zhang,Kaj Bostrom,Jin Huang,Vihang Patil,Haoyang Fang,Bernie Wang,Huzefa Rangwala,George Karypis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A closed-loop LLM-driven CUDA kernel optimizer, KernelPro, uses semantic feedback, modular micro-profiling tools, staged tool invocation, enhanced MCTS, and direct CuTe code search to achieve state-of-the-art speedups and energy efficiency across CUDA kernels; ablations confirm each component’s impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate high-performance GPU kernel design by integrating expert heuristics and hardware feedback to overcome manual, ad-hoc optimization bottlenecks and to optimize energy efficiency, not just speed.

Method: 1) Semantic feedback operator converts hardware metrics into actionable guidance via pluggable micro-profiling tools. 2) Two-stage architecture: roofline bottleneck classification directs specialized tools (kernel-level ncu, instruction-level SASS, system-level nsys). 3) Domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and cross-iteration memory. 4) From-scratch CuTe code generation via autonomous code search over CUTLASS/CuTe. Evaluation on KernelBench and VeOmni MoE kernels; ablations confirm contributions.

Result: KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on KernelBench Levels 1/2/3; 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton on VeOmni MoE kernels; 11.6% energy reduction at matched speed. Ablation: micro-profiling tools (p<0.0001), MCTS search (26% GM gain, p=0.004), proactive tool orchestration (23% gain, p=0.035). First CUDA kernel coding agent reported to optimize energy beyond speed-only prior work.

Conclusion: KernelPro demonstrates a viable path to automated, energy-aware CUDA kernel optimization using LLMs, structured profiling, and cross-iteration learning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on tested benchmarks. Strengths include integrated heuristics, robust search, and direct code synthesis; limitations likely include dependence on LLM/code search coverage, hardware specificity, reproducibility, and generalization to broader kernel classes. Future work could expand benchmarks, assess latency of closed-loop optimization, and explore broader hardware targets and energy models.

Abstract: We present KernelPro, a closed-loop multi-agent system that automatically generates, profiles, and iteratively optimizes GPU kernel code by integrating large language model (LLM) code generation with hardware profiler feedback and pluggable bottleneck detection tools. KernelPro introduces four contributions: (1) a semantic feedback operator that encodes expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, transforming raw hardware metrics into actionable natural language guidance; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture where roofline-based bottleneck classification filters which specialized analysis tools execute, combining kernel-level (ncu), instruction-level (SASS), and system-level (nsys) profiling; (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory for cross-iteration learning; and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over the CUTLASS/CuTe codebase. On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3, establishing state-of-the-art performance across all difficulty levels. On VeOmni's expert-optimized MoE training kernels, KernelPro achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablation studies demonstrate that each design component independently and significantly improves optimization quality: micro-profiling tools (p < 0.0001 vs raw metrics), MCTS search (26% higher geometric mean vs greedy, p = 0.004), and proactive tool orchestration (23% improvement, p = 0.035). Finally, KernelPro is the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond the speed-only focus of prior systems, demonstrating an 11.6% measured energy reduction at matched speed.

</details>


### [254] [Finding the Time to Think: Learning Planning Budgets in Real-Time RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26463)
*Aneesh Muppidi,Firas Darwish,Dylan Cope,João F. Henriques,Jakob Nicolaus Foerster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces variable-delay real-time RL with a gating policy to adapt planning budgets in real-time environments, improving performance across multiple games and enabling transfer to separate-GPU execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time RL incurs time costs for deliberation; naive RL ignores time progression; need adaptive budgeting to exploit environment progression and avoid paralysis in planning-heavy agents.

Method: Define variable-delay RT-RL; train a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets; evaluate on real-time variants of Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, Speed Go; test cross-GPU transfer.

Result: Gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines; budgets adapt to state; method transfers to a real-time setup with environment and agent on two GPUs.

Conclusion: Adaptive, state-aware planning budgets improve real-time decision-making; demonstrates feasibility across diverse domains; suggests broader applicability and potential improvements in budget granularity and hardware heterogeneity.

Abstract: Deliberating takes time. In real-time settings, that time is not free. Standard reinforcement learning (RL) sidesteps this as the environment waits indefinitely for the agent's decision. Instead, we study real-time RL environments where the environment progresses while waiting for the agent's action. Building on prior real-time formalizations, we introduce variable-delay real-time RL, where the agent chooses how long to deliberate at each decision point since the environment progresses. For the planning agents we use, the right delay is state-dependent, and naively planning how long to plan can paralyze the agent. We instead approach this setting by training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets. Across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, our gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines, and transfers to a real-time setup where the environment and agent run on two different GPUs.

</details>


### [255] [A Causal Foundation Model for Structure and Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26467)
*Max Zhu,Martino Mansoldo,Ching-Hao Wang,Stefan Groha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-CFM is a unified causal foundation model that jointly handles causal structure discovery and outcome prediction from observational tabular data, leveraging known graph structure when available, addressing all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, trained on synthetic data and demonstrating generalization to real datasets with superior performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a single model capable of answering questions about causal structure, interventions, and counterfactuals from observational data, while efficiently using any available graph knowledge to boost predictive performance.

Method: Train a TabPFN-CFM on synthetic tabular datasets to learn mappings from data to both causal graphs (structure) and outcome predictions. The model can incorporate prior or known graph structure when available. It is designed to answer queries across Pearl's three levels (association, intervention, counterfactual) and is evaluated on its ability to generalize from synthetic to real data, showing improved performance over structural and outcome baselines.

Result: Empirical results show improved performance over both structural discovery baselines and outcome prediction baselines and evidence of generalization from synthetic datasets to real datasets.

Conclusion: TabPFN-CFM constitutes a versatile, data-efficient causal foundation model that integrates structure learning and outcome prediction, leverages available graph structure, and supports a range of causal queries across Pearl's hierarchy, with promising generalization capabilities.

Abstract: We introduce TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems. TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy and uses known graph structure when available to improve predictions. TabPFN-CFM is trained on synthetic datasets, and generalises to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines.

</details>


### [256] [Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26472)
*Steven Kolawole,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EpiKV introduces an epiphany score to evict KV cache tokens based on the change in the model's internal forward representations, avoiding attention matrices; no training, scales to longer contexts, competitive with attention-based baselines; claims up to 72% on MATH-500 and 2.8x speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce KV cache eviction bottlenecks in long-chain reasoning by using a robust, hardware-friendly metric that doesn't require attention matrices or extra training.

Method: Compute epiphany score from forward-pass internal representations (no attention matrix), use as eviction criterion; no training, no classifiers, no custom kernels; compatible with FlashAttention; yields 16x longer feasible context.

Result: At 4096-token cache, achieves 72% on MATH-500 (matching ThinKV baseline). AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens: 37% vs 33% best; up to 2.8x speed; scales to longer context.

Conclusion: EpiKV provides a training-free, deployment-friendly eviction metric that can replace attention-based methods, delivering competitive accuracy with substantial speedups and extended context viability.

Abstract: As reasoning models emit chains of thought tens of thousands of tokens long, KV cache increasingly becomes a deployment bottleneck. Existing cache eviction methods rank tokens by attention weight, which is a noisy importance proxy in long reasoning traces, and prohibits the use of fused kernels in production inference by forcing the model to materialize the attention matrix. In this work, we instead score tokens with a metric we term the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation, read directly from the forward pass with no attention matrix and negligible extra state. Our resulting cache eviction method, EpiKV, requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel, and can be used directly in FlashAttention inference stacks unchanged -- scaling to a 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. upper-mid layers negatively) and remove a positional trend with a causal rolling z-score. At a 4096-token cache EpiKV reaches 72% on MATH-500, matching the strongest attention-based baseline (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); a lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens against the best of them (33%), at up to 2.8x the speed.

</details>


### [257] [When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26473)
*Jaden Moon,Arvind Pillai,Andrew Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diagnostic test for reliance on modality reliability in multimodal fusion by permuting reliability scores across test samples; finds no effect on performance, suggesting reliability signals do not influence fused decisions unless they predict unimodal correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address whether reliability estimates used to weight modalities in multimodal systems causally affect inference or merely correlate with performance.

Method: Train model with fixed inputs; after training, fix model and inputs and permute reliability scores across test examples; evaluate if predictions degrade. Use StressID (stress recognition) and CMU-MOSEI (sentiment) with both random and positive-control reliability signals.

Result: Permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite potential gains from modality selection; in positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same fusion rules yield significant improvements.

Conclusion: Reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness; otherwise, reliability-based weighting may not affect inference, prompting reevaluation of fusion strategies and evaluation of reliability signals' predictive power.

Abstract: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

</details>


### [258] [Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26474)
*Andrii Shportko,Shubham Bhokare,Ahmed Zeyad A Alzahrani,Bowen Cheng,Gustavo Mercier,Jessica Hullman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) isolate RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling in Qwen2.5-3B and enable capability spillover to the base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the mechanistic basis of RL-induced agentic behavior (tool use) and assess whether identified features can enable retraining-free control.

Method: 38? Wait: It was 48-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep; encode-decode reconstruction; tool-calling accuracy gains; evaluate transfer to a frozen base model; analyze DFC partitioning.

Result: DFC isolates a compact RL-specific feature set; RL tool-calling accuracy improves by 31.1 ± 9.7 percentage points; spillover to the frozen base model yields 6.8 ± 5.0 percentage points across 48 hyperparameters.

Conclusion: DFC concentrates RL-induced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set enabling runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

Abstract: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

</details>


### [259] [Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26476)
*Libo Sun,Po-Wei Harn,Zewei Zhang,Peixiong He,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Five-arm ablation framework for RW-EBR diffusion with memory isolates three confounds (class-prior bias, stochastic warm-start, graph-aligned value reuse) and identifies the first blocking component per task. Strong alignment control yields large performance gains on graph reachability; failures shift to stored-value quality (deployable pipeline) or key quality (Sudoku). Adapts LLM-RAG diagnostic philosophy to structured spatio-temporal reasoning with diffusion samplers and trajectory memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentangle and diagnose the specific contributions of retrieval-guided, warm-started diffusion reasoning. Provide an explainable, task-agnostic diagnostic framework to identify where performance is blocked in diffusion-based reasoning with memory, especially in graph-structured and spatio-temporal settings.

Method: Introduce a five-arm ablation: oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned. Apply to a retrieval-warmed energy-based diffusion model (RW-EBR) with Modern Hopfield trajectory memory. Diagnostic evaluation on connectivity-2 ER graphs for reachability using a fixed diagnostic setup, adapting the LLM-RAG evaluation scheme.

Result: Aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing yields +35 percentage points in balanced accuracy on a fixed 1,000-graph validation set when only per-graph alignment is altered (value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed). Per-query random initialization underperforms cold, indicating alignment dominates over stochasticity or class-prior shifts. Deployable cold-prediction fails due to stored-value quality. Sudoku with a task-specific key encoder shows a different blocker: key quality. The framework identifies the first blocking component per task, situating the work in structured/spatio-temporal reasoning with iterated diffusion and retrieval-based explainability.

Conclusion: The five-arm diagnostic decomposition effectively isolates the primary bottleneck in warm-started diffusion with retrieval memory across tasks, guiding design choices (prioritizing correct per-graph alignment and stored-value quality) and enabling explainability of failure modes in structured reasoning pipelines.

Abstract: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

</details>


### [260] [What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26488)
*Pearse Jim,Steven Kolawole,Opegbemi Matthias Busoye,Glory Bagai,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compression preserves local cell-level accuracy but destroys global puzzle-level reasoning in recursive models; per-channel calibrated INT4 can recover puzzle-level accuracy without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable edge deployment of recursive reasoning models by aggressively compressing parameters while preserving essential reasoning capabilities; understand how quantization errors propagate in recursive cycles and which aspects survive.

Method: Perform a full-precision sweep across quantization levels on two recursive architectures over three tasks; evaluate with three measures: local cell accuracy vs puzzle-exact accuracy; test naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention; assess token-level objectives; introduce carry-trajectory fidelity as a label-free predictor; compare architectural sensitivity (MLP-mixing vs attention); propose a deployment recipe involving streaming embeddings and calibrated INT4.

Result: Under naive INT4 pruning, puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero while cell accuracy remains; token-level quantization-aware training cannot repair this; the collapse is architectural (affects MLP-mixing recursion but not attention). Per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining reverses the collapse. Carry-trajectory fidelity correlates with damage and recovery and can predict outcomes. Deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove bottlenecks; INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy with 6x fewer FLOPs on an 8MB SoC; calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

Conclusion: Quantization must account for recursive error propagation; naive token-level or global pruning can obliterate global reasoning. Architectural choices matter (attention resists some collapse). Calibrated per-channel INT4 is an effective, retraining-free remedy; carry-trajectory fidelity offers a practical label-free diagnostic for deployment readiness.

Abstract: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

</details>


### [261] [Learning Probabilistic Filters with Strictly Proper Scoring Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26497)
*Eviatar Bach,Ricardo Baptista,Jochen Bröcker,Bohan Chen,Andrew Stuart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PSEF trains a probabilistic, ensemble-based filter using synthetic trajectories to approximate the Bayesian filtering distribution with a transformer map and strictly proper scoring rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve accurate, probabilistically calibrated online state estimation for nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors by learning directly from data generated by a forecast model.

Method: Train a permutation-invariant transformer-based analysis map that takes a forecast ensemble and observations and outputs an analysis ensemble. Use strictly proper scoring rules (energy score) for training, with a realizability assumption and a finite-ensemble objective linked to the population objective via mean-field consistency.

Result: The learned filter closely matches challenging filtering distributions and outperforms classical methods and MSE-driven learning; for near-Gaussian problems a corrected EnKF is best, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach without inductive bias excels.

Conclusion: PSEF provides a data-driven, probabilistically calibrated ensemble filter capable of handling complex posterior distributions, with theoretical backing and empirical validation; the approach highlights when to bias toward EnKF corrections versus end-to-end learning.

Abstract: Bayesian filtering of partially and noisily observed dynamical systems seeks to infer the evolving conditional distribution of the state of a dynamical system, given observations, in an online fashion. This Bayesian filtering distribution is the natural object for uncertainty quantification, but it is rarely available as a supervised learning target. However, one can often use the forecast model to generate synthetic system trajectories, along with synthetic observations. We introduce the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), an ensemble data assimilation method based on training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution using only synthetic state--observation trajectories. The analysis step is represented as a permutation-invariant, transformer-based map that takes as input a forecast ensemble and observations, producing an analysis ensemble. Training is based on strictly proper scoring rules -- with the energy score used in our implementation -- so that probabilistic accuracy is rewarded over the whole probability distribution. We prove that, under a realizability assumption, the population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution. We also derive the finite-ensemble empirical objective used in training and relate its single state--observation trajectory form to the population objective, using a mean-field consistency argument. Numerical experiments show that the learned filter accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions, including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, and achieves stronger performance in data assimilation tasks than classical methods or learning-based methods with mean-squared-error objectives. For close-to-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to the EnKF is the best approach, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach that discards this inductive bias is superior.

</details>


### [262] [Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26520)
*Johnny Peng,Thanh Tung Khuat,Ellen Otte,Katarzyna Musial,Bogdan Gabrys*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive multi-path forecasting framework for bioprocesses using GB-Latent ODE with MP-JIT-FT and Raman fusion; achieves robust early-stage forecasts under sparse data by generating multiple plausible futures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biopharmaceutical bioreactor runs have sparse, irregular measurements and diverse operating conditions. Early multi-day forecasts are needed to adjust feeding and control, but similar early dynamics can diverge later; a multi-path, regime-aware approach could improve robustness.

Method: GB-Latent ODE augments latent ODE with variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck for sparse high-dimensional inputs. MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters local regimes, and tunes separate models per regime to produce multiple plausible paths with reconstruction-based confidence scores. Raman spectroscopy data are fused via a machine-learning soft sensor to provide pseudo-observations that enrich sparse measurements.

Result: Evaluated on 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs across 14 conditions. MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Local-divergence metrics show multi-path gains are strongest when locally similar prefixes diverge; Raman fusion improves performance when early dynamics predict later behaviour.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive, regime-aware, multi-path framework yields robust early-stage forecasts under sparse data and heterogeneity, with Raman fusion providing additional predictive power; gating and a mask-aware bottleneck improve learning under limited data.

Abstract: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

</details>


### [263] [Theory-Scale Auto-Formalization of Logics for Computer Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26525)
*Yuming Feng,Frederick Pu,One An,Osbert Bastani,Li Zhang,Jiani Huang,Xujie Si,Ziyang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LCS-Bench delivers a theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark for Logics for Computer Science, using a semi-automated pipeline to translate a large, interdependent theory corpus into Lean; provides five evaluation tracks and definitional equivalence checks, with state-of-the-art models far from human performance (~20% on tasks).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in theory-scale auto-formalization: existing work focuses on isolated statements and faces consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness challenges when scaling to hundreds of interdependent lemmas and theorems.

Method: Semi-automated agentic pipeline leveraging concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by human faithfulness review; outputs 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and 85k+ lines of Lean code; data engine derives five evaluation tracks; novel evaluation protocol uses definitional equivalence checkers.

Result: Artifact is high quality, consistent, and faithful; benchmark is challenging with state-of-the-art models achieving 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; evaluation across 14 models provides insights into theory-scale auto-formalization and theorem proving.

Conclusion: LCS-Bench provides a faithful, scalable theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark, enabling nuanced evaluation; findings suggest promising directions for future work and improvements in auto-formalization and theorem proving.

Abstract: Auto-formalization is critical for scalable formal verification, but existing progress largely focuses on isolated statements, while theory-scale auto-formalization, which coherently translates hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, remains open due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness. In this paper, we introduce LCS-Bench, a stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark based on Logics for Computer Science. LCS-Bench is built through a novel semi-automated agentic pipeline that leverages concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by faithfulness review from human experts. The resulting artifact covers 327 textbook items, over 4,076 Lean declarations, and more than 85K lines of Lean code. The dataset supports broad evaluation through a data engine that automatically derives five tracks of evaluation benchmarks, measuring different aspects of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities. We also introduce a novel evaluation protocol featuring definitional equivalence checkers, enabling more fine-grained and faithful assessment. Through extensive evaluation on 14 models, we demonstrate that (1) LCS-Bench is of high quality, consistent, and faithful; (2) the benchmark is challenging, with state-of-the-art models achieving only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; and (3) our analysis reveals key findings regarding theory-scale auto-formalization and suggests promising directions for future work.

</details>


### [264] [Sample-efficient Transfer Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Reward Shaping and Policy-Ratio Reweighting Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26527)
*Wenjie Huang,Yang Li,Jingjia Teng,Mingwei Jin,Kai Song,Yougang Bian,Yongfu Li,Qisong Yang,Helai Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A safe transfer RL framework for autonomous highway lane changing using adaptive teacher interventions, teacher-guided reward shaping, and weighted sample optimization; shows major safety gains and modest efficiency gains with theoretical guarantees and NGSIM validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address transfer mismatch due to distribution shifts between source/target tasks and safety concerns during exploration in lane-changing decisions.

Method: Three components: (1) adaptive teacher intervention based on instantaneous safety cost with fading strength; (2) teacher-guided safe transfer via reward shaping and decaying guidance; (3) teacher-guided weighted optimization using a likelihood ratio to weight samples; includes dual-source samples for joint training and theoretical return bounds for mixed policies; validated on NGSIM with varied traffic densities.

Result: Outperforms baselines by >52.2% in safety and >5.0% in learning efficiency; robust across traffic densities; policy safety improved via guided transfer and safe exploration. Real-world data validation on NGSIM supports efficacy and robustness, with dual-source sampling aiding transfer.

Conclusion: The proposed safety-aware transfer RL framework effectively mitigates transfer mismatch and unsafe exploration in autonomous highway lane changing, offering theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains across traffic scenarios.

Abstract: Transfer learning improves policy learning efficiency by reusing knowledge from source tasks, providing a feasible paradigm for safe and efficient autonomous highway lane changing decision-making. Existing methods frequently encounter transfer mismatch induced by distribution shifts between source and target domains, leading to training oscillation and performance decline. Besides, target domain adaptation depends on exploratory interactions, which struggles to guarantee training safety in safety-critical lane changing cases. To tackle these limitations, this paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing. First, we design an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and fade intervention strength progressively, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy. This intervention also produces dual-source samples for joint training. Second, a teacher-guided safe transfer module embeds action evaluation information of teacher policy into student learning via reward shaping to boost training safety and efficiency, with teacher guidance decaying as policy safety rises. Third, a teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance. Experiments under varied traffic densities and validations on real-world NGSIM dataset reveal that our method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency. Results verify the efficacy and robustness of our safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions.

</details>


### [265] [CascadeFormer: Depth-Tapered Transformers Motivated by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26538)
*Huzama Ahmad,Cao Viet Hai Nam,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two gradient-informed efficiency methods for deep Transformers: CascadeFormer (width taper by depth) and CascadeFlow Pruning (layer removal via accumulated gradients), grounded in Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA); demonstrated gains in latency/throughput and perplexity, with evidence across Transformers and ResNets and proposals for future 100B+ scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Transformer layers often provide diminishing returns; need training-budget-efficient architectures and pruning methods that do not require post-hoc analysis; propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) to explain depth-wise gradient distribution and guide design.

Method: CascadeFormer reduces width progressively with depth (tapered width) to align with uneven information flow; CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers during training based on accumulated gradients, avoiding post hoc analysis; GFA theory: in Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, a layer’s gradient is identity plus all downstream functional paths, yielding a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (quadratically under deep supervision); experiments on models from scratch up to 1.2B parameters; across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated gradients follow the theoretical fan-in; interventions show structure, not magnitude, is bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter sharing restores it.

Result: CascadeFormer achieves comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget, while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%; CascadeFlow Pruning outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and remains competitive on downstream accuracy; GFA supported by correlational and interventional evidence; accumulated training gradients align with theoretical fan-in; both methods demonstrate gradient-driven structural efficiency gains; two interventions highlight that structure governs late-layer contributions.

Conclusion: Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry provides a structural explanation for diminishing returns in deeper layers; efficiency gains are achievable by adjusting architecture and training dynamics (width taper and layer pruning) guided by gradient structure rather than magnitude alone; results generalize across Transformers and ResNets, with open questions about gradient magnitude as a proxy in very large models (100B+ scale) and the behavior of the dynamics at such scales.

Abstract: Deep Transformers are composed of uniformly stacked residual blocks, yet their deepest layers often add little value. We present two efficiency methods that exploit this asymmetry. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match the uneven information flow across layers, achieving comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, with no post hoc analysis. It outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. To motivate these methods, we propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a structural account of why deeper layers contribute less. In Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, the gradient at a layer is the sum of an identity path and all downstream functional paths, producing a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (and quadratically under deep supervision), yielding richer gradients for early layers and sparser ones for later layers. We provide correlational and interventional evidence for GFA on models trained from scratch up to 1.2B parameters. Across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated training gradients follow the theoretical fan-in and are associated with post hoc layer importance. Two interventions point to structure rather than magnitude as the bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition restores and elevates it. Whether gradient magnitude proxies fan-in beyond high-rank regimes, and how these dynamics behave at the 100B+ scale, remain open questions.

</details>


### [266] [Can Large Language Models Reliably Code Qualitative Humanitarian Data? A Benchmark Study Against Human Expert Adjudication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26541)
*Jerome Marston,Tino Kreutzer,Salomé Garnier,Ella Boone,Phuong N Pham,Patrick Vinck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 46 LLMs were benchmarked against a human gold standard on 150 synthetic humanitarian transcripts; some LLMs matched experienced coders with structured, reasoning-enabled prompts, but aggregate metrics are insufficient for deployment and governance requires tiered oversight and self-hosted, open-weight models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if LLMs can reliably code qualitative humanitarian data at scale, addressing staff/time constraints while ensuring safety and governance in sensitive humanitarian contexts.

Method: Benchmark study comparing 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria (discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, non-standard communication styles).

Result: Multiple LLMs achieved deductive coding reliability comparable to experienced human coders when using structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations. However, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing indirect needs, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns (physical safety, discrimination). Open-weights models on self-hosted infrastructure may balance scalability with stronger data governance.

Conclusion: LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity but should not replace human judgment. Effective use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focusing on high-consequence categories. For sensitive data, open-weight, self-hosted models may offer governance advantages.

Abstract: Data from affected populations are crucial for informing humanitarian response, but their value depends on timely and consistent interpretation of nuanced accounts of need. Humanitarian organizations often lack the staff, time, and specialist expertise required to analyze this information at scale. Large language models (LLMs) may expand this capacity, but their reliability for coding qualitative humanitarian data has not been directly established. This benchmark study compares 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria including discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, and non-standard communication styles. The authors find that multiple LLMs can perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially when structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations are used. At the same time, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing needs expressed indirectly, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns such as physical safety and discrimination. These findings suggest that LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity, but not as substitutes for human judgment. Appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focused on categories where miscoding would have the greatest programmatic consequences. For sensitive humanitarian data, open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer a viable path for combining analytical scalability with stronger data governance.

</details>


### [267] [Revisiting Action Factorization for Complex Action Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26574)
*Timothy Flavin,Sandip Sen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-sectional empirical study of hybrid-action factorization methods across PPO, SAC, DQN (220 configurations) and two new environments; introduces VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX; finds branching-dueling architectures balance compute and performance; Auto-Regressive actions perform best overall; continuous SAC strongest but computationally costly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill gap in benchmarking how different factorization schemes perform across action-space types and RL algorithms in lightweight, scalable environments; provide insights for choosing factorization strategies in hybrid-action control.

Method: Evaluate 220 configurations combining factorization methods (independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive) with algorithms (PPO, SAC, DQN) across action spaces (discretized, hybrid, continuous) in four environments (Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush); develop two new environments CoopPush and Hybrid-Shoot that are C++ parallel, Gymnasium/PettingZoo compliant to isolate inter-action dependencies; propose VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX with a branching critic; measure performance and compute cost.

Result: Branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance; Auto-Regressive actions achieve the highest performance overall; native continuous SAC outperforms discrete and hybrid methods but at increased computational cost; VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX outperform other PPO factorizations; results highlight the importance of factorization choice and reveal state-dependent inter-action dependence through the new environments.

Conclusion: For hybrid-action control, branching architectures (e.g., VDN, dueling, branching critics) offer favorable compute/performance trade-offs; Auto-Regressive action spaces yield peak performance; continuous SAC remains strong but costlier; the introduced environments and variants provide useful benchmarks for future research.

Abstract: Many real-world control problems involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. For example, steering and signaling in autonomous driving, and aiming and firing in robotics or video-games. Despite real-world hybrid factorization and reinforcement learning framework support for complex action spaces (e.g., Gymnasium, PettingZoo, TorchRL, SeedRL, Mujoco, etc), the default environments within those frameworks often implement uniform action space configurations (LunarLander, Walker2D, Cheetah, SMAC, SUMO, Ant, Atari). Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks (RoboCup 2D HFO, SC2LE, Platform, CARLA, etc) are mostly heavyweight or archival implementations originating from papers which test one or a small number of competing factorization methods on one kind of control. This article provides a cross-sectional study of factorization methods [independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive] on each of three families of algorithms [PPO, SAC, DQN] across three action spaces [discretized, hybrid, continuous] over four lightweight environments [Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush]. Accounting for some invalid pairings such as joint-continuous, we are left with 220 configurations to analyze each method. We provide two new C++ parallel gymnasium and petting-zoo compliant environments [CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot] to isolate particular challenges such as state-dependent inter-action dependence. Finally, we introduce VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX which use a branching critic to assign credit to multi-headed PPO. These variants out-perform all other tested PPO factorizations. Our results suggest that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively, with Auto-Regressive actions reaching the highest performance overall and native continuous SAC outperforming discrete and hybrid algorithms, albiet both at increased computational cost.

</details>


### [268] [SharQ: Bridging Activation Sparsity and FP4 Quantization for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26587)
*Haoqian Meng,Yilun Luo,Yafei Zhao,Wenyuan Liu,Huaqing Zheng,Xindian Ma,Peng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Train-free SharQ bridges N:M activation sparsity with FP4 quantization via an online sparse-dense decomposition to recover accuracy while enabling speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-bit FP4 formats and semi-structured (N:M) sparsity are promising for accelerators, but their combination for LLM activations is challenging due to input-dependent outliers dominating block scales in FP4 and the coupling of sparsification with quantization error. A calibration/training-free method that robustly merges these is needed.

Method: For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning.

Result: Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43–63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2–2.4× latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2–1.4× throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58× speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Provides code at the project URL.

Conclusion: SharQ offers a practical, training-free activation compression approach that effectively bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization, achieving substantial accuracy restoration and speedups with broad format generalization and no calibration or retraining requirements.

Abstract: Low-bit floating-point formats and semi-structured sparsity are increasingly supported by modern accelerators, yet combining them for LLM activation compression remains challenging: activations contain input-dependent outliers that dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and directly applying N:M sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error. We introduce SharQ, a training-free inference method that bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through an online sparse--dense decomposition. For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for both mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning. Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43--63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2--2.4$\times$ latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2--1.4$\times$ throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58$\times$ speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Our code is available at https://github.com/actypedef/SharQ.

</details>


### [269] [Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26590)
*Manar Alsaid,Chimdumebi Nebolisa,Faris Abbas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TerraProbe reveals that SSL/LSM-generated Terraform repairs often pass static checks but fail to be planning-consistent or behaviorally secure, showing high deceptive-fix rates and the need for intent-aware evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Security misconfigurations in Terraform IaC and the rising use of LLMs as automated repair agents; existing evals reward removal of findings without verifying plan validity or security intent.

Method: Proposes TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework, and applies it to 288 first-pass repairs from Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 injected-defect modules; includes a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes; IAM permission analysis; replication package; evaluation framework for intent-aligned repairs versus scanner-passing false successes.

Result: Targeted removal of checks (e.g., Checkov) overstates repair success (83.3% at primary model) but full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4%; Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6% and plan comparison for 38.5%; human adjudication finds 71.4% of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes; deceptive-fix rates 57.1–71.4% with p-values >0.10; taxonomy with Cohen’s kappa 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha 0.76; wildcard Resource grants persist in 9 CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases.

Conclusion: TerraProbe provides a robust evaluation methodology, a replication package, and a Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework to distinguish intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

Abstract: Security misconfigurations in Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code are a growing risk in cloud deployments, and large language models are increasingly used as automated repair agents. Existing evaluations often treat a repair as successful when the targeted static-analysis finding disappears, without checking planning validity, behavioral change, or security intent. This paper presents TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair. We apply TerraProbe to 288 first-pass repairs generated by gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 controlled injected-defect modules. The results show that targeted Checkov removal overstates repair success. Although targeted removal reaches 83.3 percent for the primary model, full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4 percent, Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6 percent, and plan comparison is reachable for 38.5 percent. Human adjudication further shows that 71.4 percent of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the underlying vulnerability in place. This pattern is statistically indistinguishable across the three models, with deceptive-fix rates from 57.1 percent to 71.4 percent and pairwise Fisher exact p-values above 0.10. The paper introduces a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes, validated with Cohen kappa of 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha of 0.76. IAM permission analysis confirms that wildcard Resource grants persist in all nine CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases. TerraProbe contributes an evaluation methodology, a replication package, and the Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for distinguishing intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

</details>


### [270] [Sketched Linear Contrastive Learning: Approximation, Optimization, and Statistical Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26617)
*Ziyan Chen,Zhongzhu Zhou,Ding-Xuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First explicit scaling law for sketched contrastive learning in a two-view Gaussian setup, showing how sketch size M, data size N, and effective optimization horizon L_eff γ govern risk; a cross-term is bounded and does not affect the upper-bound scaling, revealing distinct optimization-noise behavior from standard linear regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend scaling-law theory from sketch-based linear regression to contrastive representation learning, accounting for interactions between two views and the role of sketching in learning efficiency.

Method: Model the two correlated latent variables with paired Gaussian views, observe sketched views, and learn a bilinear contrastive score via full-batch empirical gradient descent. Use a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition. Derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term, and obtain an explicit scaling with M, N, and L_eff γ.

Result: Derives an explicit scaling law in terms of sketch dimension M, sample size N, and effective optimization horizon L_eff γ. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and does not alter the upper-bound scaling. Compared to standard linear regression, contrastive learning must account for interactions between two views, altering how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time.

Conclusion: This work provides the first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning under sketching, offering guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute, and laying groundwork for more general, two-view contrastive analyses.

Abstract: Scaling laws describe how learning performance varies with model size, data size, and compute. While recent theoretical work has established scaling laws for sketched linear regression, much less is understood for contrastive representation learning. In this paper, we study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes only sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score by full-batch empirical gradient descent. We analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, where we derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and therefore does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Our main theorem gives an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension $M$, sample size $N$, and effective optimization horizon $L_{\mathrm{eff}}γ$. Compared with standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, and this changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time. This provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and gives guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

</details>


### [271] [Discovering Millions of Interpretable Features with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26620)
*XinYang He,Wei Wang,Bing Zhao,Xuan Ren,WenBo Li,WeiXu Qiao,Hu Wei,Lin Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Qwen3-Instruct SAE: layer-wise sparse autoencoders trained on Qwen3 instruction-tuned models (1.7B/4B/8B) at multiple activation sites; evaluates sparsity–fidelity trade-offs and demonstrates causal steering toward refusal; provides resource for sparse representations in instruction-tuned LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain interpretable, sparse representations of superposed LLM activations, enabling analysis and potential behavioral interventions while reducing training cost relative to full models.

Method: Train SAEs at layer-wise activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, attention outputs for 1.7B/4B; subset of residual layers for 8B. Evaluate with activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics; apply refusal-steering case study to show causal influence.

Result: Found distinct sparsity–fidelity trade-offs across layers/components; SAEs can selectively recover information and can steer model behavior toward refusal in a controlled case.

Conclusion: Provides practical resources for studying sparse representations and feature-level control in instruction-tuned LMs; enables behavioral interventions and deeper mechanistic insights; potential for broader applications and further research.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful tool for decomposing superposed language model representations into sparse and interpretable features. However, training SAEs is computationally expensive, and available open-source SAE models remain limited. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Qwen3-Instruct SAE}, a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family, covering Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B, we train layer-wise SAEs at three key activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. For Qwen3-8B, we train SAEs on a subset of residual stream layers. We systematically evaluate these SAEs using both activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, revealing distinct sparsity--fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Qwen3-Instruct SAE through a refusal-steering case study, showing that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Our release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models

</details>


### [272] [From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26629)
*Evan Ning,Wei Xue,Dong Lou,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activating-space regularization via pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) improves continual learning in large language models by addressing polysemanticity; it beats EWC without replay and is memory-efficient because feature-space is lower-dimensional.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EWC-style weight-space regularization is coarse for large, polysemantic models; per-weight importance estimates fail to isolate concept-level knowledge, leading to catastrophic forgetting in LLMs.

Method: Use pretrained Sparse Autoencoders as a monosemantic feature dictionary to regularize in activation space. Derive a constrained optimization loss that balances stability and plasticity, showing EWC as a special case of one-sided weight-space penalty. Compute a compact SAE feature mask from current-task data; retain only the mask for future training; leverage lower-dimensional feature space for memory efficiency.

Result: On TRACE and MedCL benchmarks, achieves the strongest performance among methods without task-specific architectural components, outperforming traditional weight-space regularization like EWC; no need to store previous-task data beyond the mask.

Conclusion: Activation-space regularization with an SAE dictionary mitigates forgetting by selectively protecting semantically meaningful features, providing empirical evidence that task-relevant representations are linearly separable in SAE space but not in weight space, supporting the polysemanticity thesis.

Abstract: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

</details>


### [273] [Target-Aware Bandit Allocation for Scalable Surrogate Optimization in Chemical Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26657)
*Mohammad Haddadnia,Yuvan Chali,Abhilash Jayaraj,Constance Kraay,Joana Reis,Felix Strieth-Kalthoff,Haribabu Arthanari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BOBa introduces a bandit-guided surrogate optimization that partitions a vast discrete action space and allocates inference/evaluations across partitions to avoid full-library surrogate inference, enabling scalable virtual screening with a tunable trade-off between performance and cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-utility candidate identification in massive discrete spaces with expensive evaluations (e.g., structure-based drug discovery) where full-library surrogate inference is computationally prohibitive.

Method: Partition the action space into arms; model each partition's potential; use optimism-under-uncertainty bandits to allocate inference and evaluations adaptively to promising partitions, avoiding full-library surrogate training.

Result: Real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries show that combining OUU bandits with meaningful partitioning yields effective allocation of inference and evaluations; reveals a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, enabling practical optimization over ultra-large libraries.

Conclusion: BOBa offers a scalable route for ultra-large library virtual screening by bypassing full-library surrogate inference and focusing computation where it matters most, with potential applicability beyond drug discovery to other large discrete optimization problems.

Abstract: Identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces under expensive evaluations is a recurring challenge across the sciences, with structure-based drug discovery as a prominent example. While surrogate-based optimization can increase sample efficiency by reducing the number of expensive evaluations, modern molecular libraries have reached billions to trillions of compounds, making full-library surrogate inference itself a major computational bottleneck. We introduce BOBa, a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space. By treating partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit, BOBa concentrates inference and evaluations on empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration. Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits, combined with meaningful action space partitioning, are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. Our findings reveal a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, which supports practical optimization over current libraries, and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

</details>


### [274] [Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26662)
*Mingsong Yan,Zhida Wang,Sui Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNDEs on sparse graphs converge to Graphon-NDEs in the sparse graphon regime; uniform adjoint-gradient convergence; DTO/OTD discretization consistency; zero-shot transfer demonstrated experimentally on graphon classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To justify and quantify zero-shot size-transfer for continuous-time graph dynamics modeled by GNDEs, by deriving a rigorous infinite-node limit and convergence rates in sparse graphon settings.

Method: Introduce Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of GNDEs; prove well-posedness; establish trajectory-wise convergence of GNDEs to Graphon-NDEs with rate O((α_n n)^(-1/2)) (up to log factors) with high probability; obtain uniform-in-time convergence for adjoints; analyze discretize-then-optimize (DTO) vs optimize-then-discretize (OTD) under explicit Euler with M steps, showing O(1/M) and O(1/M^2) errors respectively (plus sparsity/log factors); validate with experiments on HSBM and tent graphons and zero-shot transfers across graphon classes.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: trajectory convergence to Graphon-NDE at rate O((α_n n)^(-1/2)) up to logs; uniform adjoint convergence; DTO/OTD consistency with discretization errors O(1/M) and O(1/M^2) (modulo sparsity/log terms). Empirical results align with rates; zero-shot deployment on larger graphs across graphon classes.

Conclusion: The zero-shot size-transfer principle for GNDEs is theoretically supported in the sparse graphon regime, with explicit convergence and discretization error rates; practical training and deployment guidelines are informed by the DOB: using fine Euler steps for DTO/OTD and expect the reported error scalings; experiments corroborate theory.

Abstract: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

</details>


### [275] [PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26666)
*Muhammad Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive, page-aware decode scheduling using a native KV-head grouped attention engine (PersistentKV) yields throughput gains over a single-kernel baseline by efficient work assignment and sequence-splitting across varying batch sizes and long-context regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV-cache movement dominates LLM serving latency; mixed sequence lengths and long-context can under-utilize GPUs; a fixed-kernel approach often fails to saturate hardware across workloads; aim to close the performance gap with adaptive scheduling.

Method: Develop PersistentKV: a native block-table decode attention engine that groups by KV-head, reuses K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and uses a compact workqueue that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. Introduce an adaptive policy that selects among FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for long-context B1, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for long-context B8. Calibration-based thresholds fix policy behavior; evaluation on RTX 3060 FP16 with page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, with correctness matched to FlashInfer.

Result: Calibrated policy yields 1.063–1.265× synchronized wall throughput improvements on B8 across bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads; 1.399× on B1 bucketed traces; on the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy selects FlashInfer to avoid regression.
