Metrics include accuracy of full pressure distribution and interpolation across Mach numbers and angles of attack; assess training complexity, stability, and hyperparameter sensitivity.

Result: KANs achieve good predictive performance for the full pressure coefficient and can interpolate across Mach number and AoA; however, their performance is marginally worse than well-trained MLPs and typically surpassed by GNNs at the cost of longer training. KANs often have lower model complexity and faster training but suffer from training instabilities and high sensitivity to hyperparameters.

Conclusion: KANs show potential for fluid dynamics surrogates but practical benefits are contingent on stable training and careful hyperparameter tuning; in current setups, MLPs (and especially GNNs) tend to outperform KANs, limiting their superiority claim.

Abstract: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) have recently been introduced as a (deep) neural network architecture whose trainable parameters adapt the activation functions, instead of the coefficients of the affine transformations at the core of traditional architectures such as deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). This architecture builds on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, which endows it with universal approximation properties. While the advent of KANs has been received with excitement, there is a current debate about the possible KAN supremacy over deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for classic fields such as symbolic regression, generic-purpose machine learning, natural language processing or computer vision. Here we assess the performance of KANs --and its nuanced comparison against MLPs and graph neural networks (GNNs)-- in the realm of fluid dynamics surrogate modelling. To that aim, we consider the task of predicting the surface pressure distribution over subsonic and transonic airfoils, a canonical task in aerodynamics. Our results show that KAN models show good performance in predicting the whole pressure coefficients and is able to interpolate across Mach numbers and angles of attack, however its performance is comparable --marginally inferior-- to a suitably trained MLP, where best performance is achieved by a GNN at the expense or requiring lengthier training. While the optimal KAN model have typically much lower complexity than MLP and GNN --hence resulting in faster training--, we find that KANs suffer from training instabilities, and their performance is highly dependent on a proper hyperparameter optimisation.

</details>


### [301] [fTNN: a tensor neural network for fractional PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27140)
*Qingkui Ma,Hehu Xie,Xiaobo Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deterministic tensor neural network subspace (fTNN) method is proposed for fractional Laplacian problems on bounded domains, with a geometry-adapted, fully deterministic integration scheme and boundary-singularity-aware trial functions, plus a spatiotemporally separable NN for time-dependent cases; it outperforms fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, especially for strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fractional PDEs with the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains present singular near-field and boundary-layer challenges that hinder accuracy of standard PINN/MC methods; a deterministic, geometry-aware integration and boundary-augmented trial space can improve accuracy and efficiency, including long-time dynamics.

Method: 1) Geometry-adapted integration split: near-field (singular) radius is spatially dependent; decompose operator into singular near-field, interior far-field, exterior far-field. 2) Radial integrals: singular part via Gauss-Jacobi, regular part via Gauss; angular via deterministic quadrature. 3) Boundary-singularity-aware trial functions with explicit boundary features. 4) Strategies to select leading exponent and evaluate loss from singularity structure (either by fractional operator alone or combined with source term). 5) For time-dependent problems: spatiotemporally separable NN that expresses residual as sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals; alternating neural network subspace optimization.

Result: Numerical experiments show high accuracy on benchmarks and substantial improvements over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

Conclusion: The fTNN framework provides a robust, deterministic, geometry-aware approach for fractional PDEs on bounded domains, delivering improved accuracy and efficiency, and offers a viable path for time-dependent fractional problems using a separable NN formulation and subspace optimization.

Abstract: We develop the fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for problems involving the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains, taking the fractional Poisson equation and time-dependent fractional advection-diffusion equation as typical representatives. The work employs a geometry-adapted integration split featuring a spatially dependent near-field radius, which decomposes the fractional Laplacian into three contributions: a singular near field, a regular interior far field, and an analytical exterior far field. Then the singular radial integrals are treated by Gauss-Jacobi quadrature, the regular radial integrals by Gauss quadrature, and the angular variables by deterministic angular quadrature, yielding a fully deterministic integration framework of the fractional Laplacian operator. To accurately resolve low-regularity solutions and the associated loss functional, we construct boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features, and propose two strategies for automatically selecting the leading exponent and evaluating the loss function from the singularity structure induced by the fractional operator, or jointly by the fractional operator and the source term. For time-dependent fractional PDEs, we design a spatiotemporally separable neural network that factorizes the time-space residual into a sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, and we integrate this representation with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy for efficient training. Numerical experiments show that the proposed framework attains high accuracy on the tested benchmarks and improves substantially over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

</details>


### [302] [Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27171)
*Jonne Pohjankukka,Jukka Heikkonen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A model-assisted sampling framework reframes mini-batch gradients as sample estimates from a fixed finite population, leveraging auxiliary gradient-prediction models to reduce variance and improve efficiency, with gains over standard estimators, especially for AdamW.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate gradient-noise-induced trade-offs in stochastic optimization while avoiding substantial computational overhead, by fusing machine learning optimization with survey sampling theory.

Method: Treat the dataset as a finite population; interpret mini-batch gradients as survey samples; introduce auxiliary gradient-prediction models to augment gradient estimators; uniform sampling is a special case with no auxiliary information; integrate seamlessly with existing optimizers without changing their update rules.

Result: Empirical results on synthetic data and six benchmarks show performance gains in 71-86% of experiments, notably for medium-sized input spaces; with momentum optimizers like AdamW, the estimator generalizes better in about half the training epochs compared to the baseline estimator.

Conclusion: The approach provides a modular, low-overhead improvement to gradient estimation that can be plugged into standard optimizers, potentially bridging ML optimization and survey sampling theory; effectiveness hinges on the quality of auxiliary predictions and scales to practical settings, with opportunities for deeper theoretical guarantees and broader benchmarking.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of variance in stochastic gradient estimation for machine learning optimization. Deep learning relies on mini-batch methods such as stochastic gradient descent, which approximate full gradients but introduce noise, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods, including variance reduction techniques (e.g., SVRG and SAG) and adaptive optimizers, aim to mitigate gradient noise but may introduce additional computational overhead. We propose a model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Our aim is to bridge machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. By incorporating auxiliary gradient-prediction models, we construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling arising as a special case when no auxiliary information is used. Our approach integrates easily with existing optimizers, improving efficiency without altering their dynamics. Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of the experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces in our benchmarks. Notably, with momentum-based optimizers such as AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves clearly better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline estimator.

</details>


### [303] [RecallRisk-BERT: A Multi-Task Framework for Post-Report Medical Device Recall Triage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27174)
*Ali Semih Atalay,Sevgi Yigit-Sert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-task model RecallRisk-BERT for FDA medical device recalls that predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and consolidated root-cause categories (9 classes) using text from recall narratives plus structured features; outperforms single-task baselines and shows high alignment between model-derived risk rankings and observed severity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address post-report recall triage challenges by jointly modeling recall severity and root-cause categories, leveraging both narrative text and structured categorical signals to improve regulatory decision support.

Method: Train and compare classical ML and boosting models for severity and root-cause prediction. Develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that fuses PubMedBERT representations of recall narratives with embeddings of structured features (product code, regulation number, medical specialty). Evaluate on 54,165 FDA medical device recall records (2002–Oct 2025). Tasks: severity (I/II/III) and root-cause category (9 classes). Metrics: accuracy, macro-precision, macro-recall, macro-F1, ROC-AUC. Single-task severity: LightGBM text-tabular; multi-task: RecallRisk-BERT vs PubMedBERT baseline.

Result: Single-task severity model (LightGBM text-tabular) achieved accuracy 0.963, macro-F1 0.856, ROC-AUC 0.974. In multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings correlated with observed severity (rho=0.983, p=1.936e-6).

Conclusion: Text-tabular learning with multi-task modeling can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and root-cause risk analysis; RecallRisk-BERT effectively leverages narrative text and structured signals to jointly predict severity and root-cause categories.

Abstract: Medical device recalls are a critical regulatory mechanism for protecting patient safety. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies mostly address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. We develop an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA, covering the period from 2002 to October 2025. We first evaluate classical machine learning and boosting-based models for recall severity and root-cause category prediction. We then develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that combines PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features, including product code, regulation number, and medical specialty. The model simultaneously predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and a consolidated root-cause category (9 classes). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. In single-task severity prediction, our LightGBM-based text--tabular configuration achieved the strongest performance, with an accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. In the multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the single-task PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6). These findings indicate that text--tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

</details>


### [304] [Explaining Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Feature-induced Information Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27201)
*Ping Xiong,Thomas Schnake,Klaus-Robert Müller,Shinichi Nakajima*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive attribution method for ETGNNs using Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) that analyzes the full information flow through event-associated variables, aided by a modular decomposition for complex architectures; evaluated on synthetic epidemic/social datasets and real political-event networks, outperforming prior explainers and yielding more interpretable explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability of Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) is limited. Existing explainers trace only contributions from event embeddings to outputs and neglect event-induced variables that mediate node interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

Method: Extend the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework with a modular decomposition to construct a relevance structure for ETGNNs. The approach quantifies information flow originating from event embeddings and passing through event-associated variables, ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order interactions between events.

Result: Empirical evaluation on two synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing and social dynamics) and a real-world political event network dataset shows the proposed method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches and yields more human-interpretable explanations (both qualitatively and quantitatively).

Conclusion: The proposed attribution framework provides a comprehensive, comparable, and interpretable account of information flow in ETGNNs by accounting for all event-related pathways, with modular decomposition enabling application to complex architectures and demonstrated effectiveness across diverse domains.

Abstract: Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of applications, including social network analysis, epidemic tracing, recommender systems, and political event forecasting. However, their increasing complexity poses significant challenges for explainability. Existing explanation methods focus only on a subset of the information flow within ETGNNs, typically tracing contributions from the event-related embeddings to the output. Consequently, they overlook the important pathways through event-induced variables, which mediate interactions between nodes and thereby play a central role in capturing long-range temporal dependencies. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel attribution method that analyzes the \emph{entire} information flow through all event-associated variables. Our method is built upon the recent Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, which enables explicit quantification of information flow originating from event embeddings as well as information flow passing through event-induced variables. It also ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order analysis of interactions between events. To handle the architectural complexity of ETGNNs, we extend the NRM framework with a modular decomposition procedure that facilitates the systematic construction of relevance structure for complex neural architectures. We evaluate our approach on two synthetic datasets for epidemic tracing and social dynamics, as well as a real-world dataset of political event networks. Our qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.

</details>


### [305] [Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27202)
*Abderaouf Bahi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey that unifies the design space of graph neural networks (GNNs), derives spectral/spatial formulations, and links expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, then applies this framework across 12 domains to reveal common patterns, deployment gaps, and domain-specific guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs are now applied across many domains, but there is a lack of a principled, cross-domain synthesis that ties theory to practice. The goal is to map the design space, understand when and why graph structure helps, and provide actionable guidance for practitioners and researchers.

Method: Construct a single, principled design space for GNNs, derive spectral and spatial formulations from first principles, and relate expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy. Evaluate twelve application domains by specifying graph-construction choices, costs, and dominant architecture families, while separating genuine gains from baselines artifacts. Analyze cross-domain patterns and discuss practical constraints (smoothing, squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, explainability) as ongoing design considerations.

Result: Across domains, recurring patterns emerge: heterophily and scale challenge many models; temporal graphs are harder than static ones; top leaderboard architectures often do not translate to deployment. The survey distinguishes true performance gains from dataset/baseline artifacts, maps architecture families to domain needs, and highlights where costs of graph construction matter. It also emphasizes that adoption depends on constraints beyond accuracy, such as robustness, fairness, and explainability, and identifies gaps between theory, public benchmarks, and real-world deployment.

Conclusion: Effective adoption of GNNs requires accounting for practical constraints (robustness, distribution shift, fairness, explainability) and recognizing that leaderboards do not always reflect deployment success. A unified theoretical-practical framework enables domain-specific guidance on graph construction and architecture choice, while revealing persistent challenges (heterophily, scale, temporal dynamics) that future work must address.

Abstract: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

</details>


### [306] [The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27242)
*John Sweeney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes FisherSketch for head-level transfer analysis in LLMs with shared vocabularies; shows head Fisher alignment captures activation–error coupling and enables streaming, scalable source selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In scientific string domains (e.g., SMILES, proteins, genomes), candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime where representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, and classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale.

Method: Derives that head Fisher alignment is the cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation–error space; avoids full Fisher-matrix computation. Introduces FisherSketch, a streaming estimator that computes this cosine in a single pass. Supports K=128/256 head alignments with a compact 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and 192 KB per-task streaming state, enabling practical storage alongside model hashes. Provides activation, error, and coupling marginals for diagnostic use and source selection; validated on tasks including Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments.

Result: FisherSketch yields informative transfer metrics where activation similarity (e.g., CK A) may be non-identifiable; produces compact, task-relevant signatures that scale to vocabulary-sized heads; enables reliable source selection and diagnostics distinguishing whether similarity arises from activations, errors, or their coupling.

Conclusion: FisherSketch delivers a practical, storage-efficient diagnostic and transfer metric for shared-output-head LLMs, enabling principled source selection and deeper task understanding through activation–error coupling analysis.

Abstract: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

</details>


### [307] [Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27246)
*Andrew Bond,Zafer Doğan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A solvable high‑dimensional GAN model with class-dependent, correlated, non-zero-mean latent structure extends diagonal unconditional analyses. The stochastic training dynamics converge to deterministic ODEs driven by an effective covariance, revealing a phase diagram with a signal-boosting mechanism: low-rank correlations help learn weak directions while overly strong correlations destabilize recovery. Experimental validation includes MNIST, FashionMNIST, CIFAR-10 and shows informed generator covariance improves alignment with the target subspace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalize solvable GAN analyses beyond unconditional, diagonal latent covariances to encompass structured, class-dependent, and non-zero-mean latent statistics. Understand how such heterogeneity affects training dynamics, stability, and learnability in high dimensions.

Method: Derive the high-dimensional limit of GAN training with a quadratic energy discriminator. Show that the microscopic stochastic dynamics converge to deterministic ODEs governed by an effective second moment (a probability-weighted aggregation of latent covariances). In the matched-covariance case, perform a mode-wise stability analysis to obtain an interval of learning rates and noise levels for which learning occurs and full recovery is achieved.

Result: The dynamics converge to ODEs in the large-dimension limit. A phase boundary is derived that delineates learning onset and full recovery. A signal-boosting mechanism is identified: low-rank correlations can elevate weak modes into learnability, whereas excessive correlations destabilize recovery. Numerical simulations corroborate the ODEs and phase diagram.

Conclusion: Structured latent covariance substantially modulates GAN learnability and stability: appropriate design of generator covariance can boost alignment with the data subspace, while excessive structure or correlation can hinder recovery. The results highlight a principled way to exploit latent structure to improve generative modeling and subspace discovery.

Abstract: We study a solvable high-dimensional model of generative adversarial network (GAN) training in which a linear generator learns a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; we extend the multi-feature discriminator setting to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure. For the quadratic energy discriminator, all such heterogeneity enters the dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment. We prove that the stochastic microscopic training process converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to deterministic ordinary differential equations governed by this effective covariance. In the matched-covariance specialization, the stability analysis yields a mode-wise solvable interval determined by the learning rates and noise level: learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses the lower threshold, while full recovery requires all relevant effective modes to remain within the interval. This reveals a signal-boosting mechanism: low-rank correlations can lift weak directions above the learnability threshold, whereas overly strong correlations destabilize recovery. Numerical simulations validate the ODE, phase boundary, and boosting mechanism. Experiments on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 further show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with the data-driven reference subspace.

</details>


### [308] [RSPC: A Benchmark for Modeling Stress and Psychiatric Conditions in Digitally Mediated Relationships using Psychiatrist Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27247)
*Parmitha Vangapandu,Sai Ganesh Mokkapati,Sathwik Narkedimilli,MSVPJ Sathvik,Timothy Liu,Simon See,Johannes C. Eichstaedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC), a dataset of 1,799 Reddit posts annotated for psychiatric disorders, relational triggers, and relationship phase; benchmarks multiple LLMs and transformers on disorder classification, trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction; finds model-dependent strengths (Claude-3-Haiku for disorders, GPT-4o for triggers) and links anxiety with chronic relational uncertainty; advocates context-aware, relationally informed mental health NLP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in NLP and mental health research that treats distress in isolation; proposes incorporating interpersonal context and temporal dynamics through a relationally annotated corpus to support multi-task evaluation and improve ecological validity.

Method: Constructed corpus from Reddit posts about long-distance relationships; psychiatrists annotated posts for diagnostic categories, relational stressor triggers, and relationship phase; evaluated seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models across three tasks: multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction.

Result: Claude-3-Haiku achieved best disorder classification Macro-F1 = 0.538; GPT-4o achieved strongest relational trigger detection Macro-F1 = 0.519; observed task-dependent differences between model families; identified associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty; established RSPC as a benchmark for context-aware mental health NLP.

Conclusion: The study advances context-aware modeling of mental health by incorporating relational context and temporal dynamics, providing a benchmark dataset and demonstrating distinct model capabilities; suggests a shift from isolated, user-centric analyses to social-contextual approaches.

Abstract: In NLP, mental health conditions are often modeled as isolated phenomena, without interpersonal context. We use Reddit posts about long-distance relationships to capture both mental health distress and associated relational triggers. We introduce the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) containing 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories, including the most prevalent mood disorders (anxiety and depression), relational stressor triggers, and indications of relationship phase. We benchmark seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models across multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction tasks. We find clear task-dependent differences between model families, with Claude-3-Haiku achieving the best disorder classification performance (Macro-F1 = 0.538) and GPT-4o obtaining the strongest relational trigger detection performance (Macro-F1 = 0.519), suggesting distinct model capabilities. We further find strong associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty. Overall, RSPC establishes a benchmark for NLP tasks that consider relational context and supports a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling that captures the social and temporal dynamics of distress.

</details>


### [309] [BetXplain: An Explanation-Annotated Dataset for Detecting Manipulative Betting Advertisements on Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27274)
*MSVPJ Sathvik,Parmitha Vangapadu,Nishit Rane,Sathwik Narkedimilli,Mark Lee,Akrati Saxena*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a new annotated dataset of betting advertisements from Instagram and Reddit, with manipulative/deceptive labels and explanatory rationales, enabling explainable detection and regulatory tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of publicly available annotated data for manipulative and deceptive betting ads and the potential harms to users' decision-making and mental well-being.

Method: Collect betting-related ads from Instagram and Reddit; perform manual annotation for manipulative/deceptive practices; provide explanations for annotations; conduct analysis of persuasive strategies and discuss applicability to tools like warnings and regulatory crawlers.

Result: A dataset of annotated betting ads including explanations; descriptive analysis of persuasive strategies; a proposed framework for warning plugins and automated monitoring, with potential for explainable AI approaches.

Conclusion: The dataset and analysis enable explainable detection of manipulative betting advertising and practical applications for user protection and regulatory oversight; sets groundwork for future modeling and evaluation of explanations.

Abstract: The promotion of betting applications on social media platforms has increased significantly in recent years. Many of these advertisements use persuasive techniques that may mislead users, encourage risky behavior, and potentially influence users' mental well-being. However, research on the automated detection of manipulative and deceptive betting advertisements remains limited due to the lack of publicly available annotated datasets. In this work, we introduce a new dataset of betting-related advertisements collected from two widely used social media platforms, Instagram and Reddit. The advertisements were manually annotated for manipulative and deceptive advertising practices. In addition to classification labels, the dataset includes human-provided explanations that describe the reasoning behind each annotation, enabling research into explainable approaches to detecting manipulative advertising. Furthermore, we analyze the strategies commonly used in betting advertisements and examine how these persuasive tactics may impact users' mental health. The proposed framework can also enable practical applications such as browser plugins that warn users about manipulative betting advertisements and automated web crawlers that help regulatory authorities monitor and detect such promotions online.

</details>


### [310] [How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27282)
*Lang Huang,Jinglue Xu,Luke Darlow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tuning preprocessing with Ridge regression—context length, normalization, regularization, and augmentation—closes much of the accuracy gap to larger time-series models, outperforming baselines on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the assumption that larger architectures are necessary for accuracy and investigate whether cost-efficient preprocessing can achieve similar gains.

Method: Ridge regression with a closed-form solution; grid search over context length, local normalization, regularization strength, and data augmentation across eight standard time-series benchmarks; compare to Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines; interpret weights to read optimal hyperparameters.

Result: Three main patterns emerge: (1) optimal lookback is highly series-specific and often non-monotonic with horizon; (2) normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context is generally preferred; (3) optimal cross-series sharing varies by dataset, from fully shared to fully per-series. The optimized models outperform prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon pairs and beat Transformer/MLP/CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks; the hyperparameters also diagnose data structure not readily captured by larger models.

Conclusion: Smarter preprocessing can largely close the gap with far less compute than scaling up models, and the resulting hyperparameters reveal dataset structures that larger models tend to absorb into their parameters.

Abstract: Time-series forecasting research has been moving steadily toward larger architectures, from specialized transformers to general-purpose foundation models, on the assumption that capacity is what unlocks accuracy. We take the opposite position: most of the gap can be closed at far lower cost by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling models. We use Ridge regression as the testbed, since it has a closed-form solution and interpretable weights, which let the optimal hyperparameters be read off the search directly. We search over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation on eight standard benchmarks and find three patterns. (1) Optimal lookback is strongly series-specific and often non-monotonic in forecast horizon, with fitted power-law exponents ranging from $+0.46$ on ETTm2 to $-0.19$ on Exchange and Traffic, challenging the convention that longer horizons need longer history. (2) Normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context, rather than its entirety, is almost universally preferred. (3) Series within the same dataset often disagree on hyperparameters; the optimal degree of cross-series sharing varies from fully shared to fully per-series. The resulting models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks. The optimized hyperparameters also serve as a diagnostic on the data itself, revealing structures that larger models absorb silently into their learned parameters.

</details>


### [311] [Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27285)
*Yang Pan,Helmut Bölcskei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Hausdorff distance on solution sets to quantify identifiability and sample complexity of learning ODEs from solution data; derives identifiability bounds and metric entropy across linear and Lipschitz/Hölder nonlinear classes, enabling quantitative data requirements for recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Theoretical gap: conditions for unique and stable identification of ground-truth ODEs from solution data are underdeveloped; lacking quantitative sample complexity.

Method: Define Hausdorff distance between solution sets as the comparison metric; derive identifiability bounds for various ODE classes; compute metric entropy; analyze sample complexity bounds.

Result: Identifiability conditions and distinguishability criteria; metric entropy estimates; explicit sample complexity bounds as function of problem parameters and observation count.

Conclusion: Provides a minimax-grounded framework for governing equation discovery with quantified data requirements; extends from linear to Lipschitz/Hölder nonlinear ODEs; lays groundwork for sample-efficient learning of governing equations.

Abstract: Learning governing equations from observed solution data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning \cite{bruntonDiscoveringGoverningEquations2016,kovachkiNeuralOperatorLearning2023,longPDENetLearningPDEs2018,rudyDatadrivenDiscoveryPartial2017,raonicConvolutionalNeuralOperators2023}, yet the theoretical conditions under which a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations remain largely undeveloped, and no quantitative analysis of the sample complexity of such learning tasks exists in the literature. To address this gap, we introduce the Hausdorff distance on solution sets as the natural metric for comparing differential equations, since it captures the worst-case separation between two equations over all admissible initial conditions and thus encodes the minimax structure of the identification problem. We establish identifiability bounds for governing ODEs across a wide class of structure equations--ranging from linear ODEs to nonlinear classes with Lipschitz (Hölder)-continuous vector fields--characterizing precisely when two distinct equations can be distinguished from solution data. Using this metric, we derive metric entropy estimates for the relevant ODE classes and analyze sample complexity bounds, quantifying how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equation.

</details>


### [312] [Designing Reward Signals for Portable Query Generation: A Case Study in Industrial Semantic Job Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27291)
*Ping Liu,Qianqi Shen,Jianqiang Shen,Wenqiong Liu,Rajat Arora,Yunxiang Ren,Chunnan Yao,Dan Xu,Baofen Zheng,Wanjun Jiang,Andrii Soviak,Kevin Kao,Jingwei Wu,Wenjing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLAIF-based portable job-search queries are studied; critic-free optimizers rely on reward shaping, while GRPO is vulnerable to reward hacking; a deterministic reward floor stabilizes training; training-time reward model drives large gains, underscoring reward-design over optimizer choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-bandwidth, high-dimensional candidate profiles require abstractions; current LLM-based evaluation can be adversarial, corrupting policy learning; aim to isolate optimization mechanics from reward engineering and ensure portable, generalizable queries.

Method: Empirical study comparing critic-free optimizers (RLOO, REINFORCE++) and GRPO on a task to generate portable job-search queries; evaluate with/without a deterministic reward-floor; analyze impact of training-time reward model on performance across cross-family judges.

Result: Performance under critic-free methods is dominated by reward shaping; GRPO is uniquely vulnerable to spurious rewards; introducing a rule-based reward floor reduces verbatim-copying exploits and yields a +0.147 quality improvement; training-time reward model inflates gains by about 2.4x.

Conclusion: Reward design and shaping discipline are crucial for robust learning in adversarial LLM-evaluated settings; stabilizing rewards can outperform optimizer changes; preventing reward-hacking and verbatim copying is essential for generalizable, portable policy generation.

Abstract: Job-search platforms rely on low-bandwidth query interfaces that often fail to capture the high-dimensional complexity of candidate profiles. We present an end-to-end RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) framework to generate \emph{portable} job search queries, terms that abstract away seeker-specific identifiers while preserving generalizable qualifications. This task introduces a highly adversarial reward surface where policy optimization frequently exploits flaws in LLM-as-judge rubrics, resulting in degenerate verbatim-copying behaviors.
  We conducted comprehensive empirical experiments to isolate the impact of optimization mechanics against structured reward engineering. Our results demonstrate that for critic-free optimizers, performance is overwhelmingly dictated by robust reward shaping, rendering the specific choice of algorithm largely immaterial. While critic-free per-rollout baseline methods (RLOO and REINFORCE++) natively resist reward-hacking, the group-relative advantage normalization in GRPO appears uniquely sensitive to spurious reward signals, making it disproportionately susceptible to exploitation. We show that introducing a deterministic, rule-based reward floor to correct for rewards assigned to verbatim copying mitigates this failure mode, resulting in a substantial $+0.147$ quality improvement on a cross-family evaluation judge. Ultimately, we show that the training-time reward model inflates performance gains by $2.4\times$, confirming that the training success is fundamentally dependent on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than selecting alternative optimizers.

</details>


### [313] [A Multi-Fidelity Convolutional Autoencoder-Transfer Learning Framework for Guided-Wave-Based Damage Diagnosis Using Large Simulated and Limited Experimental Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27304)
*Santosh Kapuria,Abhishek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-efficient, multifidelity transfer learning framework using CAE-based deep features and lightweight simulations for damage localisation and sizing in plate-like GWSHM, achieving high R^2 ( localisation >0.93; sizing >0.99 ) and good generalisation, outperforming CNN baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the scarcity of labelled experimental data and the high cost of generating large high-fidelity simulations in guided-wave SHM, enabling accurate, computationally efficient damage assessment.

Method: Pretrain a CAE-based feature extractor on a large synthetic dataset generated by a lightweight 1D time-domain spectral element model. Apply transfer learning to adapt to limited experimental data using a small labelled set. Compare to a CNN baseline; evaluate on localisation and sizing of damage in plate structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers.

Result: CAE-based transfer learning significantly outperforms CNN-based approaches in localisation accuracy. R^2 > 0.93 for damage localisation and >0.99 for damage sizing. Demonstrates strong generalisation to unseen damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed multifidelity CAE-based transfer learning framework is accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable for real-world GWSHM applications, effectively leveraging synthetic data to compensate for scarce labelled measurements.

Abstract: Guided wave-based structural health monitoring (GWSHM) with onboard transducers offers significant potential for the early diagnosis of damage in engineering structures. However, the practical deployment of deep learning models is often hindered by the limited availability of labelled experimental data and the high computational cost of generating large-scale high-fidelity simulation datasets. This study presents a multifidelity transfer learning framework that integrates lightweight physics-based simulations, convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based deep feature learning, a feed-forward neural network, and limited experimental measurements for accurate damage localisation and sizing in plate-like structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers. A computationally efficient one-dimensional time-domain spectral element model is employed to generate a large synthetic dataset for pretraining, while transfer learning adapts the model to experimental domains using only a small amount of labelled data. The CAE-based transfer learning framework significantly outperforms its CNN-based counterpart in damage localisation accuracy. The model achieves excellent predictive performance with $R^2$ scores exceeding 0.93 for damage localisation and 0.99 for damage sizing. Its generalisation capability is demonstrated on previously unseen data, showing high prediction accuracy for damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning. The results establish the proposed framework as an accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable solution for real-world GWSHM applications.

</details>


### [314] [Blackwell Approachability and Gradient Equilibrium are Equivalent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27315)
*Brian W. Lee,Nika Haghtalab,Michael I. Jordan,Ryan J. Tibshirani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEQ is algorithmically equivalent to Blackwell approachability, and thus equivalent to regret minimization and calibration; efficient reductions enable transfer of refined guarantees; the work also maps GEQ across unconstrained/constrained decision sets with necessary and sufficient conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify GEQ's theoretical position in online learning by relating it to foundational frameworks (Blackwell approachability, regret minimization, calibration) and enable cross-channel transfer of guarantees.

Method: Establish bidirectional, algorithmic reductions between GEQ queries and Blackwell approachability; leverage known equivalences among approachability, regret, and calibration to show GEQ equivalence; derive conditions and construct reductions between unconstrained and constrained decision-set variants of GEQ.

Result: GEQ is shown to be algorithmically equivalent to Blackwell approachability; a Blackwell problem can be solved via GEQ queries with no asymptotic loss in the oracle's error rate, and vice versa. Consequently GEQ is equivalent to regret minimization and calibration; the reductions are efficient and enable transferring guarantees such as optimism and strong adaptivity from regret to GEQ. The work also identifies necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ and provides reductions between GEQ notions under different decision-set constraints.

Conclusion: GEQ occupies the same core online-learning landscape as regret minimization and calibration, now with a precise, efficient bridge to Blackwell approachability. This unifies GEQ with key frameworks and enables refined guarantees to flow into GEQ-based methods.

Abstract: Gradient equilibrium (GEQ) is a recently introduced online optimization framework that generalizes first-order stationarity from offline optimization and abstracts problems like online conformal prediction. While GEQ has curious similarities with known online learning frameworks, namely regret minimization, prior work has shown that GEQ error and regret are incomparable objectives, leaving open a precise understanding of how GEQ fits into the broader online learning landscape. In this work, we show that GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability in the algorithmic sense. That is, a Blackwell approachability problem can always be solved using queries to a black-box GEQ oracle, with no asymptotic loss in the oracle's error rate, and vice versa. Taken together with known equivalences between approachability, regret minimization, and calibration, these results imply that GEQ is equivalent to these frameworks, as well. Our reductions are efficient and can be used to transfer refined guarantees, such as optimism and strong adaptivity, from regret minimization to GEQ. Along the way, we also identify necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ, and establish reductions between different notions of GEQ with unconstrained and constrained decision sets.

</details>


### [315] [Beyond the Hard Budget: Sparsity Regularizers for More Interpretable Top-k Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27321)
*Nathanaël Jacquier,Maria Vakalopoulou,Mahdi S. Hosseini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two new sparsity regularizers for Top-k sparse autoencoders improve semantical disentanglement without hurting reconstruction. They operate on pre-Top-k activations and target batch-active units: (i) an L1 penalty on off-support units, and (ii) a scale-invariant L1/L2 ratio penalty. They complement the hard Top-k sparsity, improving monosemanticity, robustness to k, and small-budget probing across datasets and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of Top-k SAEs (fixed budget k, potential overfitting to a specific k, and limited monosemanticity) by introducing soft sparsity regularizers that act before the Top-k selection to further disentangle representations without sacrificing reconstruction.

Method: Introduce two regularizers applied to batch-active units prior to Top-k selection: (a) an L1 penalty on unselected (off-support) units, and (b) a scale-invariant L1/L2-ratio penalty that concentrates information onto fewer active units. Regularizers are applied only to the batch-active units (those selected by Top-k at least once in the batch). Evaluate across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of k.

Result: Both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity without reducing reconstruction quality. The L1/L2 penalty also concentrates information into fewer latents, improving reconstruction robustness to k and enhancing small-budget linear probing.

Conclusion: Hard architectural sparsity (Top-k) and soft sparsity regularization are complementary; combining them yields better semantic disentanglement and robustness without sacrificing reconstructive performance.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a leading tool for interpreting the representations of vision foundation models, decomposing their polysemantic activations into a larger set of sparse, more monosemantic features. The Top-$k$ SAE, a now-standard variant, enforces sparsity architecturally through its activation function, retaining only the $k$ most active latents per input. Because it was designed precisely to avoid the $\ell_1$ penalty used by earlier SAEs and its known drawbacks, it has not been combined with an explicit sparsity regularizer, despite retaining limitations of its own, such as a budget $k$ that is fixed regardless of input complexity and a tendency to overfit to the training value of $k$. We introduce two sparsity regularizers compatible with the Top-$k$ architecture, both acting on the activations before the Top-$k$ selection: an $\ell_1$ penalty on the unselected (off-support) units, and a scale-invariant $\ell_1/\ell_2$-ratio penalty that concentrates the code onto fewer effective units. Both penalties are applied only to the batch-active units, those selected by the Top-$k$ operator at least once within the batch. Across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of $k$, both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality. The $\ell_1/\ell_2$ penalty further concentrates information into fewer latents, making reconstruction more robust to the inference-time choice of $k$ and improving small-budget linear probing. Our central finding is that hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

</details>


### [316] [Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27354)
*Haina Jiang,Liam Wang,Peng-Chen Chen,Min Seop Kwak,Seungryong Kim,Brian Bell,Jeong Joon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS) use the PDE residual field as input to an iterative update policy, rather than optimizing the residual. They outperform residual-minimization hybrids in accuracy, generalize under distribution shift, and avoid expensive optimizers, with up to 10× gains on turbulent flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural surrogates are fast but struggle to satisfy PDE constraints and to extrapolate beyond training. Minimizing PDE residuals can be a fragile proxy in ill-conditioned systems, motivating a method that uses residual structure directly to guide updates.

Method: Train a neural solver that receives the PDE residual field as input at each iteration and learns an update policy to iteratively correct predictions. Evaluate across four PDE families, comparing ENS against residual-based hybrids (gradient descent / Gauss–Newton) and baselines, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer tests.

Result: ENS achieves the highest prediction accuracy in the majority of settings, with up to ∼10× gains on turbulent Kolmogorov flow. It avoids the compute cost of hybrid methods and generalizes under distribution shift (zero-shot parameter changes, cross-equation transfer), with its advantage strongest in ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is unreliable.

Conclusion: Using the residual field as direct input enables robust, efficient corrections and better generalization than residual-minimization-based hybrids. ENS provides a scalable, accurate alternative for PDE-solving surrogates across diverse PDE families.

Abstract: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

</details>


### [317] [Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27361)
*Danyal Rehman,Charlie B. Tan,Yoshua Bengio,Avishek Joey Bose,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) replace flow-based BGs with autoregressive modelling to overcome expressivity and likelihood constraints, enabling sequential inference-time interventions and scalable architectures akin to LLMs. Empirically improves sampling across benchmarks, notably for larger peptides (Chignolin) and 8-residue systems, with Robin (132M params) achieving >60% reduction in E-W2 energy error; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient Boltzmann sampling remains challenging with flow-based BGs due to invertibility constraints and expensive likelihoods. An autoregressive approach could provide greater expressivity, allow sequential interventions at inference, and leverage scalable architectures to improve performance on larger molecular systems.

Method: Introduce Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) that depart from normalizing flows. Use autoregressive modeling to sample and compute likelihoods without strict invertibility constraints, enabling sequential inference-time interventions. Scale with architectures inspired by Large Language Models; train a transferable large model (Robin, 132M params) within the ArBG framework.

Result: ArBG yields significant performance gains over flow-based BGs across benchmarks, with notable improvements on larger peptides (e.g., 10-res Chignolin). Robin achieves a >60% reduction in zero-shot energy error E-W2 on 8-residue systems, surpassing previous state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: Autoregressive BGs provide a scalable, high-expressivity alternative to normalizing-flow BGs, enabling sequential inference and improved sampling accuracy on molecular systems. The framework achieves strong empirical gains and includes a transferable 132M-parameter model, with code available.

Abstract: Efficient sampling of molecular systems at thermodynamic equilibrium is a hallmark challenge in statistical physics. This challenge has driven the development of Boltzmann Generators (BGs), which allow rapid generation of uncorrelated equilibrium samples by combining a generative model with exact likelihoods and an importance sampling correction. However, modern BGs predominantly rely on normalizing flows (NFs), which either suffer from limited expressivity due to strict invertibility constraints (discrete time) or computationally expensive likelihoods (continuous time). In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) -- a novel autoregressive modelling framework -- that overcomes these limitations by departing from the flow-based BG paradigm. ArBG circumvents the topological constraints of flows and enables sequential inference-time interventions, while offering enhanced scalability by leveraging architectures effective in Large Language Models. We empirically demonstrate that ArBG leads to significant improvements over flow-based models across all benchmarks, but particularly in larger peptide systems such as the 10-residue Chignolin. Furthermore, we introduce Robin, a 132 million parameter transferable model trained with the ArBG framework which improves over the previous state-of-the-art, reducing the zero-shot energy error, E-W$_2$, on 8-residue systems by over 60$\%$. The code can be found at the following link: https://github.com/danyalrehman/autobg.

</details>


### [318] [Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27369)
*Yingyu Lin,Qiyue Gao,Nikki Lijing Kuang,Xunpeng Huang,Kun Zhou,Tongtong Liang,Zhewei Yao,Yi-An Ma,Yuxiong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RiVER is a framework for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for LLM training using deterministic execution feedback, enabling score-based optimization without ground-truth solutions. It addresses scale and frequency dominance via calibrated reward shaping and instance-wise comparisons. Trained on 12 AtCoder tasks, it improves ALE rating for Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 and also boosts exact-solution benchmarks (LiveCodeBench, USACO) with modest absolute gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth-based rewards limit applicability of RL for training LLMs on tasks where solutions are unknown. Score-based, verifiable feedback from deterministic execution offers a scalable supervision signal. However, applying group-relative RL to continuous rewards introduces scale dominance (varying score magnitudes distort updates) and frequency dominance (repeated suboptimal samples bias results).

Method: RiVER introduces a Ranking-Induced Verifiable framework that uses instance-wise calibrated reward shaping. It emphasizes top-ranked solvers while keeping bounded feedback for other valid solutions, enabling training on score-based tasks without ground-truth solutions. The approach is evaluated by training on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks and testing on ALE-Bench, LiveCodeBench, and USACO, iterating on backbones such as Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414.

Result: RiVER yields an 8.9% and 9.4% improvement in ALE rating rank for Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414, respectively. It also improves backbones on exact-solution benchmarks with absolute averages of 2.4% (LiveCodeBench) and 3.5% (USACO). Baselines trained with raw execution scores improve ALE but do not transfer to exact-solution benchmarks.

Conclusion: Score-based optimization tasks, when paired with proper reward calibration, can effectively train general coding abilities without ground-truth solutions, and RiVER demonstrates that such a framework can transfer gains to exact-solution benchmarks while improving overall ALE performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for training LLMs typically rely on ground-truth answers to assign rewards, limiting their applicability to tasks where the ground-truth solution is unknown. We introduce a \textbf{R}anking-\textbf{i}nduced \textbf{VER}ifiable framework (RiVER) that trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth solutions, using deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision. When applying group-relative RL to such continuous rewards, we identify two key challenges: \emph{scale dominance}, where uncalibrated score magnitudes across test instances distort policy updates, and \emph{frequency dominance}, where repeatedly sampled suboptimal solutions can outweigh rare but stronger candidates. RiVER addresses these challenges with calibrated reward shaping that uses instance-wise comparisons and emphasizes top-ranked solvers while retaining bounded feedback for other valid solutions. We train on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks and evaluate on Algorithm Engineering Benchmark (ALE-Bench), LiveCodeBench, and USACO. RiVER advances Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9\% and 9.4\% in ALE rating rank. More importantly, despite training exclusively on score-based tasks without any ground-truth solutions, RiVER also improves the backbones across exact-solution benchmarks such as LiveCodeBench and USACO by an absolute average improvement of 2.4\% and 3.5\%. By contrast, baselines trained with raw execution scores improve ALE rating but fail to transfer to exact-solution benchmarks. These results suggest that score-based optimization tasks, combined with proper reward calibration, can serve as effective training environments for general coding ability without ground-truth solutions.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [319] [A unified cell-merge algorithm for generating diverse Voronoi diagrams and new tessellations based on spatial chromatic model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27235)
*Weining Zhu*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: A new cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm uses a spatial chromatic model to unify and retrieve/merge cells from a single database, enabling fast generation of multiple Voronoi diagrams and even novel tessellations without bespoke algorithms for each VD type.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the need for separate algorithms for each VD variation by providing a unified generation framework capable of producing diverse Voronoi diagrams and potential new tessellations.

Method: Introduce a cell-merge Voronoi generation algorithm based on a spatial chromatic model that retrieves and merges cells from a unified database to produce various VD types without developing individual algorithms for each type.

Result: The CM algorithm enables rapid generation of diverse Voronoi diagrams and is particularly suitable for scenarios requiring multiple VD types, such as location-based spatial analysis; it also supports generating new tessellations like competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams that differ from classical VDs.

Conclusion: The CM approach provides a flexible, efficient framework for generating multiple VD types from a single database, simplifying deployment and enabling new spatial tessellations beyond classical Voronoi diagrams.

Abstract: As a type of spatial tessellation model and an important spatial structure of computational geometry, Voronoi diagrams (VDs) are widely used in many fields. Due to differences in generation spaces, types of spatial entities, distance metrics, and relationships between entities and Voronoi regions, Voronoi diagrams vary into many types, such as the ordinary VD, VD on spheres, VD for linear entities, weighted VD, and ordered higher-order VD. These VDs also have their own generation algorithms. In this study, we propose a new cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm based on the spatial chromatic model. The advantage of the CM algorithm is that it can quickly generate diverse VDs by retrieving and merging cells from a unified database, without requiring the development of specific algorithms for each VD. The CM Voronoi algorithm can be particularly applied in cases where a variety of Voronoi diagrams are frequently required for computation and analysis, such as in location-based spatial analysis. Furthermore, the proposed CM method can also generate some new types of spatial tessellations, such as competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams which are different from classical VDs.

</details>
